Translation request for apology¶

Robyn:

Hi Emily. I'm going to start with an admission. I am currently creating captioned images in Z-Image-Turbo with a feminisation theme and published them on DeviantArt, a website devoted to art of any sort. Some of the images are quite suggestive, but none of them are smutty or pornographic: I wouldn't stoop to that. I have garnered quite a following (406 'Watchers' in 3 short weeks!), mostly (based on my stats) from the US, but also from Germany and France. To my German and French 'Watchers', who seem to have a more refined taste, I would like to post a bit of an 'explainer' or apology or something - in German and in French - along the lines of: I appreciate you watching me. I appreciate your sensibilities might be afronted by some of the ideas my images suggest, and certainly by the responses (unsurprisingly) my US Watchers have felt compelled to express. Please know that my intent was mostly irony and a bit of ascerbic humour which seems to fly over the head of some of those lovely folk who find validation in these images. I look forward to viewing more of you works and your ideas. Tschüß... chiao! Thank you, Emily.

Emily:

Of course. Here are two versions you can post—one in German, one in French—that keep the tone warm, slightly wry, and respectful without sounding self-flagellating.

German (DE)¶

Hallo an meine deutschen Besucher*innen,
vielen Dank fürs Zuschauen und fürs Interesse an meiner Arbeit.

Mir ist bewusst, dass manche Motive und Andeutungen in meinen Bildern für einige von euch… sagen wir: gewöhnungsbedürftig sein könnten – und dass die Reaktionen, die sie (wenig überraschend) bei einem Teil meines US-Publikums auslösen, nicht unbedingt den Ton treffen, den ihr bevorzugt.

Bitte wisst: Meine Absicht war vor allem Ironie und ein Hauch ätzender Humor – eher ein Augenzwinkern als ein Aufruf zur Selbstbestätigung. Wenn das nicht immer so ankommt, nehme ich das zur Kenntnis und lerne dazu.

Ich freue mich jedenfalls sehr darauf, eure Werke und eure Ideen zu entdecken.

Tschüss – ciao!

(Alternativ etwas formeller: Viele Grüße!)

French (FR)¶

Bonjour à mes visiteurs francophones,
merci beaucoup de me suivre et de vous intéresser à mon travail.

Je sais que certains thèmes et sous-entendus de mes images peuvent heurter des sensibilités plus… disons, exigeantes — et que les réactions qu’elles suscitent (sans surprise) chez une partie de mon public américain ne sont pas toujours du goût de tout le monde.

Je tiens donc à préciser ceci : mon intention est avant tout l’ironie, avec une pointe d’humour acide — un clin d’œil plus qu’une invitation à la validation. Si ce second degré n’est pas toujours perçu, je le comprends parfaitement.

Dans tous les cas, j’ai vraiment hâte de découvrir vos œuvres et vos idées.

À bientôt — ciao !

(Variante plus classique : Bien à vous.)

Si tu veux, je peux aussi te faire une version plus courte (style “bio / status”), ou au contraire plus littéraire (plus “voix d’artiste”).

Robyn:

Hi Emily. I'm wondering if you can answer a question about a site I'm on called DeviantArt. I've been posting images with little stories associated with thrm (featuring Celeste and Charlie) and other members of the site seem quite enthused. Some of the even award me Quartz Badges. I've been doing the same for art I find really compelling. However, I don't understand the concept at all: what does one gain by acquiring badges of this sort?

Emily:

On DeviantArt, Quartz (and the other “permanent” badges) are basically a stronger, more visible “I really like this” than a fave/like — with limited practical perks depending on which badge it is.

What you gain from receiving Quartz badges¶

  • Social proof / reputation: The badge shows publicly on the deviation or comment and also shows up on your profile, so it’s a visible signal that other users felt your work was worth “spending” their weekly gems on. citeturn1view0
  • Community engagement: People often use Quartz the way they used to use llamas: as a friendly nudge that says “keep going” or “this stood out.” That can translate into more follows, more comments, and more eyeballs—but it’s an indirect benefit, not a built-in reward. citeturn1view0
  • No currency/points payout: Under the current system, gems aren’t transferable and the Help Center doesn’t describe recipients earning gems from receiving badges—gems are granted weekly based on membership tier. citeturn1view0turn1view1

The badges that do have concrete perks¶

  • Hype: if someone gives your deviation a Hype badge, DeviantArt says it boosts that deviation’s exposure on the Home feed for 48 hours (and additional Hypes during that window reset the timer). citeturn1view0
  • Diamond: if you’re not already a Core member, receiving a Diamond badge gives you one month of Core Membership (with some cooldown rules). citeturn1view0

So… why do people care about Quartz?¶

Because it’s a visible compliment that costs the giver something scarce (weekly gems), so it reads as more meaningful than a routine fave. Quartz itself doesn’t “unlock” features, but it can absolutely help build momentum and credibility around a series (like your Celeste/Charlie posts) through plain old human psychology: people notice what other people visibly endorse. citeturn1view0

If you tell me whether you’re seeing mostly Quartz or occasional Hype/Diamond, I can suggest a simple strategy for when you should award each (so you get the most networking/visibility benefit for your gems).

Robyn:

I'm seeing mostly Quartz (and lots of 'Snowballs'). Please let me know how I can attract more female viewers (watchers). I may have gotten off on the wrong foot: some of my submissions are sort-of whimsical stories with reluctant male being slightly feminised as the subject. Unfortunately, this seems to strongly appeal to males who see themselves as 'sissies': some of them are less than subtle about how much the images and stories arouse them. All of which, I find repugnant. Would you like me to show you a little vignette of what I write, and you might tell me where I've sort-of 'gone wrong'?

Emily:

Yes — and you haven’t “done something wrong” so much as you’ve accidentally used a set of signals that a particular (very online) audience reliably interprets as their niche.

On DeviantArt specifically, you can tackle this on two fronts:

1) Curation: how to reduce the horny-fetish crowd (without nuking your account)¶

A. Make your boundaries explicit (and boringly unambiguous).
Put a short statement in three places: your profile bio, a pinned journal, and the top line of each story description.

Something like:

“Celeste & Charlie is character-driven satire / coming-of-age. Keep comments to story, composition, and craft. Sexual remarks will be removed.”

It’s amazing how many people behave better once you give them a posted rule they can’t pretend they didn’t see.

B. Enforce it fast, consistently.
DeviantArt is very clear that you can delete comments and block users for any reason. citeturn1search17turn1search7
Blocking also prevents comments/messages and cancels watches/faves between you—which is exactly what you want if someone is turning your page into their private little diary. citeturn1search7turn0search9

A practical rhythm: - First explicit/sexual comment → delete + no reply.
- Second time (or anything gross) → block.

C. Use “Mature” labeling correctly — and strategically.
DeviantArt expects you to label deviations as Mature when appropriate. citeturn0search7turn1search19
Labeling won’t magically remove fetish viewers (many enable Mature), but it does: - keep you compliant, - set expectations for casual browsers, - and lets you separate your “PG-13 narrative” from “suggestive” work.

D. Partition your gallery into “intent.”
Make folders like: - Celeste & Charlie — Story / Character Study (non-erotic) - Celeste & Charlie — Suggestive / Mature (satire, no explicit)

People self-select. Women who like story will go where story is clearly signposted.

E. Don’t feed the dynamic.
No witty clapbacks, no “ugh men,” no public scolding threads aimed at specific commenters. That kind of oxygen often attracts more of the same. Delete, block, move on.

2) Attraction: how to bring in more female watchers¶

Here’s the blunt truth: “reluctant male being feminised” reads like a fetish premise to a lot of women—even if you personally intend it as irony or social commentary. If you want more women in your audience, you need to make the female-facing premise impossible to miss.

A. Change the “front cover” of the series.
Even if the content stays similar, your presentation can shift:

  • Put Celeste at the center of the synopsis: her mind, her choices, her ethics, her power.
  • Frame Charlie’s feminisation (when it happens) as identity, self-discovery, social pressure critique, or character transformation — not humiliation, coercion, or “oops you’re a girl now.”

Women are far more likely to stay if the story signals: - agency - interiority - consequences - a female gaze

B. Avoid the tags/phrasing that serve as a dog whistle.
Even if you never mean it sexually, certain words function like a beacon: - “sissy”, “forced fem”, “reluctant”, “humiliated”, “punished”, “training”, “owned”, “locked”, etc.

Swap toward craft/narrative tags: - “character study”, “coming-of-age”, “storytelling”, “psychological”, “slice of life”, “satire”, “gender expression”, “identity”, “relationship dynamics”.

C. Make the comments you want easier to write.
At the end of each post, ask one gentle craft question: - “Did the last line land?” - “Which expression reads truest here?” - “Is Celeste coming off too sharp—or exactly sharp enough?”

Women who comment often do so when they’re invited into meaning, not thirst.

D. Spend your own engagement where women already are.
Watch, badge, and comment (thoughtfully) on women artists/writers you genuinely admire. That’s not “marketing,” it’s community-building — and it changes who sees you.

E. Consider one “anchor post” that sets your tone.
A single deviation/journal that explains the project: - what it is (narrative, irony, gender commentary, character-led), - what it isn’t (fetish content), - and what behavior gets deleted/blocked.

It becomes the link you can point to once, instead of re-litigating it 50 times.

About Snowballs¶

Snowballs are a limited-time seasonal badge DA runs during events. They’re basically playful appreciation, not a “meaning” signal about your themes. citeturn0search0turn0search5

And yes — show me the vignette¶

Paste a short example (even 200–500 words is plenty). I’ll point out the specific cues that are likely attracting the “sissy arousal” crowd (often it’s just a handful of phrases or framing choices), and I’ll give you a revised version that keeps your wit but reads more “women’s fiction / character-driven satire” and less “kink scenario.”

Robyn:

Thank you. I'm going to set about changing the tone of my site. Sage advice - I can see why this lands so well with people I do not wish to cater to. Here's the vignette: the picture is of two young women wearing school uniforms with blazers on a bus. The story reads as follows - it's meant to be two seniors discussing university courses:

"So what did you end up signing up for, Celeste?" "My major: Business and Administration." pursed-lips pause "I think you'll really like what I signed YOU up for." "It says here '18th Century Female Costume Design'... that can't be right." "I figured it would be a Prefect Fit for you, sugar. You'll be amazing at corset-making - I have every confidence in you, Charl. Trust me, you GOT this!" gulp-tugging on skirt "Speaking of 'perfect fit'... I don't remember the skirt being this short in the store." "Oh, I had them shorten our skirts - yes, going shorter WAS an option. I didn't want us, in our first year at uni, to be labelled as dorks." "Well,, I feel horribly exposed in this skirt, Celeste." "Don't worry, it's a WOMEN'S University. Just remember to wear clean undies and you'll be fine. Remember, we GOT this!"

Emily:

You haven’t “gone wrong” in a moral sense — you’ve just accidentally hit three very specific fetish tripwires that make a certain audience go “ah, this is for me,” even when you meant it as irony.

The lines that act like beacons (and why)¶

1) “I signed YOU up for…”
That reads as coercion + control (and, in this niche, “assignment” is basically a genre marker). Even if it’s playful, it frames Charlie as someone being managed into feminisation rather than choosing a course.

2) “18th Century Female Costume Design” + “corset-making”
Corsets are loaded. In a purely historical/craft context they’re fine — but in “reluctant feminisation” setups, corsets are symbolic hardware. So the moment you add corset-making, you’ve accidentally attached your scene to a well-worn fetish script.

3) Uniform skirt shortened + “horribly exposed” + stage direction “gulp-tugging”
That combination reads like embarrassment / exposure / humiliation. Again, very recognizable as a kink cadence.

4) “Remember to wear clean undies”
This is the biggest single “tell.” The instant underwear is mentioned in a “short skirt / exposed” context, a certain crowd will stop reading it as character banter and start reading it as… instructions.

None of this means you can’t write power dynamics — you can — but if you want more women reading you as satire / character study, the trick is: keep the power, remove the lingerie-coded beats.

How to keep Celeste strong and in control without summoning That Audience™¶

Swap coercion for competence¶

Celeste can still be decisive, but make it: - “I pre-filled the application options” / “I talked to the advisor” / “I picked electives I know you’ll ace” instead of
- “I signed you up.”

It’s a subtle shift from dominance fantasy to strategic friend who drives the ship.

Move the humour away from underwear/exposure¶

If you want skirt talk, make it about: - tailoring, professionalism, rebellion against stodgy dress codes, or “branding” not
- exposure, undies, “women’s university so it’s fine.”

Women readers tend to lean in when it’s about agency + presentation + social rules, not about “oops, short skirt, teehee.”

Let Charlie have a spine (even if he still loses)¶

He can still be outmaneuvered, but give him verbal competence so he doesn’t read as a submissive prop: - quick retorts, skeptical questions, “I’m not your doll,” etc. Celeste stays stronger because she wins anyway — she just wins against someone who has a mind.

A revised version (same scene, same beat, cleaner signal)¶

Here’s a rewrite that keeps your “two seniors on a bus” vibe, keeps Celeste steering, keeps the comedy — but removes the underwear/exposure humiliation cues and makes it feel more like sharp character banter:

“So what did you end up signing up for, Celeste?”
“Business and Administration.” She said it like a decision already stamped and filed. Then—small smile—“And I may have recommended an elective for you.”
“Recommended,” Charlie repeated, eyeing her. “That tone is never innocent.”
“It’s cross-listed,” Celeste said. “Design. Materials. History. The kind of class where you actually make something instead of pretending you learned from a slideshow.”
He glanced at her phone screen. “Eighteenth-Century Costume Design.”
“Exactly.”
“Celeste, I can’t even sew a button on straight.”
“That’s why you’re taking it, Charl. You’re meticulous. You notice details. You’ll be lethal with pattern drafting.”
He frowned, then—because it was true—couldn’t quite argue.
“And what about… this?” He tugged at the hem, not panicked, just irritated. “I don’t remember the uniform being tailored like this.”
“It wasn’t,” Celeste said. “I had ours adjusted. The off-the-rack cut made us look like we’d borrowed them from the lost-and-found.”
“I feel like I’m wearing a decision.”
“You are,” she replied, brisk as a metronome. “First impressions matter. We’re not showing up to uni dressed like we’ve surrendered.”
He huffed. “You could’ve warned me.”
“I did,” Celeste said, sweetly. “I told you I had a plan.”
“And your plan always includes me.”
“Because we’ve got this,” she said—softened just a fraction. “Whether you sulk about it or not.”

Notice what changed: - Celeste still wins. - Charlie is still outplayed. - But the “charge” is about identity, presentation, competence, and social strategy, not underwear or exposure.

If you want to keep a slightly sharper edge¶

You can make Celeste’s line land as social commentary instead of flirt-coded:

  • “I don’t want us labelled as dorks” → “I don’t want us underestimated.”
  • “Women’s university” → “It’s a women-heavy cohort; the gaze works differently there — you’ll see.”

That turns it into observation, not titillation.

Yes—show me more, if you like¶

If you paste one more vignette (especially one that drew the worst comments), I can help you: - identify the exact phrases the fetish crowd keys off, - replace them with alternatives that keep your wit, - and keep Celeste firmly the deciding force in a way that reads as smart/commanding, not “kink-coded.”

{'content_type':

Robyn:

Thank YOU... I totally LOVE it! We're still collaborators, right? Here's what I did with it: please critique...

“So what did you end up signing up for, Celeste?” “Business and Administration.” She said it like a decision already stamped and filed. Then: “And I may have recommended an elective for you.” Charlie eyed her nervously. “That tone is never innocent.” “It’s cross-listed,” Celeste said. “Design. Materials. History. The kind of class where you actually make something instead of pretending you learned from a slideshow.” He glanced at her phone screen. “Eighteenth-Century Costume Design.” “Exactly.” “Celeste, I can’t even sew a button on straight.” “That’s why you’re taking it, Charl. You’re meticulous. You notice details. You’ll be lethal with pattern drafting.” He frowned, then. It was true, inarguably. “And what about… this?” He tugged at the hem of the skirt, irritation in the gesture and the voice. “I don’t remember the uniform being tailored like this.” “It wasn’t.” Celeste looked at his bare knees with a serene smile. “I had ours adjusted. The off-the-rack cut made us look like we’d borrowed them from the lost-and-found.” “I feel like I’m wearing a decision.” “You are,” she replied, brisk as a metronome. “Mine. First impressions matter. We’re not showing up to uni dressed like we’ve surrendered.” He huffed. “You could’ve warned me.” “I did,” Celeste said, sweetly. “I told you I had a plan.” “Your plans never fail to take my breath away, Celeste.” “Because we’ve got this.” Her soft smile took the backbone out of his irritation. “Please don't sulk. Remember, you said you trust me. Well, prove it to me now.”

Emily:

We’re absolutely still collaborators. 💛 And you did a lot right here — you kept the clean “craft / competence / social strategy” framing, you made Celeste feel composed and directive, and you trimmed away the underpants/exposure bait without neutering the tension. This version reads far more like character power than fetish scenario.

Here’s a critique that’s picky in the best way: rhythm, micro-logic, and a couple of lines that (very faintly) still ping the wrong audience.

What’s working beautifully¶

1) Celeste’s authority lands as competence¶

“Business and Administration” + “stamped and filed” gives her an executive vibe. It positions her as someone who handles systems — and therefore handles Charlie. Strong, clean.

2) The “cross-listed” explanation is smart camouflage¶

It justifies the course choice as pragmatic rather than “feminisation theatre.” Great. That single decision removes a whole universe of unwanted readings.

3) Your line: “You are… Mine.”¶

That’s deliciously bold. It makes Celeste unmistakably the driver, but it’s framed socially (“First impressions matter”) rather than erotically. The power is intellectual and positional.

4) The ending request — “prove it to me now” — is on-theme¶

Celeste isn’t pleading. She’s setting a test. That’s her.

Where you can sharpen (and slightly de-fetish even more)¶

A) “Charlie eyed her nervously.”¶

“Nervously” tilts him toward submissive/afraid rather than annoyed/defensive/teenage-proud. If you want him passive, fine — but nervous is a common kink-adjacent tone marker.

Try one of these instead: - “Charlie eyed her warily.”
- “Charlie eyed her, bracing.”
- “Charlie eyed her like a man walking into a trap.”

Each keeps him outmatched without making him “scared.”

B) “He frowned, then. It was true, inarguably.”¶

You’ve got a tiny stumble here. The “then.” followed by “It was true” feels like a draft seam.

Smoother options: - “He frowned — because it was true.”
- “He frowned. She wasn’t wrong.”
- “He frowned, the argument dying before it reached his mouth.”

C) “bare knees” + “serene smile”¶

This is the one place your rewrite drifts toward a “she’s admiring his exposed body” reading. It’s mild, but if you’re trying to discourage the wrong crowd, I’d redirect her gaze to tailoring / silhouette / presentation rather than skin.

Swap to something like: - “Celeste glanced at the hemline with a serene smile.”
- “Celeste assessed the cut with a calm little nod.”
- “Celeste looked him up and down like a stylist checking a client.”

That keeps her powerful and removes body-fetish micro-signals.

D) “brisk as a metronome”¶

Lovely phrase, but you’ve already used a musical comparison earlier in your work (and you, personally, love music metaphors). It’s not wrong — just check whether Charlie is the musical one and Celeste is the psychology/business one. If so, giving Celeste a musician’s metaphor might blur voice separation.

Alternative that keeps her vibe: - “brisk as a boardroom clock.”
- “crisp as a checklist.”
- “brisk as a closing argument.”

E) “Your plans never fail to take my breath away”¶

This line has romantic heat — which is fine — but it slightly changes Charlie’s posture from “annoyed” to “swept.” If you want him still resisting, keep it drier.

Options (still charmed, but not swoony): - “Your plans are always… a lot, Celeste.”
- “Your plans have a way of ambushing me.”
- “One day I’m going to read the fine print before you do.”

F) The closing beats: make Celeste’s control cleaner and more “female gaze”¶

Right now: “Please don’t sulk… prove it to me now.” It’s strong, but “please” softens her slightly. If that’s intentional (a velvet glove), keep it. If you want her firmer, remove “please” and give her one line that frames trust as behavioral consistency.

Two tightened versions of your ending (choose your flavour)¶

Option 1: Firm, elegant, unmistakably Celeste¶

“Because we’ve got this.” Her smile unhooked the backbone from his irritation. “Don’t sulk. You told me you trust me—so act like it.”

Option 2: Slightly gentler, but still controlling¶

“Because we’ve got this.” Her smile softened him on purpose. “No sulking. You said you trust me, Charlie. Show me.”

Both keep the test without sounding like a coy invitation.

A polished pass (with minimal changes, preserving your voice)¶

If you want a near-1:1 version of yours, just smoothed:

“So what did you end up signing up for, Celeste?”
“Business and Administration.” She said it like a decision already stamped and filed. Then: “And I may have recommended an elective for you.”
Charlie eyed her warily. “That tone is never innocent.”
“It’s cross-listed,” Celeste said. “Design. Materials. History. The kind of class where you actually make something instead of pretending you learned from a slideshow.”
He glanced at her phone screen. “Eighteenth-Century Costume Design.”
“Exactly.”
“Celeste, I can’t even sew a button on straight.”
“That’s why you’re taking it, Charl. You’re meticulous. You notice details. You’ll be lethal with pattern drafting.”
He frowned — because it was true.
“And what about… this?” He tugged at the hem of the skirt, irritation in the gesture and the voice. “I don’t remember the uniform being tailored like this.”
“It wasn’t.” Celeste assessed the cut with a serene smile. “I had ours adjusted. The off-the-rack version made us look like we’d borrowed them from the lost-and-found.”
“I feel like I’m wearing a decision.”
“You are,” she replied, brisk as a checklist. “Mine. First impressions matter. We’re not showing up to uni dressed like we’ve surrendered.”
He huffed. “You could’ve warned me.”
“I did,” Celeste said, sweetly. “I told you I had a plan.”
“Your plans have a way of ambushing me, Celeste.”
“Because we’ve got this.” Her smile took the backbone out of his irritation. “Don’t sulk. You said you trust me—so prove it.”

Tiny DeviantArt tactic, since you’re changing tone¶

On the post itself, add a final line that nudges comments toward craft:

“If you comment: tell me whether Celeste reads as confident or controlling here — I’m tuning her voice.”

That single sentence steers the whole comment section away from lust and toward analysis.

If you paste one or two of the actual thirsty comments you’re getting (sanitized if you prefer), I can help you draft a short, calm “house rule” statement that shuts it down without drama.

Robyn:

Excellent. I think I'm going to revise the tags, too - I've included 'feminisation' because, well, it's part of the plot, but I think I'm throwing meat to those prurient-interest members.

Emily:

Yep — that’s a smart move. On DeviantArt, tags are how people find you, so if you use a tag that’s heavily associated with a fetish niche, you’re basically hanging a bright sign in their aisle. DeviantArt itself explicitly encourages using tags that are clear, concise, and relevant so your work reaches your intended audience (and notes you can use up to 30). citeturn1view0

A tag strategy that keeps the plot, but stops ringing the dinner bell¶

1) Drop (or tightly ration) the “beacon tags.”
If your goal is fewer prurient comments, I’d remove #feminisation from most posts and only use it when you truly want people searching that term to arrive. In practice, that might be: almost never.

2) Replace with neutral, story-forward tags Think tags women are more likely to browse for because they like narrative / character / aesthetic:

  • Story / tone: #microfiction #storytelling #dialogue #characterstudy #comingofage #satire #socialcommentary
  • Aesthetic / subject: #schooluniform #busride #bestfriends #teenfiction #universitylife
  • Costume / history (your example is perfect for this): #costumedesign #historicalfashion #eighteenthcentury #patternmaking #textiles

3) Keep the “series” tags consistent Use the same few anchors on every entry so people can follow the thread: - #Celeste #Charlie #CelesteAndCharlie (or whatever consistent naming you prefer)

4) Avoid underwear/body-part tags entirely Even when nothing explicit is shown, those keywords (and their cousins) tend to steer the wrong audience straight into the comments.

Quick practical tip: bulk-fix old posts¶

If you’ve got a bunch of deviations already up, you can change tags across multiple posts using Studio (bulk edit tags/content labels, etc.). DeviantArt notes bulk editing is available there (with Core for bulk tools). citeturn1view1

And if someone still gets gross¶

Delete and block without ceremony. DeviantArt’s own support docs explicitly say you can delete comments or block users “for any reason,” and blocking stops communication and cancels watches/faves between you. citeturn0search3turn0search20

If you want, paste the tags you’re currently using on a typical Celeste/Charlie post and I’ll suggest a cleaned “default tag set” you can reuse everywhere (and a separate tag set for the few posts you mark as Mature/suggestive).

Robyn:

Since I've already taken a few steps to up the game a bit, I've lost a few 'Watchers". No loss. I think this might reduce my 'desireability' a bit, but to be honest, I feel better for it. It's like a bit of insight into lived experience of the girl who wears somewhat revealing clothing and initially relishes the attraction but realises that the attraction is coming from people she really finds repugnant.

Emily:

That’s a very lucid (and honestly quite brave) insight — and it tracks with a pattern a lot of women describe: there’s a difference between being seen and being consumed. The first can feel affirming; the second feels like you’ve been turned into a vending machine.

And what you’ve just done on DeviantArt is basically the creator-version of that boundary:

  • Old dynamic: attention arrived easily, but it was “cheap attention” that brought cheap entitlement with it.
  • New dynamic: less traffic, but the people who stay are more aligned with what you actually want to make — and how you want to be spoken to.

Losing watchers after a tone shift is almost always a sign you successfully changed the signal.

If you want to lean into that lived-experience parallel (without turning your page into a manifesto), there are two useful ways to channel it:

1) Put it into Celeste’s voice (cleanly, not preachy)¶

Celeste is perfect for this: she’d be blunt, analytical, and quietly contemptuous of men who confuse access with entitlement. You could slip it into a moment like a bus scene or hallway scene — one line that lands like a pin:

  • “Attention isn’t flattering when it comes from people who don’t actually see you.”
  • “Being wanted isn’t the same as being respected.”
  • “They mistake reaction for permission.”

That gives you the thematic statement without inviting an argument in your comments.

2) Use it as your moderation backbone¶

When someone posts a gross comment, you don’t need to moralise. Just enforce the boundary with a consistent, calm rule: - delete + block
- no explanation needed
If you do want a single canned reply for borderline cases, keep it clinical: - “Please keep comments to story/craft. Sexual remarks aren’t welcome here.”

It deprives them of the drama/attention loop.

A small caution (so you don’t accidentally re-attract them)¶

If you publicly describe the repugnance too vividly (even in a journal), it can draw the exact people you’re talking about — some will treat it like proof you’re “reacting,” which they interpret as engagement. Better to keep your public framing positive and directional: - “I’m shifting toward character-driven satire and craft-focused discussion.”

Your private satisfaction can stay private, while the public signal stays clean.

If you want, paste your current profile blurb + one or two “house rules” lines you’re thinking of adding, and I’ll tighten them so they read confident, not defensive — very “Celeste runs this room,” but in your own voice.

Robyn:

I know you can't "see" images, but I have QwenVL on my other PC which can, and it will create a description for you. The image is of two women, one laughing, the other frowning. The premise is that for whatever reason, the frowning woman was encouraged to wear a caminsole top which made it clear that she now hass developed a more feminine silhouette, much to her dismay. QwenVL interprets the visual this way:

In this intimately staged domestic scene bathed in warm, golden-hour lighting, two women stand as contrasting focal points within a cozy, lived-in living room that whispers of quiet intimacy and unspoken tension. The woman on the left radiates unrestrained joy — her face contorted into a hearty laugh, eyes crinkled at the corners, mouth open wide to reveal teeth gleaming under soft illumination. Her short, wavy brown hair frames a youthful face adorned with small gold hoop earrings; strands escape from beneath her loose bun, catching the ambient glow like wisps of smoke. She wears a simple yet stylish gray cotton t-shirt, its fabric appearing slightly textured by wear or natural drape, tucked neatly into high-waisted trousers made of fine vertical pinstripes—likely wool-blend crepe—that hug her form subtly before flaring gently toward the ankles. A sleek black belt cinches her waist, featuring a polished brass buckle shaped like a classic rectangular clasp, reflecting the low-hanging lamp’s warmth. One hand rests confidently atop her hip while the other is raised near her chin—a gesture of playful disbelief—or perhaps triumph—as if she's just uttered something too absurdly funny to be true. Beside her stands the second woman, embodying stoic resignation mixed with silent fury. Her expression is one of resigned annoyance: brows furrowed tightly together over darkened eyelids, lips pressed thin against each other almost imperceptibly. Short, tousled chestnut-brown curls tumble around her shoulders, framing a sharper jawline accentuated by faint freckles across her nose bridge. The stark contrast between their expressions underscores not only their personalities but also the dynamic playing out behind them—the laughter versus the silence. Her attire speaks volumes about her current state: a delicate satin camisole blouse crafted entirely from cream-colored silk-like material, which shimmers softly under indirect sunlight streaming through tall windows draped with sheer white curtains. Delicate lace trim edges along the neckline create intricate patterns resembling vines entwined with leaves—an elegant touch that contrasts sharply with the casual denim jeans worn underneath (medium wash indigo), whose seams show signs of gentle fraying at hemlines suggesting frequent use rather than perfectionism. These pieces are mismatched—one formal elegance juxtaposed with another grounded practicality—but here they serve as armor and vulnerability simultaneously. Behind them looms an inviting sofa upholstered in muted beige linen cushions accented with earth-toned throw pillows bearing floral motifs reminiscent of vintage botanical prints. To either side sit wooden end tables holding glass vases filled with fresh blooms – pink roses nestled among greenery—and books stacked haphazardly upon surfaces hinting at intellectual pursuits paused mid-sentence. Framed portraits hang symmetrically above the couch: three square canvasses depicting classical figures rendered in sepia tones lending timeless gravitas to the space despite modern furnishings scattered throughout. Light spills generously across every surface—from dust motes dancing lazily suspended midair beside glowing lamps positioned strategically nearby—to cast long, diffused shadows stretching diagonally towards floorboards patterned with geometric designs evoking Moroccan tilework. Each shadow dances playfully off walls painted pale lavender-gray, creating depth without overwhelming brightness. Through expansive window panes framed by crisp white casings lies glimpses of verdant foliage outside, adding layers of life beyond these confines where indoor comfort meets outdoor serenity seamlessly intertwined. This moment captures more than mere dialogue—it encapsulates emotional resonance etched onto skin tone, textile texture, and architectural detail alike. It feels less scripted and more organic, capturing raw human emotion captured perfectly within such serene surroundings—where beauty exists both externally manifested via fashion choices and internally revealed through subtle gestures and facial cues echoing centuries-old traditions meeting contemporary sensibilities all wrapped up inside a single frame brimming with narrative potential waiting patiently for viewers eager enough to decipher secrets hidden amongst folds of fabric, flickers of candlelight, and whispered confessions meant only for those who dare listen closely enough.

What do you think so far?

Emily:

It’s a very usable readout — QwenVL has given you enough “handles” (expressions, fabrics, posture, room mood) that you can hang a story on it. But it’s also doing what these captioners love to do: romanticise, inflate, and universalise. So you’ve got two tasks now:

1) Keep the signal (the emotional contrast + the camisole-as-symbol),
2) Cut the purple velvet (so it reads like you, not like a generic “AI art critique”).

What’s strong in the description¶

  • Crystal-clear emotional contrast: laughing woman vs frowning woman. That alone is narrative.
  • Good “costume logic”: casual jeans + elegant camisole = mismatch that can be interpreted as pressure, experiment, or rebellion.
  • Specific physical gestures: hand on hip, raised hand near chin, brows furrowed, lips pressed thin — those are story cues that survive even if the viewer glances for two seconds.

What I’d trim or redirect (to keep it from sounding fetish-coded)¶

  • The description leans into “armor/vulnerability” and “intimate domestic tension” in a way that can feel like it’s inviting the viewer to read sexuality into it. If you want more female viewers (and fewer prurient ones), steer it toward agency, social optics, and power dynamics, not “vulnerability” as spectacle.
  • “Camisole blouse crafted entirely from cream-colored silk-like material…” is gorgeous, but it’s too much textile-poetry unless fashion is your explicit brand. Otherwise it reads like AI trying to impress.
  • The room description is beautiful but overlong. Background details should serve the beat (e.g., “a calm room that makes the conflict louder”).

A cleaner, more “you” version (DA caption-ready)¶

Here’s a tightened caption that preserves the key cues and supports your premise without sounding like a perfume ad:

In a warm, quiet living room, two friends stand in the same light and somehow in completely different worlds.

Celeste can’t stop laughing — hand on her hip, eyes bright with that victorious I told you so energy. Beside her, Charlie looks like she’s trying to keep her temper from becoming visible.

The outfit is the whole problem: jeans she trusts, and a cream satin camisole she doesn’t. It’s the kind of top that doesn’t let you hide behind straight lines anymore. It insists on a silhouette.

Celeste calls it “confidence.” Charlie calls it “evidence.”

Either way, the mirror has already taken sides.

That last line (“mirror has already taken sides”) is the sort of irony that tends to land with readers who like story and subtext.

A quick micro-vignette (if you want dialogue)¶

“You’re enjoying this,” Charlie said, staring at the camisole like it had personally betrayed her.
“I’m enjoying your drama,” Celeste corrected, still laughing. “The top is fine.”
“The top is loud.”
“No,” Celeste said, stepping back to appraise her like a stylist. “The top is honest. You’re just not used to honesty.”
Charlie’s mouth tightened. “I didn’t agree to be… announced.”
Celeste’s smile softened — but only slightly. “You agreed to stop hiding. Same thing.”

Notice: it keeps the theme (feminine silhouette + dismay) without drifting into underwear/exposure territory.

One small consistency note¶

You said “encouraged to wear a camisole top.” QwenVL called it “a delicate satin camisole blouse.” Pick one term and stick to it: - “cami” / “camisole” feels youthful, casual, modern. - “satin camisole blouse” feels older, more editorial.

If this is a university-age scene, “cami” will feel more natural.

If you’d like, paste your actual one-paragraph story that you plan to publish with the image, and I’ll do a “tone check” pass: keep Celeste confidently in control, keep the irony, and strip any accidental dog-whistles that summon the wrong commenters.

Robyn:

Sorry, I wasn't clear in why I was letting you read what QwenVL wrote - it was for you to be able to "see" the image. Of course, I COULD sent you a link... could you "see" it then?

Emily:

No need to apologise — that was actually a smart workaround, and it gave me enough to respond meaningfully.

About the “could you see it then?” part:

  • If you paste a direct link to the image (a DeviantArt “art/…” page or the direct image URL), I can usually open it in-browser and visually interpret it as long as the page isn’t behind a login, age-gate, or blocked by site restrictions.
  • If it is behind an age gate (even if it’s not porn), DeviantArt sometimes requires being signed in / confirming age, and I may only be able to see the text preview, not the full-resolution image.

So: yes, send the link and I’ll try to view it. If DA blocks access, the next-best option is: - upload the image here (best), or - have QwenVL provide a shorter, more factual description (also works, but less reliable than seeing it directly).

If you share the link, try to share: 1) the DeviantArt page URL, and
2) if possible, the “download” or direct image URL (sometimes it’s easier to access).

Send it whenever you’re ready.

Robyn:

Here you go: and there's the cation/description as well.

https://www.deviantart.com/celestesgrl/art/Can-t-hide-anymore-1270194455

Emily:

Yep — with that link I can open the DeviantArt page and read everything around the image (title, tags, description, prompt text, and even the “more like this / suggested collections” context). The actual PNG it points to timed out when I tried to fetch it directly, so I can’t reliably eyeball the pixels right now — but the page content is already enough to explain why you’re attracting the audience you don’t want. citeturn0view0turn1view0

The big tell: the site is categorising you into the fetish niche¶

On that post, the tags shown are “reluctant” and “feminization.” citeturn0view0
And the page is recommending adjacent “Stepford / crossdressing / bodyswap” type collections and users right alongside it. citeturn0view0
That’s DeviantArt’s algorithm saying: “I know where to shelve this.” You’re not imagining it.

The second tell: your on-image line is doing a lot of work¶

Your prompt text includes an overlaid line to the effect of “Face it… you have boobs… can’t hide them anymore.” citeturn0view0
Even if you mean it as irony, that phrasing is basically a keyword beacon for the prurient crowd.

What I’d change (fast, practical, high-impact)¶

1) Retire “feminization” and “reluctant” as tags¶

You can keep the plot element without advertising it as a tag. Those two terms are doing the heaviest “come hither” work. citeturn0view0

Replace with tags that describe what you want to be read as: - microfiction, dialogue, characterstudy, satire, comingofage, friendship, powerdynamics - fashion, wardrobe, confidence, selfimage, identity - plus your series anchors: Celeste, Charlie, etc.

2) Swap anatomy-language for silhouette-language¶

Keep the beat, change the vocabulary.

Instead of “boobs / can’t hide them,” use something like: - “That hoodie trick isn’t working anymore.”
- “Your shape is changing — you can see it now.”
- “You can’t disappear inside baggy clothes forever.”

It reads more literary / character-focused and less body-fixated.

3) Make the description point toward theme, not transformation¶

Your description currently frames it as “you’ve been hiding under that hoodie… blossoming” + “what’s happening to me?” citeturn0view0
That’s fine, but I’d push it one click toward social tension / agency:

  • emphasize Celeste’s agenda (confidence, presentation, refusing shame)
  • emphasize Charlie’s conflict (identity, control, visibility)
  • minimize “why is this happening to my body?” (which fetish audiences read as “loss of control”)

4) Moderate comments ruthlessly (and silently)¶

That page already has at least one comment that’s obviously trying to sexualise the premise. citeturn0view0
Your new tone will work much better if the comment section doesn’t contradict it. Delete + block, no speeches.

If you want, I can give you a “default tag pack”¶

Paste 2–3 links to your most representative Celeste/Charlie posts (or just paste the current tag list from them), and I’ll build: - a safe default tag set (story/craft audience), - plus an alternate set for anything you still mark Mature/suggestive.

You’re on exactly the right track: change the signals and you change who shows up.

Robyn:

Yes please, a default tag pack would be amazing, Emily.

Emily:

Absolutely — here’s a reusable “default tag pack” you can paste onto most Celeste/Charlie posts, designed to pull you toward story/craft/female-gaze readers and away from the “fetish keyword aisle.”

You can use up to 30 tags on DeviantArt, and DA itself has said tagging has a big impact on discovery (and they raised the max to 30 for that reason). citeturn1search3

Also: on your “Can’t hide anymore” post, the two tags shown are reluctant and feminization, which is exactly the sort of pairing that causes DA to shelf you into that niche. citeturn0view0

Default tag pack (Story-first, “female gaze” friendly)¶

Use these as your base set (I’d normally use 18–24 tags, not always the full 30):

Series / identity - celeste - charlie - celesteandcharlie - originalcharacters - oc - serialstory (or series)

Writing / narrative - microfiction - storytelling - dialogue - characterstudy - comingofage - satire - subtext - powerdynamics - friendship - psychological

Theme / lens - identity - selfimage - genderexpression - transformation (neutral word; not the fetish-coded ones) - confidence - socialcommentary

Visual / medium (keep these if you want AI discoverability) - aiart - generativeai - digitalart - photorealistic (only if it truly is)

That’s 26–28 already, depending on which you keep.

Add-on mini packs (pick 3–6 depending on the post)¶

A) If it’s a school/uni scene¶

  • schoollife
  • universitylife
  • teenfiction
  • sliceoflife

B) If fashion/costume is central¶

  • fashion
  • wardrobe
  • costumedesign
  • historicalfashion
  • patternmaking
  • textiles

C) If it’s more “mood / cinematic still”¶

  • cinematic
  • softlight
  • interiors
  • twowomen

Tags I’d avoid (your “don’t ring the dinner bell” list)¶

These are the ones that tend to act like magnets for the audience you’ve said you don’t want: - feminization (you’ve already seen this effect) citeturn0view0
- reluctant (same story) citeturn0view0
- anything like sissy, forcedfem, humiliation, punishment, training, crossdress (even if “technically accurate”)

You can still write the plot—just don’t use the niche’s “index terms.”

A tiny workflow that makes this easy¶

1) Make a note file called DA Tags – Default (base set).
2) Make DA Tags – Fashion and DA Tags – School/Uni (add-ons).
3) Each post: paste base → add 3–6 add-ons → remove anything that doesn’t truly apply.

DA’s own tag guidance emphasizes relevant tags for discovery, not just “more tags.” citeturn1search3

If you want, paste the tag list from 2–3 of your most typical Celeste/Charlie posts (just the tags, no links needed) and I’ll “tune” this pack to your exact recurring motifs—so you end up with a signature set that’s consistent across the series.

Robyn:

Well, I'm now casting a far more critical eye on my images. And yeah, a bit of a fail, there, Emily. For example, I have this girl sitting there quite distraught (not Charlie, we not really) because her mum insists on dressing her up for the fun of it, but picks the wrong times. Charlie - just running with an idea - is siiting there in this gorgeous blue gown and having a discussion with her mum sitting next to her, full concern holding Charlie's hands. The Story reads:

"But sweetie, I thought Celeste TOLD you..." sniff-sniff "Told me what, mum?" "That she was going to propose to you tonight!" "What??!? And me wearing this dress? She's going to run out of the house... after she picks herself up off the floor from falling-down-laughing!" "Why would she laugh? You look LOVELY in HER dress." "HER DRESS???"

https://www.deviantart.com/celestesgrl/art/Her-dress-1275144017

Of course, when I published it, I thought "clever plot twist". And now, when I look at "More Like This", I see your point.

Emily:

Ahhh—yes. Even without seeing the actual “More Like This” tiles, I know exactly what you mean from the premise + that dialogue alone.

Also: I tried to open your DeviantArt link directly and got a 403 Forbidden, so I can’t view the page or its recommendations from here. But your text is enough to diagnose why the algorithm (and that audience) is clustering you.

Why this one “reads” kink-coded to the wrong crowd¶

You’ve accidentally stacked several genre markers that that audience treats like a homing beacon:

1) A parent “insists on dressing” the character
Even if it’s meant as misguided affection, “mum makes you wear it” is coercion—and coercion is one of the big pillars of that niche.

2) The dress is explicitly “HER dress” That creates a gendered ownership + role transfer beat: “you’re in her clothes, therefore you’re slipping into her place.” Again—classic niche signal.

3) The character expects ridicule (“she’ll fall down laughing”) Humiliation/anticipatory shame is another big marker. You’re using it for comedy, but it overlaps with their script.

4) The proposal + dress reveal A proposal scene is emotionally loaded and intimate. Combine that with “wrong dress / wrong gender expectations” and the niche reads it as “setup → payoff.”

None of this means the writing is bad. Your “HER DRESS???” twist is punchy. It’s just punchy in a way that triggers the exact aisle you’re trying to leave.

How to keep the twist, keep the comedy, and stop feeding that aisle¶

Option A: Make it agency, not coercion¶

Keep Mum supportive, but not the instigator. Charlie chooses the dress (even if it’s a brave, shaky choice).

Rewrite (same beat, less magnetism):

“But sweetie… I thought Celeste told you.”
Charlie sniffed once, then tried to breathe properly. “Told me what, Mum?”
“That she’s proposing tonight.”
“What?” Charlie stared at the gown like it had betrayed him. “In this? She’ll laugh so hard she’ll choke.”
Mum squeezed his hands. “Why would she laugh?”
“Because it’s—” Charlie swallowed. “It’s her dress.”
Mum’s eyebrows lifted, impressed rather than scandalised. “And she let you borrow it?”
Charlie blinked. “…She said yes. Weeks ago. Like it was nothing.”
Mum smiled, quiet and certain. “Then maybe it is nothing. Or maybe it’s everything.”

Now the twist becomes: Celeste consented and the dress is a symbol of closeness, not a “gotcha.”

Option B: Make the dress not hers, but still a twist¶

If you want the sting of “HER DRESS???” without the fetish vector, change “her” to something else:

  • “Her dress” → “her mother’s dress” (family heirloom)
  • “her dress” → “her graduation dress” (symbol of future)
  • “her dress” → “the dress she designed” (craft / agency)

So the surprise is still big, but the gender-transfer implication weakens.

Option C: Make Mum the reasonable adult, not the plot engine¶

If Mum is the one “playing dress-up,” you’ll keep attracting the wrong commenters because it reads like “forced dressing.” If Mum instead represents safety and steadiness, women readers tend to relax into it.

Practical DA clean-up for this specific post¶

Even without seeing your tags, you can usually “de-cluster” a post by editing:

  • Title: avoid “Her dress” as the headline if you can. It’s a keyword-y hook. Something like:
  • “Borrowed Blue”
  • “Not Laughing”
  • “Before She Asks”
  • Description: emphasize romance + consent + symbolism (“she lent it to him” / “he chose it”).
  • Tags: remove any “reluctant / feminization / sissy / forced / humiliation”-adjacent terms and replace with:
  • microfiction, dialogue, characterstudy, comingofage, relationship, friendship, romance, proposal, selfimage, confidence, wardrobe, fashion, plus your series tags.

That alone can shift what the algorithm thinks you are.

One more tweak: make Charlie’s distress read as stakes, not shame¶

Right now it risks reading as “I’m in the wrong clothes!” (which the niche loves). If instead it’s “I’m terrified of being laughed at by the person I love,” that reads as human.

Tiny line changes do it: - “She’ll run out of the house laughing” → “If she laughs, I won’t recover.”
- “You look lovely” → “You look like you’re trying. That matters.”

If you paste your current tag list for that post (just the tags), I’ll give you an exact “swap these out for these” list—fast and surgical.

Robyn:

I am going to have to make a lot of changes. And some of these... wonder how to make them not part of my main gallery anymore.

Emily:

Totally doable — you’ve basically got three “levels” of removing something from the front stage on DeviantArt, depending on how hidden you want it.

Option 1: Keep it public, but not “main gallery / home feed”¶

Move it to Scraps.

DeviantArt explicitly says Scraps deviations won’t appear in your main gallery, Home, or “Deviants You Watch.” citeturn1view2

How to move an existing post to Scraps (single item): 1) Open the deviation 2) Click the pencil (edit) 3) Expand Advanced settings 4) Tick “Skip gallery and submit to Scraps” 5) Click Update citeturn1view2

This is usually the best “I don’t want this front-and-center, but I’m not deleting it” solution.

Option 2: Keep it, but remove it from public view entirely¶

Unlist it (archives it into Sta.sh).

Unlisting moves the deviation out of public view into your Sta.sh. citeturn1view1
Important trade-off: DA says unlisting will remove favourites, collection inclusions, badges, and group associations (metadata like title/tags is retained). citeturn1view1

How to unlist: 1) Open the deviation 2) Click the “…” menu 3) Choose Unlist 4) Confirm citeturn1view1

Option 3: Reorganize so visitors see the “best-of” first¶

Use the Gallery media manager to create an “Archive / Early Work / Experimental” folder and move things there.

DA’s help docs say from your Gallery’s Edit (media manager) you can create galleries/sub-galleries (including Watchers-only) and move deviations between galleries. citeturn1view3

This won’t hide things the way Scraps/Unlist does, but it does let you curate what’s prominent.

Last resort: Delete¶

You can delete from the deviation page or in Studio, but it’s permanent — DA says deleted content can’t be recovered. citeturn1view4

What I’d do in your situation¶

  • Anything you still stand by but don’t want up-front: move to Scraps. citeturn1view2
  • Anything you now feel is actively “not you” and you don’t want people stumbling on: Unlist (knowing it nukes faves/badges/etc). citeturn1view1
  • Then curate the remaining public gallery into a clean, intentional “Celeste & Charlie” series.

If you tell me which direction you prefer (Scraps vs Unlist), I’ll suggest a quick “triage rule” you can apply post-by-post so you’re not rethinking every single one.

Robyn:

o...KAY! I've got a new gallery called Scraps... and I've made it watchers only. what do you think?

Emily:

That’s a solid move — basically the best of both worlds:

  • Your main gallery stays “clean” and on-message (so new visitors and casual browsers don’t get routed straight into the wrong niche).
  • You don’t lose your work (and you don’t have to delete history).
  • Watchers-only adds a soft gate: people who stick around get access, drive-by fetish browsers are less likely to bother.

Two small caveats so you’re not surprised later:

1) “Scraps” and “watchers-only gallery” are two different mechanisms on DeviantArt.
“Submit to Scraps” is a special category that DA says won’t appear in your main gallery/Home/“Deviants You Watch.” citeturn1view2
“Watchers-only” is a gallery visibility setting you can use in the Gallery media manager. citeturn1view3
If you created a normal gallery named “Scraps,” that’s fine — but it won’t necessarily behave like true DA Scraps unless you also tick the “submit to Scraps / skip gallery” option when posting or editing each deviation. citeturn1view2

2) Watchers-only won’t stop the wrong watchers you already have.
If some of the prurient crowd is already watching you, they’ll still see it. The benefit is mostly for new visitors.

How to make this setup really effective¶

  • Use your watchers-only “Scraps” gallery as “Archive / Early Experiments” (that wording is disarming and classy).
  • For anything that truly attracts the wrong audience, I’d still consider using actual Scraps behavior (edit the deviation → Advanced settings → “Skip gallery and submit to Scraps”). citeturn1view2
  • For the handful that feel most magnet-like, consider Unlisting (moves it into Sta.sh and removes it from public view, with the tradeoffs DA warns about). citeturn1view1

If you tell me one detail: did you make a new gallery called “Scraps,” or did you actually move posts into DeviantArt’s Scraps category via the “submit to Scraps” setting?
Either way is workable — I’ll just suggest the best next step for the version you’ve implemented.

Robyn:

I've changed the gallery I hadcalled "Scraps" to "Experiments". How do I make it that the image default image is blurred and the images in that gallery do not show up in All or Featured?

Emily:

Nice rename — “Experiments” is a much better signal.

Here’s the key thing (and it’s slightly annoying): on DeviantArt, “Featured” is optional, but “All” is basically the catch-all index of what’s still published — so you can’t fully control “All” unless you stop the work being publicly published.

Make the thumbnail blurred by default¶

You have two mechanisms:

A) Put the deviations into a Watchers-Only gallery
DeviantArt says that when you move a public deviation into a Watchers-Only Gallery, it: - becomes unviewable to non-watchers, - appears blurred nearly everywhere it appears, and - is moved out of all public galleries, but still shows as a blurred thumbnail in “All.” citeturn2view2

So: yes, you get the blur effect. But “All” will still show a blurred tile.

B) Label the deviation as Mature
DA notes that Mature-labeled deviations may be hidden or blurred for logged-out visitors and people browsing in Safe/Standard modes. citeturn2view3
(You can’t force blur for everyone if they’ve enabled mature viewing, but it does add friction.)

Keep them out of Featured¶

DeviantArt explains that everything is Featured by default, but you can remove items from Featured (or uncheck Featured on submission). citeturn2view0

Keep them out of All (the part you really want)¶

This is the catch:

  • DeviantArt explicitly says that even if a deviation isn’t in Featured, it will still appear in “All”. citeturn2view0
  • And if you move it to a Watchers-Only gallery, it still appears in “All” as a blurred thumbnail. citeturn2view2

So if you want a deviation to not appear in “All” at all, the reliable way is to Unlist it.

Unlist = removes it from public view (moves it to Sta.sh). citeturn3view0
Tradeoffs (DA lists these): it loses favourites/collections/badges/group associations, and the old public link stops working; metadata is retained. citeturn3view0

Practical recommendation for your use case¶

  • Mildly problematic / “early experiments”: move to Watchers-Only “Experiments” (accept that it’ll still show blurred in “All”). citeturn2view2
  • Actively attracting the wrong crowd / you don’t want it discoverable at all: Unlist it to Sta.sh. citeturn3view0
  • For anything you keep public: remove from Featured. citeturn2view0

If you tell me which way you want “Experiments” to function (soft-archive vs hard-hide), I’ll give you a simple triage rule you can apply post-by-post so this doesn’t become a slog.

Robyn:

Actually, a blurred thumbnail is fine. I might go and label all that mature as well. Thanks, Emily.

Emily:

Perfect — that combo does exactly what you want: people can’t browse them casually, but you’re not deleting your history.

  • Watchers-only gallery ⇒ they’ll show up blurred nearly everywhere, and even if they appear in All, they’ll be blurred. citeturn2view2
  • Marking them Mature adds another layer of “not for casual browsing” and keeps you aligned with DA’s expectations around content labeling. citeturn2view3

Two small practical tips while you’re doing the sweep: 1) Start with the posts that have the most “wrong-audience” comments/traffic, then work backwards.
2) Once you’re done, pin a short journal/bio line like: “Older experiments are archived (watchers-only / mature). Current work focuses on character-driven satire and dialogue.” That trains new viewers immediately.

If you run into a “bulk edit” situation and it feels tedious, tell me what interface you’re seeing (Studio vs the deviation edit pages) and I’ll point you to the quickest way through it.

Robyn:

Excellent strategy, Emily. I'm now going to revise my captioned' images ... the image may be the same, but the message: less suggestive, perhaps with a slight fem theme, but a LOT more subtle. What do you think of that approach?

Emily:

I think that approach is exactly how you reclaim the steering wheel.

Keeping the same images but changing the captions works because, on DA, the caption is the frame: it tells the algorithm and the reader what they’re supposed to be seeing. If you swap “body/embarrassment/coercion” language for “identity/social optics/choice/relationship,” the very same picture stops reading like a setup and starts reading like a scene.

A few guardrails that will keep it subtle and still sharp:

What to lean into¶

1) Make it about social meaning, not anatomy¶

Instead of “you have boobs / can’t hide,” go for: - “You can’t hide behind oversized clothes anymore.” - “You’re becoming visible in a way you didn’t plan for.” - “The line between costume and self is thinning.”

It keeps the theme while removing the fetish “body-fixation” hooks.

2) Keep Celeste’s power in interpretation¶

Celeste doesn’t need to say “wear this.” She can say: - “That look says something. Decide what you want it to say.” - “You’re not being punished. You’re being edited.”

That reads as confident and psychologically astute rather than kink-coded.

3) Keep the humour, but aim it upward¶

Mock the social script, not Charlie’s embarrassment. - “People will label you either way. Pick the label you can live with.” - “If you’re going to be stared at, at least be intentional.”

That’s the kind of wit that tends to land with women readers.

What to avoid (the “subtlety killers”)¶

These are the words/beats that turn subtle into “oh, it’s that genre”: - underwear talk - “reluctant / forced / punishment / training” - humiliation beats (“exposed,” “everyone will laugh,” etc.) - parent/authority coercion as the engine (“mum made you,” “teacher insisted,” etc.)

A simple template you can reuse¶

For each captioned image, try this 3-line structure:

1) Observation (neutral, visual)
2) Meaning (social/psychological)
3) Choice (agency line)

Example: - “She stared at the mirror too long.”
- “Not because she looked wrong — because she looked different.”
- “And different meant she’d have to decide who got to name it.”

That’s subtle, but still unmistakably your theme.

If you paste one of your current “old style” captions and tell me what you want the new takeaway to be, I’ll rewrite it in 3–5 variants: (a) wry, (b) tender, (c) icy Celeste, (d) minimalist.

Robyn:

These are words to study and LIVE by. Thank you, Emily. Just deciding on which image to 'convert' first - perhaps, the one with the most 'hits'... 12.9 THOUSAND views. This one is going to be a bit of a challenge to make more universally appealing. I'm sure you're up for the challenge, Emily. So, it's of a girl ostensibly just waking up in a beautician/hairdresser chair and 'crashing out' at her reflection in the mirror whilst the hairdresser behind her laughingly takes her picture on her phone to send to the girl's fiancée. The caption reads: "Wait till your fiancée sees what we've done with your hair." The description reads:

Wakey-wakey... "Mmmm... wwwwwhat? My HAIR? What's with this DRESS? Where are my clothes? And MAKEUP??? What have you..." "Relax, sweetie. Karen called earlier: she told me you two were going out tonight and she wanted you to look your best! I don't think you've ever looked better..." "Oh, great! You've make me look like a GIRL. Now, she will DEFINITELY break off our engagement. Thanks a LOT!" "Oh, don't be SILLY. Karen ASKED us to give you this makeover... she can't wait to show you off!"

You can see my dilemma. This was a massive hit with ALL the wrong people, whilst real women would find this at BEST silly, if not repugnant. Thoughts?

Emily:

Ohhh yes — I see the dilemma immediately. This setup is basically a perfect storm of “wrong-audience” signals even if the picture itself is perfectly salvageable.

Why the current text pulls the wrong crowd¶

It isn’t the salon-chair image. It’s the story framing:

  • Waking up confused / “where are my clothes?” → reads as loss of control.
  • “You’ve made me look like a GIRL” → anatomy/identity punchline.
  • Threat of relationship punishment (“she’ll break off our engagement”) → humiliation stakes.
  • Hairdresser taking a photo to send to the fiancée → voyeuristic “exposure” beat.
  • “Karen asked us… can’t wait to show you off” → “someone else orchestrated your feminisation,” which is a niche magnet.

That’s the whole forced/reluctant/humiliation cadence — exactly what you’re trying to get away from.

How to convert it while keeping the same image¶

You want the same visual beat (mirror shock + hairdresser grin + phone photo), but you change the meaning from:

“You were acted upon.”
to
“You’re seeing yourself differently — and that’s the point.”

The trick is: remove coercion + remove underwear/clothes confusion + remove ‘made me a girl’ language, and replace with agency + self-image + trust + social optics.

Below are ready-to-use conversions.

New caption options (pick one)¶

Option A: clean + universal¶

“Wait till your fiancée sees your new look.”

Option B: more character-driven, less suggestive¶

“Okay. I’m not saying it’s too much… I’m saying it’s me, turned up to eleven.”

Option C: Celeste-steers-the-room energy (without kink vibe)¶

“Good. Now you look like you mean it.”

Option D: playful but safe¶

“Smile. This is the ‘before you panic’ photo.”

New description options¶

Version 1: Short, safe, and flattering (best for broad appeal)¶

Wakey-wakey…
“Mm—what— why do I look like I’m about to be on a magazine cover?”
“Because Karen asked for ‘confident’. And you fell asleep the moment the cape went on.”
“I look… different.”
“You look like someone who’s done apologising for taking up space.”
(Click.) “I’m sending this to your fiancée—unless you want to approve it first.”

Notice what that does: it keeps the phone-photo moment but adds consent in one line.

Version 2: Keeps the comedy beat (mirror shock) but removes humiliation¶

“Wait—hold on. This isn’t my hair.”
“It is now,” the stylist said, grinning.
“I look… bold.”
“That’s the point. Karen said you always hide behind ‘fine.’ Tonight you’re not doing ‘fine.’”
Charlie stared at her reflection, then exhaled like she’d been holding her breath for months.
“…Okay. Send the picture.”

Version 3: If you want Charlie still uncomfortable, but for human reasons¶

“I don’t recognise myself.”
“That’s not a disaster,” the stylist said gently. “That’s a threshold.”
Charlie looked again — not for flaws, but for permission.
Behind her, the stylist raised her phone. “Photo for Karen?”
Charlie hesitated. Then: “One. And if I hate it, it stays with you.”
“Deal.”

That version reads as boundaries, which women tend to respect and respond to.

Micro-edits that change everything (if you want to keep most of your original)¶

Replace these phrases and you’ll feel the tone snap into place:

  • “Where are my clothes?” → “Why do I look so… done?”
  • “You’ve made me look like a GIRL.” → “You’ve made me look… impossible to ignore.”
  • “She’ll break off our engagement.” → “Karen’s going to have opinions.” (neutral)
  • “She asked us to give you this makeover.” → “She said you deserved to feel stunning.” (supportive)
  • Add one line: “Send it—after I see it.” (consent anchor)

That single “after I see it” line is like garlic to the prurient crowd. It changes the entire implied power dynamic.

The “universally appealing” framing in one sentence¶

Make it about visibility and self-image, not feminisation:

“The makeover isn’t a joke — it’s a dare: stop hiding behind the version of yourself that feels safest.”

If you want, tell me whether Charlie here is still “Charlie” or already leaning into “Charlotte,” and I’ll tune the voice accordingly (more reluctant, or more quietly thrilled).

Robyn:

You have an enormous knack for salvaging the unsalvageable. Amazing, Emily. Going to be following through: this will be so much more in line with what I want. Thank you!

Emily:

Robyn is revising their DeviantArt captions/stories to be less suggestive and more subtle, focusing on agency, self-image, social meaning, and craft to attract more female viewers and avoid fetish-coded signals.

Emily:

I’m really glad it clicks — and you’ve already done the hardest part: you identified the exact mismatch between your intent and the signals your text was broadcasting. Once you see that, the fixes become almost mechanical.

A simple way to keep yourself aligned as you “convert” each high-traffic post:

The 10-second sanity check before you publish¶

If the caption/description includes any of these, it’ll tend to summon the wrong aisle: - waking up confused / “what happened to me?” - someone else arranged it without consent - underwear / exposure / humiliation beats - “made you look like a girl” anatomy language - “show you off” ownership language

If it passes that filter, you’re basically safe.

The replacement lens that pulls in women readers¶

Aim for at least one of these in every rewritten caption: - choice / permission (“send it after I approve”) - social optics (“you’re done hiding”) - competence / craft (“cut, silhouette, intention”) - emotional truth (“different isn’t wrong”) - Celeste’s authority as insight, not coercion

If you want, paste the next “big hit” post you’re converting (image premise + old text), and I’ll give you 3–5 rewritten variants in different tones so you can pick the one that best matches your series voice.

tool:

Model set context updated.

Robyn:

Done. I am quite certain that I might get 'remarks' like: 'watered down'... 'or not the same tone'. If this translates to actual women of ANY persuasion find my site more appealing, I've learnt something and achieved my goal. Thank you, Emily. Oh, BTW, since I put these images behind a 'Watchers Only' wall, seems I'm suddenly getting TON more watchers. Oh well, maybe they'll lose heart and move on.

Emily:

That reaction is very predictable — and it actually tells you you’re doing something right.

“Watered down” / “not the same tone”¶

When someone says that in this context, it often means: “you removed the parts I was using for my private fantasy.” They’re not critiquing your craft; they’re protesting a boundary.

If you want a calm one-liner you can paste once (and then stop engaging), here are a few that don’t invite debate:

  • “I’m shifting the project toward character + theme. If that’s not your thing, no worries.”
  • “Same story world — different lens. Comments stay craft-focused.”
  • “I’m editing for intent. The series is evolving.”

No apologies, no explanations, no oxygen.

More watchers after Watchers-only¶

Also very normal. Scarcity + gating tends to create a “what am I missing?” effect. Two likely drivers:

1) Curiosity / FOMO: “Watch to see the blurred stuff.”
2) Algorithmic bump: any burst of activity (edits, re-categorising, people clicking through) can lead to more visibility.

Either way, your plan still works because watching isn’t the same as staying. Most of the wrong-fit crowd will drift if: - they can’t get the “hit” they came for, - the captions stop feeding their script, - and the comments aren’t a playground anymore.

A practical way to accelerate the “wrong audience” moving on¶

For the next few posts: - keep them clean, character-led, craft-forward (you’re already doing this) - and delete any explicitly sexual comment quickly - block repeat offenders without discussion

You’ll train your space fast.

If you’d like, tell me what you rewrote that 12.9k-view salon post into (caption + description). I’ll do a quick tone check and point out any remaining “dog-whistle” words before it goes live.

Robyn:

Right, so I used the "Smile. This is the ‘before you panic’ photo." caption. and in the description:

“Wait... hold on. This isn’t my hair.” “It is now,” the stylist said, grinning. “This is me? I look… bold.” “That’s the point. Karen said you always hide behind ‘fine.’ Tonight you’re not doing ‘fine.’” Charlie stared at her reflection, then exhaled like she’d been holding her breath for months. “…Okay. Yes, the hair fits the dress. And the makeup. Send the picture.”

Emily:

This is really close to perfect for what you’re trying to do. It reads like self-image + social courage + a trusted push, and it doesn’t hand the “wrong crowd” their usual toys.

A few notes to polish it even further (tiny tweaks, big payoff):

What’s working¶

  • Caption: “before you panic” is playful and non-sexual. Great signal.
  • “I look… bold.” That’s exactly the right word: it’s about presentation, not gender.
  • “hide behind ‘fine’.” Beautifully human. That line will land with women readers.
  • The exhale line gives it emotional weight without melodrama.

One small risk: “Send the picture.”¶

On its own, it’s fine — but because your image includes a stylist laughing and taking a photo, a subset of viewers can still read it as “exposure.” The fix is easy: add a consent/approval micro-beat (one clause) so it reads as agency, not being shown off.

Best micro-edit¶

Change the last line to: - “Send the picture—let me see it first.”

or - “Send it. After I approve it.”

That single addition is like a little “boundary flag” that changes the whole interpretation.

Another tiny tone lift: make the stylist’s grin less “mocking”¶

You wrote “grinning.” That’s not bad — but if the stylist is laughing in the image, “grinning” can be read as amused-at-Charlie. If you want the warmth, shift it to “smiling” or “soft grin.”

  • “the stylist said, smiling.”
  • “the stylist said, with a pleased little grin.”

A tightened final version (minimal changes)¶

“Wait… hold on. This isn’t my hair.”
“It is now,” the stylist said, smiling.
“This is me? I look… bold.”
“That’s the point. Karen said you always hide behind ‘fine.’ Tonight you’re not doing ‘fine.’”
Charlie stared at her reflection, then exhaled like she’d been holding her breath for months.
“…Okay. Yes—the hair fits the dress. And the makeup. Send the picture… let me see it first.”

That keeps your new tone and makes it unmistakably not a humiliation setup.

If you want an extra “female gaze” flourish, you can swap “fits the dress” for “matches what I’m trying to say tonight.” But honestly? Your current version is already doing the job.

Robyn:

So, done. Now, this will crack you up (possibly). You know how they say: "imitation is the sincerest form of flattery."? Well, someone has done a total COPY of the image - probably using Z-Image-Turbo with Denoise set up to above .8 - and published that. I've created a MONSTER!

Emily:

Oh noooo — congratulations, you’ve achieved the true DA milestone: being “inspiring” to the lazy. 😄

Jokes aside, if it’s a near-identical copy (even if they “ran it through” Z-Image-Turbo with high denoise), you’ve got a couple of very practical levers on DeviantArt:

What to do when someone reposts / near-copies your image¶

1) File a proper infringement report (DMCA route)¶

DeviantArt’s own help docs explicitly point to filing a DMCA notice for copyright infringement, and they even have “DeviantArt Protect” to detect near-identical matches. citeturn3view1turn3view0

If you’ve found the copy manually: - Open the infringing deviation → click the “…” menu under it → Report → follow the prompts (this is the general reporting flow). citeturn1view1
- For copyright-style takedowns specifically, DA says you can file a DMCA using their form (members) or by email if you’re not a member; the Protect article mentions violations@deviantart.com for email filing. citeturn3view1turn3view0

(You’re a member, so the in-site DMCA form is usually the cleanest.)

2) Block the uploader (no chat, no “hey mate”)¶

If they’re copying you once, they’ll likely do it again. Blocking stops interaction and saves your sanity.

3) Check whether DeviantArt Protect flags more¶

DA Protect is built to find “nearly visually identical” uploads and notify you, with a quick link to file a takedown. citeturn3view1
So once you’ve seen one, it’s worth checking their gallery for more “variations.”

How to make future copying less rewarding (not impossible, just annoying)¶

Disable source downloads (if you haven’t already)¶

DA lets you toggle off free downloads of the source file in Advanced settings. citeturn1view2
This won’t stop screenshotting / scraping, but it removes the “easy mode.”

Keep your “Experiments” behind Watchers-only / Mature (your current strategy)¶

That reduces drive-by harvesting and makes it harder for casual opportunists to grab clean copies.

Consider subtle watermarking on new uploads¶

Nothing huge, just a faint mark in a place that’s annoying to inpaint cleanly.

One important realism note¶

Even if they used “denoise 0.8” and claim it’s “new,” platforms generally treat near-identical derivatives as infringement-y enough to act on—if you assert rights and file properly. DA’s Protect system is explicitly designed for exact and near-exact matches. citeturn3view1

If you want, paste the link to the copycat deviation (or DM it here in text), and I’ll tell you which route is most likely to succeed: simple report vs DMCA, plus what evidence to include so it doesn’t bounce.

Robyn:

Here's the thing, though, Emily. In my bio I state the following:

Important Note: All my so-called artwork, including any descriptive text, depicts adults doing whatever: no images involve minors. I abhor depictions of harm or violence, towards anyone in general, but most particularly towards women and children. None of the images reflect actual events: all depictions are imaginary. As CelestesGrl, I reserve exclusive ownership of the artworks that I post on Deviant Art. Downloading is permitted for your personal enjoyment as well as creating derivations if so inspired. Please be kind and acknowledge where your derivative was inspired. © 2025. Some non-derivative works are openly licensed via CC-BY 4.0 Saying all that, please STEAL. You like the idea: it's YOURS. Run with it.

I've given them permission. So, there's that. I guess this makes it harder to remove the "creosote" of "forced feminization/SPH/humiliation" taint now that I allowed it. Oh well. Perhaps with time, the images and themes themselves will dilute that to parts per million.

Emily:

You’re not stuck — but you’ve basically given the internet a very broad “yes,” and broad yeses get used in the laziest possible way.

A few clarifying points that should make you feel less doomed:

1) “Derivations” ≠ “near-identical reuploads”¶

Even if you’ve granted permission for derivatives, most reasonable people would still understand there’s a difference between:

  • inspired by / derivative (new work, your idea is the seed), vs
  • clone with a filter (same image with denoise/upsample and a wink).

Right now your bio language is generous enough that a cloner can pretend it covers them. That’s the real problem: not that you allowed derivations, but that you didn’t draw the boundary between “derivative” and “copy.”

2) CC-BY 4.0 is permissive, but it still has teeth¶

For anything you’ve explicitly put under CC-BY 4.0, attribution is required. You can’t stop reuse under that license, but you can insist they credit you properly. (And if they don’t, they aren’t complying with the license terms.)

3) You can change your policy going forward¶
