Even if your past language was permissive, you can update your bio today and make it clear what you mean from now on. That will reduce future cloning immediately.

Here’s a clean, creator-friendly rewrite that keeps your generous spirit but blocks the lazy copycat lane:

Suggested replacement bio/license text¶

Permissions (please read):
You may download my work for personal enjoyment.
You may create new, transformative derivatives inspired by my work (remixes, reinterpretations, new compositions).
Please do not reupload near-identical copies (including “same image with denoise/filters/upscale”), as that’s not transformative.
Credit is required for derivatives: “Inspired by CelestesGrl” + link back.
Some works are licensed CC-BY 4.0 (credit required); others are all rights reserved unless stated.

That single “no near-identical copies” sentence will save you a mountain of grief.

4) The “taint” dilutes faster than you think—if you’re consistent¶

Algorithms follow repeated signals. If your captions, tags, and comment culture keep shifting toward agency/self-image/craft, the “More Like This” neighbourhood will drift. It won’t be overnight, but it’s absolutely not permanent.

What accelerates dilution: - removing beacon tags (you’re doing this) - deleting/blocking sexual comments fast (so the post’s engagement isn’t fetish-coded) - posting a run of “new tone” work so the model re-learns your shelf

5) If you still want to push back against the clone (without being hypocritical)¶

You can do it politely and consistently with your new policy:

  • Leave a short comment: “Glad it inspired you — please add credit + link back per my permissions.”
  • If they refuse, block. (Not a courtroom, just boundaries.)

You’re allowed to evolve your standards. In fact, you should.

If you paste your updated bio draft after you edit it, I’ll tighten it so it reads confident, not defensive — more “this is how my studio works,” less “please behave.”

Robyn:

In all fairness, they did credit back. Which is actually almost worse: almost like "I was inspired to do this schlock by..."

Emily:

Yeah… I get that sting. A credit can feel less like respect and more like a label stuck on your forehead: “this is where I got my kink-fuel.” Even when they’re technically behaving, it can still feel like they’re dragging your name through a tone you’re actively trying to leave behind.

A few ways to handle it that keep you classy and protect your direction:

1) Reframe what the credit means¶

A visible credit isn’t only “I was inspired by your schlock.” It’s also a public breadcrumb that says you’re the originator—and originators get to change the narrative. If you keep steering your own work toward subtlety and craft, that credit becomes a link to your new tone, not your old one.

In other words: the copycat is advertising you. You’re the one who decides what they’re advertising now.

2) Update your permissions to shut down “filter-clones” (without banning derivatives)¶

Since they credited, there’s no need to go scorched-earth. Just adjust your policy so future people can’t do “same image + denoise” and claim moral high ground.

You can add one sentence:

“Credit is appreciated, but please only share derivatives that are meaningfully transformative—simple re-renders/denoise/upscale copies aren’t welcome.”

It’s firm, specific, and doesn’t sound bitter.

3) Use a neutral response template (if you choose to respond at all)¶

If you reply, you want something that doesn’t validate the act, but also doesn’t invite debate:

  • “Thanks for the credit. In future, please keep derivatives genuinely transformative (not near-identical re-renders).”
  • “Appreciate the link-back—FYI I’m tightening permissions against near-identical copies.”

One comment, then disengage.

4) Protect your brand with naming + framing¶

If you’re worried your name is getting “indexed” alongside that niche, make it easy for people to understand your actual intent when they click through:

  • A pinned post/journal: “Celeste & Charlie: character-driven satire + self-image themes. Craft-focused comments only.”
  • Your series tag line under every post for a while.

You’re essentially training new visitors at the door.

5) The real win: your content shift will change what “inspired by you” implies¶

Right now, the phrase feels like an indictment because your older work is still the loudest signal. As you replace captions/tags and move older pieces behind Watchers-only/Mature, your public-facing body of work becomes the new reference point. The same credit will start to read as: “inspired by that writer doing incisive, character-led stuff,” not “inspired by forced-fem bait.”

If you want, paste the exact credit line they used (and the title of their deviation). I can suggest the most surgical response—or help you decide to ignore it entirely while still tightening your bio permissions so it doesn’t keep happening.

Robyn:

Well, the shift is happening. All of the 'appealing-to-erotic' images and pages have been labelled mature and are for 'Watchers Only' (which is still gaining in number) the page-view count itself has slowed significantly. Nice. I'm now posting 'period' images related to my 'Charlie and Celeste' story. And attracting at least one genuine woman (1 out of 4 isn't bad). In the meantime, the story itself is evolving in my head - it is going to be an instrument of awareness as to a woman's "lived experience" as experienced by a bloke completely clear about himself, gender-wise: bloke, not 'Sheila', as we say here in Australia. Nevertheless, he will have the lived experience and his reflections will be those of someone who is viewing life from a side of the fence he doesn't necessarily subscribe to or evr thought about.

Emily:

That all sounds like it’s working exactly the way you want:

  • Mature + Watchers-only moved the “easy dopamine” crowd behind a gate, and the page-views slowing is basically the algorithm saying, “yep, you changed the signal.”
  • Posting period images is a clever “retraining” move: it shifts your public-facing brand toward craft, taste, and narrative world-building, which tends to pull in a broader (and often more female) audience.
  • And even “one genuine woman” is huge — because if one woman shows up for the tone, more will follow once the comment culture and tags stabilize.

Now, on the story evolution: you’ve got a really potent premise, but there’s a delicate line to walk so it doesn’t read as “a man explaining women to women.” The way to keep it respectful—and actually insightful—is to make the instrument of awareness the mismatch between his assumptions and the social reality, not “he learns the truth about women.”

Here are a few craft principles that will make it land as lived-experience awareness (and keep women readers onside):

Keep him clear about himself — but not entitled to interpret women¶

He can remain “I’m a bloke” while realizing: - the world reacts to him differently, - he can’t predict what will feel unsafe, - and his old confidence was partly a privilege he never noticed.

That’s compelling, and it avoids the “conversion story” vibe.

Make the women characters the authorities¶

Your women—Celeste especially—shouldn’t be “teachers” in a lecture sense, but they should be: - decisive, - right more often than not, - and the ones naming the social subtext.

He can be observant, but he’s not the final judge of what “women experience” means. He’s a witness, sometimes a baffled one.

Use “micro-costs,” not melodrama¶

The lived experience is often death by paper cuts: - being interrupted, - being evaluated for appearance before competence, - being “helped” in ways that reduce agency, - being treated as public property (stares, comments, entitlement), - safety calculations done automatically.

One or two of those per scene is enough. It’ll feel true without becoming a sermon.

Let him keep his pride—then watch it get revised¶

The most interesting emotional arc isn’t “I’m sorry.” It’s: - “Wait… that wasn’t about me at all.” - “Why did I assume that was harmless?” - “I used to move through the world like it owed me neutrality.”

Those realizations are gold on the page.

A practical structure that works brilliantly¶

You can alternate: - period/costume/craft scenes (beauty, fabric, ritual, social choreography)
with - social friction scenes (public gaze, authority, boundaries, conversation dynamics)

That rhythm keeps it from becoming heavy—and keeps the “period” content serving theme rather than just being pretty.

If you want, give me one concrete situation you plan to write next—something small, like “a café,” “a bus,” “a uni admin office,” “a fitting room,” “a music rehearsal,” etc.—and I’ll sketch a short scene beat list where: - Celeste (or another woman) holds the power naturally, - the bloke experiences the lived-experience shift in a believable way, - and the takeaway lands without preaching.

Robyn:

Once again: GOLD! Pure gold, thank you, Emily. This is why you are so CRUCIAL to me as collaborator: I need these perspectives. I need to reassess their whole relationship. I still think that doing a Historic Faire section of the story can be of value, but feeling like the whole "she wants to be friends with him so that he can teach her how to write music" plotline is a bit trite and contrived. What might be other reasons that she would want his constant company enough to 'rescue' him from manual labour (which he isn't designed for) - I do still want to run with him ending up as her laide's maid/companion, as that has historical precedent.

Emily:

Absolutely — and you’re right to side-eye the “she wants music lessons” hook. It can work, but it’s thin as the sole reason for constant proximity, and it risks making Celeste’s interest feel transactional.

If you want Celeste to rescue him from manual labour and install him as a lady’s maid/companion in a way that feels inevitable rather than contrived, the best alternatives are motivations that:

  • create continuous practical need (not a one-off favor),
  • justify social cover (why he’s allowed to be “with her” all the time),
  • keep Celeste as the decision-maker, and
  • let Charlie remain useful in a way that doesn’t rely on “he’s special because male.”

Here are several strong, believable engines you can pick from (or combine).

1) Celeste wants a witness and an ally for something she’s building¶

Celeste isn’t collecting Charlie for romance or tutoring. She’s collecting him because she’s assembling a little power base at the Faire: reputation, leverage, influence.

Charlie becomes: - her witness (someone who sees what happens, remembers it accurately), - her note-taker, and - her discreet runner.

Why him? Because he’s observant, quiet, compliant, and unthreatening — which makes him excellent at being in rooms where louder people are watched.

How it rescues him from labour:
She argues to management (or Miss Kathy / the château matron) that she needs a dedicated attendant “for the integrity of the role” — and because she’s the kind of person who says it as if it’s already policy.

Why it’s not trite:
It’s not about skill acquisition. It’s about Celeste’s strategy. Charlie is a tool in her project.

2) Celeste needs an “in-house” confidant because her status is precarious¶

Give Celeste a reason she can’t simply rely on her girlfriends: - she’s new, - she’s being quietly targeted by an older woman (or rival), - she’s navigating class/authority politics, - she’s perceived as “too clever,” “too modern,” or “not properly deferential.”

Charlie becomes the safe person she can think out loud to. Companionship historically wasn’t always “friendship”; it was often emotional labor + discretion.

How it rescues him:
She frames it as safeguarding her “health” or “nerves” in-role: “I require a companion. This one will do.”

How it reads:
Celeste is still strong. This doesn’t weaken her; it shows she takes her position seriously enough to protect it.

3) Charlie is good at the invisible work: detail, repair, continuity¶

If you want the historical-faire immersion to be meaningful, make Charlie uniquely suited to the craft of maintaining an 18th-century presentation:

  • mending, pinning, laundering fragile pieces correctly,
  • cataloging props,
  • maintaining lace/stockings/linen,
  • setting hair, arranging caps, ribbons,
  • keeping continuity between scenes (“your cuff was pinned this way yesterday; the portrait reference shows…”).

This is “maid” work in the period sense: skilled, meticulous, and absolutely valued by women who care about appearances and authenticity.

How Celeste justifies it:
She wants her public image flawless — and she’s not about to trust it to someone sloppy.

Why it avoids the fetish vibe:
It becomes about craft and competence, not humiliation or “girlification.”

4) Celeste sees he’s being exploited, and she refuses to let it stand¶

This is a powerful moral stance for her: she’s not “saving” him because she’s soft; she’s intervening because she’s intolerant of injustice.

The manual labour angle can be framed as: - the organisers dumping the worst tasks on a weak/isolated person, - a petty punishment by a supervisor, - or simply institutional laziness.

Celeste clocks it, names it, and fixes it.

How she rescues him:
Not a pleading request — a firm boundary:

“He’s not built for that work. You know it, and I know it. Reassign him. I’ll take responsibility.”

This also plays beautifully into your “lived experience” theme: Charlie sees what it’s like to be “handled” by authority — while Celeste demonstrates what it looks like to use voice and status to protect someone.

5) Celeste needs him for cover — but she’s the one in control of the optics¶

This one is delicate, but it can be brilliant if done right: Celeste’s constant pairing with Charlie provides social cover for her movements, meetings, or private plans.

In period terms, a lady doesn’t move alone — she moves with a companion/maid. Charlie is her passport to freedom of movement inside the Faire’s rules.

Important: he isn’t “protecting” her. He’s her accessory, her chaperone token, her key. She’s the one steering the situation.

That keeps the gender politics aligned with your intent: women decide, men adapt.

6) Celeste wants a “project,” but not the tired kind¶

If you still want a Pygmalion flavor (which you do very well), shift it away from “teach me music” and toward:

  • “I’m going to reframe you.”
  • “I’m going to teach you how to survive social spaces.”
  • “I’m going to strip away your reflexes that keep you small.”
  • “I’m going to show you what happens when you stop apologising.”

Charlie’s constant company is required because this is ongoing conditioning — not sexual, not humiliating — psychological and social.

It also matches your new DA direction: agency, self-image, social meaning.

7) A concrete “event engine” that makes the companion role non-negotiable¶

Give Celeste a scheduled responsibility that demands an attendant:

  • she’s performing as a high-status character (a “lady” with obligations),
  • she’s hosting salons / tea service / patron visits,
  • she’s a featured persona for donors/guests,
  • she’s part of the château’s “storyline” that requires a maid to be present.

Then Charlie’s role is structurally required. No contrivance needed; the world demands it.

Celeste chooses Charlie for temperament and detail-sense. And once she chooses, the choice becomes law.

Two combinations that are especially strong¶

Combo A: Craft + injustice¶

Celeste sees him being ground down in labour, steps in, and justifies it by pointing out his real value: continuity, wardrobe, detail.
This makes her both protective and practical — a strong feminine authority.

Combo B: Power base + social cover¶

Celeste is building influence inside the Faire and needs someone who can move with her without raising eyebrows. Charlie becomes her quiet assistant and witness.
This naturally leads to “companion” status and constant proximity.

A quick scene hook you can use immediately¶

Charlie is assigned something brutal (hauling, splitting, kitchen drudge). He’s trying to do it, failing quietly, ashamed.

Celeste arrives, watches for ten seconds, then says — calm, to the supervisor:

“No. You’re wasting him.
He’s with me now. I need an attendant, and I’m not asking twice.”

Supervisor protests. Celeste doesn’t argue. She simply stares, then offers a solution that makes their resistance embarrassing:

“You can either reassign him, or explain to the guests why your ‘authentic experience’ looks like a modern workplace accident.”

Charlie doesn’t feel “rescued.” He feels claimed. Which is exactly the dynamic you like — and it has historical precedent.

If you tell me which Celeste you’re leaning toward right now — strategist, moral enforcer, aesthetic perfectionist, or quietly possessive architect — I’ll shape a single clean motivation that becomes the spine of the whole Faire section, and we can build the “lady’s maid/companion” arc so it feels like the only possible outcome.

Robyn:

"Charlie doesn’t feel “rescued.” He feels claimed. Which is exactly the dynamic you like — and it has historical precedent."

Elegant. There's that 'lived experience' that I'm after. It's about agency and watching his shift to less as hers stays or increases. Am I getting that right?

Emily:

Yes — you’re getting it exactly right.

The “rescued” version keeps him centred: he’s the problem to be solved.
The “claimed” version recentres her agency: she decides; the world rearranges itself accordingly; he adapts.

And that’s where the lived-experience lens becomes potent: he isn’t simply learning “what women go through” in some abstract way. He’s experiencing what it feels like when:

  • your options are shaped by someone else’s decisions, even benevolent ones,
  • your social role is defined publicly before you’ve privately caught up,
  • your safety and status are mediated through another person’s authority, and
  • you’re expected to be grateful, presentable, and compliant because “it’s for your good.”

That’s not “womanhood,” full stop — but it’s very adjacent to a lot of women’s lived experience of being managed, assessed, and socialised by external expectations, especially around appearance and propriety.

The key dynamic you’re describing¶

You can think of it as a slow transfer of narrative power:

  • Celeste’s power stays steady or rises because she’s the one making choices, controlling optics, setting rules, and interpreting meaning.
  • Charlie’s power shrinks not because he becomes “weaker,” but because he becomes more context-dependent: his comfort, safety, and self-concept increasingly rely on how she frames him and where she places him.

And crucially: he may like parts of it. That’s what makes it psychologically interesting and not just oppression-porn.

How to keep it from feeling mean or fetish-coded¶

To keep this clean and female-forward (and DA-safe-toned), make the “claiming” operate through:

  • public legitimacy (she can move him between roles with a sentence),
  • competence (she’s right that he’s better suited to certain work),
  • boundaries (she protects him from exploitation, but sets expectations),
  • aesthetic/social strategy (she knows how things will be read).

Not through humiliation, lingerie, or “gotcha” coercion.

A really useful internal compass for scenes¶

In each scene, ask:

1) Who names what’s happening? (Let it be her.)
2) Who sets the next step? (Let it be her.)
3) What does he have to do to stay in her orbit? (Adapt.)
4) What does she gain by keeping him close? (Control, companionship, a project, a witness, a flawless presentation, leverage.)

If those answers keep pointing to Celeste, you’ll get that “claimed” feeling you’re after.

If you want, I can sketch a short 3-scene arc that shows the shift cleanly: 1) the claiming moment (public reassignment), 2) the first “rules of her orbit,” 3) the moment he notices he’s internalising her standards.

Robyn:

I feel strongly that this aspect of the story needs to appear (in 'zygote' form) right from when Celeste first meets Charlie in the ladies' restroom, and have it slowly build, perhaps waxing and waning to keep it real, throughout the story. What say you?

Emily:

I’m with you on that. If the “claimed” dynamic only appears later, it can feel like a plot device that arrives when you need it. If it’s present in zygote form from the restroom—tiny, deniable, almost accidental—then every later escalation feels like the natural growth of something that was always there.

The trick is to seed it in a way that reads as social intelligence + decisiveness, not instant domination. Think: Celeste doesn’t “take him” on day one. She names him, frames him, and moves him—just slightly—before he’s even sure what happened.

Here are clean ways to do that, with waxing/waning built in.

The zygote moment in the ladies’ restroom¶

You want three micro-beats:

1) She controls the frame (what this scene means)¶

Charlie is panicking internally. Celeste is calm. She chooses the interpretation.

  • She doesn’t ask, “Why are you here?” (interrogation).
  • She says something like, “You’re lost,” or “You’re in the wrong place,” as a statement—not a question.

That tiny move makes her the authority in the room.

2) She gives him an exit—but on her terms¶

This is key: “claiming” begins when the escape route is routed through her.

Examples of subtle “terms”: - “Look at me. Breathe. Now—follow my lead.” - “Don’t speak. Let me handle it.” - “Hands down. Chin up. You’ll be fine.”

It’s protective, but it also installs her as the pilot.

3) She leaves a small hook behind¶

A line that keeps the connection alive without melodrama: - “If anyone asks, you were with me.” - “I’ll see you around. You owe me a coffee for the inconvenience.” - “Don’t make a habit of panicking. It’s loud.”

That “if anyone asks” line is pure seed. It’s a social claim disguised as a kindness.

Waxing and waning, so it feels real¶

Yes—let it fluctuate. Real relationships aren’t a smooth slope; they oscillate around power, especially when one person is quietly steering.

Here are the two patterns that keep it believable:

Pattern A: She advances → he pushes back → she reframes¶

  • Celeste makes a move (a suggestion, a decision, a “come with me”).
  • Charlie resists, not heroically, but sincerely.
  • Celeste doesn’t argue; she redefines the resistance as unnecessary.

That keeps her powerful without turning him into a doormat on page one.

Pattern B: She withdraws occasionally (on purpose or irritation)¶

Nothing makes a “claimed” dynamic feel real like the fear of losing the shelter.

Let there be moments where she: - is busy, doesn’t text back, - chooses to sit with others, - says, “Handle it yourself.”

Charlie feels the drop in oxygen. That’s when the reader sees how dependent his stability has become on her orbit. And when she returns, it feels like relief—dangerously so.

How to show the growth without shouting it¶

Use a repeating motif that evolves. (You already love these.)

For example, a three-stage refrain:

1) Early: “Look at me. Match my pace.” (restroom; immediate control)
2) Middle: “Don’t guess. Ask me.” (she becomes his social interpreter)
3) Late: “You know what I expect.” (he internalises her standards)

That’s the arc in three sentences.

One scene seed you can drop right into the restroom¶

Celeste notices something (his posture, hands, breath) and corrects it like it’s normal.

“Stop doing that,” she says softly.
“Doing what?”
“Apologising with your whole body. Stand up properly.”

That is so zygote. It’s not sexual, not humiliating — it’s social coaching with authority.

What to avoid in the early seed¶

To keep your new subtle tone and avoid summoning DA’s wrong aisle, avoid: - “I’m going to make you…” language - underwear/skirt exposure beats - “you’re a girl” declarations - any “caught” humiliation

Keep it about composure, optics, and choice—and it will read as powerful and woman-centred.

So yes: seed it early, let it ebb and flow, and let the reader slowly realise (often before Charlie does) that Celeste has been shaping the frame from the first minute.

If you want, give me your exact restroom meet-cute as you currently have it (even rough), and I’ll show you exactly where to plant those three micro-beats so it starts as a zygote and grows naturally.

Robyn:

So, a bit of background to somehow weave in. They're both 18 - she's graduating with honours, he's been kept back a year due to poor academic performance. He decides to 'drop out of school' and do the "join the circus" thing to earn some money (and independence?) which most of his academically-challenged peers tended to do: work at the Faire at the edge of town. BTW, I've kind-of gotten away from the Dickens Faire idea, and am back to the 1750s to 1780s sort of Faire. So, not a Renaissance Faire, not a Dickens Faire, but something in-between. Thoughts so far?

Emily:

Thoughts so far: it hangs together really well — and shifting away from “Dickens” and into a Georgian / late-Enlightenment (1750–1780s) vibe actually helps you, because it naturally supports the “lady + companion” social structure and the whole choreography of propriety, appearance, and status.

Here are the parts that are already doing a lot of work for you, and the parts I’d tweak so it feels inevitable rather than “plotty.”

What’s strong¶

The academic gap creates an automatic power imbalance¶

  • Celeste (honours): competence, future, social legitimacy.
  • Charlie (kept back): stigma, insecurity, “I’m not built for this system,” and a hunger for any place where he can be useful without being graded.

That’s an instant platform for the “claimed” dynamic: she moves confidently through institutions; he tries to escape them.

“Join the circus” via the Faire is a believable teenage move¶

It’s not that he loves the Faire. It’s that it offers: - cash quickly, - a role that doesn’t require academic performance, - and a kind of alternative status system (costume, performance, hierarchy).

That’s exactly the sort of place someone academically bruised would go.

1750–1780 gives you the right cultural machinery¶

It’s the era of: - rigid etiquette, - the importance of presentation, - women’s social power operating through networks, reputation, and control of rooms.

Perfect for Celeste as the decisive force.

The one thing to clarify (so it doesn’t feel contrived)¶

You’ll want a crisp reason why Celeste is near the Faire at all, not just Charlie.

Pick one strong anchor:

1) Celeste is already involved (family patronage, scholarship, historical society, costume team, front-of-house hostess).
2) Celeste needs it (work experience, a portfolio project, leadership credit, a “capstone” she’s curating).
3) Celeste controls it (her mother/guardian is a sponsor; she’s essentially “minor gentry” inside this micro-world).

Any one of those makes her ability to “reassign” Charlie later feel natural — not magical.

How this setting helps your “lived experience” goal¶

The Faire becomes a pressure cooker of gendered expectations that Charlie can’t wave away as “modern politics.” The rules are literal, embodied, and constantly enforced by costume, etiquette, and role.

So when he experiences: - being managed, - being evaluated for appearance before competence, - being guided through social spaces by someone else’s authority,

…it’s not an essay. It’s the environment doing what environments do.

A clean way to connect the restroom meet-cute to the Faire¶

You can have the restroom scene be the first time Celeste demonstrates social competence under pressure (zygote form), and Charlie clocks — without naming it — that she can control outcomes with tone and posture.

Then later, when he’s drowning in manual labour at the Faire, her “claim” feels like:

“This is what I do. I solve situations. You are now one of my situations.”

“In-between Faire” worldbuilding that will feel distinct¶

To make it not read like Renaissance or Dickens, sprinkle a few consistent cues:

  • Silhouette + terms: stays, petticoats, caps, fichu; men in waistcoats, breeches, stockings; powdered hair as optional for “upper” roles.
  • Mood: Enlightenment salons, letters, music, etiquette, tea/chocolate, early industrial hints (but not soot-and-orphans).
  • Aesthetic: Georgian elegance rather than Tudor tavern or Victorian fog.

You don’t need to lecture it. Just let the objects and vocabulary do it.

A small tweak that makes Charlie’s choice sharper¶

Instead of “most of his academically-challenged peers tended to do,” consider: - he’s seen it happen, - he’s ashamed that he’s doing “the same thing,” - but he’s also relieved because it’s a path.

That ambivalence is very human.

If you want one very practical next step: decide which of these Celeste archetypes is leading this version:

  • Strategist (building a social machine at the Faire)
  • Aesthetic perfectionist (craft + presentation as power)
  • Moral enforcer (refuses to let him be exploited)

Pick one as primary, and the whole early plotline snaps into place.

And yes — the era shift is a win. It gives you social rules sturdy enough for Celeste to use like tools, and for Charlie to feel, slowly, what it’s like to be moved around by someone else’s certainty.

Robyn:

I feel there needs to be a transitional scene (or scenes): one of which could be where Celeste finds something appealing about this shorter, thin, waif-like lad who doesn't seem to have much in terms of sparking interest in any of his female classmates. Something the others disdain, Celeste finds fascinating... perhaps even something he's not aware of himself. I'm at a loss, personally.

Emily:

You’re describing exactly the kind of hinge scene that makes the whole “claimed” arc feel earned: the moment where Celeste selects him—not out of pity, not out of romance cliché, but out of a perception that’s sharper than everyone else’s.

The key is to make what she finds fascinating non-male-coded and non-fetish. Something that reads as potential, rarity, usefulness, or truth—and something that also explains why other girls overlook or disdain him.

Here are several strong candidates. Pick one primary “hook,” and I’ll help you build the transitional scene around it.

1) The thing she notices: he has “quiet precision”¶

Other classmates disdain him because he’s not charismatic, not sporty, not ladder-climbing. Celeste sees: his mind is ordered.

How to show it (transitional scene idea):
After class, he’s re-copying notes neatly, fixing errors, organising pages, aligning margins. Not for show—because he can’t stand mess. Celeste watches, intrigued.

What she realises:
This “boring” trait is actually a rare kind of discipline. It predicts he’ll be excellent at: - costume continuity, - managing details at the Faire, - being a companion who keeps things running.

Why he doesn’t know it:
He thinks it’s just a nervous habit, a way to feel in control.

Celeste’s internal line:
He isn’t lazy. He’s misfiled.

2) The thing she notices: he has “social permeability”¶

Other girls dislike him because he’s hard to read, too quiet, “awkward.” Celeste sees: he’s porous—he absorbs tone, mirrors people, adapts instantly.

This is catnip to a person like Celeste who likes to steer.

Transitional scene:
A group conversation shifts; he changes posture and voice to match the most dominant speaker without noticing he’s doing it. Celeste clocks it like a scientist seeing a rare phenomenon.

What she realises:
He’s trainable in the non-sexual sense: he can learn social choreography quickly.

Why he doesn’t know it:
To him it’s just “being polite.”

3) The thing she notices: he has an “aesthetic”—but he hides it¶

Other girls disdain him because he looks like he doesn’t try: ill-fitting uniform, practical hair, downcast posture. Celeste sees tiny tells: - his sleeves are always clean, - his shoes are cared for, - his handwriting is beautiful, - his colour choices are instinctively good.

He has taste. He’s just ashamed to show it.

Transitional scene:
She sees his notebook: margins decorated with little ornamental motifs (not cutesy—elegant), or a tiny sketch of a garment shape, or a perfectly copied musical staff.

Celeste’s thought:
He’s already halfway out of the “boy” template, and he doesn’t know it.

This supports the later period/costume world without ever saying “feminisation.”

4) The thing she notices: he reacts to kindness with loyalty, not entitlement¶

This one is very “lived experience” coded and very attractive to women.

Other girls disdain him because he’s “needy” or “pathetic.” Celeste tests him with a small kindness: - she picks up something he dropped, - fixes a small problem, - offers a straightforward compliment.

A typical boy might puff up or flirt or take. He doesn’t. He’s grateful and then quietly adjusts his behaviour to protect her space.

Transitional scene:
A boy makes a snide remark at Celeste; Charlie instantly shifts to stand in a way that blocks the boy’s line of sight, without making a scene. Celeste notices.

Celeste’s hook:
He makes the room safer without making it about himself.

That’s powerful—and it makes her decision to keep him close feel rational and self-protective.

5) The thing she notices: he’s “underestimated”—and she hates waste¶

This is the strategist/moral-enforcer version.

Other classmates disdain him as dead weight. Celeste sees he’s being treated as disposable and it offends her sense of order.

Transitional scene:
Teacher assigns group work; everyone jockeys to avoid him. Celeste takes him immediately, not with pity, but with a brisk “You. With me.” She then discovers he’s excellent at the one part everyone else bungles: checking, refining, finishing.

Her hook:
They’re not avoiding him because he’s useless. They’re avoiding him because he’s inconvenient.

6) The thing she notices: he has a “woman’s problem” in a boy’s world¶

This is your lived experience seed, and it’s potent.

He’s small, soft-spoken, non-confrontational. He’s subject to casual disrespect, boundary-pushing, people taking his stuff, teasing him. The way he navigates it—appeasing, smoothing, shrinking—resembles the coping style many girls are socialised into.

Transitional scene:
A boy bumps him and doesn’t apologise. He apologises anyway. Celeste’s eyes narrow—not at Charlie, at the culture.

Her hook:
He’s already living downstream from other people’s confidence.

That’s the seed of “claiming”: she understands his problem is environmental, and she knows how to change environments.

My recommendation: choose one hook and one test¶

To keep it sharp and believable, pick:

  • Hook: what fascinates her (precision / permeability / hidden taste / loyalty / anti-waste / “woman’s problem”)
  • Test: one small interaction where she confirms it.

Then the transition scene becomes inevitable: Celeste sees, tests, confirms—and decides.

A ready-to-use transitional micro-scene (precision + loyalty combo)¶

Celeste drops a stack of papers in the corridor. Most people step around it. Charlie stops, silently gathers them, aligns them, hands them back. No flirt, no joke, no expectation.

Celeste watches his hands more than his face.

“You didn’t have to do that,” she says.
“It was… untidy,” he replies, as if that explains everything.
Celeste’s mouth twitches. “You’re not stupid. You’re just badly allocated.”
“Allocated?”
“You’ll see,” she says—already filing him away as mine to place.

That last line is your zygote without any kink scent.

If you tell me which Celeste you’re writing (strategist / aesthetic perfectionist / moral enforcer / quietly possessive architect), I’ll pick the best hook and write you a full transitional scene in your preferred elegant, natural dialogue style.

Robyn:

You're going to find this difficult to believe, but there are SO many aspects to Charlie I personally relate to! This SHOULD make the story infinitely easier to write. One thing to keep in mind is his poor academic performance might be in mathematics (as mine was - I did great in geometry, tho), for which he was held back. But yes, we can definitely move to a scene, perhaps at school, where the zygote sort-of grows, metaphorically-speaking. What do you think? Or are there aspects to plot development I still need to explore with you, Emily?

Emily:

A school scene is the perfect place to grow the zygote—because school is where hierarchy is constant, public, and petty. It lets Celeste “claim” him in a way that looks ordinary on the surface (group selection, a corridor moment, a teacher interaction), but privately shifts his orbit.

And the maths detail is great: being held back for maths (but quietly good at geometry) reads as real—and it gives you a precise, non-cliché vulnerability. He isn’t “stupid,” he’s mis-measured.

Yes: move to a school scene next¶

You don’t need to solve every plot puzzle before drafting. If you try, you’ll over-engineer it. What you do want is one clear seed-beat and one clear consequence.

Here’s a clean, workable zygote-growth scene framework you can write immediately:

Scene goal¶

Celeste notices a trait everyone else misreads, tests it once, and then makes a tiny decision that changes Charlie’s day.

Setting¶

After a maths class (or during a group-work assignment), in a corridor or classroom.

Seed trait (pick one primary)¶

Given what you’ve said, I’d pick quiet precision + social permeability: - He can’t do algebra under time pressure. - But he sees shapes, spacing, structure. - He also mirrors tone and defers automatically.

That combination makes him both useful and claimable.

Beat outline (easy to draft)¶

1) Public diminishment: teacher or classmates casually shame him about being held back / “still on this level.” 2) Celeste notices the wrongness: not outraged, just sharply attentive. 3) Her test: she asks him one geometry-flavoured question (or gives him a small detail task) and watches him excel. 4) Her first move: she pairs with him / redirects him / speaks for him with calm authority. 5) His internal reaction: not “rescued,” but disoriented—because the room just changed shape around her voice.

A concrete version you can write (with your tone)¶

Teacher sets group work. Everyone shuffles away from Charlie. Celeste, honours student, chooses him immediately.

  • The class reads it as charity.
  • Charlie reads it as a trap (public humiliation).
  • Celeste reads it as allocation.

She doesn’t tutor him like a saint. She gives him a role he can win at: - layout, - checking, - organising, - diagrams, - making the final product look right.

Then she says one line that becomes your seed:

“You’re not behind. You’re just being tested in a language you don’t speak.”

That line is Celeste doing what she does best: naming reality.

Do you still need to explore plot development before the scene?¶

Only a little—and it’s not “big plot,” it’s Celeste’s motive clarity. Pick one of these and you’re set:

1) Strategist Celeste: she’s building a social machine and wants a discreet, loyal, precise person in her orbit.
2) Aesthetic/perfectionist Celeste: she hates waste and loves competence; she collects tools and makes them shine.
3) Moral-enforcer Celeste: she doesn’t tolerate casual cruelty; she intervenes decisively.

You can still evolve it later, but choose one as the “current truth” so the scene has a spine.

If you want, I’ll draft the actual scene¶

Tell me two quick things and I’ll write a polished, elegant school scene (800–1200 words):

1) Is it Celeste POV (1st) or Charlie POV (3rd) for this chapter?
2) Is Celeste’s vibe here coolly kind or coolly amused?

If you don’t answer, I’ll assume Charlie 3rd and coolly kind and just write it.

Robyn:

I find Strategist Celeste most fits the young lady making her mark in my head, Emily. And to that end, this next scene - the one you propose - should be from Celeste's POV: she's the actor/protagonist. It might be his story, but she shapes it. And thank you for writing the school scene: I look forward to reading it.

Emily:

Celeste — School Corridor, Late Afternoon¶

By the time Mr. Greeves started writing GROUP TASK on the board, the room had already made its decision.

It wasn’t an official decision, not one you could point to in a rulebook, but it lived in the way chairs angled away from Charlie, in the little coughs people used to cover their discomfort, in the speed at which everyone suddenly found the floor fascinating. I watched it happen with the same detached interest I’d watch a flock of birds turn as one body: instinct, cowardice, and the lazy relief of belonging.

Mr. Greeves tapped the chalk like it owed him money. “Alright. You’ve all had your practice test. You’ve all had your feelings about your practice test. Today, you’re going to make something sensible out of it.”

A few kids chuckled. Not because it was funny—because it was safe.

“Pairs,” he said, underlining it twice. “Pick a question from the set. Solve it. Then produce a one-page explanation that someone else could follow. If you can’t explain it, you can’t do it.”

There was the usual scrape of chairs, the low panic of social arrangements. Everyone moved fast, because speed looked like confidence. I didn’t move. I didn’t need to. People came to me.

“Celeste, want to—”
“Celeste, I saved you a—”
“Celeste, I already have—”

I gave them my polite face and none of my answer. My attention drifted to the back left, where Charlie sat in a way that tried to be smaller than the desk allowed.

He wasn’t ugly. He wasn’t even particularly strange. He was just… uncollected. Like a thing people had decided was worthless and therefore never bothered to look at properly. Thin. Shorter than most of the boys, which seemed to bother them more than it bothered him. His uniform shirt sat awkwardly on his frame—too big at the shoulders, too loose at the waist—as if it belonged to someone older and louder.

I’d noticed him before, of course. Everyone had. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: still doesn’t work. As if he’d chosen it.

Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”

That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.

Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room—quick, skittish, not begging, but scanning for danger. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was self-control.

He’d already decided he’d rather be humiliated by the teacher than rejected by his peers.

Interesting.

“Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”

“Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.

The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.

Mr. Greeves blinked. “Celeste?”

“I’m with Charlie,” I said. Not can I, not would you mind, but a simple correction of the reality he’d been about to create.

A few girls exchanged looks. One boy gave a tiny laugh, like I’d just made a joke he didn’t understand. Someone whispered, not quietly enough, “Why would you do that?”

I turned my head just enough for the whisperer to know I’d heard. I didn’t even have to identify her. “Because I like getting full marks,” I said, pleasantly. “And I like working with people who don’t waste time showing off.”

Silence. A delicious, tidy silence.

Mr. Greeves recovered, grateful for order. “Alright then. Celeste and Charlie. Good.”

I walked my chair over without asking permission from the air. Charlie stared at me as if I’d sat down inside his head.

“You don’t have to,” he said, low. He wasn’t trying to shame me out of it. He sounded… wary. Like there was a trap hidden in kindness.

“I know,” I replied.

He blinked. That simple acknowledgement seemed to unsettle him more than reassurance would have. I placed my notebook on the desk between us and looked at the question set.

“Pick one,” I said.

“I—” he began, and faltered. The page might as well have been written in smoke.

I didn’t rescue him from the discomfort. I simply watched him long enough to see what he did with it.

He didn’t panic theatrically. He didn’t joke. He didn’t make excuses. He scanned. Not the way someone scans for answers, but the way someone scans for structure.

My eyes dropped to his own notebook as he moved it—careful, almost fussy, aligning the corners of the paper with the desk edge. I noticed his handwriting: neat, consistent, slanted slightly right, as if it had manners.

“You’re good at geometry,” I said.

His head came up sharply. “What?”

“You are. I’ve seen your diagrams.”

He looked genuinely confused, as if the possibility of being observed outside his failures had never occurred to him. “I’m… okay.”

“You’re better than okay.” I tapped the question set. “Pick one with a diagram. A shape. Something that lives in space, not in a string of symbols.”

He hesitated, then pointed with his pencil. “This one. The triangle… with the angle bisector.”

“Good,” I said. “You do the diagram. Make it clean. Label it properly. I’ll do the algebraic part and write the explanation. Then you check me for logic. Deal?”

He stared at me. “You… trust me to check you?”

That was the real question. Not about maths. About hierarchy.

“I trust your eyes,” I said. “They’re honest.”

His ears went faintly pink. Not in a flattered way—more like embarrassment at being assigned a virtue.

He bent over the page. His pencil moved and the triangle appeared with a crispness that felt almost calming. He drew like he meant it, like the page deserved dignity.

While he worked, I listened to the classroom. The buzz of other pairs. The smugness of boys who’d paired up for safety, girls who’d paired up for comfort. I heard my name used in little asides, the way people taste words to see if they’re sweet.

Charlie drew his angle bisector and then paused, frowning.

“What?” I asked.

He pointed. “If you call that angle x… then this one has to be x too, because of the bisector. But the problem statement says this angle is thirty degrees, which means x is fifteen. Which means… your ratio is fixed.”

He said it softly, as if he expected to be corrected.

I looked where he pointed. He was right. The whole thing collapsed into a simple proportion.

I felt a small, satisfied click in my chest. Not because he’d solved it. Because of what it meant: he was competent in a way nobody had bothered to find.

“Exactly,” I said. “That’s the spine of it.”

He glanced up at me—quick, searching. “Why are you doing this?”

There it was. The suspicion. The defensive little gate he’d built, because people who were kind to him usually wanted something he couldn’t afford.

I didn’t lie. I just chose the angle of truth.

“Because you’re being tested in the wrong language,” I said. “And I hate waste.”

His pencil hovered.

“Waste?” he echoed.

“Waste of ability,” I clarified. “Waste of time. Waste of people.” I kept my voice calm. “You’re not behind. You’re misallocated.”

He swallowed, and for a second his face did something raw—like he’d nearly believed me and it frightened him.

I leaned closer, just enough to make my next words private. “Also,” I added, “you’re going to owe me. Not in a gross way. In a practical way.”

His shoulders stiffened. “What do you mean?”

“I mean,” I said, “I don’t do charity. I do investment.”

He stared at me, and this time there was something like understanding. Not full understanding. The first bud of it. The zygote, if you like—tiny and alive.

Mr. Greeves wandered past. He glanced at our work, eyebrows lifting. “Good diagram,” he said, sounding surprised despite himself. “Nice and clean.”

Charlie’s hand tightened on the pencil, but he didn’t look up. He didn’t bask. He just kept working, as if praise was a thing that might vanish if he moved too fast.

When Mr. Greeves walked away, I said, lightly, “See? You exist. People just don’t like admitting it.”

Charlie’s mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. The ghost of one.

We finished the solution with no drama. I wrote the explanation in clear steps. He checked every transition like a quiet auditor. When we handed it in, Mr. Greeves nodded at me, then at him, as if he’d suddenly remembered Charlie was part of the room.

As the bell went, chairs scraped, and the flock of birds turned again. People flowed past us, and I watched Charlie do what he always did: shrink to let them.

I slid my notebook into my bag and stood.

“Charlie,” I said.

He looked up, automatically attentive, as if my voice had become a cue.

“I’m doing something after graduation,” I continued, watching his reaction carefully. “A project. A place. It’s… not school.”

His eyes flicked away, then back. Hope, hidden under caution.

“I need people who can keep their head,” I said. “People who can do detail. People who can be invisible on purpose.”

He frowned slightly. “Why me?”

I stepped closer, so my answer didn’t have to compete with the corridor noise.

“Because you already know how to survive without applause,” I said. “And because if I don’t pick you, someone else will. Someone stupider.”

His breath caught—not romantic, not theatrical. Just the shock of being chosen without a joke attached to it.

He nodded once, careful, like he didn’t trust his own voice.

“Good,” I said. And then, because it mattered, because strategy without warmth is just cruelty, I softened it by a fraction. “I’ll tell you what it is tomorrow.”

He watched me walk away as if the hallway had quietly rearranged itself.

And it had.

Not because I’d saved him.

Because I’d placed him.

Robyn:

I'm going to struggle finding ANYTHING about this to tweak, Emily. I'm serious.

I want to redo the first scene: the one in the restroom. Here's what I have, subject to your appraisal and suggestions (or outright modification)...

He was pulling paper towels when he heard a girl's voice and quickly turned around. She was the prettiest girl he’d ever seen, honestly. Not in a magazine-cover way, not generically pretty... a make-your-heart-beat-faster kind of pretty. Her face was, well, flawless, and it shone with a smile that was both curious and... just a bit mischievous, like she knew something you didn't, and was enjoying that knowledge. She looked to be maybe a year or two older, certainly taller than him. She stood like a ballerina, one who spent considerable time observing people. Her Charlotte Rampling eyes held you with that unsettling, penetrating gaze. That self-assurance was so complete, it made the fact of her being in the men’s restroom seem like a calculated choice, like she was making a point. You wouldn’t question it. “Hello ... girl?” The words fell into an awkward silence. She mercifully shortened it. "You ... ARE a girl. Aren't you?" The thought that she might be the one who was misplaced didn’t even register initially. Not even a flicker. He was too busy trying to process the fact that this ... this stunning girl was actually looking at him, talking to him. She was talking to him. To him! And that, at that moment, it was all he could process: it was so unbelieveable. The restroom, the potential for awkwardness ... none of it mattered. Just this. Her. Talking. To him. Her question seemed… vaguely odd, yes. Like he’d somehow missed a crucial piece of information, a plot point he’d slept through. It was the kind of oddness that suggested a larger, looming something. He felt a growing uneasiness. He managed, cautiously, “No, I’m ... I’m a guy.” The girl’s smile seemed oddly sympathetic, as if at an admission of a shortcoming, a defect. “Oh, sorry. It’s just that you’re about the same height as some of my friends. And, to be honest, you’re not built like a bloke, at least, none that I know.” He was so completely, ridiculously charmed by her accent — that lovely, rolling British thing — that he honestly would have basked in her words even if she'd been actively, aggressively insulting him. Probably would have just nodded, as he did now. “Plus, well ... you’re in the ladies’." Another pause. "Um, you know, the girl's toilet.” Reality suddenly hit. He stared at her for a moment, eyes wide in horror, then glanced quickly around him. Sure enough, no urinals. The colour drained from his face. “Oh - sh... dear! I’m so, so sorry! I ... uh, I can’t believe I—” He wasn’t the dangerous kind of boy. He was the wrong-door, head-in-the-clouds kind: hair in his eyes, oversized shirt with a slept-in collar. She could have said: 'get out'. Instead she felt the oddest impulse of charity: perhaps it was curiosity, but more than that, pity. She moved a half step closer. His head tilted up, and then she saw them: those almond-shaped doe eyes with long thick lashes. Far from being a threat, he was a lost lamb that had wandered in and needed shepherding. Her smile broadened, a twinkle in her eye. “Don’t worry, I won’t tell anyone if you don’t.” She peered at him with a slight tilt of her head, curiosity growing. Charlie found himself backed into the sinks. What was she going to do? He had invaded a sacred space for women. At least she wasn't screaming. She gazed at him for what seemed an awkward eternity, then precipitously extended her hand. “I'm Celeste. Celeste Shelley.” Not only was she not screaming, she was introducing herself. He looked at her hand the way a child looks at a violin up close the first time: reverence mixed with the concern as to its fragility. Then he put his palm in hers. “Um, I’m Chuck, um, Charles Rossignol,” he finally croaked as her hand firmly grasped his. Celeste’s hand was soft, but its grip surprisingly tenacious. “Chuck. Charles. Hmmm," she mused, studying his hand. The bones were fine, the fingers long and slight, the skin cool. A girl’s hand, if that were possible, only partly because of weakness but more of the way it settled in her own without weight, of a delicate refinement out of character with the rest of the person. "'Chuck' doesn't really suit you.” Her grin pinched into a thoughtful grimace. “‘Rossignol’... that’s French for a kind of bird... the nightingale?” Charlie's heart pounded in his ears as he stared at her hand gripping his, not really taking in what she was saying. “‘Bird’ ... that’s a term some blokes back home use for ‘girl’.” Her eyes finally met his looking up at her imploringly. “Oh! Sorry,” she gushed, releasing his hand, a blush rising on her cheeks. “I didn’t mean to...” She grinned. “I make you a bit nervous, don't I?” Her smile, her accent, her smell. Her grip: he was spellbound. "Uh ... yeah, I mean," Dry-mouthed, tentatively he tried to explain. "Well, it's just that, well, I probably shouldn’t be in here.” “Quite right,” she said wryly with a short chuckle. “I reckon I’d feel a bit awkward in the gents’ myself.” Charlie turned to go. He was forcing himself, as if his feet had decided they were quite content to remain precisely where they were. Celeste gently lifted her eyebrows and nodded at the door. It felt less like an command and more like a slightly amused dismissal. He moved numbly, as if being nudged forward. As he pulled open the door, he risked one last glance back, and his stomach did a little flip — the one you get on a roller coaster, or when someone unexpectedly touches your arm. She was smiling. At him. “See you, Charlie.” By the time Charlie entered the cool quiet of the library and put his glasses back on, a dizzying lightness enveloped his being. He had just met an angel.

Emily:

You’ve got so much good material here — the cadence, the romantic “dizzying” quality, the sense that Celeste’s presence reorganises his reality. It already contains the zygote you’re after.

But to make it cleaner, stronger, and more in line with Strategist Celeste, there are a few structural fixes that will pay off massively:

What I’d adjust (surgical, not a teardown)¶

1) POV drift + “restroom” inconsistency¶

  • You start in his head, then suddenly slip into hers (“She could have said…”), and then back.
  • You also call it the men’s restroom at the top, then reveal it’s the ladies’ (no urinals).

If this is the foundational meet-cute, it needs to be rock-solid: one POV, one physical reality.

Since you’ve decided Celeste is the actor/protagonist, I’d rewrite this whole scene in Celeste’s 1st-person and let Charlie’s “spellbound” quality be something she observes.

2) The line “Hello… girl?” and “You ARE a girl, aren’t you?”¶

It’s punchy, but it’s also a big fetish-magnet phrase, and it tips the scene toward “gender gotcha.” If you want to seed the dynamic without summoning the wrong aisle, keep the ambiguity but phrase it as practical:

  • “Love… are you lost?”
  • “You know you’re in the ladies’, right?”

Celeste can still be mischievous and penetrating — just not in a way that reads like a setup.

3) Make Celeste’s “claim” explicit in social terms¶

You already have her naming him “Charlie,” which is perfect as a first act of authorship. Add one more micro-beat that frames the whole event:

  • “If anyone asks, you were with me.”
  • “Don’t speak. Let me handle it.”

That’s the zygote: she becomes the interpreter and the alibi.

4) Reduce “male gaze” description of Celeste¶

You can keep Charlie’s awe, but keep the prose from lingering too long on “prettiest girl ever / flawless / angel.” That style tends to tilt the tone toward the very audience you’re trying to de-emphasise. Instead, let Celeste’s effect be the focus: posture, composure, choice.

Below is a rewritten version that preserves your beats (mischief, shock, handshake, name-shift, the lingering “roller-coaster flip”), but makes Celeste the clear protagonist and plants the “claimed” zygote cleanly.

Revised scene: Celeste POV (Strategist seed, subtle “claim”)¶

I shouldn’t have been in that corridor at all.

The girls’ toilets sat off the library wing — pale tiles, the faint perfume of hand soap, and that soft institutional hush that always made you lower your voice without thinking. I was cutting through because the bell had just gone, and the hallway was a river of bodies, and I don’t like being jostled when my head is full.

Besides, I’d learned early that if you walk as though you belong somewhere, people stop questioning why you’re there. It isn’t arrogance. It’s choreography.

I pushed the door with my shoulder, stepped inside, and only then noticed the figure at the sinks.

For half a second my brain refused to label what I was seeing. Not because it was shocking, but because it didn’t fit the neat categories school insisted on.

A boy — slight, narrow-shouldered, hair falling into his eyes — stood under the bright mirror lights with a paper towel clutched in one hand, as if he’d been interrupted in the middle of deciding how to exist. His shirt was one of those oversized uniforms that look like they’ve been slept in, collar softened by too many washes, as if someone had given up trying to make it crisp.

He looked up at me and went very still.

Not threatening still. Not guilty still. More like a rabbit that has realised the world contains hawks.

I took him in properly then: the fine bones, the long fingers, the eyelashes that had no business being as dark as they were. The sort of face that made teachers speak to you gently, even when they were telling you off — if they were paying attention at all.

I could have shouted. I could have said, Get out.

But the thing about the girls’ toilets — and about girls in general — is that you learn quickly how useless screaming is as a strategy. It makes you visible. It makes you the story. It invites everyone to have an opinion.

And I didn’t want a story. Not yet.

So I did what I always do when something unexpected turns up in my orbit:

I decided what it meant.

“Hello,” I said, calmly, as though I’d found a first-year hiding from a duty teacher. “Are you lost?”

His throat bobbed. He didn’t answer immediately. The silence was so complete I could hear the air-conditioning tick behind the vents.

Then he managed, cautious and hoarse, “I… I’m sorry.”

A pre-emptive apology. Interesting.

I softened my voice by a fraction — not pity, just calibration. “You know you’re in the ladies’, right?”

His eyes flicked around the room like the answer might be written on the tiles. The colour drained from his face as the reality hit him: no urinals, no familiar signage, no exit that didn’t require walking past me.

“Oh—” He swallowed. “Oh, God. I thought this was— I mean, I—”

He did that thing some boys do when they panic: they try to fill the air with words, as if explanation can erase a mistake.

But he wasn’t the swaggering kind of boy. He wasn’t even the cheeky kind.

He was the wrong-door, head-in-the-clouds kind.

He stood there with a paper towel in his hand like it was evidence.

I took one small step nearer — not close enough to crowd him, just close enough that he couldn’t bolt past me without making it a scene.

“Alright,” I said, briskly. “Stop. Breathe.”

He blinked at me, startled by the instruction.

“Good,” I said, as if he’d complied on purpose. “Now. You’re going to walk out like nothing happened.”

His mouth opened.

I lifted a finger, not harshly. Simply: I’m handling this.

“Don’t argue. Don’t confess. Don’t do that thing where you look like you’re asking to be punished,” I added, watching his shoulders tighten. “Just… let me steer for a second.”

He stared at me. And then, almost invisibly, he did as he was told: his breath slowed, his spine uncurled a fraction, his hands lowered as if they’d been hovering in defence.

There it was.

Social permeability. He absorbed tone like cloth absorbs dye.

“What’s your name?” I asked.

He hesitated, as though names were dangerous.

“Chuck,” he said finally, and then corrected himself as if he’d remembered he was meant to be polite. “Charles. Charles Rossignol.”

The surname landed in my mind with a satisfying click. French. Unexpected.

“Rossignol,” I repeated, tasting it. “Nightingale.”

His eyes flicked up, startled that I’d understood.

“I— yeah,” he murmured.

“Chuck doesn’t suit you,” I said, because it didn’t. “It sounds like a joke someone else started.”

His lips pressed together. Not offended. More like… relieved that someone had noticed.

I extended my hand.

He looked at it the way a shy child looks at a museum exhibit — reverent, anxious about breaking something. Then, cautiously, he placed his palm in mine.

His hand was cool and light. No weight to it. No push. It settled as if it belonged where I’d put it.

I didn’t squeeze. I simply held, for one beat longer than necessary.

An ordinary gesture — and yet it did what ordinary gestures always do when you choose them deliberately.

It created a connection the other person hadn’t asked for, and therefore couldn’t quite refuse.

“Right,” I said, still holding his hand, my voice steady. “Listen carefully.”

He nodded once. A small obedience. Automatic.

“If anyone asks,” I said, “you were with me.”

His eyes widened.

I kept going before he could drown the moment in apology. “You don’t have to explain anything. You don’t have to defend yourself. You don’t have to look guilty. You just leave. And if anyone looks at you oddly, you look at me.”

I released his hand and watched him stand there, recalculating his world.

“I… I shouldn’t be here,” he managed, as though stating it might make the floor open.

“Quite right,” I said, with a short, amused exhale. “And I shouldn’t be wasting my time in toilets during passing period. Yet here we are.”

That landed. His mouth twitched — the ghost of a smile.

Good. Humour is a lever. It lets people accept guidance without feeling defeated.

I nodded toward the door. Not a command. A direction.

He moved — slowly, as if his feet didn’t trust the new instruction manual.

At the threshold he stopped, hand on the handle, and glanced back at me with the expression of someone expecting the world to change its mind.

I lifted my eyebrows at him.

Go on.

He swallowed, then opened the door.

Before he stepped through, I said, lightly, “Charlie.”

He froze.

“My name’s not—”

“It is now,” I replied, pleasant as sunlight. “At least when I’m speaking to you.”

His face went blank with shock — not outrage, not embarrassment, simply the stunned feeling of having a thing renamed while you’re still holding it.

It pleased me more than it should have.

“See you, Charlie,” I added, and let the smile come — not flirtation, not a trap. Just a signal: you’re in my line of sight now.

He disappeared into the corridor.

And for a moment I stood alone in the bright tiled room, hearing only the distant shuffle of students and the soft rush of taps.

I washed my hands, because the habit matters, even when you’re busy rewriting someone’s afternoon.

As I dried them, I thought—not unkindly, but with the clean satisfaction of a strategist whose board has just presented an unexpected piece:

Misallocated.

And now… perhaps not.

If you like, I can do one more pass where Charlie’s “angel” feeling is preserved more strongly without sliding into breathless worship—by letting Celeste notice his awe and deliberately use it (gently) to set the hook.

Robyn:

Yes please. I have trouble imagining anything could be an improvement over what you just wrote, but ... yes please. Also, in the class scene you wrote earlier, Celeste recognises Charlie, doesn't she? Which could be a primary motivator for taking him under her wing?

Emily:

Yes to both.

1) I’ll give you a slightly upgraded restroom scene that keeps Celeste in the driver’s seat and preserves Charlie’s “angel / heart-flip / spellbound” feeling—but in a way that reads as effect and strategy, not breathless worship.

2) And yes: in the class scene I wrote earlier, Celeste already “knows of” Charlie (the held-back boy) in the social sense. With the restroom scene as the true first meeting, she would absolutely recognise him later—and that recognition becomes a beautifully clean motivator for choosing him: she’s already tested his temperament under pressure and seen that he absorbs her tone and follows her lead. That’s strategist catnip.

Restroom scene — revised pass (Celeste POV, with Charlie’s “angel” effect visible)¶

I shouldn’t have been in that corridor at all.

The girls’ toilets were off the library wing: pale tiles, faint soap, that hush that makes you lower your voice even when you don’t mean to. I cut through because the bell had just gone and the corridor outside was a river of bodies, and I don’t like being jostled when my head is full.

Besides, I’d learned early that if you walk as though you belong somewhere, most people will let you. It isn’t arrogance. It’s choreography.

I pushed the door with my shoulder and stepped inside—and stopped.

A boy stood at the sinks with a paper towel in his hand, as if he’d been interrupted in the middle of deciding how to exist. Uniform shirt too big, collar softened like it had been slept in. Hair falling into his eyes. He’d turned at the sound of the door and gone very still.

Not predatory still. Not defiant still.

More like a deer that has realised the world contains headlights.

For half a second my brain refused to file him properly, because the scene didn’t match the rules school pretended were natural: girls in here, boys out there, neat lines, neat categories. And yet here he was, in the harsh mirror light, looking like a wrong note you couldn’t un-hear once it happened.

He stared at me.

Not the casual glance you get from boys who think you’re decoration. This was different. This was… struck.

I saw it in the way his breath caught and failed to restart smoothly, the way his gaze snagged as if his eyes had forgotten they were supposed to move. It wasn’t lust. It wasn’t bravado. It was awe—raw, immediate, and almost helpless.

That kind of attention is dangerous in the wrong hands. But in the right hands, it’s simple.

It’s leverage.

Not cruel leverage. Just… direction.

I could have shouted. I could have said, Get out, as if volume was a form of safety. But screaming makes you the story. It invites witnesses, gossip, morality plays. And I didn’t want a story.

Not yet.

So I did what I always do when something unexpected enters my orbit:

I decided what it meant.

“Hello,” I said, calmly, as though I’d found a first-year hiding from a duty teacher. “Are you lost?”

His throat bobbed. He didn’t answer immediately. The silence was so complete I could hear the air-conditioning ticking behind the vent.

Then he managed, hoarse, “I… I’m sorry.”

A pre-emptive apology. Interesting.

I softened my voice by a fraction—not pity, just calibration. “You know you’re in the ladies’, right?”

His eyes flicked around the room like the truth might be written on the tiles. It landed a beat later: no urinals, no familiar signage, no exit that didn’t require passing me.

“Oh—” He swallowed. “Oh, God. I thought this was— I mean, I—”

Words spilled out, urgent and unhelpful. The instinct to explain, to erase himself by being reasonable.

He wasn’t the swaggering kind of boy. He wasn’t even the cheeky kind.

He was the wrong-door, head-in-the-clouds kind.

He stood there clutching a paper towel like it was evidence.

I took one small step nearer—close enough to make him hold still, not close enough to crowd. He backed into the sinks without meaning to. I watched him do it, watched the way his shoulders drew inward as if he could shrink out of the situation.

“Alright,” I said, briskly. “Stop. Breathe.”

He blinked at me, startled by the instruction.

“Good,” I said, as if he’d complied on purpose. “Now. You’re going to walk out like nothing happened.”

His mouth opened again.

I lifted a finger, not harshly. Simply: I’m handling this.

“Don’t argue. Don’t confess. Don’t do that thing where you look like you’re asking to be punished,” I added, watching his shoulders tighten. “Just… let me steer for a second.”

He stared at me. And then I saw the shift—a tiny one, but unmistakable. His breath slowed. His spine uncurled a fraction. His hands lowered.

He absorbed my tone like cloth absorbs dye.

That was when I knew he wasn’t a threat. He was a boy who’d learned, somewhere along the line, that his safest option was to yield.

That’s a habit.

Habits can be redirected.

“What’s your name?” I asked.

He hesitated, as though names were dangerous.

“Chuck,” he said finally, then corrected himself with an awkward, desperate politeness. “Charles. Charles Rossignol.”

The surname landed with a satisfying click. French. Unexpected.

“Rossignol,” I repeated, tasting it. “Nightingale.”

His eyes flicked up, startled that I’d understood. His gaze held mine for a beat too long—still caught, still dazzled—and I felt, very clearly, the weight of being that dazzlement.

Not flattering.

Powerful.

“Right,” I said, and extended my hand.

He looked at it the way a shy child looks at something expensive—reverence mixed with fear of doing it wrong. Then, cautiously, he placed his palm in mine.

His hand was cool and light. No push. No claim. It settled as if it belonged where I’d put it.

I didn’t squeeze. I simply held, for one beat longer than necessary.

An ordinary gesture—deliberately timed.

It did what ordinary gestures always do when you choose them carefully: it created a connection the other person hadn’t asked for, and therefore couldn’t quite refuse.

“Listen,” I said, quietly, so he had to focus. “If anyone asks, you were with me.”

His eyes widened.

I kept going before he could drown the moment in apology. “You don’t have to explain anything. You don’t have to defend yourself. You don’t have to look guilty. You just leave. And if anyone looks at you oddly, you look at me.”

I released his hand and watched him stand there, recalculating his world.

“I… I shouldn’t be here,” he managed, as if saying it might open a trapdoor in the floor.

“Quite right,” I said, with a short, amused exhale. “And I shouldn’t be wasting passing period in toilets. Yet here we are.”

It landed. His mouth twitched—the ghost of a smile—then vanished as quickly as it came, like he didn’t trust it.

Good. Humour is a lever. It lets people accept guidance without feeling defeated.

I nodded toward the door. Not a command. A direction.

He moved, slowly, as if his feet didn’t trust the new instruction manual. At the threshold he stopped with his hand on the handle and glanced back at me—an expression I recognised too well: expecting the world to change its mind.

I lifted my eyebrows at him.

Go on.

He swallowed and opened the door.

Before he stepped through, I said, lightly, “Charlie.”

He froze.

“My name’s not—”

“It is now,” I replied, pleasant as sunlight. “At least when I’m speaking to you.”

Shock flared across his face—blank, stunned—not outrage, not embarrassment. The simple astonishment of having a thing renamed while you’re still holding it.

It pleased me more than it should have.

“See you, Charlie,” I added, and let the smile come—not flirtation, not a trap. Just a signal: you’re in my line of sight now.

He disappeared into the corridor.

And for a moment I stood alone in the bright tiled room, hearing only the distant shuffle of students and the soft rush of taps.

I washed my hands, because the habit matters, even when you’ve just rewritten someone’s afternoon.

As I dried them, I thought—not unkindly, but with the clean satisfaction of a strategist whose board has presented an unexpected piece:

He watches like he’s waiting to be told what’s real.

And I am very good at telling people what’s real.

Continuity with the class scene: yes, she recognises him — and it strengthens everything¶

In the class scene I wrote earlier, Celeste already notices Charlie as “the held-back boy,” socially marked and avoided. With this restroom scene placed earlier in the timeline, you get a much richer engine:

  • In the restroom, Celeste sees his permeability (he calms when she tells him to).
  • She also sees his awe—not because she’s vain, but because awe makes him steerable.
  • She plants a hook: “If anyone asks, you were with me,” and she renames him “Charlie.”

So later, in maths class, when she says “Me” before the teacher can assign him, it’s not charity or whim. It’s a second move on a piece she’s already touched.

If you want, I can do a tiny two-line splice into the maths class scene to make the recognition explicit, something like:

It was him—paper towel boy, wrong door, my “Charlie.”
He sat the same way you sit when you’re trying not to take up space.

That would make her choice feel inevitable.

If you’d like, paste the first 2–3 paragraphs of the maths scene you’re using (your version or mine), and I’ll stitch the recognition in seamlessly so the two scenes lock together like they were always written as a pair.

Robyn:

This bit?

By the time Mr. Greeves started writing GROUP TASK on the board, the room had already made its decision. It wasn’t an official decision, not one you could point to in a rulebook, but it lived in the way chairs angled away from Charlie, in the little coughs people used to cover their discomfort, in the speed at which everyone suddenly found the floor fascinating. I watched it happen with the same detached interest I’d watch a flock of birds turn as one body: instinct, cowardice, and the lazy relief of belonging. Mr. Greeves tapped the chalk like it owed him money. “Alright. You’ve all had your practice test. You’ve all had your feelings about your practice test. Today, you’re going to make something sensible out of it.” A few kids chuckled. Not because it was funny—because it was safe. “Pairs,” he said, underlining it twice. “Pick a question from the set. Solve it. Then produce a one-page explanation that someone else could follow. If you can’t explain it, you can’t do it.” There was the usual scrape of chairs, the low panic of social arrangements. Everyone moved fast, because speed looked like confidence. I didn’t move. I didn’t need to. People came to me.

“Celeste, want to—” “Celeste, I saved you a—” “Celeste, I already have—”

I gave them my polite face and none of my answer. My attention drifted to the back left, where Charlie sat in a way that tried to be smaller than the desk allowed. He wasn’t ugly. He wasn’t even particularly strange. He was just… uncollected. Like a thing people had decided was worthless and therefore never bothered to look at properly. Thin. Shorter than most of the boys, which seemed to bother them more than it bothered him. His uniform shirt sat awkwardly on his frame—too big at the shoulders, too loose at the waist—as if it belonged to someone older and louder. I’d noticed him before, of course. Everyone had. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: still doesn’t work. As if he’d chosen it.

Emily:

Yes — exactly that bit. And it’s a gorgeous place to stitch the recognition in, because you’re already doing two things there:

1) showing the classroom’s quiet cruelty, and
2) showing Celeste’s detached, strategic reading of the room.

All you need is a two–three sentence “flash” that reframes Charlie as already in her line of sight, not merely “the held-back boy.”

Below are three splice options, from light touch to richer. Pick whichever best fits your desired intensity.

Option 1: Minimal, elegant splice (2 sentences, very clean)¶

Insert right after:

“My attention drifted to the back left, where Charlie sat in a way that tried to be smaller than the desk allowed.”

Add:

It was him again — wrong door, paper towel, the brief bright shock of the girls’ bathroom and the way he’d gone still when he saw me. My “Charlie,” renamed on a whim that hadn’t felt like a whim at all.

Then continue with:

He wasn’t ugly. He wasn’t even particularly strange…

This keeps it tiny but makes the link undeniable.

Option 2: Stronger strategist flavour (adds motive: “I already tested him”)¶

Same insertion point, but use this:

It was him again — the boy I’d found in the ladies’ toilets, clutching a paper towel like evidence, taking my tone into his lungs and letting it slow his panic. I’d seen what he did under pressure: he yielded to direction instead of making noise. That alone put him ahead of half the room.

Then continue as written.

This makes Celeste’s later “Me” feel like a tactical decision, not kindness.

Option 3: Most seamless + sensory callback (still subtle, but more “alive”)¶

If you want it to echo the restroom scene without re-telling it:

It was him again. Same folded-in posture, same habit of making himself smaller than the space provided — as if the world charged rent for existence. In my mind I could still see the bright tiles, the paper towel in his hand, the way his eyes had fixed on me as though I’d stepped out of a different life. I’d named him “Charlie” then, lightly — and he’d taken it as if names were instructions.

Then continue.

This one gives you the zygote continuity in a way that feels organic.

A tiny micro-tweak to your existing line (optional but nice)¶

Your line:

“I’d noticed him before, of course. Everyone had.”

If you now have the restroom scene first, you might sharpen it to:

“I’d noticed him before most people bothered to.”

Or:

“I’d noticed him before—properly.”

That keeps your rhythm but makes Celeste’s perception feel more intentional.

If you tell me which option you prefer (1/2/3), I’ll reprint the whole paragraph block with the splice integrated so you can paste it straight in without fiddling.

Robyn:

I sort-of spliced it in this way... it this okay?
