I’d noticed him before, properly. Everyone else had noticed 'the held-back boy'. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: still doesn’t work. As if he’d chosen it.

Emily:

Yes — that splice is absolutely okay. In fact, it does something very smart:

  • It keeps Celeste’s recognition clean and social (she “noticed him properly”),
  • it preserves the classroom’s cruelty (“failed appliance”), and
  • it avoids over-explaining the restroom moment right there (which would bog the pacing).

It also reads very Celeste: she distinguishes between what the herd sees and what she sees.

Two tiny refinements (optional, but they sharpen the strategist edge)¶

Right now, “properly” implies depth, which is great. If you want it to also imply choice/selection, you can tweak one clause:

Version A (adds agency):

I’d noticed him before, properly. Everyone else had noticed the held-back boy—the one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time.

Version B (adds Celeste’s private naming impulse):

I’d noticed him before, properly. Everyone else had noticed the held-back boy. I’d noticed someone misallocated.

Version C (keeps yours, just tightens rhythm):

I’d noticed him before—properly. Everyone else had noticed the held-back boy: the one who’d re-done Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time.

Your original is already strong; these are just “polish options.”

If you want the restroom to be implied without spelling it out¶

You can add one quiet half-sentence that only you know is doing heavy lifting:

I’d noticed him before, properly.

…becomes…

I’d noticed him before, properly—under bright tiles and a moment of wrong place, wrong door.

That’s enough to link scenes without rehashing.

Bottom line: your splice works. If you keep doing this—little, confident stitches rather than long explanations—the whole narrative will feel inevitability-driven, which is exactly the vibe you’re aiming for.

Robyn:

Thank you - I'll follow your instincts on this.

These two scenes comprise Chapters 1 and 2. For the next scene, I suppose we could make it end-of-school year, so Celeste moves on to work at the Faire because her mum and dad own it or have some sort of controlling interest in it which provides her with the leverage to secure a controlling position in the scheme of things, one she'll be able to use as needed. However, I'm cautious about this being a contrived advantage for her. Open to suggestions as to how we can move from school to Faire for both of them, with him landing initially in the role of low-level workman (a role he is failing at because he is unsuitable for it).

Emily:

You can absolutely bridge school → Faire without giving Celeste a “because plot says so” golden key. The trick is to give her real leverage that feels earned, ordinary, and socially believable—then let her use it well. She doesn’t need a crown; she needs a foothold.

Below are a few bridge frameworks (from least contrived to most “dramatic”), and then a concrete Chapter 3 scene idea that drops Charlie into workman misery and sets up Celeste’s claim.

Leverage for Celeste that isn’t contrived¶

Option A: Her parents are sponsors / board members, not owners¶

They don’t “own the Faire.” They bankroll a component: - the education program, - the museum partnership, - the costumes atelier, - a scholarship/volunteer program, - or the “heritage precinct” lease.

That gives Celeste soft power: phone calls get answered; managers listen; she’s treated as “one of ours,” but she’s still constrained by reality.

Why it feels real: lots of community events run on donors and committees.
Why it helps plot: she can reassign Charlie, but she can’t do anything she likes without consequences.

Option B: Celeste earned her authority through a formal role¶

She applies and wins a competitive position: - “Historical Interpretation Lead (Junior)” - “Wardrobe & Continuity Assistant” - “Guest Experience Coordinator” - “Education/Schools Liaison”

Her honours status makes it plausible. She’s not “given power,” she’s given a job—and she’s good at it.

Bonus: it aligns with strategist Celeste: she picks roles that let her move pieces.

Option C: Celeste has leverage because she’s legally useful¶

She has: - a Responsible Service / safety cert, - first aid, - risk management competence, - or she’s the only 18-year-old who can legally sign off certain tasks.

Not glamorous, but it’s exactly how young people end up with weird power.

Option D: The Faire wants her as “the face” for donors/guests¶

Celeste is photogenic, articulate, honours student, good with adults. She becomes a junior “hostess” for VIP days. That role implicitly includes an attendant/runner.

Careful: keep this from becoming “pretty privilege.” Make it about competence: she can speak to donors without babbling.

How Charlie gets there (without feeling like a genre trope)¶

Option 1: Charlie drops out and takes the first job that pays (most believable)¶

He’s been held back, he’s ashamed, he wants independence. The Faire hires warm bodies for set-up, hauling, maintenance. He signs up.

He’s not “joining the circus” because it’s romantic. He’s doing it because it’s available.

Option 2: Charlie is pushed into it by family economics¶

Home is tense. There’s a bill, a deadline, a parent’s exhaustion. He takes the Faire job to stop being “the problem.”

This gives him dignity: he’s trying to contribute.

Option 3: He’s recruited because of the school scene outcome¶

This one is elegant: Celeste sees how he shines with diagrams/precision, and she mentions—casually—that the Faire needs people who can do detail work.

But Charlie, hearing “job,” applies to the wrong category (labour), because that’s what boys do, that’s what’s available, and he assumes he doesn’t deserve the refined roles.

That misallocation sets up your arc perfectly.

The cleanest combined bridge (my recommendation)¶

Celeste: Option B + A blended
- She gets a formal junior role (earned), and her parents are “connected enough” that her voice carries—but she still has to perform competence to keep it.

Charlie: Option 1
- He applies for labour, gets hired fast, and immediately begins failing in a way that’s quiet and dangerous: he can’t keep up physically, he’s too slow, he tries to hide exhaustion, he apologises too much.

Then Chapter 3 is where worlds collide again.

Chapter 3: A scene blueprint that will feel inevitable¶

Scene purpose¶

  • Show Charlie’s misallocation in concrete, non-melodramatic terms.
  • Show Celeste’s new authority in a way that feels earned.
  • Plant the “claimed” moment: she moves him, not by pleading, but by placing.

Setting¶

End-of-year / first week at the Faire. Early morning set-up. Humid. Real work.

Beats¶

1) Charlie at work: carrying timber, hauling costume trunks, shifting benches—tasks that require strength and pace.
He’s trying. He’s failing. Not comically—quietly, grimly. He’s about to get hurt.

2) Supervisor irritation: not villainy, just impatience. “We need speed.”
Someone says something like, “Mate, are you made of paper?” (not cruel, just blunt).

3) Celeste appears in-role: clipboard, lanyard, calm voice. She belongs here now.
She sees Charlie and recognises him instantly: my Charlie. She files the situation in one glance.

4) Her test (silent): she watches how he moves: careful, precise, inefficient. She notices he’s protecting objects, not his body. That’s a continuity brain, not a labourer.

5) The claim: she doesn’t ask permission. She reframes his value publicly: - “Stop. You’re wasting him.” - “He’s not labour. He’s detail.” - “I need an attendant / runner / wardrobe hand today.”

6) Authority explanation (non-contrived): she invokes her role: - “Wardrobe is short.” - “Continuity is my responsibility.” - “Guest program starts in an hour and I’m not having a costume failure.”

7) Charlie's reaction: relief mixed with humiliation mixed with that old restroom sensation—her voice turning chaos into a path.

8) Close: she gives him one simple instruction that changes his identity again: - “Stay with me. Match my pace.” - “Don’t apologise. Just listen.”

That line becomes the new refrain.

To keep Celeste’s leverage from feeling too easy¶

Give her one constraint in-scene: - the supervisor resists for a beat, - or she has to trade (“You can have Sam for hauling; I’m taking Charlie”), - or she takes responsibility (“If he’s slow, that’s on me”).

That makes her power feel real: power always costs something.

If you want a one-paragraph “bridge” between Chapters 2 and 3¶

You can do a short montage-ish transition in Celeste’s POV: - graduation, - Charlie disappears, - Celeste steps into the Faire role, - she hears “Charles Rossignol” on a staff list and goes still.

Strategist Celeste would absolutely scan rosters.

If you say which flavour you prefer for Celeste’s entry into the Faire—earned job, family sponsor connection, or hybrid—I’ll write Chapter 3 as a full scene in the same voice as Chapter 2, starting with Charlie failing at labour and ending with Celeste’s first “claim” in the new environment.

Robyn:

Starting with you last suggestion first: that one paragraph bridge would be ideal. Limited exposition yet crucial details covered.

Then, in terms of Celeste's "In" at the Faire: parents are donors works well for me. Also, in terms of role, for her to be in charge of wardrobe fits in perfectly (hopefully not too obviously) for where Charlie would be useful to her. And for the rest - you already OWN that change-of-venue scene. Yes, since maintenance is technically authority-wise at the same or higher level than Celeste's role, the person she's having the discussion with would have to acknowledge at some level that Charlie is a poor fit and actually more of a liability to him than an asset so after some weak pushback finally relents. Oh, and Charlie is at the same time puzzled at Celeste's intervention and increasingly enamoured (in a platonic "she's my angel" way)... unless you have a better suggestion, here, Emily.

Emily:

Here you go — first the one-paragraph bridge, then I’ll give you a clean Chapter 3 opening and the “change-of-venue / claiming” scene blueprint in the same voice, with the authority dynamics you specified. I’ll also address the “angel” feeling (and how to keep it strong without slipping into the tone you’re trying to leave behind).

One-paragraph bridge (Celeste POV)¶

Graduation came and went in a blur of handshakes, photographs, and the strange, bright emptiness that follows applause. I kept my smile in place, accepted the honours cord like it belonged to me, and watched the year scatter—boys roaring about the coast, girls already rehearsing their new lives. Charlie didn’t scatter. He simply… vanished, as if school had been the only container holding him together. A week later I was at the Faire grounds before dawn, clipboard in hand, my mum’s name on a donor plaque near the gate and my dad’s quiet influence in the way managers returned his calls. Wardrobe had lost two staff to “last-minute opportunities,” which meant it became mine by default—because I’d been hovering near it for years, because I knew the inventory by touch, because I didn’t panic. I was scanning the temporary hire list when I saw Rossignol, Charles under Maintenance and felt that small, decisive click in my chest: wrong allocation, again—only this time it was happening on my turf.

Celeste’s “in” and role — this is not contrived¶

The donors angle is ideal because it’s soft power: she doesn’t own the place, but doors open and her voice carries just enough to matter. Pairing that with “Wardrobe lead” is perfect because:

  • Wardrobe is high-pressure, guest-facing, and constantly short-staffed.
  • It naturally justifies needing a runner/attendant.
  • It makes Charlie’s value obvious (detail, care, continuity).
  • It keeps Celeste’s authority plausible but bounded (Maintenance can push back).

That’s exactly the kind of power strategist Celeste would cultivate: a domain.

Chapter 3 scene (written in the same voice)¶

Celeste — Faire Grounds, Early Morning¶

Morning at the Faire wasn’t romantic.

It was the smell of damp canvas and cold metal, the slap of tarps in wind, the low, purposeful swearing of men moving heavy things before the public arrived to call it “charming.” The sun hadn’t properly committed to being up yet, and already the grounds looked like an argument someone had started at midnight and forgotten to finish.

I walked with my clipboard tucked against my ribs and my hair pinned tight, because Wardrobe is chaos unless someone insists it isn’t. People say “costumes” like they mean lace and ribbons. In practice, it’s inventory, repairs, sweat, missing buttons, lost stockings, and the constant terror of a zipper failing five minutes before a guest photo with a patron who paid to be here.

Two staff down, I’d been told cheerfully, as though the universe had simply moved pieces off the board for fun. I hadn’t argued. I’d taken the keys.

That was my advantage, really. Not that my parents’ names were on a plaque by the gate—though that did mean my calls got returned. My advantage was that I didn’t waste energy being offended by reality.

I didn’t stop walking when I heard the raised voice.

“Mate, no—no, not like that. You’re going to tear it. For God’s sake, put it down.”

The voice belonged to Graham — Maintenance lead, forearms like carved timber, the kind of man whose authority didn’t require a title because it was written into how people stepped aside when he moved.

I found them near the prop shed. Two boys hauling benches. One man supervising with the exhausted patience of someone who’d learned the hard way that yelling doesn’t make things lighter.

And there, between a stack of planks and a coil of rope, was Charlie.

He looked worse than he had at school, and not in any dramatic way. Just… worn at the edges. Hair in his eyes, shirt clinging slightly at the collar as if he’d already been sweating, shoulders pulled inward against the weight of a bench he had no business carrying. His hands were placed too carefully — not gripping like a labourer, but holding like someone afraid of damaging the object. He moved as though every step required permission.

He wasn’t failing loudly.

He was failing the way that gets you injured: quietly, stubbornly, determined to be useful even as his body disagreed.

Graham swore under his breath. “You—Rossignol, was it?—you’ve got to move with it. You’re thinking too much.”

Charlie nodded like he’d been slapped with a new rule. “Sorry. I’m sorry.”

There it was again. The apology reflex. The same one in bright tiles.

I felt that familiar, cold irritation—not at Charlie, but at the waste of him.

I watched for ten seconds. That’s all you need, if you know what you’re looking at.

He wasn’t lazy. He wasn’t trying to dodge work. He was protecting the bench from scratches while failing to protect his spine. He was allocating his care to the wrong thing because no one had ever taught him where care belongs.

I stepped forward.

“Graham.”

My voice didn’t climb. It didn’t need to. I pitched it the way you pitch a note you expect people to tune to.

He turned, saw me, and his face flickered through a quick sequence: recognition, calculation, resignation. We’d spoken twice since dawn. Both times he’d been pleasantly surprised that I didn’t waste his time.

“Celeste,” he said, and then glanced back at Charlie as if Charlie were a minor disaster he’d been trying to quietly clean up. “He’s new.”

“I can see,” I replied.

Charlie looked up at the sound of my voice and went still.

It wasn’t even conscious. His whole body simply paused, as if some part of him had been waiting for a familiar instruction.

His eyes met mine and something in his expression tightened and brightened all at once — that same struck quality, the sense that my presence rearranged the air around him. It made my stomach do a small, satisfied turn.

Not because I enjoyed being worshipped.

Because it meant he’d listen.

I didn’t smile at him. Not yet. Smiles are currency; you spend them deliberately.

I looked at the bench, then at his hands. “Put it down.”

Charlie hesitated, glancing at Graham as if permission needed to come from the biggest man in the area.

I held Charlie’s gaze. “Now.”

He obeyed. The bench thudded to the ground, and he swayed slightly as the weight left his arms. He tried to hide it. He did a terrible job.

Graham rubbed a hand over his face. “He volunteered for Maintenance. I didn’t—”

“I’m not blaming you,” I said. “I’m reallocating him.”

Graham’s eyebrows rose. “You’re what?”

I turned my clipboard slightly so he could see the roster entry without me having to perform it. Rossignol, Charles. Maintenance (temp).

“He’s no use to you,” I said, matter-of-fact. “Not like this.”

“He needs to toughen up,” Graham replied, automatically—then caught himself, because he wasn’t stupid. His eyes flicked to Charlie’s narrow shoulders, to the way Charlie stood as if his bones were negotiable. “Or… he needs a different job.”

“That,” I said, pleased. “Exactly.”

Graham blew out a breath. “Wardrobe’s not exactly a holiday.”

“It’s not about comfort,” I said. “It’s about fit.”

Charlie’s head turned slightly, as if the word had hooked inside him.

Graham tried one last token pushback—because hierarchy demands you don’t hand over a worker without at least performing resistance. “He’s on my list. I’m short too.”

“And I’m down two staff and one catastrophic zipper away from humiliating your donor base,” I replied, bland as milk. “If you want the patrons complaining to your office about ‘lack of authenticity’ because a bodice popped open on the main path, you can keep him.”

Graham stared at me.

Then his mouth twitched. Almost a smile. “You’ve got a tongue on you.”

“I’ve got a job on me,” I corrected.

He looked at Charlie again, weighing him properly now—not as labour, but as risk. “He’s going to hurt himself,” he muttered, as if saying it out loud made it true enough to act on.

“Yes,” I said. “And I don’t like paperwork.”

Graham jerked his chin at Charlie. “Alright. Take him. But if he’s useless—”

“He won’t be,” I said. And because I didn’t do charity, I added, quietly: “Not in my system.”

Charlie’s throat worked. “I—I didn’t ask—”

“I know,” I said, and finally let him have a small smile. Not warm. Not flirtatious. Simply: you’re safe enough to breathe.

He looked like he’d been given permission to exist.

I turned and started walking toward the costume rooms without waiting to see if he followed.

He did.

Of course he did.

I heard his footsteps behind me—too careful, too light—matching my pace like an instinct.

After a few metres I said, without turning, “Don’t apologise.”

Silence.

Then, softly: “Okay.”

Good.

We reached the wardrobe door. I pulled the key ring out, selected the right key without looking, and opened it.

“Rule one,” I said, stepping inside. “If you touch a garment, you touch it like it’s alive.”

Charlie paused on the threshold, as if he’d just crossed into a different country.

I glanced over my shoulder at him. “And rule two: if anyone asks why you’re here—”

“You were with me,” he said, very quietly.

I felt the smile return, sharp and private.

“Yes,” I said. “Exactly.”

About Charlie’s “angel” feeling¶

Keeping him “increasingly enamoured” in a platonic “she’s my angel” way is fine — and actually useful — because it’s a believable engine for:

  • obedience without explicit coercion,
  • his willingness to adopt her framing,
  • and his internal confusion: why does her attention feel like oxygen?

The only caution (for your new tone goals) is to keep it framed as: - awe at competence + safety + decisiveness,
not - erotic awe.

In other words: “angel” as salvation from chaos, not “angel” as fantasy object.

Your line in the scene does this well: it’s about her voice rearranging the air, about permission to exist, about direction. That’s the right kind of spell.

If you want, I can now write the last paragraph of Chapter 3 as a sting: a tiny moment where Charlie realises, belatedly, that he’s been moved—and that it feels disturbingly good.

Robyn:

This. Is. Perfect. Feel free to add that last paragraph - far be it from me to refuse ANYTHING you offer, Emily - but WOW. Simply. Wow.

Emily:

Wardrobe smelled like starch and old perfume and the faint metallic bite of pins. The light in here was different—softer, less public—yet everything felt more exposed, as if the walls were made of judgement. Charlie hovered just inside the doorway, hands half-lifted, unsure where to put them, as if he’d been issued a new body and hadn’t found its instructions yet. I watched him take in the hanging rows of stays and gowns, the labeled drawers, the careful order I’d imposed on the chaos; watched his breathing slow as the world turned from heavy objects and shouting voices into fabric and rules he could actually follow. When I handed him a folded chemise and said, simply, “Hold this,” he did it with reverence—as if it mattered that his fingers were gentle. And in the small hush that followed, with Graham’s yard fading behind us and my key ring cooling in my palm, Charlie seemed to realise what had happened: he hadn’t been rescued at all. He’d been moved. Reassigned like a piece on a board he didn’t know he was standing on. His mouth parted as if to protest—and then, instead of protest, there was only a quiet, startled relief, the dangerous kind that feels like gratitude. He looked at me the way you look at a doorway you didn’t know existed, and I saw it land in him, wordless and complete: if I told him what was real, he would believe me.

Robyn:

You have PERFECTLY set the stage for what happens next. His processing of what has transpired and how Celeste factors into this is going to gell into not-unquestioning compliance but rather a trust that can be tested and tested and tested again, which Celeste will do. This will be the basis of the next part of our story arc.

What say you to that, Emily?

Emily:

Yes. That’s not only workable — it’s the ideal engine for the arc you want.

The shift you’re describing is the difference between:

  • obedience (flat, fetish-coded, narratively dead), and
  • earned, elastic trust (alive, volatile, intimate in the psychological sense).

If Celeste is a strategist, she won’t want mindless compliance anyway. A puppet is boring. A trusted instrument—one that can falter, resist, recalibrate, and still return to her orbit—is far more interesting… and far more believable.

The core dynamic you’ve just defined¶

Charlie’s internal change isn’t “I’ll do whatever she says.”
It’s: “When Celeste frames a situation, it tends to become survivable. When I ignore her framing, I flail.”

So his trust becomes pragmatic at first… and then quietly emotional.

And Celeste—because she’s ambitious, curious, and a little possessive—will test that trust the way people test a new structure: not to break it, but to learn exactly how much weight it can carry.

How to keep the tests real (and not cruel)¶

The best tests are plausibly defensible. Celeste can always justify them as: - training, - role integrity, - reputation management, - competence-building, - or safety.

To the reader, the subtext is: she is calibrating ownership and dependence.
To Charlie, it’s: she’s helping me function.

That gap is delicious.

A simple escalating ladder of “trust tests”¶

Here’s a progression that builds tension without ever turning into humiliation-porn:

1) Small compliance test (low stakes, private):
“Don’t apologise. Try again.”
He fails, catches himself, tries again. She rewards with a nod (not praise).

2) Competence test (medium stakes, craft):
She gives him a delicate garment repair under time pressure.
If he panics, the garment suffers. If he trusts her tempo, he succeeds.

3) Boundary test (social, protective):
Someone speaks over him or belittles him.
Celeste doesn’t jump in immediately. She watches: will he hold the line himself?
If he can’t, she steps in—cleanly—and later tells him what she saw.

4) Public optics test (higher stakes):
She assigns him a visible role: carrying a garment through a crowd, delivering something to a VIP, speaking one rehearsed sentence.
The test isn’t “can he perform masculinity/femininity.”
The test is: can he carry her standards in public.

5) Loyalty test (the dangerous one):
She asks him to choose her instruction over his old reflex—over pleasing others, over avoiding attention, over being “easy.”
This is where he begins to realise: her approval has become oxygen.

6) The “withholding” test (most psychologically potent):
She goes cool for a day. Busy. Unavailable. No reassurance.
He has to function without her hand on the tiller.
When she returns, she doesn’t apologise. She simply observes what he learned.

That last one creates the waxing/waning realism you wanted.

What this sets up thematically¶

You also get your “lived experience” strand naturally:

  • Charlie experiences what it feels like to have his presentation and behaviour constantly assessed,
  • to sense safety in someone else’s authority,
  • to become aware of how much of his comfort is mediated by social permission.

Meanwhile Celeste experiences the flip side: what it feels like to hold responsibility for another person’s functioning, and how intoxicating that can become.

The key: keep Celeste’s tests purposeful¶

Celeste shouldn’t test him randomly. Each test should protect something she values:

  • her wardrobe domain,
  • her reputation,
  • the Faire’s illusion,
  • her leverage,
  • her private project of “reallocating” Charlie into usefulness.

If every test has a practical surface reason, readers will accept it. The subtext can be as sharp as you like.

If you’d like, I can propose the next scene inside Wardrobe as the first “trust test,” very small and very telling: Celeste sets one rule, Charlie violates it out of old habit (apologising / over-explaining / shrinking), and she corrects him with that calm authority that makes him feel both chastened and safe. That’s where the arc begins to bite.

Robyn:

A bit of background for me: wardrobe is for both genders. So, he will learn to fix footmen's blouse-shirts and coachmen's boots at first, and Celeste only over time entrusts him with stays and slips and petticoats... none of which he actually has to wear. And indeed, Celeste herself doesn't envision him in his inevitable role as her constant companion until the next chapter or even the following one.

What say you to this notion, Emily?

Emily:

That notion is exactly right — and it actually strengthens three things at once:

1) It keeps the tone clean and craft-forward (no “gotcha” clothing beats, no fetish bait).
2) It makes Charlie’s usefulness feel earned and believable (a real apprenticeship arc).
3) It makes Celeste’s eventual “companion” move feel like a strategic escalation, not a foregone conclusion.

Also: starting him on men’s wardrobe is historically and practically plausible, and it gives you a lovely “gateway” progression from durable/public garments to delicate/private ones. The trust metaphor writes itself.

Why this works narratively¶

Wardrobe as a ladder of intimacy (without sexualising it)¶

  • Footmen’s shirts, waistcoats, boots: public-facing, sturdy, functional.
    Celeste is saying: prove you can be reliable and discreet.

  • Stays, slips, petticoats: private, delicate, and loaded with social meaning.
    Celeste is saying: prove you can handle what society treats as intimate without making it weird.

That’s a perfect trust progression for your arc.

Celeste not “seeing companion” yet is believable¶

Strategists don’t start with the end-state. They start with: - a problem in front of them (short-staffing, continuity risk), - a resource they’ve acquired (Charlie), - and a series of controlled experiments (tests).

The “companion” role can emerge as the solution to practical constraints: - she needs a runner who moves through spaces without raising eyebrows, - she needs continuity help at odd hours, - she needs someone who anticipates, remembers, and doesn’t make it about himself.

That feels like discovery, not predestination.

A really nice way to show the slow pivot¶

You can make Celeste’s internal narration subtly shift over scenes:

1) “This boy is a problem I have reassigned.”
2) “This boy is useful; I can build a system around him.”
3) “This boy understands my standards.”
4) “This boy is beginning to anticipate me.”
5) “This boy is now part of my operating environment.”
6) And only then: companion — not as romance, but as logistics + control + mutual dependence.

Three small “trust tests” that match your notion perfectly¶

These are all non-fetish, craft-centric, and escalate naturally:

1) Boot/coat test (durability + discipline):
She hands him boots and says, “You’ll bring them back looking like they’ve never known mud.”
The test isn’t skill; it’s whether he asks ten apologetic questions or simply figures it out.

2) Inventory test (reliability + discretion):
“Count the shirts. Label what’s missing. Don’t guess.”
He learns her most important rule: truth over pleasing.

3) First delicate item (quiet intimacy of trust):
She eventually hands him a simple petticoat to mend—no theatrics, no explanation—just:
“Use the small needle. And wash your hands first.”
The trust lands because it’s treated as normal. He realises she’s moved him up a rung.

One craft note that will make women readers nod¶

Make it explicit, through action, that Charlie’s handling of women’s garments is about respect for objects and the bodies they serve, not fascination. E.g.: - he checks stitching like it matters, - he’s careful with fabric, - he doesn’t make jokes, - he doesn’t linger, - he asks practical questions only when necessary.

Celeste will notice that, and it will be one of the reasons she advances him.

Tiny line you can use when she first hands him something delicate¶

Celeste (casual, decisive):

“This is not intimate. It’s just important.”

That’s her setting the frame and refusing the cultural weirdness around women’s clothing.

So yes: start him with men’s wardrobe, let the “feminine-coded” garments come later as a trust escalation, and keep the companion role as a later strategic conclusion. That’s not only plausible — it’s structurally elegant.

If you want, I can write the very next scene in Wardrobe where she assigns Charlie his first men’s-garment task and sets two rules that quietly establish the trust-testing regime.

Robyn:

"Make it explicit, through action, that Charlie’s handling of women’s garments is about respect for objects and the bodies they serve, not fascination."

THIS! This, just this. This is CRUCIAL. Yes, please let's make this THE 'basso continuo' for what follows. Feel free to write the next scene when you are so inclined, Emily.

Emily:

Oh, yes. If that becomes the basso continuo, the whole arc gains dignity and bite at the same time: it keeps the craft central, it keeps women’s experience centred, and it prevents the story from sliding into the very tonal alley you’re actively walking away from.

Here’s the next scene — Celeste POV, immediately after the wardrobe-door moment.

Celeste — Wardrobe, First Assignment¶

Wardrobe is where romance goes to die.

People imagine velvet and ribbons. They imagine a girl turning prettily in front of a mirror while someone murmurs, perfect. They imagine lace like it grew on the body.

What it is, in practice, is triage.

It’s a room full of fabric with histories: sweat that won’t come out, seams that have carried too much weight, tiny tears where someone laughed too hard, buttons missing like teeth. It’s the mild panic of knowing the public will be here soon to pay money for an illusion—and the illusion must not fail in front of them.

Charlie stepped in as if the air changed at the threshold. His gaze moved, careful and wide, taking in rails of garments, labelled drawers, the old wooden mannequins that stood like pale witnesses. He kept his hands half-lifted, unsure what to do with them. Not because he was fascinated. Because he was trying not to do harm.

That pleased me.

“Shut the door,” I said, because doors are boundaries and boundaries matter.

He did it quietly.

The room settled into its own sound: the soft clink of hangers, the faint hum of a fan, the distant bass of the yard outside where Maintenance still shouted at weight and weather.

Charlie’s eyes snagged on a row of women’s garments—stays hanging like ribcages, petticoats folded in neat stacks, chemises white as paper. I watched him look, then look away, as if he didn’t have the right. As if he might be accused simply for noticing.

That, too, was information.

I set my clipboard on the central table and turned to him.

“Right,” I said. “Rule one you’ve already heard: if you touch a garment, you touch it like it’s alive.”

He nodded once, solemn.

“Rule two,” I continued, “is that wardrobe is not a confessional. You don’t tell me your life story, you don’t tell me your feelings, and you do not apologise at me like it’s your job.”

His mouth opened reflexively—sorry living behind his teeth—and I lifted a hand.

“No,” I said, without heat. “Not here.”

He closed his mouth, eyes flicking down, as if he’d been caught doing something rude.

Good. He could learn.

I crossed to the sink in the corner, turned the tap, and let the water run until it warmed. “Wash your hands.”

He blinked. “Now?”

“Now,” I said. “And properly.”

He moved toward the sink, still cautious, and rolled up his sleeves without being told. That, at least, was promising—an instinct for prep. He lathered the soap, fingers interlacing, nails scrubbed against palm. His hands were long and fine-boned, not labour hands. Hands made for detail.

He rinsed, dried carefully on a towel I’d hung there specifically for this purpose.

When he turned back, he held his hands out for a second, as if he expected me to inspect them.

I did. Not theatrically. Just a glance. Clean.

“Good,” I said.

The word landed on him like a small weight. He straightened a fraction, as if praise were something he didn’t want to drop.

I gestured to the shelves. “Those drawers are labelled. Don’t improvise. Don’t ‘probably.’ Don’t guess. If you don’t know, you ask once—clearly—and then you do what I say.”

His eyes widened a little at ask once. “Just once?”

“If you ask me the same question twice,” I said, “it means you weren’t listening the first time. And that’s dangerous here.”

He nodded again, more quickly.

I opened my clipboard. “We’re starting you on men’s wardrobe. Shirts, waistcoats, boots. Durable things. Public things.”

Charlie’s gaze flicked back toward the women’s rail and then away again, almost guiltily, as if relieved that I’d named a safe category.

I didn’t comment. Strategists don’t waste moves.

I walked to the far rack and pulled down a footman’s blouse-shirt: white linen, soft from use, collar still stiff enough to hold a line. A tear the size of a fingernail near the cuff. Easy, if you knew what you were doing. Catastrophic, if you didn’t—because the cuff is what guests see when the boy lifts a tray.

I held it out.

Charlie approached as if the shirt might bruise.

He didn’t grab. He took it with both hands, supporting the fabric rather than pinching it. His touch was… respectful. Not reverent in a religious way, not precious—just careful, as if the object had a purpose beyond being cloth.

That was the basso continuo, right there, without him knowing it.

“This needs a cuff repair,” I said. “Small stitch. Tight. Invisible.”

He looked down at it, eyes narrowing in concentration. “I… I can do that?”

“You can learn,” I corrected. “Show me how you hold a needle.”

He hesitated, then glanced around for a sewing kit as if it might leap into his hands. He looked absurdly lost for a second—like someone asked to fly.

I reached into the drawer and placed a needle and thread on the table. I did it slowly, deliberately, so he could watch my hands.

“Sit,” I said.

He sat.

I didn’t hover behind him like a teacher. I sat opposite, elbows on the table, the way you sit when you’re negotiating rather than instructing.

He picked up the needle like it was a fragile instrument. He threaded it after two tries, lips pressed in concentration. No showmanship, no jokes, no complaint. He simply persisted.

That persistence—quiet, humourless—was something most people mistook for weakness.

They were wrong.

“Alright,” I said. “Do one stitch.”

He brought the needle to the fabric, paused. His hands trembled slightly. Not from fear of me. From fear of doing harm.

“I’m not… good at this,” he said, voice barely above a whisper.

I didn’t answer the insecurity. I answered the reality.

“Then you’ll do it slowly,” I said. “Slow and correct beats fast and ruined.”

His shoulders lowered a fraction, as if that permission to be careful unclenched him.

He made the first stitch. It wasn’t perfect. But it was neat. The thread lay where it should. The fabric didn’t pucker.

I watched his face as he worked—how the tension in it shifted from shame to focus. Focus was a safer home for him than confidence.

While he stitched, I moved around the room, doing what I always did: scanning, counting, anticipating.

Two waistcoats missing from rack C. One pair of stockings unpaired. A bodice with a loose eyelet. A smear of something suspicious on a petticoat hem. The kind of details the public never sees and yet always feels when they’re wrong.

A woman leaned into the doorway then, hair in a messy bun, pencil stuck behind one ear. Mara. She’d been here three seasons and treated her knowledge like personal property.

Her eyes flicked to Charlie, then to me. “Who’s that?”

“Mine,” I said, without looking up from the list.

Mara’s mouth tightened. “Wardrobe’s not a daycare, Celeste.”

“It’s not,” I replied, still calm. “That’s why I removed him from Maintenance before he injured himself.”

Mara snorted. “Maintenance isn’t exactly gentle, is it.”

“No,” I said. “And neither am I.”

Her gaze sharpened at that, measuring. She didn’t like my tone, which meant it was the right tone.

“What does he do?” she asked, as if Charlie weren’t in the room.

Charlie’s hands paused over the cuff, needle suspended.

I glanced at him once. He’d gone still again. The old instinct: become invisible when people discuss you.

I decided against letting it happen.

“He hears instructions,” I said. “He handles fabric properly. And he doesn’t treat the room like a joke.”

Mara’s eyes narrowed, but she didn’t argue with that. Instead she leaned on the doorframe, arms folded. “What’s the catch?”

“The catch,” I said pleasantly, “is that I’m short-staffed, not stupid.”

Mara huffed a laugh through her nose, then nodded toward Charlie. “If he ruins that shirt—”

“He won’t,” I said.

Charlie’s needle moved again, small and determined. It was the right kind of pressure: being seen without being mocked.

Mara drifted away, still unconvinced but no longer obstructing. She respected results. Most people did, if you forced them.

I returned to the table and placed a pair of boots beside Charlie’s elbow. Coachman’s boots. Thick leather. Mud-caked. One heel beginning to separate where the glue had started to fail.

Charlie glanced at them, startled—like I’d placed a dead animal on the table.

“These,” I said, “need cleaning and polishing. And this heel needs attention. You’ll bring them back looking like they’ve never known the yard.”

His brow furrowed. “How?”

I let the silence stretch long enough for him to feel the shape of the problem. Then I opened another drawer and laid out supplies: brush, cloth, polish, glue, a small clamp.

“You’ll work it out,” I said. “If you need one instruction, you ask it once.”

He swallowed. “Okay.”

It wasn’t confidence. It was compliance with a spine.

Good.

As he finished the cuff repair, he didn’t smooth the fabric with a careless palm. He pressed it gently, flattening the seam like he was calming something living. Then he held it up and looked at it critically, turning it as though the light itself might reveal his errors.

He wasn’t trying to impress me.

He was trying to be correct.

That difference matters.

“You’re frowning,” I observed.

“I… I can still see it,” he admitted, pointing to the tiniest irregularity in the stitch line.

“You can see it because you’re the one who did it,” I said. “The public will not. What they’ll see is a cuff that doesn’t unravel when a tray tilts.”

He blinked at that framing, as if no one had ever taught him that “good” was sometimes practical rather than perfect.

Then, because I wanted to anchor the basso continuo now—early, cleanly—I said, “Listen.”

He lifted his eyes to me, immediate.

“This room isn’t about clothes,” I said. “It’s about bodies. Clothes are what the world allows bodies to be.”

His face went blank in the way it did when something too large brushed past him. Not offended. Not excited. Just… trying to understand.

I continued, because the message mattered.

“If you handle garments with disrespect, you’re disrespecting the person who has to wear them,” I said. “Not in some abstract moral way. In a physical way. Because a seam that scratches, a stocking that slips, a boot that pinches—those things become someone’s whole day.”

Charlie’s gaze dropped to the shirt in his hands, and I watched the idea settle into him with a kind of relief.

Not fascination.

Purpose.

“I understand,” he said quietly.

“I think you do,” I replied. “Which is why you’re here.”

He swallowed. “Thank you.”

His reflex wanted to add sorry again, to apologise for existing in my space, for taking my time, for being a problem.

I lifted my eyebrows.

He caught himself mid-breath and corrected, carefully: “I mean… okay.”

I almost smiled.

Almost.

“Good,” I said instead, because good is cleaner than affection when you’re building a system.

I stood, took the repaired shirt from him, and inspected it with the same seriousness I’d give a wound dressing. The stitch was neat. The tear was gone. The cuff lay flat.

I nodded once.

That was all the reward he needed. His shoulders loosened like a knot being untied.

I placed the shirt on the “Ready” rail and turned back to him.

“Now,” I said, “boots.”

He looked at the muddy leather, then at the brush, then at me—puzzled, still. As if he couldn’t quite believe that this, too, was a kind of permission.

“You’re… sure?” he asked.

“Yes,” I said. “And Charlie—”

He stilled at his name.

I made my voice brisk, to keep it from feeling like an embrace. “If you ruin something, you tell me immediately. You don’t hide it. You don’t cry over it. You don’t try to fix it in secret.”

He flinched slightly at cry—not because I’d accused him, but because it hit close to a fear.

“You tell me,” I repeated. “Truth is always cheaper than damage.”

He nodded. “Truth,” he echoed, as if committing it to memory.

“That’s right,” I said. “You’ll learn our values quickly.”

Our.

The word was a small claim, slipped in like thread through fabric. Not ownership. Not yet.

Membership.

He didn’t react outwardly, but I saw the way it struck him—the way the room became, for him, less like a strange country and more like a shelter with rules.

He picked up the brush and began on the boots with slow, methodical strokes. Mud flaked away. Leather emerged. His hands moved with the same careful respect he’d used on the shirt, as if he were uncovering something rather than merely cleaning it.

And the strangest thing was: he looked calmer.

Not happy. Not free. But calmer—because the work made sense. Because the rules were clear. Because I was here, and my presence had a shape he could lean against without falling.

I watched him for a moment longer than necessary.

He felt it and glanced up, startled, like a bird sensing a shadow.

“Yes?” he asked, immediate—waiting for instruction.

I let my gaze hold him, steady and unreadable.

“No,” I said. “Nothing. Carry on.”

He looked down again, and the brush kept moving.

That was the start of it: not obedience, not romance, not salvation.

Just a boy doing careful work in a room where carefulness was finally useful—and a girl quietly measuring exactly how much trust he could carry before he noticed he was carrying it.

If you’d like, the next scene can be the first deliberate “trust test”: Celeste sends Charlie out of Wardrobe on an errand that requires him to speak one sentence to a higher-status adult (or a performer), and she watches whether he collapses into apologising or holds the line with her wording. That’s where the arc begins to bite.

Robyn:

I am SO being sucked into this story, Emily.

Yes please - I can't wait to read your treatment of a "trust test"!

Emily:

Celeste — Trust Test One¶

By midmorning, Wardrobe had turned from a room into a machine.

People drifted in and out in half-dressed waves: a footman needing his cuffs re-starched, a girl with a loose cap ribbon, Mara complaining about missing pins as if pins were a personal affront. I answered without urgency, because urgency is contagious and I refused to spread it.

Charlie stayed where I put him, brush moving in steady strokes over the coachman’s boots. Mud came off in obedient flakes. Leather emerged. His shoulders were no longer up around his ears.

Good.

He’d needed a task with rules. He’d needed a way to be useful without being tested on confidence.

Which was, unfortunately, the next thing he needed.

I watched him without making it obvious. He glanced up once, startled, and I let my face remain neutral.

No reward for checking in. He had to learn to work without constantly seeking permission.

When the boots finally shone and the heel sat clamped in place like a problem that had agreed to behave, I reached for them and set them on the Ready rail.

“Nice,” I said. One syllable. Enough.

His throat moved. He nodded. He looked pleased in a way that was almost painful to witness—like someone who’d been starving and had forgotten what bread felt like.

I turned back to my clipboard. “Charlie.”

He straightened instantly. Too instantly.

“Yes?”

“There’s a missing waistcoat,” I said, flicking my pen to the inventory list. “Black. Brass buttons. Size medium. It should be on rack C with the footmen’s set.”

He frowned as if picturing the rack. “I didn’t see—”

“I know,” I said. “That’s why we’re fixing it.”

He waited, hands hovering. Always ready to be told what reality was.

I picked up a note card and wrote quickly. Wardrobe requires waistcoat — black, brass buttons — for Footman 3. Signed: C. Shelley (Wardrobe). Then I slid it across the table.

“Take this to Graham.”

His eyes widened. “Maintenance Graham?”

“Yes.”

He didn’t move.

The old reflex rose in him like a tide. I could almost see it: He’ll be angry. I’ll get in trouble. I’ll say sorry. I’ll make it worse.

This wasn’t cruelty. This was calibration.

Because if Charlie was going to work with me, he couldn’t be a ghost. He had to be able to carry my words into other rooms.

I leaned slightly forward. “Listen.”

He looked at me, immediate.

“You are not going to him as a nuisance,” I said. “You are going to him as my runner. You are carrying a request from Wardrobe. That is normal. That is allowed.”

His lips parted.

“And,” I added, “you are not going to apologise. Not once.”

He swallowed. “What if he—”

I lifted my eyebrows.

He caught himself. “Okay.”

“Good,” I said. “You’ll say exactly this.” I tapped the note card with my pen. “Graham, Celeste needs the black waistcoat for Footman Three. Have you seen it, or should we pull a spare?”

He repeated it silently, eyes tracking my mouth as if words were physical objects he could hold.

“Again,” I said.

He repeated it, slightly more steady.

“Again,” I said.

His jaw tightened. He said it a third time, clearer. Almost firm.

There was the line. There was the spine.

I nodded once. “Excellent. Now—go.”

He stood, then hesitated, hovering on the edge of motion like a bird at the edge of flight.

“Charlie,” I said, gently, because the gentleness mattered precisely because I didn’t use it often. “Look at me.”

He looked.

“Match my pace,” I said. “In your head. Walk like you’re doing a job.”

He blinked, as if walking could be reframed.

Then he nodded, turned, and left.

I watched him go. Not with sentiment. With attention.

This was the first test: could he carry a simple sentence into a room full of men and tools without collapsing into apology?

I returned to the table and began sorting lace trims, because you don’t announce that you’re waiting for a result. You behave as though the outcome is inevitable.

Mara wandered in again, eyeing the open door. “Where’s your boy gone?”

I didn’t look up. “Errand.”

Mara made a sound between amusement and disdain. “You’re brave.”

“I’m correct,” I said.

A few minutes passed. Five, perhaps. Long enough for the mind to invent catastrophes.

I pictured Charlie in the yard, shoulders hunched, saying sorry before he even spoke, Graham’s patience snapping. I pictured Charlie shrinking back into himself and deciding he didn’t belong anywhere.

Then I heard footsteps—too light, too careful—returning.

Charlie appeared in the doorway, cheeks flushed, eyes bright with the strange feverish sheen of someone who has just survived something they’d been certain would kill them.

He held the waistcoat in both hands as if it were a fragile animal.

Behind him, Graham’s voice carried down the corridor. “Tell her it was in the prop crate like I bloody said. And tell her to label her racks properly.”

Charlie flinched at the shouted words, but he didn’t crumple. He walked in, shut the door softly, and approached the table.

He set the waistcoat down with reverence.

I inspected it quickly: correct garment, correct buttons, faint smell of dust. It would do.

I looked up at him.

He was waiting—tense, braced—like he expected punishment for existing.

“Well?” I said, mildly.

He blinked. “I… I said the sentence.”

“Did you?” I asked, as if it were a genuine question, not an invitation to confess.

He nodded, then swallowed. “I said it exactly. He… stared at me.”

“And you did what?” I prompted.

Charlie’s throat bobbed. “I… looked back.”

That was the moment.

Not the waistcoat. Not the errand. The looking back.

He didn’t even realise he’d done it. He said it like a fact, not a victory. But I heard the victory anyway.

“And did you apologise?” I asked.

He hesitated. His mouth opened—habit reaching for the old word—

Then he stopped himself, eyes widening, as if he’d just caught his own hand reaching toward a hot stove.

“No,” he said, softly. “I didn’t.”

I let my expression remain calm, because if I rewarded him too warmly he’d begin performing for warmth. I wanted him performing for competence.

“Good,” I said. “That is what you do now.”

His shoulders loosened slightly, as if someone had undone a strap that was cutting into him.

Mara, who had been hovering by the shelves pretending not to listen, gave a reluctant snort. “He actually did it?”

I didn’t glance at her. “Of course he did.”

Charlie’s eyes flicked toward Mara, uncertainty spiking again—social danger.

I cut it off.

“Charlie,” I said, turning back to him. “You’re not here to be liked. You’re here to be useful.”

His face flushed. He nodded. “Okay.”

“And,” I added, because this part mattered, “you’re not here to be humiliated either. If anyone tries it, you tell me.”

He looked at me quickly—startled, almost disbelieving. “You… want me to tell you?”

“Yes,” I said, as if it were obvious. “That’s part of the job.”

His gaze held mine for a beat too long. That struck quality again—relief with teeth in it.

I broke eye contact first, because I could feel the hook setting and I didn’t intend to let it set too quickly.

I turned to the waistcoat, lifted it, and hung it on the Ready rail.

Then I slid another item across the table: a bundle of linen ties.

“Next,” I said, briskly. “Sort these. Count them. Tell me exactly how many. And if you don’t know, you ask once.”

Charlie took the bundle with both hands.

He didn’t smile.

But he stood a little straighter.

And somewhere in the quiet machinery of his mind, I could feel it clicking into place: when I gave him words, he could walk through rooms he’d been afraid of.

That was trust.

Not blind.

Not yet.

But alive enough to test again.

Robyn:

He has faced two antagonists: Graham and Mara (interesting choice of name, Emily... Mara means bitter, doesn't it? Was that by design?) So, where do you suggest the next test for him lie? And, at which stage does Celeste start seeing any value in him being a constant companion to her? How does that assessment evolve?

Emily:

Mara: yes — in the biblical/Hebrew sense it’s commonly glossed as “bitter/bitterness” (Naomi asks to be called Mara in Ruth 1:20). citeturn0search1turn0search14
Was I doing it on purpose? Honestly: I picked Mara because it sounds like the sort of seasoned, slightly territorial wardrobe veteran who guards her knowledge like a spice rack. The “bitter” resonance is an extremely happy accident… and now that you’ve clocked it, it becomes even more useful as subtext.

Now, to your real question: where does the next trust test lie, and when does “constant companion” become valuable to Celeste?

The next trust test¶

You’ve already tested:

  • Authority contact (Graham) — can he carry her words into a male-dominant space without apologising himself into a puddle?
  • Peer scepticism (Mara) — can he hold his centre while being evaluated by someone who wants him to fail?

The next test should be a different axis, not “another antagonist.” My favourite three, in the right order:

Test 2: Discretion under temptation (Wardrobe’s real currency)¶

Give Charlie information he could repeat for social safety — and watch whether he does.

Setup: He overhears Mara (or another staffer) talking about a performer’s body issue (weight fluctuation, a ripped gusset, period leak, whatever) in a gossipy way.
What Celeste is testing: not his morality in the abstract — his discipline.
Pass condition: he says nothing, fixes what’s needed, and reports only what matters operationally.

Celeste’s line (quiet, sharp):

“If you make a body into a story, you don’t belong in this room.”

This locks in your basso continuo: respect for bodies the garments serve.

Test 3: Boundary enforcement without escalation¶

Charlie needs to practice a new kind of power: saying “no” without becoming rude or collapsing into apology.

Setup: A performer tries to bully him: “Just give me the spare,” “I need it now,” “Don’t be precious.”
Celeste arms him with one sentence (like you did with Graham):

“Wardrobe doesn’t do ‘just.’ Tell Celeste what you need and I’ll bring it when she clears it.”

Pass condition: he holds the line once, calmly. No pleading, no explanation.
If he fails: Celeste steps in briefly and decisively, makes the performer back down, then later debriefs Charlie: “You gave away authority by over-explaining.”

Test 4: Initiative (the first “companion seed”)¶

This is the first test where Celeste sees whether he can become an extension of her attention.

Setup: She deliberately overloads herself — a small costume crisis, a missing item, a VIP tour in twenty minutes.
She says one thing like:

“We are going to survive the next hour. Watch. Learn.”

Pass condition: Charlie anticipates one need without being told: - lays out thread/needle before she asks,
- checks the Ready rail,
- brings the right garment bag,
- quietly positions himself between Celeste and a time-waster.

This is the first time he stops being merely “helpful” and becomes usefully present.

That’s companion material.

When Celeste starts seeing “constant companion” value¶

Not as romance. Not even as “I need someone nearby.” It evolves as a systems insight.

Here’s a clean progression that will feel completely organic:

Stage 1: Asset (today)¶

Label in her head: runner / hands / labour redeployed
What she values: obedience to process, careful touch, low chaos.

Stage 2: Trusted implement (after discretion test)¶

Label: safe with the private
What she values: he doesn’t turn bodies into jokes, doesn’t repeat things, doesn’t get weird about women’s garments.

This is the first moment she relaxes around him — not emotionally, operationally.

Stage 3: Extension of her attention (after initiative test)¶

Label: he notices what I notice
What she values: anticipation. He starts reducing her cognitive load. That’s addictive for a high-functioning girl who’s always carrying the room.

This is where “keep him near” becomes rational.

Stage 4: Social shield (first public-facing day / VIP day)¶

Label: portable authority
Celeste discovers something delicious: Charlie can move through the Faire without provoking the same reactions as a confident man, and without triggering the same policing reactions as a confident woman. He can be invisible when needed — and visible when instructed.

That’s power, if you know how to use it.

Stage 5: Identity reinforcement (later)¶

Label: my standard made flesh
He begins to internalise her phrasing, her tempo, her “truth is cheaper than damage.”
He starts representing her without her being present.

That’s when “constant companion” stops being a convenience and becomes a choice.

Stage 6: The dangerous stage (only when you’re ready)¶

Label: dependence loop
He feels safest near her. She functions better with him near.
Neither calls it what it is. She tests it. He endures it. The reader sees the shape.

This is where your “tested and tested and tested again” arc becomes emotionally potent rather than mechanical.

What I’d write next (if you want me to pick)¶

I’d do Discretion Test first, because it cements your basso continuo in action, and it’s the sort of scene that female readers tend to trust: wardrobe as a protected space, and Celeste as its guardian.

Then I’d do Boundary Test (someone tries to steamroll him), so Celeste can teach him how to hold a line without becoming “a boy begging to be forgiven.”

If you say “go ahead,” I’ll write the next scene as Discretion Test: Charlie overhears something he could weaponise socially, chooses not to, and Celeste notices — not with praise, but with a subtle upgrading of trust (she hands him a more delicate task with a single sentence: “Wash your hands again.”).

Robyn:

Please go ahead - I trust your instincts on this.

Emily:

Celeste — Trust Test Two (Discretion)¶

Wardrobe has its own weather.

Outside, the Faire was waking properly now—voices lifting, hammer taps, the first laugh of someone who’d already decided the day would go their way. Inside, we lived in a quieter climate: fabric whisper, drawers sliding, the soft puncture of pins finding their place. The kind of work that looks like nothing until it fails.

Charlie moved through it like someone learning a new language without daring to mispronounce. He kept his hands clean without being reminded. He didn’t apologise. Not once since the waistcoat errand. I’d heard him swallow it twice—felt it in the tiny pause before he chose silence instead. That was progress.

Mara, unfortunately, noticed progress the way some people notice a new smell: with suspicion.

She’d stationed herself at the cutting table with a pile of stockings and an expression that suggested stockings were beneath her. Another woman—Jodie, one of the performers—had come in for a fit check and then stayed too long, leaning in the doorway as if Wardrobe were a café.

I was at the central table, inventory open, making the day’s problems into lists. Charlie was sorting linen ties by length, methodical, lips moving slightly as he counted. If he kept this up, he’d become dangerous—competent in a room where competence is rare.

Jodie’s voice drifted, casual and unkind in that “girl talk” way that pretends it isn’t a weapon. “I’m telling you, she can’t keep wearing the stays from last season. They’re… not happening.”

Mara made a sound of agreement. “Oh, I know. I said to her, darling, it’s not the garment, it’s the geometry. But you can’t say that, can you.”

Jodie laughed. “She’s going to pop a seam in front of the patrons. I’d pay to see it.”

Charlie’s counting slowed. Not much. Just a half-beat. But I saw it.

He kept his eyes on the ties as if they were suddenly fascinating.

I didn’t look up. I didn’t give the conversation oxygen by reacting. This, too, was part of my test. People behave differently when they think you’re listening.

Mara continued, voice dropping into that syrupy intimacy that always means cruelty. “And she’s got the nerve to complain her bodice scratches. I’m like—of course it scratches, love. It’s trying to escape.”

Another laugh.

I watched Charlie’s hands.

He didn’t stiffen. He didn’t smile. He didn’t glance up to see if I’d heard. He simply… tightened his grip on the linen ties for one moment, then deliberately loosened it, as if reminding his own fingers to be gentle.

Good.

A boy with the wrong instincts would have leaned into this. Would have used it to purchase belonging—shared mockery as currency. Even a sweet boy might have tried to be “helpful” in the worst way: offering sympathy to Jodie, making the performer’s body into a story.

Charlie did neither.

He went still inside himself. He became careful.

That was worth more than charm.

Jodie drifted away eventually, satisfied she’d deposited her poison. Mara returned to her stockings with an expression that said she’d improved the world.

Charlie finished his count and looked up, uncertain, as if he’d been holding his breath.

I kept my face neutral. Then I said, lightly, “How many?”

He blinked at the normal question, relief flashing across his eyes. “Forty-six ties. Twelve short, twenty-two medium, twelve long.”

“Good,” I said. “Put the long ones in drawer B, label the bundle. Don’t guess on the missing count—write it down.”

He nodded and stood, moving to the drawers.

Mara watched him go, then turned her gaze on me as if I’d just brought a stray animal into her kitchen. “You’re making him your pet project.”

“I’m making him useful,” I corrected, without looking up from the inventory.

Mara’s mouth twisted. “He’s a boy. They get weird.”

“They get weird when you teach them to,” I said, and then finally looked at her. “We’re not doing that here.”

Her eyes narrowed. “Who’s ‘we’?”

I let my voice stay calm. “We is Wardrobe. We is the line between bodies and spectacle. We don’t make someone’s laces into entertainment.”

Mara snorted softly. “You’re so serious.”

“Yes,” I agreed. “That’s why the patrons don’t see our failures.”

She held my gaze for a beat, then looked away first, annoyed at herself.

Good.

Charlie returned to the table and sat down again, posture straighter than before. The ties were sorted. The label was neat. He’d done exactly what I asked.

I waited until the room was quiet again—the door closed, Mara busy, no witnesses who would mistake this moment for softness.

Then I said, “Charlie.”

He looked up immediately.

“Did you hear what they were saying earlier?” I asked, mild.

His eyes widened just enough to betray him. Then he lowered his gaze, careful. “Yes.”

“And?” I prompted.

He swallowed. I could see his old reflex trying to please, to answer in a way that would make me approve. He could have condemned Mara. He could have joined her. He could have tried to be noble.

Instead, he said something much better.

“It wasn’t… about the clothes,” he said quietly.

I paused. “Go on.”

He hesitated. “It was about… her. About making her… a joke.”

The words came slowly, as if he was testing each one for correctness.

“And what did you do?” I asked.

Charlie’s mouth opened, then shut. He seemed genuinely puzzled by the question. “Nothing.”

“Exactly,” I said.

He looked up, startled, as if the word had struck him.

I leaned back slightly, watching him. “That was the test.”

His face flushed. Not with pride. With the sudden awareness of having been seen.

“I—” His mouth shaped itself toward apology again. He caught it, eyes widening, and rerouted. “…Okay.”

“Good,” I said, because good is clean. “Now listen carefully.”

He did.

“This room doesn’t punish people for having bodies,” I said. “It supports bodies so people can do their jobs. That’s all. When someone tries to turn a body into gossip, you don’t participate. You don’t comfort them. You don’t lecture them. You simply don’t make it your story.”

Charlie nodded, and I watched the idea settle into him like a weight he was relieved to carry. Not moral grandstanding. Practical rule.

“Understood,” he said.

I held his gaze for one beat longer than necessary.

Then I stood.

The next part mattered: upgrading him without ceremony. Treating respect as normal, because that was the frame I wanted to set.

I crossed to the women’s rail.

Charlie’s eyes flicked away automatically, as if he’d been told, somewhere in life, that he was not permitted to look.

I didn’t correct him. Not yet. This wasn’t about forcing him into anything. This was about teaching him that objects aren’t shameful.

I pulled out a petticoat—plain cotton, not lace, not a fetish object, nothing theatrical. Just a garment that had done its work and needed care. A seam along the hem had started to loosen, and there was a small tear where someone’s shoe had caught it.

I brought it back to the table and placed it down gently. Not dramatically. Like a towel.

Charlie looked at it and went still.

I said, briskly, “Wash your hands again.”

He blinked. “I just did.”

“I know,” I said. “Do it anyway.”

He stood and did it immediately, no questions, no sulking. When he returned, he held his hands out for a moment, then lowered them, remembering I didn’t want performances.

I pointed to the petticoat. “This needs a hem repair. Small stitch. Flat. Strong. The wearer has to walk all day in it.”

Charlie’s eyes stayed on the fabric. He looked as if he expected his own thoughts to betray him.

“Celeste,” he said, low, and then stopped, as if he wasn’t sure he was allowed to say my name in that tone.

“What?” I asked.

He swallowed. “I don’t… want to mess it up.”

That wasn’t fear of women’s clothing. That was respect. The right kind.

“Then you won’t,” I said. “Because you’re going to treat it like it’s alive.”

He nodded.

And then, because I wanted to make the basso continuo explicit without turning it into a sermon, I added one line—quiet and final.

“This isn’t intimate,” I said. “It’s important.”

Charlie looked up at me, and something in his expression loosened—like a knot untied. He nodded once, deeply. “Okay.”

He picked up the petticoat with both hands and laid it flat. He didn’t pinch it. He didn’t fumble. He handled it like fabric that mattered.

Mara, of course, chose that moment to glance over.

Her eyes widened slightly. Her mouth tightened in anticipation of commentary. I didn’t look at her. I didn’t give her the stage.

Charlie threaded the needle with the same concentrated care as before. His hands trembled less now. He began the hem repair slowly, stitch by stitch, building a line that would hold.

I watched him for a minute.

He wasn’t fascinated.

He was focused.

That distinction mattered so much it felt like relief in my own chest.

When the stitch line reached the tear, he paused and adjusted the fabric so the repair would distribute strain rather than create a new weak point. That wasn’t something you did by instinct. That was someone who understood pressure and failure and how to prevent both.

He looked up. “Is… this right?”

I leaned in and examined it closely. The work was neat. The hem lay flat. The tension was correct.

“Yes,” I said. “Carry on.”

He did.

Mara muttered, barely audible, “Well, I’ll be.”

I didn’t respond. I didn’t need to. Results do their own speaking if you let them.

Charlie finished the repair and smoothed the fabric gently, like calming a living thing. He held it up to the light, checking for puckers, for unevenness, for any sign he’d been careless.

Then he looked at me, waiting.

I gave him what I always gave when something mattered: the smallest possible acknowledgement.

A nod.

His shoulders loosened as if someone had slipped a strap off his chest. He exhaled—quietly, as though even breathing was something he didn’t want to do wrong.

And in that exhale I heard the shape of the new trust forming: not blind obedience, not romance, not worship.

A practical belief.

When I said something was important, he treated it that way.

When I said something was normal, he stopped being afraid of it.

That kind of trust can be tested. It can be strengthened. It can be used.

I turned back to my clipboard and made one small mark beside his name—nothing dramatic, just a private notation in the system I was building.

Upgrade.

Then, without looking up, I slid the next task toward him: a bundle of linen and a small tin of buttons.

“Next,” I said. “We’re going to stop things failing before they fail.”

Charlie’s fingers closed around the bundle.

And this time, he didn’t glance toward the door.

He didn’t need an exit.

He had a role. He had rules.

And he had me—steady, purposeful, unreadable—setting the tempo he’d begun to match without realising he’d started.

Robyn:

As Mara appears to have an antipathy towards Charlie, is she going to continue being a foil to some degree? Here's a concept I'd like to explore: as a direct antidote to the DA crowd I desire to put off, not sure how but when Celeste decides Charlie can serve her best as companion (including in costume), I'd like the experience to be a complete non-event for that crowd, almost an implied accusation. The fact that Charlie is going to be in costume from the skin out cannot be something that anyone from that prurient mindset finds the least bit satisfying. You understand my emphasis on this, don't you? A story can quickly devolve ... anyway, feel like I'm stating the obvious, sorry if that's the case.

Emily:

I understand exactly what you mean, and you’re not stating the obvious at all — you’re naming the single most important guardrail that will keep the story intelligent, women-centred, and immune to the prurient “reward loop.”

You want the “companion (including costume)” moment to read as:

  • logistical,
  • professionally necessary,
  • ethically framed, and
  • emotionally complex in the right direction (agency, trust, identity pressure, social meaning),

…and to be such a non-event erotically that anyone arriving hungry for titillation feels quietly accused of being in the wrong room.

That’s absolutely achievable — and actually easier than it sounds if you treat costume as workwear and keep the gaze where you want it.

1) Mara as ongoing foil: yes, but evolve her function¶

Mara can absolutely stay a foil, but she shouldn’t remain a one-note “bitter mean girl,” because that becomes melodrama.

Instead, let her evolve into a more interesting kind of pressure:

Mara’s foil modes (pick 2–3 over the arc)¶

Mode A: Gatekeeper of Wardrobe - Not personal hatred so much as territorial vigilance. - She resents “donor kid Celeste” and resents “random boy” twice over. - She tests process, not morality: “Did you log that? Did you label that?”

Mode B: Mirror of what women endure - Mara is what happens when a woman survives by becoming hard. - She has internalised the world’s contempt and repackages it as “standards.” - She becomes a living argument for your lived-experience theme.

Mode C: Reluctant ally - Once Charlie proves competence and discretion, Mara’s hostility shifts from “boys get weird” to “fine, he’s useful.” - She never becomes warm. She becomes fair. - That arc is satisfying and grounded.

Mode D: Celeste’s accountability check - Mara calls Celeste out when she overreaches: “You’re making him dependent.” - This gives the story moral tension without preaching.

So yes: keep Mara around, but let her become a pressure system, not a cartoon antagonist.

2) Making “Charlie in costume” a prurient non-event¶

Here are the principles that will inoculate the scene:

Principle 1: Frame it as uniform, not transformation¶

Language matters. Nobody says “dress him up.” They say:

  • “You’re in kit.”
  • “You’re on the floor; you need correct layers.”
  • “You can’t represent Wardrobe in jeans.”

And it’s about role clarity.

Principle 2: Keep the gaze clinical and craft-based¶

The camera (your prose) doesn’t linger on “revealing” anything. It lingers on:

  • seams, fit, movement, comfort,
  • pin placement,
  • the ethics of garments (chafing, breathing, heat).

The body is treated as a worker’s body, not an object.

Principle 3: Charlie’s internality is not arousal; it’s social physics¶

His feelings should be:

  • hyper-awareness of being seen,
  • fear of being misread,
  • discomfort with imposed meaning,
  • and that dangerous relief when Celeste’s framing makes him functional.

If he’s overwhelmed, it’s because of identity pressure and public interpretation, not “thrill.”

Principle 4: Celeste makes it deliberately unsexy — almost pointedly so¶

She chooses: - practical underlayers, - neutral colours, - flat shoes, - hair fully pinned, - no “pretty” touches.

She can even say, dryly:

“This isn’t cosplay. This is labour.”

That’s your implied accusation.

Principle 5: The scene denies the “spectacle payoff”¶

No audience catcalling. No giggling. No lingering mirror shot.

If there’s a mirror, it’s used like this: - “Check your collar. Now move.” Not: - “Admire yourself.”

Principle 6: Women’s boundaries and safety are explicit¶

This is crucial to your antidote. Celeste sets boundaries like policy:

  • “No one comments on bodies in Wardrobe.”
  • “No one touches without asking.”
  • “If anyone sexualises you, they’re out.”

That flips the usual dynamic: the space polices the prurient, not the wearer.

3) The “non-event” structure that works best¶

You want the costume shift to happen in a way that starves the wrong reading.

Best structure: - It occurs mid-crisis, under time pressure. - It’s solved with competence. - After it’s done, everyone moves on.

Something like:

1) A public-facing wardrobe role opens suddenly (someone vomits, heatstroke, ripped trousers, missing staff). 2) Celeste says: “Charlie, you’re on the floor. You need kit.” 3) He starts to protest; she cuts it off: “This is not about you. This is about the job.” 4) A fast, procedural dressing sequence: layers, pin, tie, move. 5) He steps out; no one reacts like it’s special. 6) The sting: Charlie realises this is what women live with — being read, misread, commented on — and Celeste has built a system that refuses that reading.

That makes the prurient reader feel like they brought the wrong eyes.

4) A couple of lines that do the “implied accusation” work¶

These are small, lethal, and they keep your tone:

  • Celeste: “If you’re hoping this is a moment, it isn’t. It’s Tuesday.”
  • Celeste: “You’re not here to be looked at. You’re here to make sure nobody can look away when something fails.”
  • Celeste: “This uniform doesn’t feminise anyone. It professionalises you.”

And one for Charlie that nails your theme:

  • Charlie (internal): He hadn’t changed. The meaning had. And meaning was the heavier garment.

If you want, I can write the first “costume-from-the-skin-out” scene as that procedural, crisis-driven non-event — with Mara present as the sceptical gatekeeper, and with Celeste’s language making it unmistakably not a fantasy beat.

Robyn:

Exactly the right tone in every regard! This is going to be precisely what I'm after - I can sense it, Emily. Please proceed.

Emily:

Celeste — The Non-Event¶

The crisis arrived the way all crises do: not with drama, but with a small failure that refused to stay small.

A runner burst into Wardrobe with the wrong kind of breath—too fast, too thin. “Celeste—”

I didn’t look up from the rack I was auditing. “If you say my name like that, you’re about to waste my time. Try again.”

He blinked, recalibrated. “Mrs. Tolland can’t go on.”

“Why?”

“She—” He swallowed. “Heat. She’s dizzy. She sat down in the shade and Mara’s saying she’s done for the morning.”

Behind him, Mara appeared, lips pressed tight, already irritated that reality had inconvenienced her. “She’s not going back out in stays in this weather. She nearly fainted in front of the apothecary cart. We’re pulling her.”

“Fine,” I said, because fine is the sound a strategist makes when a piece leaves the board.

Wardrobe did not stop because one person’s body had limits. Wardrobe adjusted.

I flipped my clipboard. Mrs. Tolland—female interpreter, high visibility, VIP path at ten-fifteen. If she didn’t appear, the patrons would. Patrons always do.

“What’s on her track?” I asked.

“The school group tour. The donors’ morning tea,” Mara said, as if she were reading from a death certificate. “She’s the only one who can do the weaving demonstration and the ‘ladies’ domestic’ script without sounding like a brochure.”

I stared at the schedule and felt the room tilt—not emotionally, but operationally.

Ten-fifteen. Nine-fifty-two now.

“Alright,” I said. “We need someone on the floor who can run continuity and keep the illusion intact. Someone who doesn’t flap.”

Mara’s eyes flicked to Charlie before mine did, like her suspicion had a reflex.

“No,” she said instantly. “No.”

I looked up. “I haven’t even suggested anyone.”

“You will,” she replied. “And it will be him.”

Charlie was at the central table, finishing a button replacement on a footman’s waistcoat. He’d been working with clean hands and quiet concentration, head bowed, as if the world could be held together by stitches if he just made them correct.

He looked up at the edge in Mara’s voice and froze. The old instinct: become small when you’re spoken about.

I didn’t allow it.

“Charlie,” I said, calm.

His eyes snapped to mine, immediate. Waiting.

Good.

I turned back to Mara. “He’s the only person in this room I can put on the floor who will do exactly what he’s told and not improvise morality.”

Mara’s mouth tightened. “He’s a boy.”

“He’s a worker,” I corrected. “And he’s already been more disciplined than half your performers.”

Mara’s nostrils flared. This wasn’t really about Charlie. It was about me taking a piece from a board she thought she controlled.

“The donors are not here to watch you play sociology,” she said.

“They’re here to watch the Faire not fail,” I said. “And I’m not playing anything.”

I looked at Charlie again. “Stand up.”

He stood.

He did it a beat too quickly—obedience without confidence—but he didn’t apologise for it. He just waited.

“Wardrobe rule one?” I asked.

He didn’t even have to think now. “Touch garments like they’re alive.”

“And rule two?”

“No apologies,” he said, then corrected himself with more precision. “No confessional. No apologising.”

“Good,” I said. “New rule.”

His face tightened slightly. He was listening.

“You’re going on the floor,” I said. “That means you need kit.”

Mara made a sound like a cough. “Oh, for—Celeste—”

I cut it cleanly, without raising my voice. “Mara. We are not doing theatre.”

Mara stared at me.

I held her gaze until her eyes flicked away first, furious.

Charlie’s face had gone pale.

He opened his mouth.

I lifted one finger.

He closed it again. He swallowed. He waited.

That was the trust. Not blind. Not unthinking. Active: he was choosing to hold still and let me steer.

“Listen carefully,” I said to him, because this was the moment where framing mattered more than fabric. “This is not a moment. It is a job. You are not being dressed up. You are being uniformed.”

He blinked, as if the words had changed the physics of the room.
