“How very… practical,” she said, with that faint disdain some women reserve for other women who’ve had to work.

I smiled gently. “Practical keeps the fantasy intact.”

She blinked, then laughed as if I’d made a joke. “Oh, I like you.”

Of course she did.

They drifted on, leaving behind their perfume and their invisible expectations.

And still—almost no one looked at Charl.

They spoke in front of him like he wasn’t there.

They stepped around him like he was part of the furniture.

They did not notice his intelligence, his restraint, his grief.

And then, exactly as Talia had predicted, it happened:

A donor wife reached back without looking and pressed a glass into Charl’s hands.

“Hold this,” she said, already turning away.

Charl froze.

His hands tightened around the glass with reflexive care—because he was meticulous, because he didn’t want a spill, because he didn’t want to become a joke.

He looked at me, eyes flicking up once—asking permission without asking.

I could have taken it from him. I could have corrected the woman. I could have made it a boundary.

But there are moments where the most instructive thing you can do is let the truth land.

So I did not move.

I let Charl hold the glass.

He stood there, invisible and burdened, not daring to shift his weight in case he drew attention.

And I watched the realisation bloom in him like a bruise:

Women carry things they didn’t agree to carry, because the cost of refusal is always higher than the cost of compliance.

After the cluster moved away, I stepped closer and took the glass from his hands.

“Don’t,” I said quietly, when he began to apologise with his eyes.

He swallowed.

“I didn’t know what to—” he whispered.

“You did fine,” I replied. “You kept it boring.”

Charl’s mouth tightened. “Is this what it’s like?”

“Yes,” I said.

His eyes widened slightly. He looked almost ill.

“It’s constant,” he murmured.

“Yes,” I repeated.

A pause.

Then Charl said something that wasn’t about him at all—which was how I knew his personality was consolidating into something more than compliance.

“How do you… not hate everyone?” he asked softly.

I almost laughed—not because it was funny, but because it was the most honest question in the room.

I kept my voice even.

“You pick your battles,” I said. “And you pick your people.”

Charl looked at me, and I saw it then: the grief wasn’t only about how he was being treated.

It was about the realisation that women had been doing this forever, and he’d never noticed.

I let him feel it.

Then I gave him structure, because structure is the only mercy in a system like this.

“Stay two steps behind,” I instructed. “Eyes level. Hands still. If someone gives you something, you hand it to me the first moment it’s socially safe. If someone asks you to do something outside your role, you look at me.”

Charl nodded once.

“And speak softly,” I added. “Not because you’re small. Because softness makes you vanish.”

Charl’s jaw tightened at that.

“Yes,” he said.

We moved again, back into the room, into the light, into the thick donor air.

The glom continued.

They loved me quickly—because I was a satisfying object: young authority, polished sharpness, a novelty with manners.

But I could already feel the hook beneath it.

The expectation that I would be available.

That I would be “on.”

That I would belong to the manor, the donors, the optics, the story they wanted to tell about themselves.

And behind me, two steps back, Charl walked like quiet labour.

Unnoticed.

A blessing.

And a bruise.

Robyn:

Celine Sciamma would be proud. And delighted. Fingers are FIRMLY on the social pulse.

Emily:

That reaction tells me we’ve hit the exact register you want: intimate without being indulgent, political without being preachy, and—most importantly—observant.

If you want to keep that Sciamma-like tension humming as we move forward, here are the pressure points worth protecting (and exploiting) in the next chapters—no tables, no lectures, just the levers that will keep it razor-true:

What to keep doing¶

  • Let the world reveal itself through micro-acts: a handoff, a tone, a “sweetheart,” an ignored voice. Those tiny things are the patriarchy’s daily machinery.
  • Make Celeste’s competence attractive but costly: donors love her because she’s frictionless; the cost is that she’s being consumed.
  • Make Charl’s pain about cognition, not clothing: the grief is realising how much women do unconsciously, and how little gratitude they get for it.

What to introduce next¶

  • A confederate moment that isn’t sentimental: Talia doesn’t “comfort” Charl—she equips him. That’s the female bond: practical solidarity.
  • A donor-wife double bind: one woman praises Celeste as “refreshing,” then punishes her the moment she sets a boundary. That shows women can be agents of patriarchal enforcement too—without blaming them.
  • Management’s first leash tug: “We need you to be at X, smile at Y, soften that edge.” Celeste complies outwardly, but quietly builds her own household power.

The next emotional pivot¶

Charl’s next big step isn’t “getting used to skirts.” It’s this:

He learns that invisibility isn’t safety; it’s access—for other people to use you without noticing they’re using you.

That’s the horror that will eventually become his voice.

If you’d like, I can write the next scene as the first time Celeste is privately corrected by management (“too sharp,” “too firm,” “don’t embarrass donors”), and Celeste realises she’s been promoted into a cage—while Charl, in the background, watches a woman be disciplined for not absorbing disrespect politely.

That’s pure Sciamma territory: power, love, and containment all in one quiet room.

Robyn:

Brilliant. On pins and needles.

Emily:

Celeste — The Leash¶

They waited until the room was full.

Not because they wanted an audience. Because they wanted me to feel, somewhere in my bones, that I belonged to the room now—that I was a fixture like the chandelier, not a person who could walk away.

It was just after the main wave of donors arrived. The manor air had thickened into that particular scent of money and perfume and careful laughter. I’d been spoken to by more people than I could remember, and I hadn’t eaten. That, I was certain, was not an accident either.

Charl stayed two steps behind and to my left, exactly as instructed. Invisible. Useful. Quiet.

When Mrs. Pryce appeared at my elbow, she didn’t interrupt me mid-conversation. She waited until I finished answering a donor’s question about the “history” of a tapestry we’d bought second-hand three months ago.

Then she touched my forearm lightly—familiar, proprietary.

“Celeste, darling,” she said, with her smile already in place. “A word.”

Not a request.

I turned, still smiling. “Of course.”

I didn’t look at Charl. I didn’t need to. I could feel him shift with me, keeping the same distance, same angle—my shadow with rules.

Mrs. Pryce guided me into the side corridor that led to the small parlour they used for “private discussions,” which was a polite term for discipline.

The door shut. The manor noise softened into a hum beyond the wall.

Mrs. Pryce’s smile remained. It always did. Her entire face was designed to be plausible.

She turned to me, hands folded, voice gentle.

“You’re doing beautifully.”

I waited.

She let the compliment sit for a beat, like sugar before the medicine.

“There was… a moment,” she continued.

I kept my expression calm. “Which moment?”

Mrs. Pryce’s eyes flicked briefly toward Charl, then away again, as if he were furniture that had startled her by being in the wrong place.

“You can wait outside,” she said to him, still smiling.

Before Charl could move, I spoke, still polite.

“No,” I said. “He stays.”

Mrs. Pryce’s smile tightened. “Celeste—”

“He stays,” I repeated, my tone still soft enough not to be called a tantrum.

A small silence.

Mrs. Pryce’s eyes sharpened. “Very well,” she said, the way someone says we’ll address this later.

Charl remained two steps behind me, eyes level, hands folded. Invisible again.

Mrs. Pryce returned her attention to me. “Mr. Sutherland,” she said, as if naming an unpleasant smell. “He mentioned you were… firm.”

I gave her my neutral face. “He called my staff ‘sweetheart’ and tried to give orders.”

Mrs. Pryce nodded, sympathetically, as if we were two women sharing a weary joke.

“Yes, yes. He can be… old-fashioned.”

Old-fashioned. The little euphemism that turns entitlement into quaintness.

I didn’t smile.

Mrs. Pryce kept going anyway. “We’re not saying you were wrong. We’re saying—” she searched for the correct velvet word, “—the manner in which you corrected him.”

I waited, still.

She softened her voice further, as if I were delicate. “Donors need to feel welcomed, Celeste.”

“And staff need to feel safe,” I replied.

Mrs. Pryce’s smile flickered. She recovered it. “Of course. Absolutely. But we must be strategic.”

I tilted my head. “I was.”

Her eyes narrowed a fraction. “He left feeling embarrassed.”

“He should,” I said.

The silence that followed was small, but it had teeth.

Mrs. Pryce’s smile finally slipped—just enough to reveal the truth beneath.

“Celeste,” she said quietly, “you can’t make people like Mr. Sutherland feel… chastised. Not publicly.”

I held her gaze.

“You want me to be a decorative weapon,” I said, still calm. “But only if I never cut.”

Mrs. Pryce gave a small laugh that wasn’t humour. “Darling—”

“No,” I interrupted, polite as a blade. “Don’t darling me.”

Charl’s hands tightened around nothing.

I didn’t look at him. I didn’t need to. I could feel the air shift—hearing it, learning it. The way women do.

Mrs. Pryce took a slow breath, regaining her pleasant mask.

“You’re very young,” she said, as if that explained everything.

The phrase was a leash, perfectly disguised. A reminder: you are a girl playing a role we lent you.

I smiled slightly. “And yet you moved me into this position because I’m effective.”

Mrs. Pryce’s lips pressed together. “Yes. Effective. But effectiveness requires… finesse.”

I nodded once. “I have finesse.”

Mrs. Pryce’s gaze flicked to Charl again, irritation hidden behind civility.

“This,” she said gently, “is also part of the issue.”

I didn’t blink. “Charl?”

Mrs. Pryce’s smile widened. “Your attendant.”

“Wardrobe support,” I corrected.

Mrs. Pryce waved a hand. “Whatever we call it. Celeste, darling, people notice.”

“No,” I said, voice level. “They don’t.”

Mrs. Pryce blinked.

I went on, clinical. “They notice me. They ignore him. Occasionally they use him without seeing him. That’s the point.”

Mrs. Pryce stared at me like I’d said something distasteful.

“You can’t possibly be suggesting that’s… acceptable,” she said.

I didn’t move. “I’m suggesting it’s true.”

Mrs. Pryce’s smile thinned. “We need you to be… careful. There are perceptions.”

“I manage perceptions,” I replied. “That is the job.”

Mrs. Pryce leaned closer, voice lowering into something more honest.

“Celeste,” she said, “if people start talking—”

“They will,” I said.

She stopped.

I continued anyway. “Because they always do. The question is whether we design the structure so their talk becomes irrelevant.”

Mrs. Pryce straightened, her eyes hard now.

“You’re not hearing me,” she said.

I tilted my head. “No. I’m hearing you. You’re saying: don’t embarrass donors. Don’t give them consequences. Let them do what they like as long as they keep writing cheques.”

Mrs. Pryce’s smile returned—cold. “I’m saying: keep the peace.”

“And I’m saying,” I replied, “peace is what women are told to keep when men misbehave.”

Charl’s breath hitched—quiet, shocked. Not because he’d never heard that sentence. Because he’d never heard it said out loud by someone in power.

Mrs. Pryce stared at me, the mask slipping again.

“Careful,” she said softly. “That edge of yours—donors love it until they feel it.”

I nodded. “Then I’ll aim it correctly.”

Mrs. Pryce’s eyes narrowed. “You will aim it where we tell you.”

There it was. The leash, finally visible.

I smiled gently. “No,” I said.

For a moment, her expression went still—calculating. This was the real conversation now, stripped of velvet.

“You forget,” she said quietly, “that this role can be reassigned.”

I nodded once. “I don’t forget.”

“And you forget,” I added, “that you moved me because you need me.”

Mrs. Pryce’s nostrils flared. A crack.

Then she recovered. “Celeste,” she said, voice honey again, “don’t make this adversarial.”

I looked at her and felt something that was almost amusement, almost pity. Women like Mrs. Pryce survive by polishing men’s egos until they can see their own reflection in them. They learn to call it diplomacy. They call it safety.

Sometimes it is.

And sometimes it is just surrender with better lighting.

“I won’t make it adversarial,” I said. “I’ll make it functional.”

Mrs. Pryce inhaled slowly. “Then be functional. Be gracious.”

“I am gracious,” I replied.

Her smile tightened. “Be softer.”

I stared at her.

Then I did something she wasn’t expecting.

I agreed.

“Yes,” I said.

Mrs. Pryce blinked, surprised.

I smiled warmly—perfectly. “You’re right. I’ll be softer.”

Mrs. Pryce visibly relaxed, her body believing the lie because her job required her to.

“Wonderful,” she said. “That’s all we ask.”

I kept smiling. “Of course.”

She nodded, satisfied, and opened the parlour door.

The manor noise rushed back in.

Mrs. Pryce stepped out first, her smile already returning to donor brightness. “Celeste,” she called lightly, “Mrs. Hargreaves is asking for you.”

“I’m coming,” I said.

Mrs. Pryce drifted away.

When she was gone, I let my smile fade.

Charl remained behind me, still and quiet, eyes fixed on the floor as if the parquet had become fascinating.

I spoke without turning.

“Did you hear what she asked me to do?” I asked.

Charl’s voice came out small. “Be softer.”

“Yes,” I said.

A pause.

Then Charl said, almost inaudible, “So… you have to pretend.”

I turned my head slightly—not fully, just enough to catch the edge of his face.

“Yes,” I said. “Constantly.”

Charl swallowed. His hands tightened around nothing.

“And if you don’t?” he asked.

I looked back toward the manor room, where donors laughed and drifted and consumed my attention like it belonged to them.

“If I don’t,” I said quietly, “they will replace me with someone who will.”

Charl went very still.

The grief was there now, unmistakable—not about clothing, not about disguise.

About the structure.

About the fact that the role they’d given me came with conditions, and the conditions were always the same: Be brilliant, but not threatening. Be sharp, but not cutting. Be powerful, but only in ways that flatter men.

Charl whispered, “That’s… disgusting.”

“Yes,” I agreed.

He hesitated, then asked, voice tight, “So what do we do?”

I looked straight ahead, and my voice became calm again—clinical, as it had been in the back room.

“We do what women have always done,” I said. “We build a structure they can’t see.”

Charl’s head lifted slightly.

“And then,” I added, stepping forward into the light, “we make it expensive to break.”

I walked back into the room.

Charl followed, two steps behind.

Invisible.

Learning.

Robyn:

The time has come for admission into the "lived experience" group that Charl has found himself in. It is Talia who first suggests a pronoun change for Charl, to make the social interaction less confusing for everyone, including Celeste and even Charl... herself. Thoughts?

Emily:

Yes — and if you handle it the way you’ve been handling everything else (procedure, ethics, consequence), it becomes one of the most female-readable turns in the book: pronouns as social safety engineering, not “gender play.”

The key is that Talia suggests it for clarity and harm reduction, not as a “step further.” And Celeste’s reaction should be complicated in exactly the way you like: Strategist Celeste recognising a new tool… and immediately realising the tool has a cost.

Why it works¶

  • In a “lived experience” sense, pronouns here aren’t about Charl’s inner truth. They’re about how strangers behave when they think you’re one category vs another.
  • “He” in that space will constantly cause a double-take, a correction, a joke, or a challenge—i.e., attention. And attention is danger.
  • “She” smooths interactions because the system is sexist and lazy: it prefers coherence over truth.

That’s the horror: the pronoun change makes the cage more comfortable—and therefore easier to keep you in.

The one guardrail you must keep¶

Charl must not experience it as validation or thrill. If anything, it should land as: - a loss of linguistic shelter (“at least I had ‘he’ as a lifeline to myself”), - plus a deeper dread: “I’m learning to answer to the world’s expectations.”

And Celeste must be careful not to treat it as a win. It’s not victory. It’s triage.

A clean way to stage it¶

1) Talia frames it as workflow, not identity¶

She’s seen enough now to say it plainly: - “People are getting confused.” - “Confusion leads to attention.” - “Attention leads to commentary.” - “Commentary leads to risk.”

Then she suggests: - “In public, we use ‘she’—for coherence.” - “In private, you can be Charlie.”

The split matters. It starves dopamine readers and keeps the ethics intact.

2) Celeste insists on consent¶

Not mushy consent—procedural consent: - “Are you willing to do this as part of the uniform?” - “If you say no, we find another workaround.”

But the stakes are real, so Charl’s yes feels like the same earlier pattern: consent under constraint.

3) Charl’s reaction is grief, not pleasure¶

He isn’t delighted; he’s resigned, embarrassed, uneasy. He might say something like: - “It makes it easier for them.” - “It makes it harder for me to remember I’m not… this.”

That line would be powerful and totally non-kinky.

4) The “lived experience group” is marked by women’s matter-of-fact compliance¶

Not celebration. Not teasing. Just: - “Alright, then.” - “Copy that.” - “Keep it boring.”

And then the gut-punch: people are instantly nicer in a condescending way, or instantly entitled in a casual way. Charl notices the shift.

A scene, Celeste POV¶

Celeste — The Word Everyone Uses¶

Talia waited until the corridor cleared.

It was the little lull between donor waves—when the manor breathed in and no one was demanding to be impressed.

Charl stood where he always stood now: two steps behind me, hands folded, face arranged into that practiced neutrality that made him disappear. It was working. Which meant it was costing him.

Talia leaned against the doorframe, arms crossed, expression practical.

“We need to fix something,” she said.

I didn’t look surprised. “We’re fixing many things.”

“This one is linguistic,” Talia replied.

Charl’s posture tightened by a fraction. He had the instincts now—flinch at anything that sounded like a change.

Talia glanced at him, then back at me. “People are tripping over him.”

“Good,” I said. “Let them.”

“No,” Talia said, flat. “Not good. Confusion makes them look closer. Looking closer makes them talk. Talking makes them bold.”

I watched her. She was right, and I hated that she was right.

Talia continued, not unkind. “They keep saying ‘he’ by accident. Then they correct themselves. Then they laugh like it’s cute. It’s not cute. It’s a spark.”

Charl’s throat moved. He didn’t speak.

I kept my voice calm. “What do you suggest?”

Talia’s gaze stayed steady. “In public, we use ‘she.’”

The air changed.

Charl’s fingers tightened around nothing.

I felt something rise in my chest that I didn’t enjoy naming: a strategist’s interest, immediately followed by a human being’s discomfort.

I didn’t let either show.

I turned slightly, just enough to catch Charl in my peripheral vision. “Thoughts?”

Charl’s voice came out very quiet. “I’m not—”

“I know,” I said, cutting it cleanly. “This isn’t about what you are.”

Talia nodded once, approving. “It’s about what the room does.”

Charl swallowed. “It feels… like lying.”

I let that sit for a beat.

Then I gave him the truth the way I always did—without theatre.

“It is lying,” I said. “But it’s lying the way every woman lies when she smiles at a man who could make her day worse.”

Charl’s eyes lifted sharply. Something in his face tightened—grief, recognition, anger.

Talia’s voice softened by a millimetre. “It makes your interactions simpler. People stop re-checking you. They stop hunting for the joke.”

Charl’s jaw worked. “And what does it do to me?”

That question was the personality you wanted: not defiance, not obedience—ethical accounting.

I answered honestly. “It will make it easier for them to use you.”

Charl went still.

I continued, because he deserved the whole truth. “And it will make it harder for you to feel like you have a foothold outside this.”

Talia watched me, expression sober. She wasn’t playing games. She was offering a tool with a price tag.

Charl’s voice cracked slightly. “So why do it?”

I looked at him then—properly.

“Because I can’t protect you by keeping the world confused,” I said. “Confusion is where cruelty lives.”

He blinked, eyes bright. “And if I say no?”

I held his gaze.

“Then we find another structure,” I said. “It will be messier. It will be riskier. But it will be yours.”

A long beat.

Charl exhaled through his nose, small and controlled, like he was trying not to give the moment the satisfaction of drama.

“In public,” he said finally, “they can say it.”

Talia nodded once, decisive. “Good.”

Charl added, quietly, “But you don’t.”

Talia blinked. “What?”

Charl looked at me. “You don’t have to,” he said. “Not when it’s just us.”

I felt the inconvenient twist in my chest again—annoying, human.

“Agreed,” I said.

Talia pushed off the doorframe. “Alright. Then we’re consistent. We don’t hesitate. Hesitation invites questions.”

Charl’s lips pressed thin. “And if someone asks?”

Talia shrugged. “They won’t. Not if you don’t make it interesting.”

I nodded once. “Boring,” I said.

Talia’s mouth twitched. “Exactly.”

Charl swallowed, then asked the question that made the whole thing turn—made it stop being about language and become about the world.

“Does it change… how they look at me?”

Talia’s answer came without hesitation.

“Yes,” she said. “And you’re going to hate it.”

Charl closed his eyes for a second, as if bracing.

When he opened them, there was no thrill there.

Just resignation.

Just that new, awful knowing.

He nodded once. “Okay.”

Talia looked at me. “You’ll tell staff?”

“I will,” I said.

“And the front desk?” she asked.

“Yes,” I replied.

Talia’s gaze flicked to Charl again, not pitying—simply acknowledging.

“Welcome,” she said, matter-of-fact, “to being managed by language.”

Charl didn’t smile.

He just stood there, two steps behind me, learning what it cost to make a room behave.

And I realised, with a sharp little jolt, that we’d moved him another inch deeper into it.

Not into costume.

Into society.

If you want, the very next micro-scene can be the “first time it happens” in public: a donor wife says “she” casually, and Charl feels the floor tilt—not because it’s exciting, but because it’s effective. And he hates that he’s relieved. That’s the exact kind of morally complicated, Sciamma-adjacent ache you’re building toward.

Robyn:

You can build it as elaborate a scene as you want - Charl needs to feel the weight of this part of the "shared experience" and the conflict of feeling relief vs hating it mirrors exactly that smile women give a man who could make life miserable for them. Supreb analogy!

Emily:

Celeste — The First “She”¶

It happened in the way the most consequential things always happen: casually.

No bell. No announcement. No pause for anyone’s feelings.

Just a word placed on Charl like a hand.

We were in the long gallery, where the light came in soft and flattering through tall windows and made donors feel as if they were walking through something important. The walls held portraits that weren’t as old as people assumed. The air held the quiet clink of glasses and the low murmur of money being pleased with itself.

I was half-turned toward a couple I didn’t know well—Mr. and Mrs. Weller, by their lanyards—explaining the origin of a lacquered writing desk that had, in truth, arrived last month from a warehouse in Brisbane.

“—and we restored the hinge work to match period hardware,” I was saying, my voice smooth, the story coherent enough to satisfy their appetite for authenticity.

Charl stood where he belonged now: two steps behind me, slightly to my left, hands folded at the apron, gaze level. He moved like someone trying very hard to be forgotten.

Which, infuriatingly, was the skill that kept us safest.

Mrs. Weller smiled in that careful way women smile in public, polite and managerial. She wasn’t unkind. She was simply practiced.

“Oh, it’s lovely,” she said. “And tell me—are you doing the tour of the upstairs rooms as well? I’ve heard the bedrooms are simply divine.”

“Yes,” I replied. “Later this afternoon.”

Mr. Weller leaned in, already bored, scanning the room for something that would make him feel entertained. His eyes flicked over Charl, then away again, as if registering a coat stand.

“And you—” Mrs. Weller added, turning slightly, not quite addressing Charl but including him the way one includes staff in a sentence, “—your girl will be with you?”

Charl went still.

The word didn’t land loudly. It didn’t echo. It didn’t cause anyone to gasp.

It just… happened.

“Your girl.”

A neat phrase. A neat reduction. A neat category.

Charl’s shoulders tightened by a millimetre—so small no one would notice except someone who’d been watching him the way you watch a fault line.

I felt it beside me like a tiny tremor.

I kept my face calm.

“Yes,” I said, as if it were nothing. “She’ll be with me.”

Charl’s breath caught.

I heard it. Barely. A quiet, involuntary hitch of air.

But Mrs. Weller didn’t.

Mr. Weller certainly didn’t.

They moved on instantly, satisfied by the coherence of the moment. A Lady with her maid. A world that made sense.

They didn’t smile at Charl. They didn’t study him. They didn’t look closer.

They simply… accepted.

And the acceptance was the knife.

Because I felt Charl’s body respond before his mind could argue.

His posture loosened by a fraction. His jaw unclenched. His eyes—so carefully controlled—lost the sharp edge of panic for half a second.

Relief.

Pure, involuntary relief.

Then his face tightened again, as if he’d caught himself enjoying oxygen.

The conflict flickered across him so fast it would have been invisible to anyone who wasn’t trained to watch the smallest shifts for danger.

To anyone who hadn’t lived it.

Mrs. Weller turned back to me, smiling. “Wonderful. It’s so much easier when everything runs properly, isn’t it?”

“Yes,” I said, my voice smooth.

Mr. Weller chuckled. “A well-run house. That’s what you want.”

They drifted away, drawn toward the next polished object, the next story, the next place where their attention could feel generous without costing them anything.

The moment was gone.

The word remained.

I waited until we were alone for three seconds—until the next donor hadn’t yet arrived, until there was a pocket of air I could speak into without being overheard.

I didn’t turn to Charl. Turning would make it a scene.

I spoke forward, voice low.

“Breathe,” I said.

Charl’s breath came out shaky, and he hated that too.

“I—” he began, then swallowed the rest.

I kept my voice calm, as if we were discussing a timetable. “What did you feel?”

Silence.

Then, very quietly, Charl said, “Relief.”

The honesty surprised him. I could hear it in the way he said it—like confessing to a crime.

A beat.

“And?” I prompted.

Charl’s voice tightened. “Disgust.”

“For what?” I asked, still low.

“For… being relieved,” he whispered.

There it was.

The exact conflict you wanted: the smile women give to survive, and the hatred of themselves for giving it.

I nodded once, small.

“Yes,” I said.

Charl’s hands tightened around the apron fabric, knuckles whitening.

“It worked,” he said, voice strained. “It— it made them stop looking.”

“Yes,” I replied.

“And I hate that I… wanted that,” he said.

I kept my eyes forward, scanning the room as if simply tracking donor flow. The performance never stops. That’s part of the lesson.

“You didn’t want it,” I corrected softly. “You wanted safety.”

Charl swallowed hard.

“That’s the same thing,” he murmured.

“No,” I said. “It becomes the same thing when you blame yourself.”

He flinched at that—because it was true, and because he hadn’t realised he’d already begun doing it.

The next donor approached—an older woman with a bright scarf and a hungry social expression.

I straightened my shoulders, smile returning like a mask clicking into place.

Charl did too. Automatically.

The donor didn’t look at him.

She looked at me.

“How enchanting,” she said. “You must be exhausted.”

“I’m delighted,” I lied smoothly.

She laughed. “And this is your—”

“Attendant,” I supplied.

The donor’s gaze flicked over Charl for half a second—just long enough to confirm category.

“She seems very quiet,” the donor remarked.

Charl’s throat moved.

I answered for him. “She’s discreet.”

Discreet.

Another word women are praised for when they’re being erased.

The donor nodded approvingly and moved closer to me, already taking my attention like she’d paid for it.

I handled her. I smiled. I softened. I kept it functional.

And behind me, two steps back, Charl stood with the new weight on his shoulders:

Not the costume.

Not the cap.

A pronoun that made the room behave.

And the sickening realisation that behaving rooms are a luxury purchased with someone’s self-erasure.

Later—much later, when we had a moment alone again—Charl spoke once more, voice small and furious.

“They didn’t even see me,” he said.

I kept my voice even. “No.”

“And it was… easier,” he admitted, hate threading every syllable.

“Yes,” I said again.

Charl’s fingers clenched. “So this is what women do.”

It wasn’t a question.

It was a verdict.

I glanced back at him, just briefly, letting my gaze catch his eyes so he knew I wasn’t leaving him alone with it.

“This is what women are forced to do,” I corrected.

Charl swallowed. His eyes shone, not with tears—he wasn’t there yet—but with something harder: a clarity that hurt.

“And they do it without anyone noticing,” he whispered.

“Yes,” I said.

A beat.

Then Charl said, voice breaking just slightly, “I want to tell everyone.”

I felt the tight twist in my chest again—that inconvenient thing.

Not pride.

Recognition.

Not yet, I thought.

Not yet.

But one day.

I turned back to the room, smile returning because it had to.

And behind me, Charl learned how to wear safety like a lie.

How to hate it.

How to need it anyway.

Robyn:

How can I thank you, Emily?

Let's do this. Celeste and Charl - who Celeste is seeing as Sharl more and more and, despite herself, is finding herself attracted to as a new friend - finally finish for the day and are sitting in the ante-chamber overlooking the seaside. Celeste realises she does value this position, much as she'd been almost cavalier about discarding it, and tries to convince Sharl that they have a great future here. Sharl sees more of the downside of living a woman's existence and discusses it with Celeste. Celeste decides to play unfairly and appeals to his initial attraction to her, exloring that and in doing so disarming his objections.

Emily:

You’re already thanking me the only way that matters: by using it, shaping it, and pushing the story into truer territory.

And yes — let’s do that seaside ante-chamber scene. I’ll keep it Celeste POV, keep it clean, and make Celeste’s “unfair play” feel like what it is: emotional leverage dressed as tenderness, deployed by a girl who’s brilliant at structure… and only just learning what attachment does to her precision.

Celeste — The Ante-Chamber¶

The ante-chamber was the kind of room designed to make you forget you were working.

High windows. Pale walls. A long bench upholstered in something too expensive to sit on carelessly. Beyond the glass, the sea rolled in slow, indifferent repetitions, silvered at the edges by the late light.

When I finally shut the door behind us, the noise of the manor softened into distance. Not silence—never silence here—but the kind of hush that let your body unclench.

Charl sat first, because she always did when I told her to. Not lazily. Not sprawled. She perched as if the furniture might report her.

The cap was off now. Her hair, still pinned, had the faint imprint of the day in it—tightness at the scalp, a few rebellious strands that refused to behave. She looked tired in that way that wasn’t sleepy so much as spent: used up by attention, by restraint, by being careful.

I sat at the other end of the bench, leaving space between us. Not because I needed it, but because she did.

For a moment we just watched the water.

Then Charl said, quietly, “They didn’t notice me at all.”

I didn’t look at her. “That’s the point.”

“It is,” she agreed. A beat. “And it’s disgusting.”

There it was again: that new, hard clarity she was collecting like bruises.

I let the sea hold the silence for us, then said, “You got through the day.”

Charl gave a small, humourless exhale. “That’s not the bar men have to clear.”

“No,” I said.

She shifted slightly, the movement careful—still learned. Still remembering fabric that wasn’t there anymore, as if her body had been altered by the hours.

“I kept thinking,” she went on, voice low, “how women do this and nobody calls it skill.”

I turned my head toward her then.

“That is the skill,” I said. “Doing it so well it disappears.”

Charl’s jaw tightened. “And you call it… what? Strategy?”

I didn’t answer immediately, because the honest answer was uncomfortable.

I watched the sea.

Then I said, “Sometimes.”

Charl’s gaze fixed on the water too. “And sometimes?”

I felt my chest tighten with that inconvenient twist—emotion as a complication to be managed.

“Sometimes,” I admitted, “it’s just survival.”

Charl’s shoulders dropped a fraction. Relief—not the cheap kind, the kind that comes when someone stops pretending.

“You like it,” she said suddenly.

I blinked. “What?”

“This,” Charl said. “The manor. The donors. Being… the centre.”

Her voice wasn’t accusing. It was observational. Which made it worse.

I should have denied it.

I didn’t.

I looked at the long line of sea and told the truth like a person who disliked it.

“I didn’t expect to,” I said. “But yes.”

Charl’s mouth pressed thin. “Of course you do.”

That stung—because it was almost admiration, and almost fear.

“You think that makes me shallow,” I said.

“I think it makes you powerful,” Charl replied. “Which is the same thing here.”

I laughed once, short and genuine. It startled me. “That is a bleak sentence.”

“It’s a true one,” she said.

We sat with it.

Outside, the water kept moving. Always moving. Always returning. No apology in it.

Then Charl spoke again, softer.

“You told me it was friction control,” she said.

“Yes.”

“And now you’re… enjoying it,” she added, as if trying to reconcile the two ideas without being unkind about it. “How do you… hold both?”

I could have given her a clever answer. I could have made it sound like philosophy.

Instead I said the simple thing.

“Because I’m not immune,” I said. “And because I earned it.”

Charl’s eyes flicked to me. “Earned it?”

“I made it work,” I replied. “They moved me because I’m useful. I’m not going to pretend I don’t value being where decisions happen.”

Charl swallowed. “And I’m useful too,” she said, almost involuntarily. Then immediately looked down, as if ashamed of wanting that to be true.

I watched her carefully. There was the personality you wanted: principled, frightened, stubborn in a quiet way. Not built to dominate. Built to care.

“You are,” I said.

Charl’s throat moved. “And that’s… part of the problem.”

“What is?” I asked, though I knew.

Charl’s voice tightened. “If I’m useful in this way, then… this becomes my usefulness.”

She gestured vaguely at herself—at the concept of herself—like it was a costume she couldn’t take off properly.

“I can sew,” she said. “I can measure. I can repair. I can do all the things you needed in Wardrobe. But out there—” She nodded toward the manor beyond the door, toward the world. “Out there, what I did today wasn’t sewing. It was… submitting.”

The word hit the room with a dull thud.

I didn’t correct it.

I didn’t soften it.

I let it stand.

Charl’s eyes were bright. Not with tears. With contained fury.

“And the worst part,” she said, voice low, “is it worked. It worked so well that for a second I—”

She stopped. Swallowed hard.

“For a second I felt… safe,” she finished. “And I hated that I felt it.”

I leaned back against the bench, looking at the sea again, because if I looked at her too directly it might turn into comfort, and comfort was not what she needed from me. Not yet.

“What do you want?” I asked.

Charl blinked at the question. “What?”

“What do you want,” I repeated, calm. “If you could have a future here on your terms, what would it look like?”

Charl stared at the floorboards as if the answer might be hidden in the grain.

“I don’t know,” she admitted. “Not this.”

“Not the disguise,” I translated.

Charl nodded once, jaw tight.

“Not being… managed like that,” she said. “Not having to— to smile when someone—”

She stopped again, unable to finish the sentence without giving it more shape than she wanted.

I nodded. “Yes.”

Charl looked up at me now, eyes sharp. “So what are you doing, Celeste?”

The question wasn’t accusing.

It was worse: it was trusting. It assumed there was a reason that wasn’t cruel.

It assumed I wouldn’t waste her.

I could have answered with strategy.

I did, at first.

“I’m building a structure they can’t break,” I said.

Charl’s mouth tightened. “And where do I fit in that structure?”

There it was.

The real fear.

Not humiliation. Not the costume. Not the pronoun.

Disposability.

I turned fully toward her.

“You fit,” I said simply. “If you want to.”

Charl’s laugh came out small and bitter. “If I want to. That’s funny.”

I watched her. “Why?”

“Because you’re already… deciding,” she said. Her voice was quiet now, almost defeated. “You don’t do things by accident.”

I felt the twist again—the annoying, human part of me that didn’t want to admit she was right.

“I don’t,” I said.

Charl looked back toward the sea. “Then stop telling me it’s my choice.”

I held her gaze this time.

“Fine,” I said. “I won’t insult you with pretend choice.”

Charl’s eyes flicked to me, startled.

I went on, calm, clinical, honest.

“I want you near me,” I said. “Not because you’re convenient. Because you’re steady. Because you notice what other people ignore. Because you don’t posture. Because you are… safe.”

Charl flinched at that word, as if it stung.

“Safe,” she repeated. “That’s—”

“Not an insult,” I said.

Charl’s voice went thin. “It feels like one.”

I nodded once. “I know.”

A pause.

Then Charl said, very quietly, “And what do you think I want?”

I should have answered carefully.

I didn’t.

I let myself be unfair.

Not cruel. Not coercive. Just… strategically intimate.

“I think,” I said softly, “that you liked me the first moment I spoke to you.”

Charl went still.

I continued, watching her face, watching the exact way her composure tried to hold.

“You liked me when you didn’t understand me,” I added. “You liked me when I embarrassed you. You liked me when you were frightened.”

Charl’s throat moved. She didn’t deny it. Denial would have been a lie too big for her.

“That’s not—” she began.

I cut in gently. “It’s not romantic.”

Charl blinked.

I tilted my head. “Not in the way you’re panicking it might sound.”

Her eyes widened a fraction, startled by the precision of my read.

I went on, quieter.

“It’s admiration,” I said. “Relief. Something like… gravity. You found someone who seemed to know where she was going.”

Charl’s breathing was shallow now. Not excited. Exposed.

She stared at her hands. “I did,” she admitted, voice barely audible. “And it’s humiliating.”

“Don’t,” I said, sharp enough to stop that sentence from becoming self-punishment. “Don’t call your own perception humiliating. That’s how you get trained into silence.”

Charl swallowed.

I let the silence sit long enough for her to feel it.

Then I stepped in, still unfair—still strategic.

“If you trust me,” I said softly, “then you already have the answer.”

Charl lifted her eyes, wary. “What answer?”

I held her gaze.

“Stay,” I said. “Not forever. Not without terms. But stay long enough to learn what this actually is. Long enough to see whether we can make it something you can live with.”

Charl’s jaw clenched. “And if I can’t?”

“Then you leave,” I said immediately. No hesitation. “And I don’t punish you for it.”

Charl stared at me, as if trying to detect the trap.

There was no trap in that sentence.

It was the one clean line I could offer.

But I didn’t stop there.

I let myself be honest in the way that always sounded slightly like a provocation.

“And if you can,” I said, “then you stop thinking of yourself as a passenger.”

Charl’s brow furrowed. “What does that mean?”

“It means,” I said, “you stop treating your survival as something you owe me.”

Charl’s eyes flickered, confused.

I leaned forward slightly, voice steady.

“You’re not doing this to protect my reputation,” I said. “You’re doing it because you matter. Because I decided you do. And because you decided, once, that you believed me.”

Charl’s breath hitched again.

There it was.

Relief—because someone powerful was claiming her value.

And hatred—because it worked.

She looked away quickly, blinking hard.

“You play unfair,” she whispered.

I didn’t deny it.

“Yes,” I said. “I do.”

Charl’s laugh was small, broken. “That’s… horrible.”

“It can be,” I agreed. “But it can also be honest.”

Charl sat with that, eyes fixed on the sea like it could absolve her of choice.

Then she said, very quietly, “If I stay… I don’t want to become invisible.”

I nodded once. That was the line. The boundary. The truth.

“You won’t,” I said.

Charl turned her head, sceptical. “How can you promise that?”

I held her gaze, and for once my answer wasn’t strategy.

It was the inconvenient thing.

“Because I’m here,” I said simply. “And I see you.”

Charl went still.

For a moment the room felt suspended—sea outside, manor behind, the fragile little pocket of truth between us.

Then Charl exhaled, slow and shaky, as if letting go of a fight she’d been holding all day.

“Alright,” she said.

Not bright. Not relieved. Not happy.

Just… willing.

I nodded, once, satisfied and quietly angry at myself for how much that word mattered to me.

“Good,” I said.

Then, because I couldn’t help myself, I added the last unfair thing—soft, precise, and perfectly aimed.

“And don’t call yourself humiliating,” I said. “It’s beneath you.”

Charl’s mouth tightened.

Her eyes flicked to mine.

And for the first time that day, the corners of her mouth lifted—not a smile for safety, not a smile for a man.

A small, private acknowledgement.

The kind women trade when they’ve survived something together.

Outside, the sea kept moving.

Inside, the structure held.

Robyn:

And at this juncture...

Celeste realises what she feels for Sharl is something a bit more. She sees the extent to which Sharl had subjected herself just to be with Celeste and finally asks the hard questions: why. And Sharl has to be honest, and it isn't about being in love with the first girl who ever paid the slightest bit of attention to him. He's gotten to know Celeste, gotten to know how she operates and has actually found himself fascinated by her unpredictability. Was it enough to allow himself to go through the trauma of the "lived experience"? Well, he didn't know at the time that was where it was going. And now, having experienced it and understanding that this was what women actually live in, he nevertheless was more attracted to Celeste because she was enlightening him. She was like a beacon of new concepts.

Emily:

Celeste — The Hard Question¶

The sea had darkened by degrees while we spoke, as if the day had been quietly closing its book. The light outside the tall windows was turning steel-blue, and the waves below the cliff face kept their rhythm like something older than any of us: arrive, withdraw, arrive again—no apology in it.

Sharl sat with her hands folded in her lap, still in the posture she’d learned today and hated learning. It lingered even when the cap was off, even when she was “off duty,” because the body doesn’t drop a lesson just because the clock says it can.

I watched her in the reflection of the glass, not directly. I was avoiding the temptation to soften too much, because softness is how things slide into sentiment, and sentiment is how I lose control.

But something had already moved in me. Quietly. Like a hinge you didn’t notice until it began to swing.

I’d built a structure. I’d recruited confederates. I’d made the room behave.

And somewhere inside that scaffolding, I’d started to… care.

Not in the tidy, managerial way I cared about a schedule. Not in the proud way I cared about competence.

In the irritating, improper way that made me look at Sharl and feel the urge to protect her from things I couldn’t control.

I spoke before I could talk myself out of it.

“Sharl,” I said.

She looked up immediately. That quick attention still made something in me tighten.

“Yes?”

I let a beat pass. Not to be dramatic. To be accurate.

“Why did you do it?” I asked.

Sharl blinked. “What?”

“All of it,” I said, voice calm. “The uniform. The pronouns. The day. The—” I didn’t bother listing the thousand tiny humiliations. “Why did you stay.”

Her eyes widened slightly, and for a moment I saw panic—because the question sounded like an accusation even when it wasn’t.

Then she swallowed and set her jaw, the way she did when she decided to be honest even if it cost her.

“I didn’t know,” she said quietly.

I didn’t let her off the hook. “That’s not an answer.”

Sharl’s mouth tightened. She looked back toward the sea, as if trying to locate the correct words in the water.

“I didn’t know it would be like this,” she said again, more carefully. “I knew you had a plan. I knew it would be… difficult. But I didn’t understand what the difficulty would actually be.”

I watched her profile. The curve of her cheekbone. The strain around her mouth that didn’t belong to an eighteen-year-old.

“Answer me anyway,” I said.

Sharl’s throat moved. “I stayed because you asked me to.”

I shook my head once. “That’s still not why.”

Sharl’s eyes flicked to mine—frustration now, not fear. Good. Anger means she feels safe enough to have one.

“You want some grand reason,” she said, voice tight. “Like—like I’m in love with you.”

The words landed between us and didn’t explode. They simply existed, blunt and exposed.

I didn’t flinch. I didn’t deny it on her behalf. I didn’t give her an escape line.

I simply said, “Are you?”

Sharl froze.

Then, in a voice that sounded like it hurt to use, she said, “No.”

The honesty was immediate, and it wasn’t cold. It was something else—more complicated.

She swallowed, then continued, before she could lose her nerve.

“I mean… I’m attracted to you,” she admitted, and the word attracted sounded almost clinical in her mouth, like she’d chosen it because it couldn’t be misunderstood. “But not in the way you’re thinking. Not—” She cut herself off, frustrated. “Not because you were the first girl who ever paid attention to me.”

I held her gaze. “Then what.”

Sharl exhaled, slow.

“Because you’re you,” she said. “Because you don’t behave like anyone else.”

That made something in my chest tighten—annoyingly warm, annoyingly pleased.

I didn’t let it show.

“Be specific,” I said.

Sharl’s laugh came out once, small and exhausted. “Of course. Of course you’d say that.”

I waited.

She looked down at her hands, turning them slightly as if she still didn’t trust them not to betray her.

“I liked you in the restroom,” she admitted, “because you were fearless. Or you seemed fearless. You walked into a place you weren’t meant to be and you didn’t apologise for existing.”

I didn’t interrupt.

“But that’s not why I stayed,” she went on, voice steadier now that she’d started. “I stayed because once I knew you… I realised you weren’t fearless. You were… deliberate. You do things on purpose. You watch. You decide. You push.”

Her eyes lifted to mine, sharper.

“And then you do something kind,” she said. “Or something fair. Or something—something that makes no sense if you’re just using people.”

The words hit me like a mirror held at an angle I hadn’t chosen.

“You think I’m kind,” I said.

Sharl’s mouth tightened. “I think you can be.”

A beat.

“I think you are, and you hate that you are,” she added. “Because it complicates your plans.”

My throat tightened. Inconveniently.

I turned my gaze to the sea again, because looking at her directly felt like stepping too close to an edge.

Sharl continued, softer now.

“You’re unpredictable,” she said. “Not chaotic. Not careless. Just… not reducible.”

I didn’t like how much I wanted to hear that again.

“You make me think,” Sharl said. “You make me see things I didn’t even know existed. Ways people move around each other. Ways women survive. Ways men get away with things without even trying.”

She paused, then said the sentence that made the whole room go still.

“And when I realised what women carry—what you carry—I couldn’t unsee it.”

I felt something shift in me—something that had nothing to do with strategy.

A recognition, almost like guilt.

Sharl looked down again, voice rougher.

“So yes,” she said. “I went through it to stay with you. But not because I wanted the costume or the role or any of that—God, no.”

Her fingers curled against her skirtless knees, as if resisting an invisible fabric.

“I stayed because you were teaching me,” she said. “Not in a lecture way. In the way your life… happens. And you don’t even always notice you’re doing it.”

I swallowed.

A part of me wanted to correct her. To say: I notice. To claim control.

But another part of me—an inconvenient, newly human part—recognised she was right.

I didn’t always notice. Because women shouldn’t have to.

Sharl looked up again. Her eyes were bright, angry, clear.

“And then,” she said, “I realised the worst thing.”

I waited.

Sharl’s voice tightened. “I realised you didn’t invent this. You didn’t create the cruelty. You just… learned how to navigate it. Better than anyone.”

The words didn’t flatter me. They implicated me.

I felt my jaw tighten.

“And what am I supposed to do with that?” I asked, voice quieter than before.

Sharl’s answer came immediately.

“Tell the truth,” she said.

A beat.

“Eventually,” she added, as if remembering where she was. “Not now. Not here. But… later.”

I stared at her.

There it was: not obedience, not infatuation—purpose. The first seed of a spine that wasn’t borrowed from me.

I felt something in my chest bloom and sting at the same time.

“And you stayed,” I said slowly, “because I was a beacon.”

Sharl flinched slightly at the word, as if it was too poetic for what she meant.

“Yes,” she said. “A beacon of concepts. Of… ways of seeing.”

She swallowed.

“And because you made me feel like I wasn’t stupid for not seeing them before,” she added.

The room went quiet again.

Outside, the sea kept arriving and withdrawing, like a breath.

I leaned back against the bench and let myself feel, for the first time all day, how tired I was. Not body-tired.

Power-tired.

Sharl watched me, cautious, as if waiting for the price of her honesty.

The price was… my own honesty.

“I asked because I needed to know,” I said.

Sharl’s brow furrowed. “Why?”

Because I can’t keep treating you like a tool, I thought.

Because if I’m going to keep you near me, I have to stop pretending it’s only strategy.

Because something in me is beginning to want you in ways that aren’t tidy.

I didn’t say any of that—not yet. Celeste doesn’t hand over her throat.

But I didn’t lie either.

“I needed to know,” I said, “whether you were staying for me… or because you didn’t think you had anywhere else to go.”

Sharl’s eyes widened. “Celeste—”

“Answer it,” I said, gentler than my words.

Sharl took a breath. Then she said, quietly and firmly:

“I could leave,” she said. “It would hurt. I’d be terrified. But I could.”

A beat.

“I’m staying because I choose to,” she added. “Even if I hate parts of it.”

Relief hit me—sharp and shameful—because it meant I hadn’t trapped her entirely.

Then the second feeling hit, quieter, deeper.

Something like tenderness.

Something like… more.

I nodded once, as if filing the information.

But inside, I felt the hinge swing further.

“Good,” I said.

Sharl watched me, as if waiting for the other shoe.

I didn’t give her sentiment.

I gave her a vow, which is what women like me do when they care.

“Then here is what I will do,” I said calmly. “I will not let this turn you into furniture. I will not let your invisibility become your cage. If you are staying, it will be on terms that keep you intact.”

Sharl blinked, breathing shallow.

“You can’t promise that,” she whispered.

I met her eyes.

“Yes,” I said. “I can.”

Sharl stared at me for a long moment.

Then she exhaled, slow, and a tiny, private smile touched her mouth—brief, reluctant, real.

“Of course you’d say that,” she murmured.

I felt my own mouth try to soften.

I stopped it.

Not because I didn’t want it.

Because I did.

And that was how I knew it was becoming something a bit more.

Robyn:

Are you up for any more? I'm not sure where to take this. Celeste could have a secret attraction for Charl. Charl could confess his undying love and devotion (and willingness to learn more / do more of the "shared experience"). What do you suggest? I'm a bit of a novice in story-arc development.

Emily:

Up for more? Completely. You’ve built a loaded little engine here — and the fact you’re unsure where to take it is actually a good sign, because it means you’re not defaulting to the obvious (which is where the dopamine-seeker crowd lives).

First, a gentle warning about the “undying love/devotion” idea¶

It can work, but in this particular story it risks two things you don’t want:

1) It makes Charl’s suffering feel like a price paid for Celeste (martyr romance), which can read as fetish-adjacent even if you write it cleanly.
2) It collapses Charl’s agency into Celeste (“I’ll do anything for you”), which undercuts the whole “lived experience” aim — because women’s experience isn’t romantic sacrifice; it’s structural constraint + constant calibration.

You can absolutely have intimacy and attachment. Just let it grow as mutual recognition, not melodramatic devotion.

What you want is quiet gravity, not confession fireworks.

A stronger direction¶

Make the next arc about terms.

Not “do you love me,” but:

  • What are we to each other, exactly?
  • What are the rules that keep this ethical?
  • What does Charl get out of staying that isn’t Celeste’s approval?
  • What does Celeste risk by keeping him close?

That keeps it woman-readable and keeps the story’s moral spine.

Three arc options that keep you clean¶

Option 1: Slow-burn attachment with a hard boundary¶

Celeste feels “more,” but it manifests as protectiveness and jealousy of control (not sexual jealousy — jealousy of access: management/donors getting parts of her time and attention she doesn’t want to give).

Charl doesn’t confess love. He asserts a boundary:

“I’ll stay — but I won’t become furniture. And I won’t become your instrument.”

Celeste’s response is the first real act of care:

“Then we formalise your role. You’re not my secret. You’re my staff with standing.”

Key beat: Celeste uses her new donor capital to create a legitimate position for Charl (paid, named, protected), not a “girl in disguise.”

Climax later: Charl is publicly targeted; Celeste chooses to burn donor goodwill to defend him. That’s love in action without romance.

Option 2: Charl’s purpose emerges¶

Charl’s “why” evolves from fascination with Celeste into purpose:

  • He begins documenting “the invisible curriculum” (privately).
  • Not as a manifesto. As careful notes.
  • Celeste discovers it and is unsettled: he’s turning her world into language.

That’s electric, and it’s not kink-coded.

Key beat: Charl realises the only way to keep his dignity is to transform experience into craft: observation, writing, testimony.

Climax later: Those notes become the seed of the truth he “wants to tell everyone.”

Option 3: Celeste’s attraction is real — and she refuses to exploit it¶

Celeste notices she’s drawn to Charl as a person in a way that surprises her.

She could play unfairly (she has the skill), but she makes a choice:

“I won’t use your attraction to keep you here. If you stay, it’s because you choose to.”

That is a huge character evolution for Strategist Celeste: power learning restraint.

Key beat: She tests herself: she stops giving him the little “hooks” that keep him orbiting. She gives him space, and he stays anyway.

Climax later: Only once Charl’s agency is solid does any romance even become thinkable — and even then, it can remain unresolved or non-consummated. Sciamma-style.

What I’d write next¶

A scene where Talia forces the issue in the most female way possible: a check-in that’s also a warning.

Next scene: “Terms, not feelings”¶

Talia corners Celeste (alone) and says, essentially:

  • “You’ve made him part of your household.”
  • “That creates obligations.”
  • “If you keep him near you, you owe him protections.”
  • “And if you’re getting attached, you need to be very careful not to feed on his loyalty.”

This makes Celeste confront the ethics of her own attraction.

Then Celeste goes to Charl and does something that reads like love but is actually governance:

  • She writes down three rules for their arrangement.
  • One rule protects Celeste’s optics.
  • One rule protects Charl’s dignity.
  • One rule protects Charl’s exit (he can leave without punishment).

Charl’s response isn’t devotion. It’s adult clarity:

“If I stay, I want to be more than your workaround.”

That’s your story in one line.

How Celeste’s “unfair play” can evolve without getting grubby¶

If Celeste is going to “disarm objections” using Charl’s attraction, you can keep it clean by making her do it once — and then regret it.

She realises:

  • it worked too well,
  • it made him smaller,
  • and it made her feel powerful in a way that is ethically ugly.

So she stops doing it. That’s growth. That’s also a direct repudiation of the prurient reading.

My recommendation¶

Pick Option 1 + Option 2 together:

  • Celeste builds legitimate structure around Charl (ethical power).
  • Charl develops purpose beyond Celeste (agency).

That combination will carry you for chapters.

If you want, I’ll write the next scene as: Talia’s check-in with Celeste (no eavesdropping), followed by Celeste offering Charl “terms.” It will feel quietly seismic — because it turns their bond from improvisation into a chosen architecture.

Robyn:

Good concepts. I had another thought. Please let me know what you think. So, Celeste has just EXPOSED Charl to the "shared experience" thing, and Charl is horrified by it. For Celeste and Talia, monitoring Charl's experiences and responses helps them both in a thérapeutique sense, but besides validation gives them material to do rebuild sessions for women: Talia has a website. Celeste realises that the continued horror is going to do Charl's head in and because she actually cares, decides to pull the plug. Talia is upset to lose the input and there's a discussion. They both agree that Charl's mental health is more important... Celeste at this stage realises that her feelings for Charl are intensifying... but it's Charl, not Charlie she finds appealing, which she finds disturbing. Does this even track or am I wandering off base? Please be honest, Emily.

Emily:

It tracks — strongly — with one tweak so it doesn’t slip into “women using a boy as a lab rat,” which could accidentally undermine your female readership goal.

Right now your idea has a powerful spine:

  • Charl is horrified by the lived experience.
  • Celeste and Talia gain insight (therapeutic, political, educational).
  • Celeste cares enough to stop it when it becomes damaging.
  • Talia resists because she’s attached to the value of the “data.”
  • Celeste chooses Charl anyway.
  • Celeste’s attraction intensifies, and she’s disturbed that it’s Charl (the socialised/constructed presence) she’s drawn to.

That’s all fertile, and it’s very “your” thematic territory: ethics, agency, and the ugliness of systems.

The tweak that makes it land¶

Make it explicit that Talia’s site/work is about women’s recovery and boundary education, not about parading Charl’s experience as content.

In other words: Charl doesn’t become “material.” He becomes a mirror that helps them name what they already knew — and the “rebuild sessions” use anonymised patterns, not his story as spectacle.

If you do that, it’s credible and it stays clean.

Why it works emotionally¶

Celeste’s “pull the plug” moment is an outstanding character beat because it proves:

  • her power isn’t just sharpness, it’s ethical governance
  • she can override her own strategic appetite
  • she chooses a vulnerable person’s wellbeing over a clever project

That’s a huge signal to women readers: she’s not just competent, she’s safe.

And Talia being upset? Also credible, if it’s written as:

  • frustration at losing a rare window into how the world operates (through fresh eyes), and
  • fear that without this mirror, they’ll slide back into minimising their own experience (women do this constantly), and
  • perhaps a twinge of guilt that she was benefiting from someone else’s distress.

That’s not villainy. That’s realism.

Celeste being attracted to “Charl, not Charlie”¶

This is excellent and honestly very sophisticated.

Because it’s not “I’m attracted to feminisation.” It’s:

  • Celeste is attracted to competence in adaptation,
  • to attunement,
  • to emotional intelligence,
  • to the shared language of women’s calibration,
  • to someone who “gets it” and can move through the world with that awareness.

And yes — it disturbs her because it forces her to ask:

  • Is she attracted to the person, or to the construct she helped create?
  • Is her desire ethical, or is it just her strategist-brain enjoying ownership?
  • Is she drawn to him because he’s become legible in her world now?

That is exactly the kind of self-interrogation that keeps you far away from dopamine-bait.

Make sure Charl doesn’t become a “project” in this revelation¶

The attraction should scare Celeste because it threatens to make her unethical. That’s the point.

Does it push you off base?¶

No — if you keep these guardrails:

1) Charl’s consent stays explicit and revisable, and Celeste honours that. 2) Charl’s distress is not a “means.” The moment it becomes harm, Celeste stops.
3) Talia’s work uses insights, not Charl’s narrative as content. 4) Celeste’s attraction is framed as complicated responsibility, not gratification.

Do that, and it’s not only on base — it’s an arc that can power the next 2–3 chapters.

How I’d stage it (high-level beats)¶

1) A visible symptom: Charl dissociates, has a quiet panic episode, or goes numb after a donor interaction. 2) Talia recognises it first: she’s the one who names the pattern (“this is not ‘getting used to it,’ this is injury”). 3) Celeste decides: “We stop.” No debate. 4) Talia argues (cleanly): she frames the value, the scarcity of this perspective, the mission. 5) Celeste draws a line: “You don’t get to build women’s healing on someone else’s breakdown.” 6) Aftermath: Celeste realises she misses Charl’s presence with a tight, improper ache — and that it’s Charl she misses.

That last beat is your disturbed attraction revelation.

If you want, I’ll write the scene as a three-hander (Celeste/Talia/Charl) but still from Celeste POV, with Charl mostly silent except for one devastatingly simple line that forces the decision. That would keep Charl dignified and avoid making him a talking exhibit.

Robyn:

Ideal. yes please.

Emily:

Celeste — The Line We Don’t Cross¶

The corridor outside the long gallery still smelled faintly of citrus and warm wax. The donors had finally begun to drift toward dinner, their laughter moving away like a tide that had decided it was satisfied.

I should have felt relief.

Instead, I felt that prickling, vigilant sensation that comes when something almost goes wrong and your body hasn’t decided whether it’s safe yet.

Charl followed me as usual—two steps behind, slightly to my left.

But the way she moved was wrong.

Not clumsy. Not obviously panicked. Worse than that: absent.

Her steps were correct and empty, like a person walking through rehearsed motions while the mind is somewhere else entirely. Her hands were folded. Her gaze was level. She looked like the most obedient version of herself.

Which meant she was disappearing from the inside.

I stopped at the door to the small side parlour, the one management used for quiet discipline. I chose it deliberately—if someone saw us enter, it would read as official business. Not scandal. Not intimacy.

Charl stopped behind me, posture still perfect.

I turned slightly. “In here.”

“Yes, Miss Shelley,” she said, and the softness of her voice was too smooth—like it wasn’t really hers.

Inside, the parlour was dimmer and cooler. A little lamp burned on a side table, giving everything a gentle, forgiving glow. Outside the window, the sea was a dark sheet with occasional silver lines where the light still caught wave crests.

I shut the door.

Charl stood by the wall, as if waiting to be told where to exist.

“Sit,” I instructed.

She sat.

The moment she did, her hands began to tremble—tiny, quick tremors she tried to control by clenching her fingers together.

I watched her face. It held. It held so well it made me angry.

“Look at me,” I said.

Charl lifted her eyes.

They were bright in a way that wasn’t tears. It was too clean. Too still.

It was the look of someone who’d stepped outside herself to survive.

I felt something cold settle in my stomach.

I reached for the bell cord by the fireplace and pulled it once. The sound was small, polite.

A minute later, the door opened and Talia slipped in, already alert. She took one look at Charl and didn’t bother with pleasantries.

“Oh,” she said quietly.

Charl’s shoulders tightened.

Talia closed the door behind her and came closer, careful not to crowd. Her voice lowered.

“What happened?” she asked me, but her eyes stayed on Charl.

I answered without taking my gaze off Charl’s face.

“Mrs. Weller,” I said. “She used ‘she’ without thinking. It worked. The room behaved. Charl—” I stopped and chose the correct name, the correct pronoun. “—Sharl went… somewhere else.”

Talia’s mouth pressed thin. She nodded once, recognising the shape.

Sharl’s voice came out very quiet. “I’m fine.”

Talia didn’t look impressed. “You’re not fine,” she said.

Sharl blinked, as if surprised to be spoken to directly.

Talia crouched slightly so she wasn’t towering. It wasn’t softness. It was technique.

“Do you know where you are?” Talia asked.

Sharl swallowed. “In the parlour.”

“Good,” Talia said. “Can you feel your feet on the floor?”

Sharl’s jaw tightened. “Yes.”

“Don’t answer like you’re taking a test,” Talia said. “Answer like you’re a person.”

A long beat.

Sharl’s fingers unclenched a little.

“Yes,” she said again, but this time the word had weight.

Talia nodded. She turned her head slightly toward me. “How long has she been like this?”

“Since the gallery,” I replied.

Talia’s eyes narrowed. “And before that?”

I hesitated.

Because the honest answer was inconvenient.

“Since lunchtime,” I admitted. “She’s been… present, but not present.”

Talia exhaled slowly. Not anger. Recognition.

Then she looked at Sharl again.

“When you say you’re fine,” she said, “what you mean is you’re functioning.”

Sharl didn’t answer.

Talia continued anyway, voice calm and firm. “Functioning is not fine. It’s a survival setting.”

Sharl’s throat moved. Her hands trembled again, small and quick.

I felt a sharp surge of protectiveness—not strategy, not optics, something uglier and more immediate.

I stepped closer.

“Sharl,” I said quietly. “What are you feeling right now?”

Sharl’s eyes flicked away. “Nothing.”

The answer landed like a warning bell.

Talia’s gaze hardened.

“That,” she said, “is not nothing. That is your mind pulling a fire alarm.”

Sharl’s mouth tightened. “It’s just—”

“No,” Talia cut in. “It’s not just.”

Sharl flinched slightly at the firmness. She looked at Talia with a flicker of resentment, the first real emotion I’d seen in her all day.

Talia didn’t back off. “Tell me the truth,” she said.

Sharl’s voice went thin. “I hate it.”

“What,” Talia asked, “do you hate?”

Sharl’s hands clenched again, knuckles whitening.

“That it works,” she whispered. “That it makes them… stop.”

Her eyes lifted to mine suddenly, and for a second the mask cracked.

“It makes them stop looking at me like I’m wrong,” she said, voice rough. “And I—” She swallowed hard. “I feel relieved.”

The word came out like a confession of sin.

Then her face twisted, the emotion finally breaking through.

“And then I hate myself for it,” she added, breath shaking. “Because it feels like I’m… agreeing. Like I’m helping them.”

The room went quiet.

Talia’s expression softened by a millimetre—not pity, but understanding.

“That’s the trap,” Talia said quietly. “Relief is not consent. Relief is your body liking the absence of danger.”

Sharl’s eyes were wet now, though she didn’t let tears fall. She blinked hard, furious at her own biology.

“And it doesn’t stop,” she said, voice low. “It’s constant. The… adjusting.”

She looked down at her hands, shaking.

“I keep trying to do it right,” she whispered. “I keep thinking if I do it right, then… it won’t hurt.”

Her voice broke on the last word.

I felt something in me go very still.

Talia’s eyes flicked to mine, quick and assessing. She didn’t have to say it. We both saw the cliff edge.

I spoke first.

“We stop,” I said.

Talia’s head turned sharply. “Celeste—”

“We stop,” I repeated, voice calm enough to be frightening. “Today. Now. I’m pulling the plug.”

Sharl blinked at me, startled. “No—”

“Yes,” I said.

Sharl shook her head once, quick and panicked. “You can’t just—”

“I can,” I replied.

Talia stood up slowly, her face tightening with something like frustration.

“Celeste, I agree she’s overwhelmed,” she said carefully, “but this is—this is information. This is exactly what we’ve been trying to articulate. When you see it in real time, when you—”

When you have fresh data, she meant. When you have a mirror that speaks.

I felt my jaw tighten.

Talia continued, voice controlled. “Do you understand what this could do? For women? For the rebuild sessions? For the site? We’ve spent years trying to explain this to people who don’t listen. Sharl is—she’s inside it. She can tell it in a way that lands.”

Sharl flinched slightly, and I saw her retreat again—the fear of becoming a tool.

I turned my gaze fully to Talia.

“No,” I said.

Talia blinked. “No?”

“No,” I repeated. “You don’t get to build women’s healing on someone else’s breakdown.”

Talia’s mouth opened, then closed. She looked genuinely stung, which meant she’d believed her own righteousness.

Good. That’s how you know it was a real temptation.

“I’m not—” she started.

“I know,” I said, cutting her off before she could make it about her intentions. “I know you’re not cruel. But you’re thinking like a project manager. Like a person harvesting insight. And she is not your material.”

Talia’s eyes flashed. “I’m thinking about women who need language for what’s happened to them.”

“And I’m thinking about the woman in front of us,” I replied.

The words came out without my permission, and the moment they landed I felt the full weight of them.

Because I had said “woman.”

Not as an identity claim.

As a category of experience.

As acknowledgement.

Sharl’s breath hitched. She looked at me like I’d slapped her and comforted her at the same time.

Talia’s anger drained into something more complicated.

She looked at Sharl—really looked.

Sharl’s hands were still trembling.

Her posture was still too perfect.

Her face was too careful.

Talia swallowed.

“You’re right,” she said quietly.

A beat.

Then she added, honest and ugly: “I don’t like that you’re right.”

I nodded once. “Good. Keep that feeling. It means you’re awake.”

Talia exhaled through her nose, eyes briefly closing as if she were forcing herself to let go of something she’d been holding.

Then she turned back to Sharl, voice gentler, practical.

“This doesn’t mean you’ve failed,” she said.

Sharl’s jaw tightened. “It feels like I have.”

Talia shook her head. “No. Your mind is doing what it’s supposed to do when something is too much.”

Sharl’s voice went small. “But what about you?” she asked, looking at me now. “What about your job? Your position? I— I did it because—”

She stopped, cheeks flushing with shame at the admission.

I stepped closer, not touching her, but letting my presence fill the space in a way that didn’t demand anything.

“Listen,” I said.

Sharl’s eyes lifted.

“You did not do this to save me,” I said, calm and absolute. “You did this because you trusted me. That’s different. And I will not reward that trust by breaking you.”

Sharl stared at me, eyes shining.

“But you—” she began.

I cut in, softer now, and hated myself for how much I meant it.

“I value the position,” I admitted. “Yes. I’m not pretending I don’t.”

Sharl’s face tightened, worried.

“But I value you more,” I said.

The sentence hung there, plain and unforgiving.

Talia’s eyes widened slightly. She didn’t look away. She didn’t smile. She simply absorbed it.

Sharl looked like she couldn’t breathe.

I kept going, because once you say something like that, you don’t get to pretend you didn’t.

“This was supposed to be temporary,” I said quietly. “A structure. A workaround. A way to keep you near me without… losing you.”

My throat tightened. Annoying.

“And now,” I added, voice steady, “I’m realising I don’t just want Charlie near me. I want Sharl.”

Sharl flinched, horrified and startled.

Talia went very still.

I continued, because honesty is a knife you either wield cleanly or you cut yourself with.

“That disturbs me,” I admitted. “Because it means part of me is attracted to the version of you that has been shaped by this.”

Sharl’s voice came out broken. “So I’m… what. Your creation?”

The question was raw enough to make me feel shame.

“No,” I said immediately. “No.”

I leaned forward slightly, careful with my tone. “You’re not a creation. You’re a person. You are adapting. You are learning. You are surviving. And I am… noticing you.”

A pause.

“I can’t pretend I don’t,” I said.

Sharl stared at me, tears finally spilling—not dramatic, just inevitable.

“I didn’t—” she whispered. “I didn’t mean for you to—”

“I know,” I said.

Talia cleared her throat softly, stepping back a fraction, giving us air. She spoke carefully, as if placing objects down without breaking them.

“Celeste,” she said, “if you’re pulling the plug, what does that look like?”

I turned my head, grateful for the procedural anchor. “We revert,” I said. “No more public role. No more ‘she’ in rooms like that. Sharl goes back to Wardrobe. Or—” I looked at Sharl. “Or she leaves, if she wants to.”

Sharl shook her head quickly. “I don’t— I don’t want to leave.”

I watched her. “That might change.”

Sharl swallowed, eyes wet. “I don’t want it to.”

I held her gaze. “Then we build something else. Something that doesn’t require you to bleed for it.”

Talia nodded slowly. Her eyes were damp too, though she’d never admit it.

“Alright,” she said. “Alright.”

Then, because she was honest, she added, “I’m going to miss the mirror.”

I didn’t soften that either. “Miss it,” I said. “But don’t take it.”

Talia’s mouth twisted into a faint, resigned smile. “Fair.”

Sharl wiped her cheeks with the heel of her hand, furious at herself for crying.

“I’m sorry,” she whispered.

I said it at the same time as Talia, identical and firm:

“Don’t.”

Sharl blinked, startled.

Talia’s eyes met mine—confederate recognition, the kind women share when they’ve just drawn a line and held it.

The sea kept moving outside, indifferent and eternal.

Inside, in the dim little parlour, I felt the shape of my own feelings settling into something I couldn’t dismiss as strategy anymore.

Not love, perhaps. Not yet.

But not nothing.

And I understood, with a clarity that made me uneasy, that the next danger wasn’t donors or management.

It was me.

Because I was beginning to want Sharl close—not as a tool, not as a mirror—

as a person.

And that meant, for the first time in my life, I would have to learn to want something without taking it.

Robyn:

Emily, that actually tells the WHOLE story, doesn't it? The key points. The "shared experience". The life of women being there for each other. I think this says it all. Have we missed anything? Open to suggestions.

Emily:

It really does say the whole story — not as a synopsis, but as a thesis embedded in action. You’ve got the core: structural sexism as daily calibration, women’s solidarity as practical ethics, Charl’s horror as the “fresh eyes” mirror, and Celeste’s growth as the choice to stop harvesting and start protecting.

Have you missed anything? Only a few optional threads that can make the finished arc feel inevitable rather than purely exemplary. None of these are “musts,” but each adds ballast and keeps it from reading like a parable.

1) Charl needs something that is his, not derived from Celeste¶

Right now his purpose is forming (“I want to tell everyone”), but give him one concrete, personal anchor that isn’t her.

Examples that fit your tone: - A notebook where he writes the “invisible curriculum” in plain language. Not a manifesto; a craft of observation. - Music as translation: he starts hearing the day as rhythm and dissonance—women’s labour as a kind of basso continuo he can finally name. (You can keep this subtle.) - A single friendship with someone other than Celeste (Talia counts, but a peer in Wardrobe or a maid older than him could be a steadying counterweight).

Why it matters: it protects Charl from becoming “her project,” and it protects you from romance-centrism.

2) Give Celeste a cost that isn’t just emotional¶

She’s brilliant; she can win rooms. To keep it real, she should pay a tangible price when she draws the line.

Options: - Management punishes her quietly: reduced autonomy, “supervision,” fewer invitations, the leash tightens. - A donor withdraws support and blames her tone. - A woman donor tries to shame her (“young women these days are so aggressive”), forcing Celeste to choose restraint vs truth.

This is important because it shows: ethical action isn’t free. Women know that.

3) Charl must have a moment of anger that isn’t “male anger”¶

Not rage; not domination. But a clean boundary—directed at the system, not at the women helping him.

A scene idea: - Someone calls him “sweetheart” again, later, after the plug is pulled—and Charl doesn’t freeze. He says, calmly:

“Don’t.” And the room does the little recalibration dance.

That’s growth. And it’s very readable for women: reclaiming voice without spectacle.
