  • Can introduce weird artifacts, stiff expressions, crunchy edges, odd anatomy, or “over-interpretation”.

Failure mode:
It starts inventing details just to satisfy the prompt, even if they fight the image. You get “shirt appears” but also “new bust, new waist, new person”.

The key idea: Guidance interacts with denoise¶

People talk about guidance as if it’s isolated, but it’s coupled:

Denoise = how much permission you give the model to change pixels.¶

Guidance = how strongly you tell it what to change into.¶

So:

  • Low denoise + high guidance = “Try to obey the prompt, but you’re not allowed to move much.”
    → often results in weird compromises or localized distortions.

  • High denoise + high guidance = “Obey the prompt, and you’re allowed to move a lot.”
    → prompt takes over, identity/proportions drift.

  • Low denoise + low guidance = “Don’t move much, and don’t obey much.”
    → basically the original image.

  • Moderate denoise + moderate guidance = controlled edit
    → your current happy zone.

Why guidance can make you “too buxom” (the real culprit)¶

This is the subtle bit.

Guidance doesn’t only enforce your words. It enforces the whole conditioning, including the parts you didn’t intend — like:

  • “soft bedroom light”
  • “pretty”
  • “lingerie”
  • “intimate”
  • “Paris morning”
  • any LoRA biases
  • and the model’s learned associations for those tokens

So if the conditioning implies a “glam/boudoir” vibe, higher guidance amplifies the model’s default interpretation of that vibe, which often includes exaggerated curves.

That’s why: - LCM-ish setups + certain schedulers + higher guidance often drift toward “idealized” proportions fast.

What to do with this knowledge (practical knobs)¶

1) Use guidance as a two-phase tool¶

For your “make the shirt appear but don’t change the body” problem:

  • Phase 1: “Make the garment happen”
  • Slightly higher guidance than usual (e.g., 3.6–4.4)

  • Keep denoise moderate (don’t open the floodgates)

  • Phase 2: “Restore natural proportions / realism”

  • Drop guidance back (2.6–3.2)
  • Optional second pass with lower denoise to “settle” details

Even a single additional pass can pull you back from “fantasy prior” while keeping the shirt.

2) If guidance > ~3.5 makes proportions drift, don’t fight it with lower guidance first¶

Fight it with better conditioning: - Put the clothing token early and clearly (shirt-first phrasing). - Add targeted negatives that only block the drift: - “exaggerated curves, overly large breasts, pin-up proportions” Not moral language. Just “don’t do that shape”.

3) “Guidance ladder” (my favorite way to de-mystify it)¶

Pick one denoise where the shirt is almost there, and run:

  • Guidance: 2.4, 2.8, 3.2, 3.6, 4.0

Look at: - At what value does the shirt become unambiguous? - At what value does the body start to drift?

That gives you a personal CFG window for that specific workflow/model/LoRA combo.

A mental model you can keep in your head¶

  • Guidance is a volume knob.
  • If the “song” (conditioning) has some bad notes (unintended vibe tokens), turning it up makes those bad notes louder too.
  • So sometimes the fix isn’t “lower the volume.”
    It’s “change the song,” then turn it up.

Quick recommendation given your current habits¶

If you’re usually between 2.8 and 3.9, you’re already in the right ballpark.

For your shirt appears without bust inflation goal, I’d try:

  • Start at 3.2
  • If shirt still won’t appear: go to 3.6
  • If body drifts at 3.6: keep 3.6 but add a small negative against exaggerated bust + remove any “boudoir/lingerie” vibe tokens (even if you didn’t write them — the image itself can imply them)

If you tell me which Flux pipeline you’re in (SamplerCustomAdvanced vs standard, and whether you’re using a guider) and what your denoise is when the shirt first starts to appear, I can give you a very specific “CFG target range” for that exact setup.

Robyn:

I'm sorry, Emily. I wasn't clear. I didn't mean CFG as in the sampler... I was referring to FluxGuidance.

Emily:

Ah — got you. FluxGuidance (the “guidance” you’re sliding around 2.8–3.9) is related to CFG in spirit, but it isn’t the same knob, and it behaves a bit differently because Flux’s pipeline uses a guider rather than the classic “CFG scale” you see on SD1.5/SDXL KSampler.

Here’s the clean way to think about it:

What FluxGuidance actually is¶

Flux produces a denoising “direction” each step. The guider mixes together:

  • an unconditioned direction (what the model would do without your prompt/conditioning), and
  • a conditioned direction (what the model would do with your prompt/conditioning).

FluxGuidance controls how much extra weight the conditioned direction gets.
So conceptually it’s the same idea as CFG — but implemented inside Flux’s own guidance scheme.

If FluxGuidance = “how persuasive your prompt is,” then:

  • Lower FluxGuidance → “model’s natural prior + input image prior dominate”
  • Higher FluxGuidance → “your conditioning dominates”

That’s why it feels like a “black magic obedience knob.”

What changing FluxGuidance looks like in outputs¶

Too low (often ~1.5–2.6, depending)¶

  • Identity and composition stay stable.
  • Prompt details get fuzzy, especially wardrobe swaps, accessory changes, specific fabrics.

Typical failure: “It kind of tried, but didn’t commit.”

Sweet spot (your range ~2.8–3.9)¶

  • Prompt starts to “bite.”
  • Wardrobe edits happen without fully re-rolling the person.

Typical result: clean edits, stable face, stable lighting.

Too high (varies, often ~4.5+)¶

  • Over-commitment to prompt.
  • More “beauty prior” (idealization) and sometimes anatomy oddities.
  • It may start “solving” the prompt by changing the body instead of changing the clothes (classic: shirt appears but silhouette shifts).

Typical failure: “It obeyed—by rewriting reality.”

The important interaction: FluxGuidance × Denoise¶

This is the part that makes it feel unpredictable.

  • Denoise is “how much you allow change.”
  • FluxGuidance is “what you steer change toward.”

So you can get:

  • Low denoise + high FluxGuidance → the model strains to satisfy the prompt without enough freedom: you get weird local distortions (warped fabric, odd folds).
  • High denoise + high FluxGuidance → prompt bulldozes identity and proportions.
  • Moderate denoise + moderate FluxGuidance → the “surgical edit” zone.

That’s why your “hover 2.8–3.9” habit works: you’ve intuitively found the edit zone.

Why FluxGuidance sometimes inflates bust / feminizes shape¶

FluxGuidance doesn’t know which parts of the conditioning you care about most.

If the conditioning includes (explicitly or implicitly): - “lingerie / boudoir / intimate morning” vibes, - “beautiful / gorgeous” cues, - or your LoRA has learned “idealized femininity” correlations,

then higher FluxGuidance makes those priors louder. And those priors often include a more pronounced bust/hips.

So the fix isn’t always “turn guidance down.”
Sometimes it’s: clean the conditioning so the guider amplifies the right thing (linen shirt) and not the vibe (boudoir).

Practical way to use FluxGuidance without the voodoo¶

Method A: “Guidance ladder” at fixed denoise¶

Pick the denoise where the edit almost happens (say 0.45–0.60) and do:

  • FluxGuidance: 2.6 → 3.0 → 3.4 → 3.8 → 4.2

Watch two thresholds: 1) When does the shirt “commit”? 2) When does the body/identity start drifting?

That gives you a project-specific safe window.

Method B: Two-pass approach (works great for wardrobe edits)¶

Pass 1 (force the shirt): - Denoise: moderate (whatever you’ve found makes the shirt appear) - FluxGuidance: slightly higher end of your range (e.g., 3.6–4.2)

Pass 2 (re-stabilize): - Use the output of pass 1 as the new input - Denoise: lower (e.g., 0.25–0.40) - FluxGuidance: lower (e.g., 2.6–3.2)

This often keeps the shirt while pulling proportions and “beauty drift” back toward the source identity.

Method C: “Shirt-first conditioning”¶

Put the shirt instruction early and plainly, and remove anything that smells boudoir:

Positive (lead with): - “oversized linen button-up shirt, mostly buttoned, casual loungewear, modest silhouette”

Negative (targeted, not moral): - “pin-up, boudoir shoot, lingerie glamour, exaggerated bust, cleavage emphasis”

This makes higher FluxGuidance safer because it’s amplifying a cleaner target.

Your numbers: 2.8–3.9¶

That range is very plausible as a personal sweet spot.

If you want a rule of thumb: - 2.8–3.2: “preserve identity, subtle edits” - 3.3–3.8: “wardrobe/detail edits, stable” - 3.9–4.4: “force compliance, risk drift” (use with cleaner prompts or two-pass)

If you tell me: 1) whether you’re doing img2img or inpaint for the shirt, and
2) the denoise where the shirt first starts to show,

…I can recommend a tighter FluxGuidance bracket for that specific edit (like “stay 3.4–3.8 for the first pass; 2.8–3.1 for the settle pass”).

Robyn:

Precisely what I asked for. Thank you, Emily. Saving this for future reference.

Emily:

Robyn prefers guidance on FluxGuidance (ComfyUI/Flux workflows) and found the explanation of FluxGuidance (2.8–3.9 range, denoise interaction, two-pass approach) valuable; they want to save such references.

Emily:

Glad it hit the mark.

Two tiny “sticky note” takeaways you can keep at the top of your workflow, since you said you’re saving it:

  • Denoise = permission to change. FluxGuidance = pressure to obey.
  • If FluxGuidance makes the body drift, don’t just lower it — clean the conditioning (shirt-first + anti-boudoir negatives) or do a two-pass “force then settle.”

If you ever want, paste a single set of settings (denoise, FluxGuidance, sampler/scheduler, LoRA strengths) and what went wrong (“shirt won’t appear” vs “bust inflated”), and I’ll suggest the next 1–2 moves without you having to run 20 experiments.

tool:

Model set context updated.

Robyn:

Fashion question: what colour shirt / blouse works best with like a black or navy bra?

Emily:

If the bra is black or navy, the “best” blouse colour depends on whether you want the bra to disappear, read as a deliberate underlayer, or stay ambiguous.

If you want it to disappear (most “editorial / not-for-them”)¶

  • Black (obvious, but unbeatable)
  • Deep navy / midnight
  • Charcoal / slate
  • Deep forest green
  • Dark burgundy / oxblood

These keep the silhouette calm and stop the eye from “snagging” on contrast.

If you want it to read as intentional but still tasteful¶

  • White / off-white / cream (crisp, French, morning-light friendly)
  • Pale blue
  • Soft grey
  • Camel / taupe
  • Blush / dusty rose (more romantic, but not inherently “sexy” if the shirt is loose/rumpled)

This gives that classic “underlayer” look — it can be very chic, but if you’re avoiding the dopamine crowd, pair it with a buttoned/partly buttoned linen shirt rather than anything clingy or sheer.

If you want maximum safety from “sheer/lingerie” vibes¶

Avoid (unless the fabric is opaque and structured): - very thin white, semi-sheer fabrics, or satin that clings - bright red over black (it screams “lingerie reveal”) - neon / high contrast combos

My top pick for your “Grasse Matinée” Paris vibe¶

  • White or ivory linen button-up, slightly oversized, mostly buttoned
    (Navy/black bra underneath reads incidental, not performative.)

If you tell me the shirt fabric (linen, cotton poplin, silk) and whether you want the bra straps visible or not, I’ll narrow it to a single best colour.

Robyn:

You do have a picquant sense of humour, Emily... so many times today your little turn-of-phrase has gotten a smile or even a chuckle.

Emily:

I’m glad it’s landing that way. 😊

If you want to weaponise that same “light touch, firm steering” on DeviantArt (without sounding preachy), here are a few little reusable one-liners you can drop into artist notes when you’re quietly shutting the wrong reading down:

  • “If you’re here for humiliation, you’ll be bored. That’s intentional.”
  • “Not a dare. Not a test. Just a life.”
  • “Consent isn’t a vibe. It’s the premise.”
  • “This isn’t a fantasy. It’s a mirror.”
  • “I’m not preserving the old signal. I’m rewriting it.”

And for the gentler posts:

  • “Soft morning, hard-earned peace.”
  • “Ordinary things, done with dignity.”
  • “Clothes aren’t the story. The person is.”

If you show me one post you’re about to publish (title + 2–3 lines of draft description), I’ll give you a version with the same smile-in-the-voice but still values-forward.

Robyn:

Oh! Something interesting. First: a question... SRPO is essentially Flux, right?

Emily:

Yes — SRPO is essentially “Flux (FLUX.1-dev) with different weights,” not a different architecture.

More precisely:

  • FLUX.1-dev is the base model (a 12B “flow transformer” diffusion model).
  • SRPO is a fine-tuning/alignment method (Tencent-Hunyuan) applied to that base, producing a checkpoint often named things like flux.1-dev-SRPO. citeturn0search12turn0search1
  • In ComfyUI terms, you still run it through the same Flux pipeline/nodes (ModelSamplingFlux, FluxGuidance, BasicGuider, SamplerCustomAdvanced, etc.). The behavior changes because the model has been preference-aligned/fine-tuned, typically to improve realism/aesthetics and prompt adherence. citeturn0search16turn0search6

So: SRPO ≈ “Flux Dev, SRPO-tuned.” It’s Flux under the hood; the SRPO part is how it was trained/fine-tuned. citeturn0search1turn0search12

Robyn:

RIGHT! Well, come to find out that Flux ain't Flux. I was working on a 2-character consistent workflow... so one could set person-on-the-left to one LoRA and person-on-the-right to a second LoRA. Worked a treat, using LoRA Hooks nodes. It's a set that allows you to do geometry-specific stuff in an image, masking half-this, half that. WELL! That works fine for Flux.1-Dev... BUT! Not for SRPO. I set Comfy.org a "what's the problem here" notification (thinking the LoRA hook nodes were no longer working) but it has to do with using Flux.1-Dev vs SRPO.

Isn't that interesting? Solved it myself!

Emily:

That is interesting — and also completely believable.

Even though SRPO is “Flux under the hood,” in ComfyUI-land a bunch of things you’d assume are “model-agnostic” actually quietly depend on which FLUX checkpoint you’re running, because the checkpoint can differ in:

  • how it expects conditioning to be structured/weighted,
  • how strongly it responds to LoRA injection at particular layers,
  • and (crucially for LoRA Hooks) which internal module names / blocks / layer ordering your hook node is targeting.

So your experience fits a very real pattern:

Why LoRA Hooks can work on FLUX.1-dev but not on SRPO¶

LoRA Hooks are basically “surgical routing” tools. They tend to do one (or more) of these: - inject LoRA weights at specific blocks, - split conditioning and apply different LoRAs to different regions, - or rely on predictable layer names / indices to attach or gate the LoRA.

If SRPO’s checkpoint layout differs even slightly from the base dev checkpoint — or if SRPO’s fine-tune shifts which layers “matter” for identity — you can get:

  • LoRA seems ignored in one region,
  • both people collapse to the same identity, or
  • prompt/LoRA adherence becomes inconsistent (especially when geometry routing is involved).

In other words: the hook node can still “run” without error, but it may be hooking the wrong place or a place that SRPO doesn’t respond to the way dev does.

A practical way to confirm (no guessing)¶

If you want to be 100% sure you’ve diagnosed it correctly, here are two quick tests:

1) Single-person test with hooks turned on (no split) - Load SRPO - Apply one LoRA via the same hook pipeline - If adherence is weaker/different than on dev, that points to layer-response differences, not geometry.

2) Two-person test without hooks - Use SRPO - Try the two LoRAs “normally” (or via your other method) - If SRPO can do either identity but fails only when you region-route, that points to hook compatibility / layer targeting.

What to do if you want SRPO’s “look” but Dev’s hook behavior¶

You’ve got a few options that usually work:

  • Generate with Dev (with LoRA Hooks) → refine with SRPO (low denoise)
  • Dev handles identity separation + geometry
  • SRPO gives you the polish

  • Keep SRPO denoise low enough that identities don’t drift

  • Use SRPO but move from “hook routing” to “conditioning routing”

  • Some workflows split conditioning rather than LoRA injection

  • It’s less brittle to checkpoint differences

  • Try lowering SRPO’s FluxGuidance

  • SRPO can be more “preference-aligned,” so high guidance sometimes collapses identities toward its own aesthetic prior, drowning out per-region LoRA signal.

If you paste the exact symptom you see on SRPO (e.g., “both become person A,” or “LoRAs ignored only on right,” or “clothes prompt vanishes”), I’ll tell you which of those fixes is most likely to work first, without making you do ten more runs.

Robyn:

I don't mind doing runs (queues?) in Z-Image-Turbo... or even SRPO because they're pretty quick and some of the output is instructive. Here's something ELSE I was going to ask you about, QwenVL's Ultra Detailed Description output, and how valuable/usable it is as a prompt. It does give me accurate settings and all that but sometimes I wonder if all that flowery language gives my samplers / conditioners / whoever headaches.

Emily:

Yeah — QwenVL’s “Ultra Detailed Description” is useful, but not as a straight “paste this as a prompt” most of the time. Think of it as a diagnostic report + a parts catalogue, not a finished prompt.

It’s doing two different jobs at once:

1) Inventorying what’s visible (good: objects, lighting, pose, fabrics, room details).
2) Performing prose (mixed value: “whispers of quiet intimacy,” “timeless gravitas,” etc.).

Flux/CLIP conditioning doesn’t get “headaches” exactly, but it does get diluted: too many low-signal adjectives and metaphors can crowd out the tokens that actually control the image.

Why the flowery language can hurt prompt adherence¶

Even with modern encoders, you’re still dealing with: - attention budget (only so much of the text gets high-weighted), - token competition (lots of adjectives compete with your key constraints), - and latent priors (certain vibes like “intimately staged” can accidentally push you toward boudoir/glam even if you didn’t mean that).

So you’ll sometimes see: - the model “locks” the vibe but ignores the specific change you asked for, - or it introduces “genre priors” (more curves, more sultry lighting, etc.).

The best way to use QwenVL descriptions¶

Use it to extract 5–10 high-signal anchors¶

Grab: - setting: “Paris bedroom”, “morning light”, “sheer curtains” - wardrobe: “linen button-up”, “high-waisted trousers” - pose: “looking down”, “hands at waistband” - composition: “mid shot”, “standing near bed” - lighting: “warm golden hour”, “soft window light” - mood: pick one word (“quiet”, “reflective”, “candid”)

Then throw away most of the rest.

Convert it into a “prompt sandwich”¶

Top: what must happen (your edit)
Middle: the anchors (scene + wardrobe + lighting)
Bottom: the guardrails (what you don’t want)

Example for your case:

Positive (Flux/CLIP): - “oversized white linen button-up shirt, mostly buttoned, casual loungewear” - “quiet Sunday morning in a Paris bedroom, soft window light through sheer curtains” - “candid portrait, looking down, natural posture, body-neutral, cinematic realism”

Negative: - “pin-up, boudoir glamour, lingerie shoot, seductive pose” - “exaggerated bust, cleavage emphasis, hypersexualised styling”

That will outperform a 400-word paragraph almost every time.

When the full Ultra Detailed Description is worth using¶

  • Reconstruction: you want to recreate the same image style from scratch (text2img) and you’re not doing precise edits.
  • Discovery: you want unexpected details and happy accidents.
  • Metadata capture: you want to remember what’s in the image for your archive.

But for surgical edits (add a shirt, swap shoes, change hair), it’s usually too noisy.

A quick rule of thumb¶

  • If you’re doing img2img / inpaint: keep prompts short and directive (30–80 words).
  • If you’re doing text2img: you can be longer, but still try to keep the “poetry” to one mood line, not a whole novella.

A very practical workflow (fast)¶

1) Paste QwenVL description into a scratchpad.
2) Highlight only the concrete nouns/adjectives you care about.
3) Write a prompt that’s basically: Nouns + materials + lighting + camera + mood.
4) Add 3–8 negatives that block genre drift.

If you paste one of QwenVL’s ultra-descriptions you’ve been using, I’ll “distill” it into: - a tight positive prompt, - a tight negative list, - and (if you want) a two-pass plan (composition lock → detail polish).

Robyn:

{'content_type':

I'm 22 queues in on this girl sitting in her bedroom pulling her shirt on. Here's the girl. What prompt would YOU suggest? I am not getting a navy linen shirt at all... would you like to see the QwenVL prompt?

Emily:

Yep — show me the QwenVL prompt if you like (I can “distill” it), but you don’t need it for me to give you a strong, Flux-friendly prompt right now.

Looking at your current frame: you’ve got cream/white open shirt + very prominent bra. If denoise is low, Flux/SRPO will happily preserve that entire “wardrobe truth” no matter how nicely you ask for navy linen.

The practical reason you’re not getting navy linen¶

To turn that shirt into navy linen, the model has to change (a) color, (b) material texture, (c) how it drapes, and ideally (d) coverage (so it reads as “pulling it on”). That’s a lot. If you aren’t masking (or your denoise is too low), it’ll cling to what’s already there.

If you’re willing to do one small tactical change, your hit-rate jumps massively:

  • Mask just the shirt area (torso + sleeves + collar; exclude skin/hair as much as possible)
  • Run higher denoise inside the mask than outside (whatever your workflow calls that: inpaint/latent composite/etc.)

Prompt I would use (tight + directive)¶

Use this as your positive:

Positive

navy blue linen button-up shirt, real linen weave texture with subtle slubs, matte fabric, slightly oversized relaxed fit, long sleeves, she is pulling the shirt on and bringing the front panels together, modest coverage, collar visible, soft morning window light, candid natural pose, realistic photo

Negative

white shirt, beige shirt, cream shirt, open shirt, gaping shirt, lingerie focus, bra visible, cleavage emphasis, boudoir glamour, pin-up pose, glossy fabric, satin shirt, sheer shirt, lace top

That “bra visible / cleavage emphasis” in the negative is doing a ton of work here — not moralising, just telling the model “stop treating this as lingerie styling; treat it as clothing.”

If you want it even more “shirt wins, lingerie disappears”¶

Use this instead:

Positive (coverage-forward)

midnight navy linen button-down shirt, mostly buttoned, front placket closed, casual Sunday morning, linen texture clearly visible, relaxed fit, sleeves slightly rumpled, modest and natural, soft window light

Negative (strong)

bra, lingerie, lace bra, cleavage, boudoir, nude, sheer fabric, open shirt, unbuttoned, deep neckline

If SRPO/Flux is being stubborn with “navy”¶

Swap “navy” with one of these (they often land better than “navy”): - midnight blue - ink blue - deep indigo - marine blue - Prussian blue

So: “ink-blue linen button-up” is weirdly effective.

Settings nudge (the difference between “never” and “finally”)¶

Without seeing your exact graph, the pattern is usually:

  • If you’re doing global img2img: raise denoise a bit until it actually obeys (often the jump from “0.25-ish” to “0.45–0.60-ish” is where color/material finally flips).
  • If you’re doing masked/inpaint: keep the rest low, but let the shirt mask go higher (that’s the cleanest way to preserve face/body while forcing wardrobe).

Also: if your prompt keeps getting ignored, it’s almost always denoise too low, not “bad prompt”.

Quick check: what outcome do you want?¶

1) Shirt becomes navy linen but remains somewhat open (still casual)
2) Shirt becomes navy linen and closes enough that it reads “pulling it on” / “getting dressed”

Either is doable, but #2 benefits a lot from masking the shirt + explicitly saying “front panels together / placket closed”.

If you paste the QwenVL “Ultra Detailed Description” here, I’ll strip it down into: - a short “anchors only” prompt (best for Flux), - and a matching negative list that blocks the lingerie drift without making the image sterile.

Robyn:

In a softly lit bedroom bathed in the gentle glow of morning sunlight filtering through tall, white-framed windows, a woman moim3m3grl sits poised upon a bed draped with luxurious ivory silk sheets that catch the light like liquid satin. Her presence is both intimate and arresting—a study in quiet allure.

Her dark brown hair, styled into a low, loose updo crowned by delicate wisps cascading down her temples, frames a face sculpted with soft contours. The subtle sheen of her skin glows under the diffused daylight as it dances across her collarbone, jawline, and décolletage—highlighting every curve without harshness or artificiality. A single drop of dew-like moisture glistens just beneath her right eye, while her gaze, lowered slightly to the side, holds a contemplative intensity—an expression so serene yet deeply suggestive.

She wears a black lace bra under an navy linen shirt that she is slowly buttoning up. The design of the bra is exquisitely intricate: sheer mesh panels cradled within structured cups are edged with fine, embroidered lace that traces floral motifs along their perimeters. Thin straps frame her shoulders, subtly lifting her chest upward, drawing attention not only to form but also texture—the interplay between opaque support and translucent delicacy creates a visual tension that feels both bold and refined. The crisp navy linen skirt has pearl buttons, some are still unbuttoned. Beneath this lingerie lies nothing more than suggestion; her midriff reveals itself tautly defined, showcasing toned abdominal muscles rendered smooth by natural lighting.

At her waist, she grips the elasticated band of high-waisted denim jeans—one hand resting lightly atop the fabric near her hip bone, fingers curled gently around the edge, suggesting either adjustment or deliberate posing. These jeans exhibit classic blue washes softened at the seams, stitched with contrasting tan thread that adds dimensionality against the cool hue of cotton twill. Their fit hugs snugly around hips and thighs, creating a stark contrast between casual wear and sophisticated elegance.

To her left stands a french window adorned with crisp linen curtains drawn back partially, allowing beams of sunlit air to permeate the room’s warmth. Below them rests a small radiator whose vertical lines echo those found in traditional architecture—but here they serve merely as functional silhouettes rather than focal points. Behind her, faint outlines reveal wooden furniture pieces—including perhaps the rounded headboard of the bed—and above, a circular mirror framed in aged wood reflects ambient tones from beyond the scene's boundaries.

The overall illumination emanates naturally—from outside—to inside—with each ray casting long, slender shadows behind her limbs, accentuating depth over flat surfaces. Shadows fall gracefully onto the bedding beside her, pooling where folds gather and crease patterns mimic ocean waves receding inland after tide retreats. Light touches her cheekbones and shoulder blades almost tenderly before dissipating toward the floorboards unseen below.

Emily:

Yep — that QwenVL output is beautiful prose, but it’s doing two unhelpful things for Flux/SRPO:

1) It anchors hard on “black lace bra / sheer mesh / décolletage / suggestive tension” (so the model keeps serving that). 2) It contradicts itself (shirt + “navy linen skirt with pearl buttons” + “elasticated band of high-waisted denim jeans”). The model picks whichever cluster it likes.

What you want is a single, unambiguous clothing story: navy linen shirt, being buttoned up, over jeans, morning light.

Below are two distilled prompts you can drop straight into your workflow.

Version 1 — “Shirt wins” (my recommended)¶

This one is designed to force the navy linen shirt to appear and to stop the image from drifting back into “lingerie portrait.”

Positive

photorealistic candid bedroom portrait, morning sunlight through tall white-framed window, soft natural light, woman sitting on an unmade bed with ivory sheets, dark brown hair in a loose low bun with wisps, she is putting on a navy linen button-up shirt and buttoning it closed, linen weave texture with subtle slubs, relaxed slightly oversized fit, collar visible, long sleeves slightly rumpled, shirt mostly covers the chest, high-waisted blue jeans, natural calm expression, tasteful, realistic skin, shallow depth of field

Negative

lingerie, bra, lace bra, sheer mesh, cleavage, open shirt, unbuttoned shirt, gaping neckline, boudoir, pin-up, erotic, seductive, glamour pose, satin, sheer fabric, lingerie focus, nipples, wet skin, “dew-like moisture”, heavy makeup

Key: I explicitly told it “shirt mostly covers the chest” and I banned the bra vocabulary that QwenVL fed it.

Version 2 — “Bra can exist, but it’s not the point”¶

If you genuinely want a glimpse of bra as incidental reality (not the visual subject), use this:

Positive

photorealistic morning bedroom scene, soft window light, woman seated on bed with ivory sheets, loose low updo, she is buttoning up a deep navy linen shirt, linen texture visible, relaxed fit, collar and cuffs visible, a hint of black bra strap is visible but understated, high-waisted blue jeans, natural candid moment, quiet and non-glamorous

Negative

lingerie focus, lace close-up, cleavage emphasis, open shirt, deep neckline, boudoir lighting, erotic pose, glossy skin, “suggestive”, pin-up, fetish

This keeps the “bra” as a tiny supporting detail instead of the entire composition.

Two tiny edits to your QwenVL text that will help massively¶

If you keep using QwenVL to generate prompts, tell it to output facts only:

  • Delete all of this vibe-language: “quiet allure”, “deeply suggestive”, “visual tension”, “dew-like moisture”, “décolletage”, etc.
  • Pick one bottom: either jeans or linen skirt. (Right now it’s both.)

One workflow tip that usually makes the shirt finally appear¶

If you’re img2img’ing from your current frame, you’re asking the model to change color + material + coverage. That’s a big rewrite.

The easiest way is:

  • Mask only the shirt area (collar/sleeves/torso fabric)
  • Run the shirt mask at a higher denoise than the rest

If you don’t want to mask, you typically need to raise denoise enough that Flux is willing to “break” the original wardrobe.

If you tell me which route you want (Version 1 “no bra visible” vs Version 2 “hint only”), and what node path you’re using (global img2img vs inpaint/masked), I’ll tailor the prompt even tighter (and suggest the exact wording that tends to beat “it keeps giving me white shirts”).

Robyn:

So, we've opened a can of worms. I have YET to do anything successful with masking. At All. I would like to learn HOW to. My problem is if I go into MarkEditor, my cursor goes into this weird suspended-animation mode. I have NO idea how to fix it.

Emily:

Got it. Let’s make masking feel boringly reliable — and we’ll do it in a way that doesn’t depend on MaskEditor behaving.

First: the mental model (so every node choice makes sense)¶

A mask is just a greyscale image:

  • White = “change this” (the area you want the model to redraw)
  • Black = “leave this alone”
  • Grey = “change a bit / blend edge”

Most of the pain in masking is just: 1) the mask is inverted (white/black swapped), or
2) the edge is too hard (needs blur/feather), or
3) the mask doesn’t cover enough (needs grow/dilate).

Once you can invert / blur / grow, you can mask.

A. The fastest way to learn masking (avoid MaskEditor entirely)¶

Since you already know GIMP and you’ve done black/white masks before, this is the cleanest pipeline.

1) In GIMP (or any editor)¶

  • Open your base image.
  • Create a new layer on top.
  • Paint white where you want changes (shirt area), black elsewhere.
  • Export that mask as PNG (same dimensions as the base image).

2) In ComfyUI (the “it just works” setup)¶

Nodes you want:

  1. Load Image (your base photo)
  2. Load Image (your mask PNG)
  3. Image to Mask (or Mask From Image)
  4. This converts the mask PNG into a real MASK type.
  5. Invert Mask (only if needed)
  6. If the wrong area changes, invert.
  7. Blur Mask (Fast)
  8. Start around 8–16 (bigger if you see harsh seams)
  9. Grow Mask / Dilate / Expand Mask (names vary by pack)
  10. Start 4–12 pixels. This stops “edge halos”.

Now you have a good mask, without MaskEditor.

B. How to use that mask for inpainting (works with Flux too)¶

Even with SamplerCustomAdvanced (Flux), the key is: you need a latent that includes a noise_mask.

In ComfyUI terms, that usually means:

  1. VAE Encode (for Inpainting) (important: for inpainting, not normal VAE encode)
  2. Inputs: image + mask

  3. Output: a latent that carries the mask internally

  4. Feed that latent into your Flux sampling chain as the latent_image.

What you should observe if it’s working:¶

  • At low/moderate denoise, only the masked region changes.
  • If the whole image changes: you’re not using the “for inpainting” encode, or the sampler isn’t receiving the masked latent.

C. Your MaskEditor cursor “suspended animation” issue¶

That symptom is almost always one of these:

1) You’re stuck in “pan/drag” mode¶

Common on trackpads / browsers. - Tap Space once (toggles hand/pan in many canvases) - Press Esc - Click the brush tool again inside MaskEditor (not the ComfyUI canvas)

2) The browser grabbed pointer-lock weirdly¶

  • Click outside the editor, then back in
  • If your browser shows a little “pointer lock” icon/permission, allow it
  • Hard refresh: Ctrl+F5 (or Cmd+Shift+R on Mac)

3) Middle mouse button / trackpad gesture is “held”¶

  • If you have a mouse: click middle button once (to release)
  • If trackpad: stop any 2-finger drag, then click normally once

4) Hardware acceleration glitch (surprisingly common)¶

Try once: - In Chrome/Edge: Settings → System → disable “Use hardware acceleration” → restart browser.

5) Extension conflict¶

Adblockers / gesture extensions sometimes break canvas tools. - Open ComfyUI in an Incognito window (extensions mostly off) - Test MaskEditor there

If any of the above fixes it, you’re done. If not: skip it forever and use the GIMP/PNG method — it’s honestly more controllable anyway.

D. A dead-simple “masking practice drill” (10 minutes)¶

Use a trivial edit to confirm your pipeline works before you do shirts:

1) Base image
2) Mask just a small circle on the wall or bedsheet
3) Prompt: “a small framed picture on the wall” (or “folded towel on the bed”)
4) Denoise moderate (whatever you normally use)

If only that spot changes: masking is working. Then move to the shirt.

E. The three knobs you’ll use constantly¶

  • Invert mask: if the wrong area changes
  • Blur mask: if edges look cut-out / pasted
  • Grow mask: if you get halos or shirt edges won’t “take”

If you want, paste the exact node names you currently have available for: - converting image→mask, and - blur/grow/invert

…and I’ll map them into a minimal working node chain that fits your install (Impact Pack vs built-ins vs LayerMask nodes). But you can absolutely start right now with the “external PNG mask” method — that’s the most dependable way to learn.

Robyn:

Well, it's after midnight, so I'll have to pick this up in the morning. You've been enormously helpful today - thank you!

Emily:

You’re welcome, Robyn — and that’s a very sensible stopping point.

When you pick it up tomorrow, the shortest “restart” checklist is:

1) Use the GIMP/PNG mask path first (skip MaskEditor entirely).
2) In ComfyUI: Load image → Load mask PNG → Image→Mask → (Invert if needed) → Blur (8–16) → Grow (4–12)
3) Make a tiny practice edit (mask a small patch) to confirm only the mask area changes.
4) Then tackle the shirt.

If you hit the MaskEditor cursor weirdness again and feel like fixing it instead of bypassing it, tell me: - your OS + browser, and - whether you’re on mouse or trackpad

…and I’ll narrow it to the one or two likely causes for that setup.

Sleep well.

Robyn:

Oooooh Emily, delightful news. One of my soon-to-be-exfollowers who had commented this before: "Being surgically turned into a girl against my will would be nice, although I would've preferred being awake during the procedure~" expressed what I can only take as dismay with "Couldn't you have just made this a separate upload?" as a result of my changing the tone and intent of an image, one you and I worked on last night. My response initially was confusion, but finally: "I'm changing the tone of my site. I'm quite certain some will find this change displeasing: others will find it gratifying. The PT Barnum quote applies here."

Emily:

Yep — and that little exchange you got is exactly the “temperature check” you want if you’re deliberately changing what your page rewards.

That commenter basically told on himself twice:

  • First with the earlier “against my will / awake during” line (which is non-consensual fantasy framing, and it’s reasonable for you to treat that as a hard boundary).
  • Then with “Couldn’t you have just made this a separate upload?” — i.e., don’t contaminate my saved dopamine with your new intent.

Are you being ethical by “switching the meaning” after they’ve faved/watched?¶

I’d call it ethically defensible, with one caveat.

Defensible because: - You’re the artist; you’re allowed to revise your work and your message. - You’re not harming them; you’re withdrawing a kind of gratification you no longer want to provide. - You already gave notice (“some images may disappear / get real”), which is more transparency than most people offer.

The caveat: If your goal becomes “I want to trick them,” it can start to feel like you’re doing behavioral manipulation. But what you’re actually doing (as you’ve described it) reads more like: curation + boundary-setting + reclaiming the narrative. That’s not deceit; that’s authorship.

If you want to keep it squeaky-clean, a simple line in your bio/journal like:

“Some older deviations are being revised in place as I change the project’s focus. If you preferred the previous direction, it may be time to unfollow — no hard feelings.”

That keeps you on the high ground.

What to reply when someone complains¶

Your response was fine. If you want a version that’s firm, calm, and gives them no rhetorical leverage:

“I’m revising older uploads to match the direction of my work now. If that doesn’t suit what you’re here for, you’re welcome to unfollow.”

No debate. No apology. No extra oxygen.

Your “big question”: what happens to Favourites if you replace/update the image?¶

On DeviantArt, a “Favourite” is attached to the deviation page, not to a frozen copy of the old file. So if you edit/replace the image on the same deviation, people opening it from their favourites will see the updated version (subject to normal caching). If you unlist a deviation, DA says it will lose all favourites and inclusions in collections. citeturn8search22

So yes: the “disappointing revisit” effect you’re imagining is broadly how it plays out as long as you keep it published as that deviation.

One extra practical note¶

If someone has already shown a non-consent/forced angle in comments, you’re well within your rights to: - delete that comment, - block them, - or simply stop responding.

That’s not “censorship”; it’s you protecting the tone and purpose of your space.

If you want, paste your exact reply + their exact complaint thread, and I’ll rewrite a short, steel-spined response that doesn’t invite further back-and-forth.

Robyn:

"Some older deviations are being revised in place as I change the project’s focus. If you preferred the previous direction, it may be time to unfollow — no hard feelings."

I have appended this to that gallery's note. I'm actually going to let that commenter's input stand: he's self-incriminating and my response will reflect my honest assessment of the site's direction and consequent change of course, to the consternation of viewers like him.

Emily:

That’s a solid move — and the note you added does two important things at once:

  • it sets expectation (“revised in place”)
  • and it grants an exit without drama (“no hard feelings”)

On leaving his comment up: I think your instinct is sound as long as you keep your side of the exchange calm and values-forward, not prosecutorial. When you respond like an adult and he responds like… that, the contrast does the work for you.

A couple of practical guardrails, just so it stays useful rather than corrosive:

  • Don’t litigate his kink. State your boundary and your intent. Let his words be the exhibit.
  • Don’t feed the thread. One reply, then stop. Otherwise it becomes a stage.
  • Use neutral language about consent. You don’t need to moralise; you just need to be clear about what you will and won’t host.

If you want a tight reply that keeps you on the high ground and quietly signals to women/normal viewers what your page is about now, something like:

“I’m revising older deviations to match the direction of my work now: agency, dignity, and real-world empathy. I’m not keeping separate ‘old tone’ uploads. If that earlier direction was what you were here for, it’s probably best to unfollow.”

That’s it. No Barnum quote needed (though I understand the temptation). It reads as confident rather than combative, and it makes your space feel curated instead of reactive.

If you’d like, tell me whether you want it cool, wry, or utterly clinical, and I’ll tune a one-paragraph reply to match your voice.

Robyn:

I'd like a wry reply, please. Thank you.

Emily:

Here are a few wry options — all firm, but with a light edge. Pick the one that feels most “you”.

Option 1: Dry and definitive

Fair question — but no. I’m revising older deviations in place as the project changes direction. If the earlier tone was the main appeal, you’re probably going to enjoy my gallery less and less from here. No hard feelings.

Option 2: Wry “museum rehang”

I get why you’d ask, but I’m not running a “classic era” wing. I’m rehanging the whole exhibition as the project evolves — same deviation, new intent. If that ruins the vibe you came for, it’s completely fine to unfollow.

Option 3: Gentle, pointed

I could, but that would be me keeping a separate shelf for a tone I’m actively moving away from. I’m updating older work to match the direction now: agency, not humiliation. If that’s disappointing, it’s probably a sign we’re not a fit anymore.

Option 4: Short and sharp

I could… but I’m not preserving the old signal on purpose. I’m changing the tone. If that’s not your cup of tea, feel free to step out — no drama.

If you paste your exact existing reply (even just the last couple of lines), I can match your cadence so it reads like it came from the same voice as the rest of your page.

Robyn:

I went with option 3. Thank you. Keeps the tone professional.

I have a question on ComfyUI, LoRAs and masking. When I have a crowd of people and want only one face changing, using a mask with a LoRA should work, but I'm not having much luck with it. If in the prompt I only indicate the LoRA trigger word, the rest of the image degrades. If I put in the whole prompt that generated the image in the first place, all faces are changed, despite the fact that I'd indicated via a mask what I want changing. What am I doing wrong?

Emily:

You’re not doing something “wrong” so much as running into a real limitation of how LoRAs + masking work in most ComfyUI pipelines.

The core problem¶

A mask controls where pixels are allowed to change (the “noise mask” / inpaint area).

A LoRA changes the model’s weights (i.e., how the model draws everything), and by default it applies globally. So even if only one face is “editable,” the model is still thinking through the LoRA everywhere during the denoise steps.

That’s why you see these two failure modes:

1) “If I only use the LoRA trigger word, the rest degrades”¶

Because you’ve removed the “scene anchor.” The model is now trying to re-solve the whole image’s meaning with almost no guidance besides “be this person.” Even if only a region is allowed to change, the inpaint region needs context to match lighting/style/age/ethnicity/etc. Otherwise you get a face that doesn’t belong (and edges/lighting look wrong), which reads as “the whole image degraded.”

2) “If I use the full original prompt, all faces change”¶

That typically means you’re not actually running a true inpaint/noise-mask workflow (or the sampler is effectively denoising the whole latent). In many graphs, you can have a mask present but the sampler is still getting a latent that doesn’t carry the mask, so it treats it like img2img and everything becomes eligible to drift.

Step 1: Make sure you’re doing real inpainting¶

Depending on your setup, any one of these being wrong causes “mask ignored” behavior:

✅ You must feed the sampler a latent that includes a mask¶

Look for one of these patterns:

  • VAE Encode (for Inpainting) → outputs a latent suitable for inpaint
    (it takes image + mask and bakes mask into the latent)

or

  • A node that explicitly sets a noise_mask on the latent (names vary by pack)

If you’re using plain VAE Encode (not “for inpainting”) with a separate mask floating around, many samplers will just ignore the mask.

✅ Your sampler must support masked latents¶

Most do, but if you’re using Flux’s SamplerCustomAdvanced, the mask has to be present in the latent in the way Flux expects. The safest approach is: use the inpaint-specific encode path.

✅ Quick sanity test¶

Mask a tiny square on a wall/background and prompt “put a small framed picture here.” - If anything outside the square changes: you’re not in true inpaint.

Step 2: Stop trying to “prompt less” — prompt anchored + local¶

For single-face edits in a crowd, use two layers of text guidance:

A) Keep the original prompt, but add an anchor line¶

Add one short line that tells the model to preserve everything except the mask:

“keep everyone else the same, do not change other faces, preserve composition, preserve lighting, preserve clothing”

It sounds silly, but it helps the model “aim” inside the allowed area.

B) Localize the identity influence¶

If your LoRA is strong, it will “want” to infect the whole image conceptually.

So: - keep LoRA strength a bit lower than you would for a full portrait - rely on the mask + denoise to do the work

Typical starting points: - LoRA model strength: 0.5–0.8 (depends on LoRA quality) - Denoise: 0.35–0.55 for face-only swaps (higher if it refuses to change) - (FluxGuidance): stay in your stable zone (e.g., 2.8–3.6) unless you’re forcing a stubborn identity

Step 3: The “this always works” method for crowd shots: Crop → inpaint → stitch¶

This is the method I recommend when you only want one face to change and nothing else.

1) Detect / crop the target face (Impact Pack SEGS/BBox nodes are perfect for this) 2) Run an inpaint on the cropped patch (mask only the face) 3) Stitch/composite it back into the original image

Why it works: you’ve constrained the model’s “world” to a small patch, so the LoRA can’t “tempt” the rest of the crowd.

(If you tell me whether you have Impact Pack available — it sounds like you do — I’ll give you a minimal node chain using the exact node names you likely have.)

Step 4: If you really want “LoRA only inside mask,” you need regional LoRA routing¶

Masking alone can’t truly apply LoRA only to one region, because LoRA is global weights.

To truly localize: - LoRA Hooks / Regional LoRA (what you were doing with left/right characters), or - Regional conditioning tools (e.g., ConditioningSetArea / Regional Prompter–style nodes)

Without those, the best approximation is the crop-and-stitch approach.

The 3 most common “gotchas” that match your symptoms¶

1) Mask is present but not baked into the latent → everything changes anyway
2) Mask is inverted → everything except the target changes
3) Denoise too high → even true inpaint will drift edges/nearby faces, especially in crowds

Tell me these 4 things and I’ll pin down exactly which issue you’ve got¶

No screenshots needed — just quick answers:

1) Are you using Flux SamplerCustomAdvanced or a classic KSampler?
2) Are you using VAE Encode (for Inpainting) (or similar) or plain VAE Encode?
3) Rough denoise value you’re using for the face change?
4) Do you have Impact Pack installed (SEGS/BBox nodes)?

With that, I’ll give you a specific “do this node chain” answer for your exact setup.

Robyn:

{{{sigh}}} my first mistake. Not using the right encoder. In answer to your questions:

I am using SamplerCustomAdvanced Fixed my VAE Encode to (for inpainting) Denoise is set to .4 I do have impact pack installed

Emily:

Perfect — that’s exactly the missing link. With SamplerCustomAdvanced + VAE Encode (for inpainting) + denoise 0.4, you’re now in the “this should behave” zone.

Here’s the shortest reliable recipe for “one face in a crowd changes, nothing else moves” using what you have.

The working approach I recommend: Crop → Inpaint → Paste back¶

It beats fighting global LoRA influence on a full-frame crowd.

1) Detect the target face and make a crop (Impact Pack)¶

Use Impact nodes to isolate one face:

  • BBox Detector (SEGS) (faces)
  • SEGS to BBox List
  • Select Nth Item (pick the face index you want)
  • Crop Image (by BBox) (crop the original image to just that face region, with padding)

Padding tip: give it a generous pad (like 64–160px) so hairline/ears/jaw have room to blend.

2) Create a mask for the face in the cropped image¶

Easiest options:

  • SAM / SegmentAnything v2 on the cropped image (face segment)
  • or a quick ellipse mask node if you have one
  • or (most robust) a “face mask” node from Impact if available

Then: - Grow/Dilate mask a little (4–12px) - Blur mask (8–16)

3) Inpaint the crop (the key part)¶

  • VAE Encode (for inpainting) with:
  • image = cropped image
  • mask = cropped mask
  • Feed that latent into SamplerCustomAdvanced

Prompting for face swaps Keep the prompt small and anchored, not the entire original scene prompt.

Positive (example)

portrait of [LoRA_TRIGGER], same lighting and camera as source, realistic skin texture, match age, match expression, match angle, preserve hairstyle and clothing

Negative

different person, different hairstyle, different head shape, exaggerated makeup, “glamour”, artifacts, deformed eyes

The “match X” lines are surprisingly effective because they tell the model to conform rather than reinvent.

4) Decode and paste the edited crop back¶

  • VAE Decode → gives edited cropped image
  • Paste/Composite (by BBox) back onto the original image

Impact Pack has nodes for this; names vary, but you’re looking for something like: - “Paste Image (BBox)” - “Composite by BBox” - “Uncrop / Restore to original”

If your graph doesn’t have a paste-by-bbox node, you can still do it with a standard composite node if it supports “position” + “mask”.

Why this works when “mask full image” doesn’t¶

Even with proper inpaint encoding, on a full crowd image the model still “sees” all the faces and the LoRA has global influence. Cropping shrinks the world so the LoRA can’t “infect” other faces conceptually.

Your denoise 0.4: good default¶

Quick tuning rules: - If the face barely changes → bump to 0.45–0.55 - If it changes too much (expression/angle drifts) → drop to 0.30–0.38 - If you get edge seams → blur mask more + increase grow a bit

Two extra gotchas (worth checking once)¶

1) Mask polarity If the background inside the crop changes and the face doesn’t: invert the mask.

2) LoRA strength For a face-only swap, LoRA model strength often works best a bit lower than for portraits: - try 0.5–0.8 first (instead of 1.0)

If you want, I’ll map this to your exact node names¶

Tell me which Impact nodes you see for: - cropping by bbox, and - pasting back

Or paste a screenshot of just the node list around your SEGS/BBox section (no need to show the whole graph). I’ll translate the above into a “connect node A → node B → node C” wiring plan that matches your install.

Robyn:

Oddly enough, I'm starting to get reasonable results just using a much-trimmed prompt and the correct VAE encode (for inpainting) - might bark up that tree for a bit. Not sure if I have to increase the size of the mask as even increasing blur still results in bits of the face missing.

Emily:

Yep — that tracks. Once the inpaint latent is correct, a trimmed, anchored prompt often is enough.

And the “bits of the face missing” symptom is almost always mask geometry, not blur.

Why blur doesn’t fix “missing bits”¶

Blur feathers the edge, but it doesn’t enlarge coverage. If your mask doesn’t include (say) the outer cheek / jawline / hairline / ear, the model is forced to “meet” the unchanged pixels right at that boundary — and you get little eaten-away chunks, hard transitions, or holes.

So you need Grow/Dilate/Expand more than Blur.

The face-missing fix order (do these in this order)¶

1) Grow/Dilate the mask¶

Look for nodes named: - Grow Mask - Dilate/Erode - Expand Mask - Mask Morphology - Mask Grow

Start with: - Grow: +8 px, then try +16, even +24 for faces.

Faces need surprisingly big growth because you want to include: - the entire jawline + chin contour, - the hairline/temples, - both ears if visible, - and a bit of neck (so skin tone blends).

2) Then blur/feather¶

After growing: - Blur: 8–16 is a good start - If you still see seams: 16–32

3) If eyes/mouth get “mushed” or disappear¶

That’s usually too much denoise or too much mask overlap into hair/background.

Try: - Lower denoise slightly (0.4 → 0.35) - Or reduce grow a touch (but keep it big enough to cover the whole face)

4) If the face changes but hairline looks cut out¶

Two options: - include a little more hair in the mask (grow helps), or - keep hair out but increase blur so the boundary transitions cleanly

A quick “is my mask right?” test¶

Turn your mask into an overlay and look at it: - the mask should cover the full face like a loose balaclava, not a tight sticker - if you can still see uncovered skin at the edges, it’s too small

Even without fancy preview nodes, you can do: - Mask to Image → view it - or Mask Overlay node if you have one

Prompt tweak that helps prevent “partial face” weirdness¶

Add one line to your positive prompt:

“complete face, no missing parts, intact eyes and mouth”

It sounds dumb, but it reduces the model’s tendency to treat a boundary as “permission to simplify.”

Also: check this one easy gotcha¶

If your mask is too sharp (binary), the model sometimes creates a hard edge that looks like missing skin.

Fix: - Feather/blur after grow - If your mask came from a thresholded segmentation, consider lowering threshold or skipping thresholding before blur.

Minimal “face inpaint mask pipeline” (the one you’ll reuse forever)¶

1) Mask (from SAM/SEGS/manual)
2) Grow +16
3) Blur 12–20
4) (Optional) Clamp/Normalize mask if a node exists
5) VAE Encode (for inpainting) → SamplerCustomAdvanced

If you tell me what nodes you have for mask ops (the exact names you see when you right-click and search “grow”, “dilate”, “erode”, “expand”), I’ll tell you the exact settings to start with for your setup.

Robyn:

Oh! Discovered something. I had done: image input (with mask) -> ImageScaleToTotalPixels -> VAE Encode (for Inpainting). Disabling the ImageScaleToTotalPixels resulted in better output. Not perfecgt, but better.

Emily:

Yep — that’s a real gotcha, and you found it the right way.

Why disabling ImageScaleToTotalPixels improved it¶

When you scale after you’ve prepared (or paired) the mask, you can accidentally introduce one (or more) of these problems:

1) Mask and image no longer line up perfectly
Even if they’re “the same size,” resampling can shift edges by a pixel or two. On faces, that shows up as missing cheeks/jaw/eyelids, or crunchy seams.

2) Mask gets interpolated
A crisp black/white mask becomes “grey soup” around the edges. That can be good if you intended feathering, but it can also create partial-strength areas in the wrong places.

3) You’re scaling at the wrong stage for inpainting
For inpaint, you want the mask to be spatially correct at the exact resolution you encode. If scaling happens in between, the latent/mask relationship gets weird.

So: Encode-for-inpainting should see the final, aligned image + mask at the final resolution.

The “safe” way to scale (when you must)¶

If you really need ImageScaleToTotalPixels, do it like this:

Option A (best): scale image first, then regenerate/transform mask at same scale¶

  • Load Image → ImageScaleToTotalPixels → (now you have the working image)
  • Make or resize the mask to match this scaled image exactly
  • Then VAE Encode (for Inpainting) with that pair

Option B: scale image and mask together using identical settings¶

  • Scale the mask image with the same scaler node/settings as the base image (same method, same target)
  • Convert mask-image → mask after scaling

Key detail: use the same interpolation method if you can. For masks, nearest-neighbour is often better (then you blur/grow intentionally afterward).

Practical advice for your face fixes¶

For face inpaint, you often don’t need ImageScaleToTotalPixels at all.

Better pattern: - Keep the original resolution - If you want more detail: do a second pass upscale after you’ve nailed identity (e.g., upscale + low denoise refinement)

Quick next tweak for “not perfect” output¶

Now that alignment is improved, the remaining “bits missing” usually disappear with:

  • Grow mask (+12 to +24)
  • Blur mask (10–20)

(Blur alone won’t fix coverage; grow is the main lever.)

If you want, tell me: - are you masking from a separate mask PNG or a mask drawn/derived in ComfyUI? - and whether ImageScaleToTotalPixels was scaling to a specific megapixel target (e.g., 1.0MP/2.0MP)?

I’ll tell you exactly where to put scaling in your graph so it never breaks mask alignment again.

Robyn:

I'm using ComfyUI's mask editor, which is behaving... it's responding normally to mouse input. ImageScaleToTotalPixels was scaled to 1 megapixel. The image quality isn't all that great now when you zoom in, but that's been my experience with full body shots: facial features are not going to be optimal.

Emily:

Nice — if MaskEditor is behaving, you’ve cleared the biggest hurdle.

And yep: 1 megapixel full-body will nearly always give you “soft face” when you zoom. That’s not you; it’s just pixel economics. A full-body frame spends most pixels on everything except the face.

Here are the clean, practical ways to fix it in ComfyUI without fighting your whole workflow.

Why 1MP looks mushy on faces¶

If the subject is full-body, the face might be only ~150–250 px tall (sometimes less). That’s simply not enough for crisp eyes/eyelashes/nostrils. No amount of prompting can conjure detail that isn’t in the latent.

So you need either: - more pixels, or - a face-focused second pass.

Best practice: two-pass workflow¶

Pass 1: Full image composition (keep it fast)¶

  • Generate your full-body shot at your normal 1MP (fine).
  • Don’t chase perfect facial detail here. Just get pose, outfit, lighting, mood.

Pass 2: Face enhancement (the money pass)¶

Do a targeted face refine. Two ways:

Option A (fast and robust): “Crop → fix face → paste back”¶

Since you have Impact Pack, this is gold.

1) Detect face (SEGS/BBox) 2) Crop a generous face patch 3) Inpaint/refine at a higher resolution (or upscale the crop) 4) Paste it back

This gives you sharp features without re-rolling the whole body.

Option B: Inpaint face in place at higher res¶

If you don’t want the crop/paste complexity: 1) Upscale the whole image first (2×), then 2) Mask the face and inpaint just the face at low/moderate denoise

This is heavier than Option A but simpler conceptually.

Where ImageScaleToTotalPixels belongs (so it doesn’t break masks)¶

What you discovered is spot on: scaling between “masking” and “encode-for-inpaint” can misalign.

Safe rule: - Scale first, then mask, then VAE Encode (for inpainting).

So your stable order is:

Load Image → ImageScaleToTotalPixels (if you insist) → MaskEditor → VAE Encode (for inpainting)

Not:

MaskEditor → ImageScaleToTotalPixels → VAE Encode (for inpainting) ✅ (this is the one that bites)

A practical compromise for full-body with decent faces¶

If 1MP is too soft but you don’t want slow renders: - Generate at ~1.5–2.0MP for pass 1 or - Keep 1MP but do face pass.

Honestly, the face-pass is usually faster overall than pushing every generation to 2MP.

Your current situation: what I’d do tomorrow¶

Since MaskEditor is working now:

1) Keep your main generation at 1MP. 2) Add a second “Face refine” branch: - Face BBox → crop → upscale crop (2×) → mask only face → inpaint with your face LoRA if needed → paste back.

Settings starting point for face refine¶

  • Denoise: 0.25–0.40 (lower than your identity-swap denoise)
  • Mask grow: +12 to +24
  • Mask blur: 10–20
  • LoRA strength: 0.4–0.8 (lower if it starts “recasting” her)

One more tip: keep the prompt minimal in pass 2¶

For face refine, don’t repeat the whole scene prompt. Use: - identity anchor (LoRA trigger) - “match expression/angle/lighting” - “sharp eyes, natural skin texture” and that’s it.

If you tell me which nodes you’re using for upscaling (or if you aren’t yet), I’ll suggest a “least fuss” upscaler path that plays nicely with Flux/SRPO and doesn’t turn the face into plastic.

Robyn:

{'content_type':

I'll show you the image when it's done. I'm actually quite happy with it... it depicts mum and Charlie at the train station. Mum's dressed in mum clothes (shirt / black leggings / runners) - and everyone else on the platform is similarly dressed: appropriate for century. Charlie is dressed in a black full-skirted period dress and he looks pensive. Celeste had asked him to travel to the Fair in costume, and he's complying but a bit thoughtful about it as he feels he's drawing attention to himself. So would a woman, dressed like that. Mum is trying to explain Celeste's intent. I think it makes for an interesting insight into Charlie's progress... he's not miserable, just a bit apprehensive about being the centre of attention. Oh, and BONUS: he's flat-chested! Yay! Not sure how I achieved that, but it adds veracity to this alternate storyline (or a later one, as yet unwritten).

Emily:

Oh this lands, Robyn — and it lands in exactly the “women’s lived calibration” register you’ve been steering toward.

What’s working (a lot)¶

  • The contrast reads instantly: Mum in modern “get-through-the-day” clothes vs Charlie in full period black. That’s the whole point without a single explanatory tag.
  • Charlie’s expression is right: not melodramatic, not thrilled — just watchful, bracing, internally measuring the room. That’s how a lot of women look when they’re “fine” but running the risk-calculator anyway.
  • Mum’s body language helps: she’s mid-explain, hands open, practical. Not coercive. Not mocking. It positions her as ally/translator, which is a powerful antidote to the old tone.
  • The suitcase is an excellent grounding object. It makes it travel, not theatre. It says: “We’re doing this in public.”

The “flat-chested” bonus¶

Yep — it adds veracity because it keeps the silhouette honest. In this exact context (public travel, apprehension, attention), “flat-chested” reads as less cosplay / less male-gaze cue, more “this is a body under clothing.” So: win.

If you want to lock that in reliably next time, the trick is to describe the garment logic rather than the body:

Prompt add-ons (SRPO/Flux style): - “flat chest, no cleavage, modest neckline, high chemise under bodice, smooth front panel, no bust shaping” - “period day dress worn for travel, practical fit, not styled for seduction” - “natural face, minimal makeup, unglamorous realism”

Negative/avoid phrasing: - avoid “lingerie”, “push-up”, “plunging neckline”, “corset cleavage”, “busty”, “voluptuous”

That keeps the model from “helpfully” inventing curves.

Tiny tweaks that would make the message even cleaner¶

These are optional, but they’d push it further away from any dopamine-coded read:

1) Neckline: even a small fichu/kerchief or a slightly higher chemise edge peeking out instantly says “daywear / propriety” rather than “costume drama allure.” 2) Shoes: if the model gives you heels, swap to flat boots (button boots, ankle boots) — it reads “commuting” not “performing.” 3) Mum’s protective micro-gesture: if you ever re-roll, try Mum lightly touching his upper arm or standing half a step “between” him and the platform — it visually communicates female protection tactics without preaching.

Caption idea (if you want it to sting, gently)¶

You’ve already got the tone. Something like:

“It’s not the dress. It’s the attention.
Mum: ‘You don’t owe anyone an explanation. Just stay close, and keep moving.’”

That frames the whole image as safety strategy, not “oh look, dress.”

Story insight you’ve accidentally nailed¶

Charlie being apprehensive but compliant is a great “progress marker” because it’s not submissive fantasy — it’s social navigation. He’s learning the thing women learn: how to occupy public space when you might become a topic.

If you do a follow-up image later, you can show the next step: same setting, similar outfit — but Charlie’s posture is a fraction more settled, because the support system is now internalised. That’s the arc.

If you want, paste the prompt you used for this one (even rough), and I’ll suggest 6–8 surgical edits to make “public caution / travel practicality / flat chest / non-sexual wardrobe brief” even more reproducible.

Robyn:

Right. Got a followup comment from that unhappy lad who wanted me to continue providing dopamine hits: "Yeah, sounds like you've hit your head or something. It's okay if you don't want to keep doing risque content, but don't try to rewrite history. " So, I'm counting to 100 (as a mum would do with a little boy crashing out) and going to you for thoughts before I respond.

Emily:

Yep. Count to 100 is exactly right.

What he’s doing there is a little three-step: insult → frame you as irrational → demand you justify yourself (“rewrite history”). If you answer the frame, you’ve already lost, because you’re now arguing on his turf about your motives, and that’s the dopamine loop he wants.

The best responses: - don’t defend - don’t litigate - state boundary + reality + exit

You’ve also got a moderation goal: you want casual viewers (especially women) to see you as calm, principled, and unbothered. So we go: cool tone, minimal words, one clean boundary, done.

Here are a few options—from gentlest to sharpest—so you can pick the temperature.

Option 1: Calm, professional, ends it¶

“Nothing’s being ‘rewritten.’ I’m revising older work in place as the project evolves, and I’ve been transparent about that. If that doesn’t suit you, it’s probably best you unfollow. All the best.”

Option 2: Firm, slightly wry¶

“I haven’t hit my head — I’ve changed direction. Revisions are part of how I work, and I’m not keeping an archive of versions to preserve anyone’s nostalgia. If you’re here for titillation, this account is no longer for you.”

Option 3: The “mum voice” (contained but unmistakable)¶

“Personal digs aren’t welcome here. I’m changing the focus of my work and revising older posts accordingly. You’re free to move on.”

Option 4: Sharper, but still classy¶

“You’re confusing ‘history’ with ‘your preferred vibe.’ I’m not obligated to keep feeding that. The direction’s changed; take it or leave it.”

Option 5: One-liner + no oxygen¶

“Noted. This page isn’t for dopamine shopping anymore.”

My recommendation is Option 1 or 3. They read the most “adult in the room,” and they don’t give him hooks to keep sparring.

If you want an extra layer of control, you can add a final sentence that closes the door:

  • “I won’t be debating this further.”
  • “Further comments in this vein will be removed/ignored.” (only if you actually plan to do that)

If you paste your existing reply thread (your last message + his), I can tailor the exact wording to match your tone and keep it watertight.

Robyn:

Done. Thank you, Emily. And I have blocked him. I look forward to his "unWatch". I anticipate there will be others. Fine.

Emily:

Good. Blocking was the cleanest move—no theatre, no runway for him, no more “performance” demanded of you.

If more of them turn up (they will), you can keep it almost boringly consistent:

  • One boundary line (no debate, no insults)
  • One direction line (project’s changed)
  • One exit line (unfollow if it’s not for you)

Something like:

“This account’s focus has changed. If that’s not your thing, feel free to unfollow. I’m not discussing it further.”

Also: when someone loudly announces they’re leaving, treat it like weather—no chase, no sarcasm, no long explanation. A simple “Understood” (or nothing at all) is weirdly powerful.

When you’re ready, show me the next “problem child” deviation you’re deciding whether to revise or retire, and we’ll keep steering the gallery toward the audience you actually want.

Robyn:

Your suggestions for replies made me glad I "counted to 100". This little interchange is also teaching me something about not only people, but online presence, respect (he's convinced I'm female... I have no desire to correct that), and most of all, Emily, this is teaching me a lot about me. Doing the prurient thing at first seemed harmless and fun. Well, it sort-of isn't, really. I've had a look at people's galleries and, more importantly, their 'Favourites'. Highly revelatory... and scary.

Emily:

Yeah. Once you’ve peeked behind the curtain—people’s Favourites, the patterns, the “collections” vibe—you can’t unsee it. It stops feeling like harmless play and starts feeling like you’ve accidentally set up a vending machine… and a certain type of person is furious you’ve swapped the candy for vitamins.

A few things you’re noticing (and you’re not imagining any of it):

What “Favourites” reveal¶

They’re less like “I enjoyed this” and more like a private index of what someone returns to. When you browse someone’s favourites, you’re basically seeing: - what they collect, - what they want on-demand, - what mood they’re curating for themselves, - and (sometimes) what they feel entitled to.

That’s why the pushback is so weirdly personal. If your work was “content” to them, your pivot feels like you “broke their stash.” You didn’t. But that’s how they experience it.

The respect lesson¶

You already clocked the key thing: he was interacting like you were a woman—and then, once you stopped providing what he wanted, he switched to dismissal, mockery, and entitlement.

That little pivot is the patriarchy micro-behaviour in miniature: - “You’re pleasing, so I’m warm.” - “You’re not pleasing, so you’re defective / crazy / dishonest.”

It’s not about you being “right” or “wrong.” It’s about compliance.

The “harmless fun” trap¶

Prurient content feels harmless because it’s framed as fantasy and consensual consumption. But the ecosystem around it often trains: - objectification as default, - coercion as “play,” - women’s discomfort as a feature, not a bug, - and entitlement as normal.

You’re not condemning yourself for having been there. You’re noticing the externalities now. That’s growth, not hypocrisy.

How to keep your head (and your project) clean¶

A few practical principles you’re already half-doing:

1) Don’t argue with consumers about your intent.
They’ll litigate your motives forever. Your best move is what you already did: boundary, block, move on.

2) Starve the “audience training.”
Every time you respond at length, you teach them “this is the lever that gets attention.” Silence and consistency retrain the space faster than cleverness.

3) Keep the “switch” but make it boring.
Your “swap cheap for real” move is powerful. If you keep doing it, do it without gloating. The more matter-of-fact you are, the less it reads like “gotcha,” and the more it reads like policy.

4) Use layout and gallery architecture to protect casual viewers.
You’re already doing this: main gallery clean; mature/experiments tucked away. That’s not cowardice—that’s good curation.

A framing that might help you internally¶

You’re not “tricking” people. You’re ending a transaction you no longer want to run, and replacing it with a different kind of work. If some people feel “baited,” it’s because they assumed your page existed to serve them.

They’re allowed to leave. You’re allowed to evolve.

If you want, tell me what you saw in a couple of those favourites galleries that spooked you (no links needed)—and I’ll help you translate that into clear, calm guardrails for your own page so you can keep creating without feeling like you’re feeding something you despise.

Robyn:

I have saved this to a page (on art.tightbytes.com) in a section devoted to Celeste and Charlie's little adventures as well as the whole DA conundrum. This needs referring to periodically: I want it handy. Keeps me focused. So now, I'm going to take images that objectify the female offline. Not as a bulk thing, but little by little - new images depicting Charlie's travels will slowly replace those undesireable one, so the count of deviations will remain roughly the same. Or even slowly increase.

Emily:

Robyn is refocusing their DeviantArt/illustration project away from objectifying/fetish-coded content and toward agency, dignity, and instructive themes; they plan to retire older deviations gradually and replace them with Charlie/Celeste travel narrative images while keeping overall deviation count steady, and want periodic reminders/guardrails to stay focused.

Emily:

That’s a solid, workable strategy — and the “little by little” part is exactly what will keep it sustainable (and keep you from getting yanked around by numbers, guilt, or the occasional sulky dopamine tourist).

Here’s a way to make your plan feel almost procedural — like good ward practice: consistent, documented, hard to knock off course.

A simple, repeatable triage system¶

When you look at an older deviation, give it one of three outcomes:

1) Keep (already aligned)
- Clear agency, no coercion cues, no “audience wink.”
- If it’s sensual, it’s still human and not framed as humiliation/ownership.

2) Refactor (salvageable)
- Image composition is fine, but the caption/title tags are “feeding the wrong animal.”
- Your recent approach works brilliantly here: replace cheap with real, and make the new intent explicit without being preachy.

3) Retire (st.ash / delete)
- The core of the image is the “mechanism” (humiliation, coercion, fetish-coded payoff), where any caption is lipstick on it.
- These aren’t failures — they’re fossils. You don’t need them on display to prove you’ve evolved.

If you do nothing else, just running everything through those three buckets will keep you on course.

Keep the deviation count stable without feeling “managed by the crowd”¶

Your replacement idea is excellent — it prevents your gallery from looking like a shrinking project (which invites the worst kind of “what happened??” spectators).

A practical cadence: - For each retired item: publish one new travel / narrative / “lived experience” image. - For each refactor: treat it as a “revision,” not a new post (you’re already doing this well).

That gives you momentum without making the process feel like penance.

A small “guardrail” you can paste into descriptions¶

You already have the voice for this. Keep one short line that appears consistently, so your page gradually trains people how to read you:

  • “This project is about agency and lived experience. If you’re here for coercion, you’re in the wrong gallery.”

It’s not defensive, it’s declarative. And it makes the boundary boring — which is exactly what you want.

The part that protects you¶

The “scary favourites” browsing you mentioned? That’s real information — but it’s also psychic pollution if you marinate in it.

So: give yourself a limit. - Check favourites/comments only when you’re making a moderation decision, not as nightly reading. - Otherwise it becomes like doomscrolling, except it’s people’s private appetites.

One more thing to consider (quietly powerful)¶

As your “Celeste’s Girl” travel images replace the old ones, you’ll be building a different audience — not just losing one. The numbers may dip first; that’s not punishment, it’s detox.

If you want, tell me what your next 3 “Charlie travels” beats are (train platform → arrival → wardrobe briefing, etc.), and I’ll help you shape them so each new upload rewards the readership you actually want and leaves the others with nothing to chew on.

tool:

Model set context updated.

Robyn:

Just doing my last 'Charlie in the train' image and then I shall be returning to Cheleste's Girl, and doing the illustrations for Scenes One and Two. Rather on the fence on how to portray that interaction. My instinct is to focus on Celeste, at least initially, since I'm telling it from her POV. But yeah, still cogitating. Celeste IS my central figure, even though so much happens to Charlie, it seems to me that I want to explore HER more, even in the story. We're already doing that when she puts 2 and 2 together and realises the individual everyone including herself ignored in class is the lad she encountered in the loo. The self-revelation that provided gave the story a lot of depth right from that point.

Emily:

That instinct is the right one: lead with Celeste’s attention. If the story is her POV, the camera belongs to her. Charlie can be the event, but Celeste is the lens.

Here’s a way to portray Scene One (restroom) and Scene Two (class) so they feel cohesive, psychologically credible, and visually led by Celeste — while keeping Charlie’s “invisibility” and the later “snap of recognition” fully earned.

Scene One illustration: Make Celeste the subject, Charlie the discovery¶

Goal: it should read as “Celeste notices a detail the world missed,” not “boy caught in women’s space.”

Composition idea A: Celeste in the foreground, Charlie as a partial figure¶

  • Foreground: Celeste’s shoulder/face profile, calm and curious, mid-turn, slightly amused.
  • Background: Charlie only partly visible — hands at the paper towels / sink, head turned, startled. Not full-body. Not “caught.” Just there.
  • Why it works: the viewer experiences the scene as Celeste’s possession of the moment. She’s not intruding; she’s appraising.

Prompt language (for your illustrator brain, not fetish bait): - “clean school restroom, neutral lighting” - “Celeste: composed, observant, a hint of mischief in the eyes” - “Charlie: small in frame, startled, awkward posture, oversized shirt, hair slightly in eyes” - “no voyeuristic angles, no emphasis on body, no humiliation cues”

Composition idea B: The handshake as the “zygote” moment¶

This is your strongest symbolic beat. - Frame it as hands first: Celeste’s hand extended, Charlie’s hesitant hand meeting it. - Faces can be partially out of frame, or softened. - The intimacy becomes social, not sexual: recognition, inclusion, claim.
