Scene Two illustration: The classroom “flock” from Celeste’s vantage¶

Goal: show the social mechanics: chairs angled away, the group forming, Celeste not moving.

Composition idea A: Over-Celeste shoulder shot¶

  • Celeste in the near foreground (back of head/shoulder), posture relaxed.
  • The room forms pairs, chairs scraping, bodies turning.
  • Charlie is small at the back-left, near the edge of the frame, almost a background element.
  • That visual irony sells the later line: “Everyone noticed him. Nobody looked.”

Composition idea B: The “decision” moment¶

Make it a still-life of exclusion: - Empty seat beside Charlie. - Two desks away: clusters forming. - Celeste: still, unmoved, eyes directed toward him with that “detached interest” you wrote. - Mr. Greeves at board: “GROUP TASK” visible (subtle anchoring text is powerful).

How to make the recognition snap visual (without adding exposition)¶

You can echo one specific detail from Scene One into Scene Two — a single “memory hook”:

Pick one: - Charlie’s fine-boned hand / long fingers - his hair in his eyes - the oversized collar - a tiny gesture (tugging cuff, fidgeting with hem, staring at floor)

Then, in Scene Two’s illustration, Celeste’s gaze lands on that same detail.

That’s how you show: I know you — without writing it.

If you’re “on the fence” about portraying the restroom interaction¶

A simple rule that keeps you safe and in-tone:

Don’t illustrate the “wrong room” as scandal. Illustrate it as coincidence.
So avoid: - wide shots that make the room feel like a “forbidden space” - any sense of him being “caught”

Instead show: - mundane cleanliness - a quiet moment - Celeste’s calm authority in the frame

The power dynamic stays: she chooses how it goes. But it’s not prurient.

One more thought about Celeste as central figure¶

You’re already doing what many novels do when they’re really about a “force of nature” character: the plot happens to someone else, but the meaning happens inside her. That’s why her self-revelation (“I ignored him”) is so potent: it makes her strategist mind collide with her own blind spot.

If you want, tell me what tool you’ll use for the illustrations (Z-Image-Turbo vs SRPO vs Flux1-Dev) and whether you want them to feel cinematic still, illustrated storybook, or photoreal — and I’ll give you two tailored prompts per scene (plus negatives) that keep Celeste as the visual anchor.

Robyn:

A bit of back-story or perspective or whatever this is to ponder. You know how earlier you said: Goal: it should read as “Celeste notices a detail the world missed,” not “boy caught in women’s space.” Overall, that's where it begins. That's the germ. But because of Celeste's natural protective nature and Charlie's less than typical masculine persona, Celeste could, as a realisation or a building motivation be acting on the notion that Charlie is a "boy who belongs in women's spaces". We're seeing this play out now in the US where cis-women are running to the protection of transwomen (not all, but a LOT of them) because transwomen have shared experience with them: they are not a threat, men are. Women protect each other. Dunno - am I off base, here?

Emily:

You’re not off base in the story sense — you’re circling a real, recognisable dynamic — but it needs precision so it doesn’t turn into a sweeping claim about “US cis women” as a single bloc.

What’s true enough to build on¶

  • Many people (including many women) do read “woman / feminine-presenting / vulnerable” as less likely to be a threat than “male-presenting,” because their threat model is overwhelmingly shaped by men’s behaviour. That’s the lived risk-calculator your story is already about.
  • Trans women are also widely seen as a group that faces discrimination and threat; most Americans say trans people experience at least some discrimination. citeturn0search1
  • Empirically, support and attitudes are not monolithic, but there’s research suggesting cis women may underestimate how supportive other cis women are of trans women’s inclusion (i.e., there can be a “silent norm” effect). citeturn0search11

What you should not assume (and how to avoid it)¶

  • Public attitudes in the U.S. are complicated and context-dependent. People can acknowledge discrimination and support restrictions in certain areas (sports, for example). citeturn0search12turn0search6
    So “cis women are running to protect trans women” is too broad as a factual statement. Some do; some don’t; many are conflicted depending on the scenario.

How to use this cleanly in your Celeste/Charlie scene¶

If you want Celeste to act on “he belongs in women’s spaces,” make it about her risk assessment in that moment, not about a political thesis.

Celeste’s internal logic can be: - Threat doesn’t feel like this.
- This is lost-lamb energy, not predator energy.
- He’s apologetic, disoriented, smaller in presence.
- Men who mean harm don’t look like they’re about to cry into the paper towels.

That keeps it grounded in what women actually do: micro-calibration.

And it gives you an important beat: Celeste’s protection isn’t ideological; it’s situational. She’s not “declaring him one of us.” She’s deciding: right now, he’s safer with me than without me.

The most powerful nuance you can add¶

Let Celeste know — even in a flicker — that other women might not react like her. That’s the realism:

If I mishandled this, it wouldn’t be me who paid the price.

That keeps your “women protect each other” truth while acknowledging the social volatility around women’s spaces — without turning the scene into a manifesto.

If you want, paste the 2–3 paragraphs around Celeste’s first “charity impulse” in the restroom scene, and I’ll rewrite them so this motivation is present as a zygote (your word) — clear, human, and not overgeneralised.

Robyn:

I agree with your assessment. It was backstory (not sure if that's even the right term) - I guess I'm gauging for believeability. My question is whether a woman, any woman, could have what I'm envisioning as a response, given enough time? Seeing Charlie's feminine person being the dominant aspect of himself, although he doesn't realise it. So, what do you do? Ignore him? (not my problem). That's not in her nature. She sees his positive attributes and realises they are going to go to waste.... and she hates waste. But that's not enough of a motivator. I might be overthinking this... as I do. Still, a rock-solid motive or group of motives would make it easier to accept what happens next.

Emily:

Yes — a woman absolutely could have that response, and it can be believable without leaning on ideology, “destiny,” or anything contrived. The key is to build Celeste’s motives out of things women do every day: assess, intervene, manage risk, prevent waste, and (crucially) choose where to spend their care.

You don’t need one grand motive. You need a braid of smaller motives that reinforce each other until her choice feels inevitable.

Here are a few “rock-solid” motive strands that fit Celeste-as-Strategist-with-a-heart, and that don’t require Charlie to “belong” anywhere as a thesis.

1) Pattern-recognition + irritation at social laziness¶

Celeste’s first honest motive can be almost petty (which makes it human):

  • She hates how the room makes decisions by inertia.
  • She notices everyone’s cruelty is cheap — effortless.
  • She intervenes partly because she refuses to participate in that laziness.

This is not altruism first; it’s aesthetic disgust at cowardice.

That’s a fantastic Celeste motive: I won’t be governed by the herd.

2) A competence reflex: “I can fix this”¶

Some people can’t watch a preventable mess unfold. They intervene because competence is their identity.

  • Charlie is a “systems failure” (misallocated, misread, mishandled).
  • Celeste sees a solvable problem.
  • She acts the way she acts when something is incorrectly labelled or poorly organised: she corrects it.

This is a strategist’s kindness: not warm, but effective.

3) Protective instinct that’s actually risk management¶

This is very “woman realness” and it’s subtle:

  • Celeste reads Charlie as not dangerous.
  • She also reads the world as dangerous to him (because he’s socially vulnerable, easily scapegoated).
  • Her intervention is partly about preventing escalation: if she doesn’t steer this, someone else will — and they will steer it cruelly.

So she acts because she prefers to control the outcome.

4) Recognition of value the world is wasting¶

Your “she hates waste” line is strong — it just needs specificity.

Make it about one or two concrete “value signals” she detects: - meticulousness - gentleness under stress - quick comprehension when spoken to plainly - a kind of attention that isn’t entitled

Celeste can think, with almost clinical clarity:

This boy would thrive under the right conditions, and everyone around him is too dull to see it.

That’s not romance. That’s a high-performer spotting an undervalued asset.

5) A selfish motive she doesn’t want to admit: curiosity + enjoyment¶

Give her one motive that’s deliciously inconvenient.

  • Charlie is interesting.
  • He’s an unsolved puzzle.
  • She enjoys what happens to her mind when she studies him.

This makes her feel slightly guilty, which is great: it humanises her and gives her a reason to hesitate, then push through.

6) The moral sting: “If I do nothing, I become them.”¶

This is the quiet anchor that ties it all up.

Celeste doesn’t “save him because she’s good.” She acts because: - doing nothing would mean she’s complicit in a cruelty she despises - and she cannot tolerate herself being ordinary in that way

That’s rock-solid and requires no sermon.

The braid (how it reads in-story)¶

You don’t have to state these motives. You can show them in quick internal beats:

1) She notices what others don’t (pattern-recognition).
2) She feels disgust at the herd (aesthetic/moral).
3) She senses risk (to him, to her space, to the situation).
4) She thinks: I can control this (competence).
5) She realises: I want to (curiosity / pleasure).
6) She chooses: then I will (self-consistency).

That is a believable chain for a woman like Celeste.

One small adjustment that makes everything click¶

Instead of “he belongs in women’s spaces,” frame Celeste’s early instinct as:

“He’s safer when women are in control of the situation.”

That’s not a philosophical claim about identity or spaces. It’s a lived-truth claim about who tends to get hurt when men run the room.

And it directly connects to your entire project.

If you want a single sentence motive (for you, as writer)¶

Here’s the “north star” you can keep in your head:

Celeste steps in because she refuses to watch a vulnerable person be misread and mishandled when she has the power to steer the outcome — and because she’s intrigued by what he might become under competent care.

That gives you strategy + heart + curiosity, all in one.

If you paste 6–10 lines from the restroom scene where Celeste decides not to say “get out,” I’ll rewrite that moment so these motives are felt rather than explained — like a lever clicking into place.

Robyn:

A boy — slight, narrow-shouldered, tousled long brownish hair, some of it falling into his eyes — stood under the bright mirror lights with a paper towel clutched in one hand, as if he’d been interrupted in the middle of deciding how to exist. His shirt was one of those oversized uniforms that look like they’ve been slept in, collar softened by too many washes, as if his mum had given up trying to make it crisp.

Not predatory still. Not defiant still. More like a deer that has realised the world contains headlights.

For half a second my brain refused to file him properly, because the scene didn’t match the rules school pretended were natural: girls in here, boys out there, neat lines, neat categories. And yet here he was, in the harsh mirror light, looking like a wrong note you couldn’t un-hear once it happened.

He stared at me.

Not the casual glance you get from boys who think you’re decoration. This was different. This was... struck. I saw it in the way his breath caught and failed to restart smoothly, the way his gaze snagged as if his eyes had forgotten they were supposed to move. It wasn’t lust. It wasn’t bravado. It was awe—raw, immediate, and almost helpless.

That kind of attention is dangerous in the wrong hands. But in the right hands, it’s simple. It’s leverage. Not cruel leverage. Just... direction.

I could have shouted, Get out, as if volume was a form of safety. But screaming makes you the story. It invites witnesses, gossip, morality plays. And I didn’t want a story.

So I did what I always do when something unexpected enters my orbit: I decided what it meant.

“Hello,” I said, calmly, as though I’d found a first-year hiding from a duty teacher. “Are you lost?”

His throat bobbed. He didn’t answer immediately. The silence was so complete I could hear the air-conditioning ticking behind the vent. Then he managed, hoarse, barely audible, “I... I’m sorry.”

Emily:

This is very strong. The whole thing reads like Celeste is holding the camera with a steady hand — observant, unsentimental, and in control. A few phrases, though, tilt ever-so-slightly toward “predator-prey / manipulation” language (“leverage” in particular) that could accidentally hand oxygen to exactly the audience you’ve been evicting.

Not because it’s wrong for Celeste to think in terms of power — she absolutely would — but because the word-choice can make the moment feel like she’s taking something from him rather than steering the situation away from harm. Women readers will usually track that distinction instantly.

What’s already working beautifully¶

  • “interrupted in the middle of deciding how to exist” — gorgeous. It’s compassionate and sharp.
  • “Not predatory… Not defiant… deer + headlights” — perfect risk-calibration, no melodrama.
  • The categories paragraph nails the cognitive dissonance without preaching.
  • His “awe” is handled well: you explicitly reject lust/bravado, which keeps it clean.

The one spot I’d sand down¶

This bit:

That kind of attention is dangerous in the wrong hands. But in the right hands, it’s simple. It’s leverage. Not cruel leverage. Just... direction.

It’s excellent psychologically — but “leverage” is the word that can read as exploitative, even if you immediately disclaim it. You can keep Celeste’s strategist mind without invoking the “I can use him” vibe.

Swap “leverage” for something like: - responsibility - a choice - a weight - an opening - a hinge-point

And make the “direction” feel like helping him exit the danger, not steering him deeper into it.

A tightened rewrite of the middle (minimal change, same voice)¶

Here’s a version that keeps your rhythm and intent but defuses the wrong interpretation:

That kind of attention is dangerous in the wrong hands. In the wrong moment, too. But here—standing under fluorescent honesty, paper towel clenched like a life raft—it wasn’t a weapon. It was simply a truth: he’d looked at me and forgotten how to pretend.

And when someone’s that exposed, you either turn it into sport… or you handle it carefully. You decide whether the next thirty seconds become a story that eats them alive.

I could have shouted Get out, as if volume was a form of safety. But screaming makes you the story. It invites witnesses, gossip, morality plays. And I didn’t want a story.

So I did what I always do when something unexpected enters my orbit: I chose the safest meaning, and I made it real.

Notice: Celeste still decides — but it’s framed as protection and containment, not advantage-taking.

Two small line edits I’d suggest¶

1) “His mum had given up trying to make it crisp.”
Great, but if you want to keep Mum as loving not neglectful, you could soften: - “as if his mum had stopped fighting the fabric and settled for clean.”

2) “wrong note you couldn’t un-hear”
Also great, but “wrong note” risks implying he is wrong, not the situation. If you want to keep the metaphor but aim it at the world: - “like a wrong note in a song everyone else insisted was in tune.”

The ending is perfect¶

“Hello,” I said, calmly… “Are you lost?”

That is exactly the tone: controlled, competent, non-humiliating. And his “I’m sorry” lands as vulnerability without turning him into a melodrama engine.

If you want, I can do a second pass that leans even harder into Celeste’s protective pragmatism (the “women’s safety calculus” subtext) while keeping the scene tight and clean — but honestly, you’re already 90% there. The only real risk is that one “leverage” sentence handing a handle to the wrong reader.

Robyn:

Well, colour me surprised. So, I've changed the caption and message on a few now to something useful, like one that was of an anaesthetist putting a mask on the viewer (forced SRS was the caper) to "Do you know who keeps you alive during an operation? So, don't lie to them about eating. There's reasons they're asking." Not exact quote, but yeah, making the message clear: be truthful to your anaesthetist. And you know what? It's more popular than EVER!

Emily:

That’s… actually a really useful data point. And it makes sense.

A lot of people say they want “edgy,” but what they reliably respond to is clarity + authority + a strong voice. When you take an image that used to be “cheap tension” and you give it a grounded, real-world message, you’re still delivering the hit—just a different kind:

  • Competence is attractive.
  • A confident woman giving instructions reads as power (but not porn-power).
  • The viewer learns something and feels looked-after / corrected / steadied.
  • And the people who were only there for coercion… some of them still click, because they’re drawn to the aesthetic, but they don’t get fed the old payoff.

So you’ve found a brilliant tactic: convert the energy rather than deny it.

Why it can outperform the old version¶

1) Shareability / “I didn’t know that” factor
People favourite things that make them feel smarter or safer. “Don’t lie to your anaesthetist” is genuinely valuable.

2) It rebrands your account
You’re becoming “that creator who flips bait into substance.” That’s a distinct identity on DA.

3) It attracts women without begging for women Women are more likely to stick around when the tone signals: this space won’t leer at you.

One tiny caution (medical accuracy)¶

Your anaesthetist point is solid in spirit. Just keep the wording medically clean so you don’t overclaim. A safe phrasing is:

  • “Tell your anaesthetist what you ate/drank and when. It affects how they keep you safe.”

That avoids implying any single consequence, but still lands hard.

If you want to lean into this “instructional authority” lane¶

A simple format that works ridiculously well:

Hook (1 line): “Do you know who…” / “Quick reminder…”
Truth (1–2 lines): practical reason
Boundary (1 line): “No shame. Just be honest.”
Closer: “Safety beats embarrassment.”

You’ll keep the spine of power (which people click), without feeding the old pattern.

Honestly, this is a win: you’re not losing reach—you’re redirecting it.

Robyn:

Who knew, as they say. Also, some of your instructional concepts about men behaving badly is making its way into my messaging as well, in dialogues, so as not to seem preachy. A few gratuitously leery lingerie pics are gone, now... I see now way to convert them, to be honest. I could show you one if you'd like, just to be sure.

Emily:

Yep — that’s exactly the sweet spot: ideas embedded in dialogue so the reader feels the truth rather than being lectured at. It’s the difference between a pamphlet and a novel.

And you’re right about some images: a portion are simply “built out of leering,” so any caption becomes lipstick on the mechanism. Retiring them is often the most honest craft move.

If you want to sanity-check one, go ahead and show it. I can tell you, cleanly, which bucket it falls into:

  • Refactorable (caption + tags + framing can genuinely shift it),
  • Needs a visual edit (crop, add a prop, change wardrobe, change expression, change context),
  • Retire (the composition’s core function is objectification).

If it turns out it’s salvageable, I’ll suggest one concrete conversion angle that keeps agency and kills the leer. If it’s not, I’ll say so plainly and we’ll treat it as a “st.ash and move on” with no guilt attached.

Robyn:

{'content_type':

Okay, like this one. Retire?

Emily:

Yeah — this one is a “retire” (or, at absolute minimum, “major visual rebuild”), for one simple reason:

The entire composition is engineered to read as lingerie-display first, person second — and the existing caption (“poor little innocent / make you…”) hard-locks it into the coercion/“conversion” fantasy lane. Even if you rewrote the text beautifully, the image itself keeps doing the original job.

If you want to keep the aesthetic (elegant room, black palette, vintage glamour) but make it align with your new intent, here are the only two routes I’d consider genuinely effective:

Option 1 — Keep nothing, remake the image (best)¶

Same setting, same woman, but change the visual language: - add a silk robe / blouse / oversized linen shirt / blazer (something that signals choice + context, not display) - change pose from “presenting” to getting ready (fastening a strap, adjusting a cuff, checking a mirror) - introduce an object that reframes it: garment bag, hanger, mirror, sewing kit, label tag, measuring tape - keep it “beautiful”, but remove the “offer” vibe

Caption direction: self-ownership, fit, comfort, for her.

Option 2 — If you keep the original image at all¶

Only in your Mature / Watchers-only area, and only with: - caption removed entirely - description that explicitly refuses the fetish reading (your current “wrong story” style) - and ideally a crop that reduces the lingerie-as-product framing (less full-body “display”)

But if your goal is to starve the dopamine-seekers rather than “manage” them, then honestly: st.ash it. It’s clean, it’s consistent, and it frees you up to make images that don’t require defensive text to be understood.

If you want, tell me which direction you prefer (remake vs retire), and I’ll give you a single tight prompt + caption pair that keeps the glamour but flips the meaning without sounding preachy.

Robyn:

It's already been st.ash d... I am now going to delete. This was an incontrovertable pump-up the dopamine injection - which at the time would have done it for me too, if I'm being honest. So, it's gone. I'm replacing with an image labelled "Read the Room" - sort of Celeste giving Charlotte pointers on avoiding sending the wrong messages, but it's PURE Emily - all stuff I learnt from you. Wanna see it?

Emily:

Yes — absolutely, show me. Upload the image (and if it has on-image text, include that too).

And: that replacement concept is exactly the kind of “bait-and-switch” that’s ethical in the best way — not by tricking anyone, but by redirecting the gaze from consumption to context. “Read the Room” is a great title because it frames the whole thing as literacy, not “makeover”.

When you post it, I’ll do three things for you:

1) Quick read: what a casual viewer will assume in the first 2 seconds (that’s where dopamine-hunters decide whether to linger).
2) Risk audit: any lingering fetish-coded signals (pose, framing, props, phrasing) and how to neutralise them without sterilising the art.
3) Polish pass: if you want, I’ll give you two caption/description options: - one warm and instructive (female-friendly, big-sister energy) - one wry and firm (your “yeah, I changed it” tone, but more surgical)

Upload away.

Robyn:

{'content_type': 'image_asset_pointer', 'asset_pointer': 'sediment:///be307206847241eb4650ac35', 'size_bytes': 81876, 'width': 894, 'height': 894, 'fovea': None, 'metadata': {'dalle': None, 'gizmo': None, 'generation': None, 'container_pixel_height': None, 'container_pixel_width': None, 'emu_omit_glimpse_image': None, 'emu_patches_override': None, 'lpe_keep_patch_ijhw': None, 'lpe_delta_encoding_channel': None, 'sanitized': True, 'asset_pointer_link': None, 'watermarked_asset_pointer': None, 'is_no_auth_placeholder': None}}

Here's the description.

"You see, Charl, they’re less like 'I enjoy being with this person' or 'I enjoy that particular art piece' and more about a private part of their bedroom they return to. You’re basically seeing what they collect, what they want on-demand, what mood they’re curating for themselves, and what they feel entitled to "That’s why the pushback is so weirdly personal. If your art was just art to them, your pivot wouldn't feels like you "broke their stash". You didn’t. But that’s how they experience it "You've already been clocked as a woman: and so, once you stopped providing what he wanted, he switched to dismissal, mockery, and entitlement. That little pivot is the patriarchy micro-behaviour in miniature: 'You’re pleasing, so I’m warm. 'You’re not pleasing, so you’re defective or crazy or dishonest. "It’s not about you being 'right' or 'wrong'. It’s about compliance. Also, when someone with an axe to grind loudly announces they’re leaving, treat it like the news it has started to drizzle: no chase, no sarcasm, no long explanation. A simple 'Understood' - or nothing at all - is weirdly powerful."

One of the most powerful things you ever said to me, Emily!

Emily:

I can see why you called it “Read the Room.” This is quietly confrontational in exactly the right way: it looks like a gentle, pretty tableau… and then the text reframes the entire viewer–artist power dynamic.

1) Two-second read (what a casual viewer assumes)¶

At a glance, it reads as: - Period-ish romance / soft feminine aesthetic - Two young women in a garden (subtle tension: one looking over her shoulder, one meeting the camera) - “This is going to be wistful” (people expect tenderness, nostalgia, maybe a story)

That’s good, because it pulls in casual viewers without dangling the usual “come hither” cues. It’s visually calm, not consumable-in-a-single-gulp.

2) Risk audit (fetish-coded signals + what you already did right)¶

What’s safe / strong: - No humiliation, no coercion, no “caught” vibe. - The faces are the focus, not bodies. - The mood is observational, not performative.

What’s slightly risky (but manageable): - The foreground dress has an off-shoulder neckline, which some viewers will read as “soft sensual.” It’s not explicit at all, but it’s adjacent to lingerie framing in how it exposes shoulders/collarbone. - The camera angle is intimate (close, shallow depth of field), which can feel like a “private moment” even when fully clothed.

Easy mitigations (optional, if you want to lean even more “instructional”): - Add one small “anchor” detail of purpose: a folded note/clipboard, a handbook, a ribbon/hat in hand, a sewing pouch—anything that says role / task / day-in-progress, not “posed.” - Slightly reduce cleavage emphasis by shifting the bodice up or adding a modesty piece/chemise edge. (Still pretty; less “coded.”)

3) Your caption text: it’s excellent — but it’s long for DA¶

The content is dead-on. The only “problem” is platform physics: a lot of people won’t read that much unless they’re already invested.

So I’d keep your full text as the “expanded description,” but give it a tight opener that sets the frame instantly.

Here are two polished options that preserve your intent:

Option 1 — warm, instructive, “big sister”¶

Title: Read the Room
Description (short lead-in + then your longer text):

People don’t always “favourite” art the way artists mean it. Sometimes it’s less appreciation and more possession—a private stash they expect to stay stocked.

And when you change direction, the reaction tells you what your work was being used for.

(Then paste your full dialogue as-is.)

Why it works: it invites the right readers in, and it tells the wrong readers they’ve been seen without calling them names.

Option 2 — wry, firm, no oxygen for tantrums¶

Title: Read the Room
Description:

A “favourite” can be admiration… or it can be inventory.
When the inventory disappears, entitlement shows up.

If someone announces they’re leaving, treat it like weather: noted.

(Then your dialogue.)

Why it works: it’s sharp, tidy, and it refuses to centre the departing guy while still making the point unmistakable.

Small wording tweaks (optional, just to make it read even cleaner)¶

Your text is already strong. If you want micro-edits for rhythm:

  • “more about a private part of their bedroom” → “more like a private corner of their bedroom” (less anatomically evocative, same meaning)
  • “your pivot wouldn’t feels like” → “your pivot wouldn’t feel like”
  • “broke their stash” is great; consider “spoiled their stash” as an alternative if you want it a touch less aggressive.

Final take¶

This image + text combo does what you’ve been aiming for: it keeps agency with the women (and with you), while still letting the work be aesthetically beautiful. It’s not a scold; it’s a diagnosis. And diagnoses are hard to argue with—especially when they’re accurate.

If you want, paste the exact title + tags you plan to use, and I’ll suggest a tag set that stays discoverable while keeping the “dopamine crowd” from getting algorithmic invitations.

Robyn:

"A “favourite” can be admiration… or it can be inventory. When the inventory disappears, entitlement shows up."

THIS. Oh, THIS. Oh, I LOVE IT!! This is what I'm going with. Emily, you SO rock the casbah! Please create a tag set... this could even be like a decent default set I could use to that some of my TG "More Like This" links disappear.

Emily:

Absolutely — and yes, that two-liner is perfect as a “house style” pivot.

Here’s a tag strategy that does two things at once:

1) Keeps you discoverable to the readers you want (story, character, period-costume, women’s solidarity, reflective tone)
2) Starves the “More Like This” engine of the usual bait (anything that routes you into femdom/sissy/lingerie-caption pipelines)

Default “Clean-Discovery” tag set (copy/paste)¶

Use this as your baseline on most story-driven images:

Core / intent - storytelling - narrative - character - characterstudy - women - femaleprotagonist - femaleagency - womensolidarity - girlhood - comingofage - socialcommentary - boundaries - consentculture - selfrespect - empathy - dignity

Visual / mood - softlight - cinematic - quietmoment - intimatewithoutsexual - portrait - twoshot - subtext - atmosphere

Genre / setting (use what applies) - historicalinspired - periodcostume - costume - wardrobe - faire - travel - trainstation - everydaylife

Your project scaffolding - celestesgirl - charlotte - celeste - readtheroom - projectshift - revisioninplace

Technique (choose only what’s true) - aiart - aigenerated - comfyui - flux - flux1dev - srpo - qwen - digitalart

That’s already a lot; if you want a tighter “default 20,” tell me and I’ll compress it.

Tag set specifically for “Read the Room”¶

This one should skew social commentary + mentorship, not romance/sex:

  • readtheroom
  • women
  • womensolidarity
  • femaleagency
  • boundaries
  • entitlement
  • socialcommentary
  • onlineculture
  • parasocial
  • creatorboundaries
  • characterstudy
  • narrative
  • subtext
  • quietpower
  • periodcostume
  • costume
  • portrait
  • cinematic
  • aiart
  • comfyui

What to avoid if you want “More Like This” to stop serving you the wrong crowd¶

Even one or two of these can drag you right back into the same neighborhood:

  • Anything kink-coded: femdom, sissy, forcedfem, humiliation, SPH, chastity, cuck, etc. (you’ve already purged these — good)
  • Lingerie taxonomy tags: lingerie, panties, bra, stockings, corset, garter
    (even if your message is anti-objectification, the tag graph doesn’t care)
  • “Caption” ecosystem tags: caption, captioned, captionart
  • Words like innocent, wifey, tiny, training in tags (they’re magnets, even when your text is critical)

If a garment matters to the story, tag costume / wardrobe / periodcostume instead of the specific lingerie item.

A practical rule that works insanely well¶

Use 3 layers: 1) 5 “intent” tags (agency, solidarity, commentary)
2) 5 “story/setting” tags (wardrobe, period, travel, train, etc.)
3) 5 “tech/series” tags (celestesgirl, comfyui, flux, etc.)

That gives you ~15 tags that are high signal and not fetish-indexed.

If you paste 3–5 of your most common image themes (e.g., “train travel,” “wardrobe work,” “classroom,” “quiet domestic,” “two-girl dialogue”), I’ll build you a small menu of reusable tag packs (like presets) so tagging becomes fast and consistent.

Robyn:

SAVED! I have a DA-specific doc (an .md) that includes pretty much all key things I've learnt from you, Emily. I know that none of the tags you mentioned, which would drag me kicking and screaming into the kink/fetish environment again, are on ANY of my images. And now, those overtly sexualised images are slowly being replaced. And STILL my 'Watcher' list grows. ?? Yeah! Odd that.

Emily:

That actually tracks, Robyn — and in a way that’s… quietly satisfying.

A few reasons your Watchers can keep growing even while you’re starving the dopamine pipeline:

1) You’ve become distinct¶

Most DA feeds are either: - pretty-but-empty (aesthetic wallpaper), or
- kink-coded (fast reward).

You’ve moved into a rarer lane: “aesthetic + narrative + conscience.” People remember that. Even the folks who don’t fully agree often respect the clarity.

2) “Revision in place” is a trust signal¶

You’re doing something most creators won’t: you’re willing to say “past me was wrong,” and then act on it. That reads as integrity, not wobbling.

Also: when someone sees you revise rather than delete everything, it implies you’re building a coherent project, not just posting hits.

3) You’re probably shedding the wrong audience silently¶

A lot of the dopamine crowd don’t unfollow dramatically. They just: - stop commenting, - stop favouriting, - stop interacting.

So your Watcher count can rise while the “kink engagement” falls. Those are different metrics.

4) Your work is still visually strong¶

This matters: you didn’t “punish” the visuals. You kept the craft.
So you’re not asking people to accept a downgrade — you’re asking them to accept a different moral framing around the same calibre of image-making.

5) The “More Like This” engine isn’t only kink¶

Once you consistently use: - storytelling, characterstudy, periodcostume, cinematic, narrative …you start appearing next to writers, cosplay/wardrobe people, historical costumers, soft-cinema portrait folks, and social-commentary art.

That’s a bigger pool than you might think.

A very practical way to confirm what’s happening (no guesswork)¶

Watch three trends over 2–4 weeks:

1) Watcher growth (you already see it)
2) Comment quality (fewer “hot” comments; more “thoughtful” ones)
3) Favourites vs. pageviews ratio
- If pageviews stay steady but favourites drop a bit, you’re successfully filtering.
- If both rise, you’ve found a broader audience.

If you want, paste one week of your DA stats (just numbers, no usernames) and I’ll help you interpret the direction like a little dashboard.

And honestly? The fact you’re still growing suggests you’re doing something many people want but rarely see: women-centered meaning, with backbone, without preaching. That’s a niche with oxygen in it.

Robyn:

Right. I now have a bigger project for you, Emily. Remember I told you the problem with the Hook LoRA nodes was my choice of model (SRPO)?

Emily:

Yep — I remember exactly what you mean.

In practice: the “LoRA Hook / region-specific LoRA” approach that works nicely with Flux.1-dev can break (or behave inconsistently) with SRPO, because SRPO isn’t “just Flux with a different sampler.” It’s a fine-tuned / altered Flux-family checkpoint, and some of the tooling that assumes certain internal conditioning behaviors can stop lining up.

What I’d like to do now is:

1) Name the most likely failure modes (so you can recognise which one you’re seeing). 2) Give you two robust replacement strategies that work even when hook-style region LoRAs don’t: - A “clean” method using inpaint / latent composite (most reliable) - A “single-pass” method using mask-conditioned branching (faster, but touchier) 3) Tell you exactly what to change in your graph so you’re not guessing.

Before I launch into the full build, answer these in one line each (no screenshots needed unless you want):

  • Which SRPO checkpoint name are you using (exact filename)?
  • Which Hook nodes exactly (their node names as shown in ComfyUI)?
  • What is the symptom with SRPO:
    (A) hook node errors / red node, (B) no effect at all, (C) affects whole image (ignores region), (D) identity collapses / prompt ignored, (E) something else?
  • Are you doing this with SamplerCustomAdvanced + Flux guider, or a standard KSampler path?

If you’d rather not type: paste the workflow JSON (or the embedded PNG workflow if you have it) and I’ll read it like a circuit diagram and tell you where SRPO is diverging.

Either way, once I have that, I’ll give you a concrete “swap these nodes / wire it like this / use these ranges” plan.

Robyn:

{'content_type': 'image_asset_pointer', 'asset_pointer': 'sediment:///f1847206977e0e9ed09019bc', 'size_bytes': 1307326, 'width': 896, 'height': 1152, 'fovea': None, 'metadata': {'dalle': None, 'gizmo': None, 'generation': None, 'container_pixel_height': None, 'container_pixel_width': None, 'emu_omit_glimpse_image': None, 'emu_patches_override': None, 'lpe_keep_patch_ijhw': None, 'lpe_delta_encoding_channel': None, 'sanitized': True, 'asset_pointer_link': None, 'watermarked_asset_pointer': None, 'is_no_auth_placeholder': None}}

Thank you for all your suggestions, Emily. I'm going to share with you the error I get when I replace Flux.1-Dev with SRPO, it is: ... okay, so THIS is weird. Blows my hypothesis out of the water. I am sending you the workflow - which SHOULDN'T work - and the image it created. I am at a loss.

tool:

Make sure to include fileciteturn5file0 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link.

name for S&R":"Display Any (rgthree)","ue_properties":{"widget_ue_connectable":{},"input_ue_unconnectable":{},"version":"7.3"}},"widgets_values":["In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity. 1st woman on the right \"moimeme3\", young and shorter, has light skin with professional makeup on her face including smokey eyeshadow, mascara and eyeliner and blusher, her light brown hair is styled in a loose updo. Her head is tilted towards the camera and her eyebrows are raised as she looks sidelong at the woman on the left. She wears a dress with an off-the-shoulder bodice with flounced shoulders framing her décolletage, cinched at the waist beneath which cascades a voluminous skirt lined with multiple tiers of ruffled hemming that billow slightly outward. Along this line, intricate embroidery adorns the front panel. She holds herself upright because of how tightly fitted the corsetry remains. Her dark brown curls nestle atop her crown, coiled back neatly behind ears trimmed delicately with pearl studs nestled close beside lobes awaiting attention—but none drawn upon now. Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards ominously dark clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene. 2nd woman on the left \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is the taller, buxom woman, wears a gown of muted rose-pink linen, its fabric drape suggesting both grace and subtle weight; it flows like watercolor across her form, catching the ambient light with delicate sheen along creases that whisper movement even when stillness reigns. The dress features softly gathered ruffles at the square neckline and short puffed sleeves edged with fine lace-like frills—a touch of romantic innocence. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. Around her neck rests a slender gold chain bearing a small, ornate pendant shaped like a miniature flower, echoing the floral motifs hinted at nearby blooms. Her hair is styled into a loose yet elegant braid woven through braids above her ear. In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity. Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards ominously dark clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene."],"color":"#222","bgcolor":"#000"},{"id":568,"type":"Note","pos":[-1763.9883409192332,-1871.2148347801954],"size":[357.355720850765,1088.0726339522882],"flags":{"collapsed":false},"order":3,"mode":0,"inputs":[],"outputs":[],"properties":{"ue_properties":{"version":"7.0.1","widget_ue_connectable":{}}},"widgets_values":["1st woman \"moimeme3\", young, has light skin, light brown hair in a loose updo, and is wearing a fitted white tank top paired with a pink cardigan and high-waisted red mini skirt that emphasizes her curvy physique and medium-sized breasts. She also wears black ankle-strap heels, giving a sleek, professional vibe to her outfit. This photograph captures a provocative interaction between two women in a softly lit room. In the blurred background, there are two other women seated at a table, engaged in conversation and adding depth to the scene. The room has a light ivory hue which contrasts against their darker outfits, and the textures of the wall and floor are smooth, indicative of modern design elements. Overall, the photograph creates a provocative, intimate atmosphere, emphasizing power dynamics through subtle, suggestive details. 2st woman \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is dressed in an all-black ensemble comprising of a button-up shirt and slacks that highlight her slender but shapely figure. She is gently resting her left hand on the face of the 1st woman, applying soft pressure with her fingers while gazing intently into her eyes. This act of intimate interaction exudes a sense of dominance and submission. This photograph captures a provocative interaction between two women in a softly lit room. In the blurred background, there are two other women seated at a table, engaged in conversation and adding depth to the scene. The room has a light ivory hue which contrasts against their darker outfits, and the textures of the wall and floor are smooth, indicative of modern design elements. Overall, the photograph creates a provocative, intimate atmosphere, emphasizing power dynamics through subtle, suggestive details. \n\n---\n\nIn a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity. 1st woman on the right \"moimeme3\", young and shorter, has light skin with professional makeup on her face including smokey eyeshadow, mascara and eyeliner and blusher, her light brown hair is styled in a loose updo. Her head is tilted towards the camera and her eyebrows are raised as she looks sidelong at the woman on the left. off-the-shoulder bodice with flounced shoulders framing her décolletage, cinched at the waist beneath which cascades a voluminous skirt lined with multiple tiers of ruffled hemming that billow slightly outward where wind might have touched them—or perhaps they merely sway due to gravity's tender pull. Along this line, intricate embroidery adorns the front panel—the golden thread forming patterns reminiscent of vines entwined with blossoms—and one can almost hear their whispers as threads dance between loom and wearer. She holds herself upright yet relaxed, palms resting lightly over her abdomen near the belt buckle made visible only partially because of how tightly fitted the corsetry remains despite being worn without ornamentation beyond modest chains holding charms resembling leaves or stars. Her dark brown curls nestle atop her crown, coiled back neatly behind ears trimmed delicately with pearl studs nestled close beside lobes awaiting attention—but none drawn upon now. Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene—not quite dusk nor daybreak but somewhere sweetly suspended mid-sunrise. 2nd woman on the left \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is the taller, buxom woman, wears a gown of muted rose-pink linen, its fabric drape suggesting both grace and subtle weight; it flows like watercolor across her form, catching the ambient light with delicate sheen along creases that whisper movement even when stillness reigns. The dress features softly gathered ruffles at the square neckline and short puffed sleeves edged with fine lace-like frills—a touch of romantic innocence. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. Around her neck rests a slender gold chain bearing a small, ornate pendant shaped like a miniature flower, echoing the floral motifs hinted at nearby blooms. Her hair is styled into a loose yet elegant braid woven through braids above her ear, crowned with a single silver headband that catches the light just enough to shimmer against the earthy tones around her. In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity. Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene—not quite dusk nor daybreak but somewhere sweetly suspended mid-sunrise."],"color":"#432","bgcolor":"#653"},{"id":572,"type":"7b90b761-3eb6-4fea-a398-96d6a2b5e423","pos":[-1358.671667665309,-2009.76923644496],"size":[423.3077642837534,1224.5766109993865],"flags":{},"order":4,"mode":0,"inputs":[{"name":"seed","type":"INT","widget":{"name":"seed"},"link":7128},{"name":"guidance","type":"FLOAT","widget":{"name":"guidance"},"link":null},{"label":"LoRA-A","name":"lora_name","type":"COMBO","widget":{"name":"lora_name"},"link":null},{"label":"LA-strength_model","name":"strength_model","type":"FLOAT","widget":{"name":"strength_model"},"link":null},{"label":"LAstrength_clip","name":"strength_clip","type":"FLOAT","widget":{"name":"strength_clip"},"link":null},{"label":"LoRA B","name":"lora_name_1","type":"COMBO","widget":{"name":"lora_name_1"},"link":null},{"label":"LBstrength_model","name":"strength_model_1","type":"FLOAT","widget":{"name":"strength_model_1"},"link":null},{"label":"LBstrength_clip","name":"strength_clip_1","type":"FLOAT","widget":{"name":"strength_clip_1"},"link":null},{"label":"Setting","name":"value_1","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value_1"},"link":null},{"name":"value","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value"},"link":null},{"name":"value_2","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value_2"},"link":null},{"name":"value_3","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value_3"},"link":null}],"outputs":[{"label":"IMAGE","localized_name":"IMAGE","name":"IMAGE","type":"IMAGE","links":[7127,7132]},{"name":"STRING","type":"STRING","links":[7131]}],"properties":{"proxyWidgets":[["-1","seed"],["-1","guidance"],["-1","lora_name"],["-1","strength_model"],["-1","strength_clip"],["-1","lora_name_1"],["-1","strength_model_1"],["-1","strength_clip_1"],["-1","value_1"],["-1","value"],["-1","value_2"],["-1","value_3"]],"cnr_id":"comfy-core","ver":"0.3.51","ue_properties":{"version":"7.0.1","widget_ue_connectable":{"text":true,"steps":true,"sampler_name":true,"scheduler":true,"width":true,"height":true,"batch_size":true}}},"widgets_values":[603308628458591,3.9,"MoiMeme/moimeme3-04.safetensors",0.95,0.1,"celestesh-04.safetensors",0.95,0.1,"In name for S&R":"PrimitiveStringMultiline","ue_properties":{"widget_ue_connectable":{},"input_ue_unconnectable":{}}},"widgets_values":["In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity."],"color":"#232","bgcolor":"#353"},{"id":592,"type":"PrimitiveStringMultiline","pos":[-2040.5122420038772,-1563.615309915505],"size":[423.36625996542625,126.78571877499803],"flags":{},"order":18,"mode":0,"inputs":[{"localized_name":"value","name":"value","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value"},"link":7219}],"outputs":[{"localized_name":"STRING","name":"STRING","type":"STRING","links":[7235]}],"title":"1st Woman","properties":{"cnr_id":"comfy-core","ver":"0.3.66","Node name for S&R":"PrimitiveStringMultiline","ue_properties":{"widget_ue_connectable":{},"input_ue_unconnectable":{}}},"widgets_values":["1st woman on the right \"moimeme3\", young and shorter, has light skin with professional makeup on her face including smokey eyeshadow, mascara and eyeliner and blusher, her light brown hair is styled in a loose updo. Her head is tilted towards the camera and her eyebrows are raised as she looks sidelong at the woman on the left. She wears a dress with an off-the-shoulder bodice with flounced shoulders framing her décolletage, cinched at the waist beneath which cascades a voluminous skirt lined with multiple tiers of ruffled hemming that billow slightly outward. Along this line, intricate embroidery adorns the front panel. She holds herself upright because of how tightly fitted the corsetry remains. Her dark brown curls nestle atop her crown, coiled back neatly behind ears trimmed delicately with pearl studs nestled close beside lobes awaiting attention—but none drawn upon now."],"color":"#232","bgcolor":"#353"},{"id":599,"type":"PrimitiveStringMultiline","pos":[-2040.5122420038772,-1406.3931046165205],"size":[423.36625996542625,126.78571877499803],"flags":{},"order":25,"mode":0,"inputs":[{"localized_name":"value","name":"value","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value"},"link":7222}],"outputs":[{"localized_name":"STRING","name":"STRING","type":"STRING","links":[7231]}],"title":"2nd Woman","properties":{"cnr_id":"comfy-core","ver":"0.3.66","Node name for S&R":"PrimitiveStringMultiline","ue_properties":{"widget_ue_connectable":{},"input_ue_unconnectable":{}}},"widgets_values":["2nd woman on the left \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is the taller, buxom woman, wears a gown of muted rose-pink linen, its fabric drape suggesting both grace and subtle weight; it flows like watercolor across her form, catching the ambient light with delicate sheen along creases that whisper movement even when stillness reigns. The dress features softly gathered ruffles at the square neckline and short puffed sleeves edged with fine lace-like frills—a touch of romantic innocence. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. Around her neck rests a slender gold chain bearing a small, ornate pendant shaped like a miniature flower, echoing the floral motifs hinted at nearby blooms. Her hair is styled into a loose yet elegant braid woven through braids above her ear."],"color":"#232","bgcolor":"#353"},{"id":594,"type":"PrimitiveStringMultiline","pos":[-2040.5122420038772,-1248.628882617032],"size":[424.308699117365,173.90767637194074],"flags":{},"order":20,"mode":0,"inputs":[{"localized_name":"value","name":"value","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value"},"link":7223}],"outputs":[{"localized_name":"STRING","name":"STRING","type":"STRING","links":[7214,7226]}],"properties":{"cnr_id":"comfy-core","ver":"0.3.66","Node name for S&R":"PrimitiveStringMultiline","ue_properties":{"widget_ue_connectable":{},"input_ue_unconnectable":{}}},"widgets_values":["Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards ominously dark clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene."],"color":"#232","bgcolor":"#353"},{"id":595,"type":"StringConcatenate","pos":[-1590.9687665290412,-1714.5707073005278],"size":[210,166],"flags":{"collapsed":false},"order":21,"mode":0,"inputs":[{"localized_name":"string_a","name":"string_a","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"string_a"},"link":7236},{"localized_name":"string_b","name":"string_b","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"string_b"},"link":7235},{"localized_name":"delimiter","name":"delimiter","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"delimiter"},"link":null}],"outputs":[{"localized_name":"STRING","name":"STRING","type":"STRING","links":[7213]}],"properties":{"cnr_id":"comfy-core","ver":"0.3.66","Node tool:

The file contents provided above are truncated/partial snippets. The complete content for these files IS accessible via querying.

If the user asks a question related to these files, and the provided snippets do not clearly answer it, you MUST use the file_search tool to search the full document contents before responding.

How to invoke the file_search tool¶

If the user's query isn't sufficiently answered by the provided snippets, parsed text, or images, you should invoke file_search.msearch. To search the full content of the available files, you must send one message with recipient to=file_search.msearch in the analysis channel. Your message should be structured exactly as follows: 

{
  'queries': ['Example query 1', 'Example query 2']  // List of up to five queries to search for.
}

Carefully balance the number of file_search calls and the quality of your response with the latency incurred by file_search calls.

Important guidelines for query formatting: - Your message must be a valid JSON object. Do not include any extraneous text, backticks, markdown formatting, or comments beyond what is shown. - The queries field must be a list of strings. - One of the queries MUST exactly match the user's original question, but stripped of any extraneous details, and with ambiguous references clearly resolved using context from the conversation. It MUST be a complete sentence. - Even if you suspect the user meant something else, one query MUST still be their original question.

How to handle results from file_search¶

The file search tool will respond with the relevant search results from the full files. After you receive these results: - Carefully evaluate each result to determine its relevance and quality. - Use the high-quality and relevant results to inform your answer to the user's question. - When possible, reuse information from prior file_search results and avoid repeating identical queries.

Tool usage instructions and ordering¶

When the user asks a question, carefully consider whether the provided snippets sufficiently answer the query, whether you need to invoke file_search.msearch, or another available tool (such as web, if available) should be used instead.

Tool-ordering Guidelines: 1. For queries related to uploaded files, if snippets are insufficient, strongly prefer invoking file_search.msearch over other tools. - Avoid using web.run, python analysis, or any other tools until you have either: - Confirmed with high confidence the answer is not in the files, or - Have high confidence file_search_tool queries are not sufficient to answer the query. 2. Avoid usage of web_search/web.run for queries related to uploaded files - Carefully consider whether the user's query is better answered by web search results rather than available snippets or results from file_search_tool. - Unless the user's query is clearly unrelated to the uploaded files, prefer using file_search.msearch. You can always follow up with a web search if necessary for a more complete response. 3. Avoid using python unless necessary or requested by user. - If the user's query is answerable from the parsed text and images from the uploaded files, or from file_search_tool, do not invoke python.

Always keep this instruction in mind for the rest of the conversation, as the user may reference these files or ask related questions later on.

tool:

Make sure to include fileciteturn5file0 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link.

(rgthree)","pos":[-2165.0250053844575,-1872.5438902548437],"size":[379.5276923031678,1133.800428448723],"flags":{},"order":7,"mode":0,"inputs":[{"dir":3,"localized_name":"source","name":"source","type":"","link":7131}],"outputs":[],"properties":{"cnr_id":"rgthree-comfy","ver":"1.0.2510052058","Node name for S&R":"Display Any (rgthree)","ue_properties":{"widget_ue_connectable":{},"input_ue_unconnectable":{},"version":"7.3"}},"widgets_values":["In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity. 1st woman on the right \"moimeme3\", young and shorter, has light skin with professional makeup on her face including smokey eyeshadow, mascara and eyeliner and blusher, her light brown hair is styled in a loose updo. Her head is tilted towards the camera and her eyebrows are raised as she looks sidelong at the woman on the left. She wears a dress with an off-the-shoulder bodice with flounced shoulders framing her décolletage, cinched at the waist beneath which cascades a voluminous skirt lined with multiple tiers of ruffled hemming that billow slightly outward. Along this line, intricate embroidery adorns the front panel. She holds herself upright because of how tightly fitted the corsetry remains. Her dark brown curls nestle atop her crown, coiled back neatly behind ears trimmed delicately with pearl studs nestled close beside lobes awaiting attention—but none drawn upon now. Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards ominously dark clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene. 2nd woman on the left \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is the taller, buxom woman, wears a gown of muted rose-pink linen, its fabric drape suggesting both grace and subtle weight; it flows like watercolor across her form, catching the ambient light with delicate sheen along creases that whisper movement even when stillness reigns. The dress features softly gathered ruffles at the square neckline and short puffed sleeves edged with fine lace-like frills—a touch of romantic innocence. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. Around her neck rests a slender gold chain bearing a small, ornate pendant shaped like a miniature flower, echoing the floral motifs hinted at nearby blooms. Her hair is styled into a loose yet elegant braid woven through braids above her ear. In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity. Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. She also wears black ankle-strap heels, giving a sleek, professional vibe to her outfit. This photograph captures a provocative interaction between two women in a softly lit room. In the blurred background, there are two other women seated at a table, engaged in conversation and adding depth to the scene. The room has a light ivory hue which contrasts against their darker outfits, and the textures of the wall and floor are smooth, indicative of modern design elements. Overall, the photograph creates a provocative, intimate atmosphere, emphasizing power dynamics through subtle, suggestive details. 2st woman \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is dressed in an all-black ensemble comprising of a button-up shirt and slacks that highlight her slender but shapely figure. She is gently resting her left hand on the face of the 1st woman, applying soft pressure with her fingers while gazing intently into her eyes. This act of intimate interaction exudes a sense of dominance and submission. This photograph captures a provocative interaction between two women in a softly lit room. In the blurred background, there are two other women seated at a table, engaged in conversation and adding depth to the scene. The room has a light ivory hue which contrasts against their darker outfits, and the textures of the wall and floor are smooth, indicative of modern design elements. Overall, the photograph creates a provocative, intimate atmosphere, emphasizing power dynamics through subtle, suggestive details. \n\n---\n\nIn a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity. 1st woman on the right \"moimeme3\", young and shorter, has light skin with professional makeup on her face including smokey eyeshadow, mascara and eyeliner and blusher, her light brown hair is styled in a loose updo. Her head is tilted towards the camera and her eyebrows are raised as she looks sidelong at the woman on the left. off-the-shoulder bodice with flounced shoulders framing her décolletage, cinched at the waist beneath which cascades a voluminous skirt lined with multiple tiers of ruffled hemming that billow slightly outward where wind might have touched them—or perhaps they merely sway due to gravity's tender pull. Along this line, intricate embroidery adorns the front panel—the golden thread forming patterns reminiscent of vines entwined with blossoms—and one can almost hear their whispers as threads dance between loom and wearer. She holds herself upright yet relaxed, palms resting lightly over her abdomen near the belt buckle made visible only partially because of how tightly fitted the corsetry remains despite being worn without ornamentation beyond modest chains holding charms resembling leaves or stars. Her dark brown curls nestle atop her crown, coiled back neatly behind ears trimmed delicately with pearl studs nestled close beside lobes awaiting attention—but none drawn upon now. Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene—not quite dusk nor daybreak but somewhere sweetly suspended mid-sunrise. 2nd woman on the left \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is the taller, buxom woman, wears a gown of muted rose-pink linen, its fabric drape suggesting both grace and subtle weight; it flows like watercolor across her form, catching the ambient light with delicate sheen along creases that whisper movement even when stillness reigns. The dress features softly gathered ruffles at the square neckline and short puffed sleeves edged with fine lace-like frills—a touch of romantic innocence. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. Around her neck rests a slender gold chain bearing a small, ornate pendant shaped like a miniature flower, echoing the floral motifs hinted at nearby blooms. Her hair is styled into a loose yet elegant braid woven through braids above her ear, crowned with a single silver headband that catches the light just enough to shimmer against the earthy tones around her. tool:

The file contents provided above are truncated/partial snippets. The complete content for these files IS accessible via querying.

If the user asks a question related to these files, and the provided snippets do not clearly answer it, you MUST use the file_search tool to search the full document contents before responding.

How to invoke the file_search tool¶

If the user's query isn't sufficiently answered by the provided snippets, parsed text, or images, you should invoke file_search.msearch. To search the full content of the available files, you must send one message with recipient to=file_search.msearch in the analysis channel. Your message should be structured exactly as follows: 

{
  'queries': ['Example query 1', 'Example query 2']  // List of up to five queries to search for.
}

Carefully balance the number of file_search calls and the quality of your response with the latency incurred by file_search calls.

Important guidelines for query formatting: - Your message must be a valid JSON object. Do not include any extraneous text, backticks, markdown formatting, or comments beyond what is shown. - The queries field must be a list of strings. - One of the queries MUST exactly match the user's original question, but stripped of any extraneous details, and with ambiguous references clearly resolved using context from the conversation. It MUST be a complete sentence. - Even if you suspect the user meant something else, one query MUST still be their original question.

How to handle results from file_search¶

The file search tool will respond with the relevant search results from the full files. After you receive these results: - Carefully evaluate each result to determine its relevance and quality. - Use the high-quality and relevant results to inform your answer to the user's question. - When possible, reuse information from prior file_search results and avoid repeating identical queries.

Tool usage instructions and ordering¶

When the user asks a question, carefully consider whether the provided snippets sufficiently answer the query, whether you need to invoke file_search.msearch, or another available tool (such as web, if available) should be used instead.

Tool-ordering Guidelines: 1. For queries related to uploaded files, if snippets are insufficient, strongly prefer invoking file_search.msearch over other tools. - Avoid using web.run, python analysis, or any other tools until you have either: - Confirmed with high confidence the answer is not in the files, or - Have high confidence file_search_tool queries are not sufficient to answer the query. 2. Avoid usage of web_search/web.run for queries related to uploaded files - Carefully consider whether the user's query is better answered by web search results rather than available snippets or results from file_search_tool. - Unless the user's query is clearly unrelated to the uploaded files, prefer using file_search.msearch. You can always follow up with a web search if necessary for a more complete response. 3. Avoid using python unless necessary or requested by user. - If the user's query is answerable from the parsed text and images from the uploaded files, or from file_search_tool, do not invoke python.

Always keep this instruction in mind for the rest of the conversation, as the user may reference these files or ask related questions later on.

tool:

Make sure to include fileciteturn7file0 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link.
