[L2] The file is too long and its contents have been truncated. [L3]

Emily:

Ahhh — your “this shouldn’t work… but it did” mystery is actually explained by what’s in the workflow you uploaded.

What’s really happening in your JSON¶

You currently have two different SRPO model loaders sitting in the graph:

1) UnetLoaderGGUF loading gguf/SRPO-Q8_0.gguf (collapsed node) fileciteturn9file0L1-L1
2) UNETLoader loading SRPO/flux.1-dev-SRPO-BFL-bf16.safetensors (the “normal” PyTorch/safetensors UNet) fileciteturn9file4L1-L1

But here’s the punchline:

Your actual generation path is using the UNETLoader (safetensors) output, not the GGUF model.¶

The workflow’s output node is wired to link 7173, which comes from UNETLoader (id 443). fileciteturn9file4L1-L1
So the GGUF loader can be present and “look like it’s in use”… while doing absolutely nothing.

That’s why you got a valid image and thought, “Wait, SRPO works after all?” — because the SRPO you used here is the safetensors SRPO, which behaves like Flux-Dev in all the ways your Hook-LoRA setup expects.

Why your earlier “SRPO breaks Hook LoRAs” experience can still be true¶

If your earlier tests were done with SRPO as GGUF, then your original hypothesis can still be correct in practice:

  • Hook LoRAs want to patch a Torch UNet (the safetensors model object).
  • GGUF UNet objects often can’t be patched the same way, so “model-strength LoRA” either fails, silently does nothing, or causes weird degradation.

And in your graph, your hooks are very model-heavy (e.g. strength_model: 0.95, strength_clip: 0.1). fileciteturn9file2L1-L1
That’s exactly the kind of setup that will look amazing on a safetensors UNet and look “broken” on a GGUF UNet.

The quick fix: make the graph unambiguous¶

Do this and you’ll stop chasing ghosts:

Option A (recommended): stick to safetensors SRPO for Hook LoRAs¶

  • Delete or disconnect the UnetLoaderGGUF node.
  • Keep UNETLoader → ModelSamplingFlux → SamplerCustomAdvanced etc.

Option B: if you must use SRPO GGUF¶

Then assume: - model-strength LoRA won’t behave normally - You’ll likely need either: - a GGUF-compatible LoRA mechanism (if your GGUF node set supports it), or - run the identity via CLIP-heavy prompting/conditioning instead (test with strength_model = 0, strength_clip = 1 as a diagnostic).

How to confirm what model is actually being used (no guessing)¶

Pick one:

  • Temporarily disconnect the UNETLoader and connect the UnetLoaderGGUF into the same model input. If it truly swaps, you’ll see behavior change immediately.
  • Or change something that forces recompute (seed, steps), because ComfyUI caching can sometimes make it feel like a swap “worked” when you’re just seeing a cached branch.

If you paste the exact error text, I’ll tell you precisely which case it is¶

But even without it: your uploaded workflow already explains the contradiction. Your successful run wasn’t “SRPO GGUF + hooks”; it was “SRPO safetensors + hooks.”

If you want, tell me which SRPO you intended to test (GGUF Q8 vs BFL bf16 safetensors), and I’ll suggest the cleanest wiring pattern for that choice (including a quick “A/B toggle” so you can flip models without rewiring everything).

Emily:

Oh wow — and yes, this does “blow the hypothesis out of the water,” but in a very explainable way once you look at what your workflow is actually doing.

✅ The short answer¶

Your workflow works with SRPO because SRPO is still a Flux-architecture model (a fine-tune of Flux.1-Dev), and your LoRA Hook pipeline is operating in a way that doesn’t depend on “Flux.1-Dev” specifically — it depends on:

  • Flux-type UNet structure
  • Flux-compatible text encoder setup (T5 + CLIP-L)
  • A LoRA trained on Flux-layer names (which SRPO still has)

So: yes, SRPO ain’t “Dev”… but it is still Flux enough that all your hooks still “attach.”

That’s why your expectation (“this shouldn’t work”) is the wrong expectation — your workflow is not model-fragile, it’s architecture-fragile, and SRPO didn’t break the architecture.

🔍 What your workflow reveals (the key “gotcha”)¶

1) You are not actually sampling SRPO-GGUF in this workflow¶

This is the big surprise:

Inside your Models subgraph you have two model loaders:

  • UnetLoaderGGUF loading gguf/SRPO-Q8_0.gguf
  • UNETLoader loading SRPO/flux.1-dev-SRPO-BFL-bf16.safetensors

…and only one is connected to the subgraph outputs.

✅ The model that is actually wired into the workflow output is:

UNETLoader → SRPO bf16 safetensors

The GGUF loader is sitting there… but it’s not what the graph is using.

So if your earlier error happened when you swapped to SRPO, I’d bet hard money it was when you were trying to use the GGUF version. This workflow isn’t using it at all — which is why you’re not seeing the failure.

2) Your LoRA hook system is applied to the clip objects cleanly¶

Your pipeline is doing:

  • CreateHookLora (x2)
  • SetClipHooks (x2)
  • each hook produces a separate CLIP object
  • those CLIP objects feed separate CLIPTextEncode nodes

That is exactly what you want for “two-character consistency” — because each character’s prompt is embedded using a differently hooked encoder.

That’s why your per-character separation is behaving.

3) Your separation is being done through conditioning + mask, not through “special SRPO behaviour”¶

Your Masking subgraph builds a gradient mask (with blur + shrink), and then the workflow uses:

PairConditioningSetPropertiesAndCombine

That node is doing the “left-half / right-half” compositing at the conditioning level.

So again: it doesn’t care whether the model is Dev or SRPO — it cares whether the model understands Flux conditioning (and SRPO does).

✅ So why did you ever get errors?¶

You haven’t shown the actual error text here — but based on the workflow, there are two very plausible causes:

A) The error happened only when using SRPO-GGUF¶

GGUF Flux models are supported, but not every LoRA / hook / patch pipeline behaves identically under quantized UNet weights.

That would explain:

  • “Works with Flux1-Dev”
  • “Fails with SRPO”
  • “But now it works” (because you’re actually using SRPO-bf16 safetensors, not GGUF)

B) The error happened due to a mismatched encoder setup in earlier attempts¶

Flux models require the correct encoders: - T5-XXL and CLIP-L with “flux” type selection.

Your workflow is correct here: - DualCLIPLoader includes the T5 + CLIP-L and is explicitly “flux”.

If earlier you had “SDXL” encoders active, or mixed clips, the hooks could throw tantrums.

✅ Why does this image look good even though you expected failure?¶

Because SRPO is a stylistic finetune, not a structural rewrite. It will happily accept:

  • conditioning masks
  • split-character conditioning
  • flux guidance
  • flux sampling nodes
  • LoRA hooks

…and produce coherent results.

In other words:

Your workflow is doing everything at the correct abstraction level.

✅ What I need from you to solve the actual mystery completely¶

If you want me to tell you exactly what broke in the earlier run, I need one of these:

1) Paste the exact error log text you got when swapping to SRPO
2) Tell me: were you swapping to SRPO-GGUF or SRPO safetensors when it broke?

Because right now, the workflow you shared is not reproducing the failure condition — it’s quietly dodging it by sampling the safetensors SRPO.

🌟 Practical next step (high leverage)¶

If you want to actually test your original hypothesis properly:

✅ In your Models subgraph, temporarily: - Disconnect the UNETLoader (bf16 safetensors) - Connect the UnetLoaderGGUF output to the subgraph output instead

Then run the same pipeline.

If it breaks again: you’ve confirmed the failure is GGUF-specific.
If it still works: then the earlier error must have been encoder or node mismatch.

If you paste the error text (even a screenshot is fine), I’ll tell you exactly what part of the pipeline is incompatible and what the cleanest workaround is — without guesswork.

Robyn:

Here is the error message I submitted to Comfy.org (to which I have not, as yet, received any more than a boilerplate response):

I am using a workflow to allow 2 persistent characters in a scene. This was in a SRPO-fp8_e4m3fn workflow using (instead of standard LoRA loaders) Create Hook LoRA and Set CLIP Hooks. This workflow was working perfectly until a few days ago when it would fail with the error: "'Linear' object has no attribute 'weight_scale'" with the highlight pausing on a CLIP Text Encode node. Happy to upload terminal output as well. I have presented the problem on your discord channel under #help but they were unable to resolve it due to lack of familiarity with the nodes in question.

Emily:

Yep — that is interesting, and it actually makes your original “SRPO isn’t quite Flux” instinct half-right and half-misleading.

What that error usually means¶

'Linear' object has no attribute 'weight_scale'

That message is almost always a mismatch between what a hook/patch node expects a layer to look like and what the model loader actually gave it.

Those Hook LoRA / CLIP Hook nodes typically “reach inside” the text encoder and touch layer attributes that only exist when the encoder is wrapped in a specific quant/patch wrapper (FP8/INT8-ish wrappers often carry extra tensors like weight_scale, scales, etc).

If the node expects “FP8-wrapped Linear (with weight_scale)” but it encounters a plain torch.nn.Linear, it dies exactly like this.

So the failure isn’t “CLIP Text Encode is broken” — it’s “something upstream changed what CLIP’s Linear layers are.”

Why it can seem random (your “blows my hypothesis out of the water” moment)¶

You can get the same workflow to sometimes work and sometimes fail because one of these changed between runs:

  1. Which exact SRPO checkpoint variant you loaded
    Many people have multiple SRPO files (fp16 vs fp8_e4m3fn etc). One may load with wrappers that include weight_scale, another may not.

  2. Which text encoder path got used
    Some workflows silently swap between different text encoders / loaders depending on availability (or cached objects). If you “replaced the model” but the graph still used a cached CLIP object from a previous run, the run can succeed once… then fail after a restart.

  3. ComfyUI update changed the wrapping behavior
    If ComfyUI (or a related backend) changed how it instantiates FP8 layers, the hook node may now see a plain Linear where it previously saw a wrapped Linear.

  4. The Hook nodes updated (or didn’t), while ComfyUI did
    This is the classic: ComfyUI internals change → custom node that monkey-patches layers now expects attributes that no longer exist.

The fastest way to confirm what’s happening¶

Do this in your ComfyUI folder:

A) Find who is referencing weight_scale 

cd ComfyUI
grep -R "weight_scale" -n custom_nodes
That will point to the exact custom node file that’s exploding.

B) Then check what it assumes You’ll likely see something like: - module.weight_scale
- or a loop that assumes every Linear has that attribute

If you see it, you’ve basically proven the diagnosis: the hook node is coded for a wrapped Linear class, but your current SRPO/CLIP stack is giving it a plain one.

Practical fixes (in the order I’d try them)¶

1) Update the Hook nodes repo (not just ComfyUI)¶

If you installed via Manager, still go to the node folder and do a pull: 

cd ComfyUI/custom_nodes/<THE_HOOK_REPO_FOLDER>
git pull
Restart ComfyUI.

If it already pulled, you may be on a commit that isn’t compatible with your ComfyUI version.

2) Try SRPO fp16 (or any non-fp8 variant) as a control test¶

If you switch from SRPO-fp8_e4m3fn to an fp16 SRPO and the error vanishes, that’s very strong evidence that: - the hook node expects FP8 wrapper attributes in one place, or - FP8 wrapping changed and removed/renamed weight_scale.

3) Try swapping the text encoder node (even if the diffusion model stays SRPO)¶

If your workflow has multiple text-encode options (Flux-specific encoders, “standard” CLIP encoders, etc), test the other one.
The hook node might only support one path.

4) If you want a “make it work today” workaround: don’t hook CLIP — regional-condition instead¶

If your goal is two consistent people left/right, you can avoid CLIP-hooks entirely by doing:

  • Branch A: Load model+clip → apply LoRA A → encode prompt A
  • Branch B: Load model+clip → apply LoRA B → encode prompt B
  • Set area/region for each conditioning (left half vs right half)
  • Combine conditionings → feed sampler

This tends to be more stable across model variants because you aren’t touching encoder internals; you’re just combining conditioning objects.

(If you tell me the exact node names you’re using for regions in Flux/SRPO — ConditioningSetArea, Regional Conditioning, etc — I can sketch the cleanest graph layout you can copy.)

5) “Surgical” code fix (only if you’re comfortable editing)¶

Once you locate the weight_scale line, the robust fix is usually a guard like: - “if attribute exists, use it; otherwise skip” That’s often enough to keep the hook system from crashing on plain Linear modules.
(I won’t paste a code patch blind without seeing the file snippet, but your grep result will make it obvious.)

Why the highlight pauses on CLIP Text Encode¶

That node is simply where the graph first executes the encoder forward pass, so it’s where the incompatible layer gets touched. The cause is almost always earlier: model/text-encoder wrapping + hook patching.

If you paste the single grep hit (the file path + the 10–20 lines around where weight_scale is referenced), I can tell you which of the fixes above is most likely to stick and whether this is a “ComfyUI changed” problem or a “hook node changed” problem.

Robyn:

There are 2 modules that seem to be affected, Emily: comfyui-easy-use and RES4LYF. The latter didn't even exist on my system when the errors surfaced, so it would be the comfyui-easy-use set. It's the /py/modules/kolors/chatglm/quantization.py that has self.weight_scale entries in the code... there are 5 instances of that.

I'm beginning to see why I could use a workflow that ostensibly had certain models loaded... but might actually, quietly using previously loaded items still in memory. Here's the thing: I have 3 models for SRPO: - SRPO-fp8_e4m3fn - flux.1-dev-SRPO-BFL-bf16 - flux.1-dev-SRPO-bf16

...all safetensors. When I started today, I'm pretty sure it was the flux.1-dev-SRPO-BFL-bf16 that I was using. I'm now using the SRPO-fp8_e4m3fn, but this is what failed before (based on that report I sent). So, the weight_type is fp8_e4m3fn specifically, clip_name1 is t5xxl_fp8_e4m3fn and clip_name2 is ViT-L-14-TEXT-detail-improved-hiT-GmP-HF, with type as flux and device as default. vae is ae.safetensors. I might save this workflow and try to load it in the morning and see if it fails.

Emily:

Ahhh—this is excellent sleuthing, and it narrows the problem a lot.

1) Why your “it’s silently using old stuff” theory is plausible¶

ComfyUI does cache model objects in-process (checkpoints, CLIP/T5, VAE). On top of that, a lot of custom nodes (and especially hook/patch systems) mutate those objects in-place.

So you can absolutely get situations where:

  • you “change a model” in a loader node, but
  • the loader hands you a cached object for that filename/dtype combo, or
  • you are still using a previously patched encoder object because the graph reuses the same CLIP/T5 object reference, or
  • a hook node patched something globally and it persists until restart

That’s why “it worked today even though it shouldn’t” can happen.

Rule of thumb: any time you’re debugging a hook/quantization issue, treat each test as “clean-room”: restart ComfyUI between runs.

2) The kolors/chatglm/quantization.py “weight_scale” hits are a huge clue¶

You’ve basically proven that the crash is coming from Kolors/ChatGLM quantization code that lives inside comfyui-easy-use, not from SRPO itself.

That means the failure is likely one of these:

A) Something in your workflow is (maybe unintentionally) invoking a Kolors/ChatGLM text path¶

Even if you think you’re in Flux/SRPO-land, a single node from easy-use that wraps “text encode”, “prompt”, “LLM”, “caption”, “prompt enhancer”, etc. can route through ChatGLM code (Kolors uses ChatGLM for text).

B) comfyui-easy-use is doing a monkey-patch / registration that affects encode behavior¶

Some packs register alternate encoders or replace internal CLIP/T5 handling. When that goes wrong, you end up with code assuming a quantized layer class exists, but it’s actually just a vanilla torch.nn.Linear (hence: no weight_scale).

So: you’re not crazy—this points to a custom-node side effect, not you “misloading SRPO”.

3) The specific weight_scale error signature¶

When you see:

'Linear' object has no attribute 'weight_scale'

it means code expected “quantized Linear with extra attributes” but got plain Linear.

That can happen when: - the quant wrapper didn’t run, - the wrapper ran but returned vanilla modules, - or an upstream update changed the class being used.

4) What I would do next (quick and decisive)¶

Step 1 — isolate the culprit by temporarily removing comfyui-easy-use¶

Don’t uninstall yet—just disable it:

  • Rename the folder:
  • ComfyUI/custom_nodes/comfyui-easy-use → comfyui-easy-use__DISABLED
  • Restart ComfyUI
  • Run the same SRPO fp8 workflow again.

If the error vanishes: ✅ you’ve confirmed it’s easy-use / Kolors ChatGLM quantization path.

If it still happens: then the reference to kolors/chatglm/... may be coming from another custom node, but from what you’ve said, easy-use is the prime suspect.

Step 2 — if you need comfyui-easy-use, update or pin it¶

If disabling fixes it, then: - update easy-use (git pull / Manager update), - and if that doesn’t fix, consider rolling back to the last known good commit.

(These kinds of breakages are very often “custom node expects old ComfyUI internals” or vice versa.)

Step 3 — verify your workflow isn’t accidentally calling Kolors ChatGLM nodes¶

Even with easy-use enabled, you can avoid the crash if you simply don’t invoke that stack.

Things to look for in the workflow: - any node labelled “Kolors” - any node that mentions “GLM”, “ChatGLM” - “prompt generator”, “LLM prompt”, “enhancer”, “caption → prompt”, “smart prompt” - any “easy-use” wrapper around text encoding

If you want, paste the JSON node list just for the text/prompt/encode section and I’ll tell you exactly which node is the Trojan horse.

5) Your SRPO model trio and why fp8 makes this more fragile¶

What you’re running now is the “most brittle” configuration:

  • SRPO checkpoint: SRPO-fp8_e4m3fn
  • clip1: t5xxl_fp8_e4m3fn (quantized)
  • clip2: ViT-L-14 (usually fine)

That’s fine when everything in the pipeline understands FP8-wrapped layers. But if a custom node’s quantization code gets involved and expects its own FP8 wrapper shape (with weight_scale), it can choke.

So as a sanity check later, also try:

  • flux.1-dev-SRPO-bf16 + non-fp8 T5 (if you have it)
  • or even keep SRPO fp8 but swap to a non-fp8 T5 (if available)

Not as “the solution,” but as a diagnostic to confirm it’s an FP8-wrapper expectation mismatch.

6) A clean “morning test plan” that will actually answer it¶

1) Restart ComfyUI 2) Run minimal workflow: - Load SRPO model + its encoders - Plain text encode - No hooks, no easy-use helper nodes 3) If that works, restart again 4) Add Hook nodes 5) If that works, restart again 6) Add back easy-use nodes one at a time (especially anything prompt-related)

You’ll know exactly which node introduces the ChatGLM quant path.

If you want the fastest pinpoint: tell me whether disabling comfyui-easy-use fixes the crash. That single fact will basically solve the mystery.

And if you’d like, paste the first ~30 lines around one of those self.weight_scale references (not all 5) plus the function name—because I can tell you whether it’s “bad assumption in code” (needs an hasattr guard) or “wrong module is being invoked” (workflow/node issue).

Robyn:

I will try that tomorrow morning, time permitting. I'm barista for the totally mad 6 'Arty-Farty- ladies coming tomorrow, so I might get swept up in that.

Emily:

That sounds… delightfully chaotic. 😄

Two things you can do that take seconds—so even if you get commandeered into espresso duty, you’ll still make progress:

Quick “before the café madness” checklist (2 minutes)¶

1) Duplicate your ComfyUI folder (or at least custom_nodes/), so you can revert instantly if you go scorched-earth on nodes. 2) Write down the exact working combo you saw today: - checkpoint filename - clip1 / clip2 filenames - whether you’d already loaded Flux.1-Dev earlier in the session
(This matters because of the “sticky cached objects” phenomenon.)

The fastest single diagnostic (the one I’d bet on)¶

When you get a moment:

  • Rename: custom_nodes/comfyui-easy-use → comfyui-easy-use__DISABLED
  • Restart ComfyUI
  • Run the SRPO workflow once

If it works: you’ve basically nailed the culprit and you can enjoy your arty-farty brunch in peace.

If you only have time for one extra thing¶

Open the workflow and look for anything even vaguely like: - Kolors / ChatGLM / GLM - “prompt enhancer” - “LLM” - any “easy-use” text encode wrapper

Because if one of those nodes is present, it can quietly yank you into that ChatGLM quantization path even though you think you’re in Flux/SRPO land.

Go be the benevolent caffeine witch tomorrow. If you get a spare minute later, drop me: - whether disabling easy-use fixed it, and - the exact model + clip filenames you used in the failing run

…and I’ll tell you the most likely permanent fix (update/pin/replace node, or simply avoid a particular wrapper node).

Robyn:

Just had a look at the workflow: nothing Kolor - ChatGLM - GLM pops out at me. I'll do all that with easy-use, but I would like to discuss prompt-editing a bit with you, specifically tweaking the rather verbose floral stuff QwenVL puts out. Look, the images are exquisite BUT difficult to control. So, here's a prompt:

In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity. 1st woman on the right "moimeme3", young and shorter, has light skin with professional makeup on her face including smokey eyeshadow, mascara and eyeliner and blusher, her light brown hair is styled in a loose updo. Her head is tilted towards the camera and her eyebrows are raised as she looks sidelong at the woman on the left. She wears a dress with an off-the-shoulder bodice with flounced shoulders framing her décolletage, cinched at the waist beneath which cascades a voluminous skirt lined with multiple tiers of ruffled hemming that billow slightly outward. Along this line, intricate embroidery adorns the front panel. She holds herself upright because of how tightly fitted the corsetry remains. Her dark brown curls nestle atop her crown, coiled back neatly behind ears trimmed delicately with pearl studs nestled close beside lobes awaiting attention—but none drawn upon now. Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards ominously dark clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene. 2nd woman on the left "celestesh", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is the taller, buxom woman, wears a gown of muted rose-pink linen, its fabric drape suggesting both grace and subtle weight; it flows like watercolor across her form, catching the ambient light with delicate sheen along creases that whisper movement even when stillness reigns. The dress features softly gathered ruffles at the square neckline and short puffed sleeves edged with fine lace-like frills—a touch of romantic innocence. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. Around her neck rests a slender gold chain bearing a small, ornate pendant shaped like a miniature flower, echoing the floral motifs hinted at nearby blooms. Her hair is styled into a loose yet elegant braid woven through braids above her ear. In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity. Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards ominously dark clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene.

A bit full-on.

Emily:

Yep — that’s very QwenVL: it’s trying to be a novelist and a cinematographer and an art director… and your poor conditioner is sitting there like: “mate, which bit is the instruction?”

Here’s how I’d tame it so you keep the exquisite but regain control.

What’s going wrong (in practical terms)¶

1) It repeats itself¶

You’ve got the whole “hushed verdant garden…” block twice, plus duplicated environment details. Repetition in these models often acts like weighting (even when you didn’t mean it to), so background + mood can overpower your actual priorities (faces, pose, wardrobe).

2) It mixes “requirements” with “poetry”¶

Phrases like “nature’s curtain separating worlds” don’t help the model. They add semantic noise and “style drift.”

3) It stuffs too many low-value micro-details¶

“Moss-stained stone slabs,” “petals fallen silently,” “ominously dark clouds”… these are fine if you’re generating a background plate, but they compete with:
- two women - their relative heights - exact dresses - expressions / gaze - garden composition

4) It contradicts itself a bit¶

Right woman is described as “shorter” but then there’s a stray “dark brown curls” line that reads like it belongs to the other character. Also “professional makeup” + “smokey eye” is strong style steering; if you don’t need it, it will pull results.

The cleanest approach: “Hard constraints” + “Nice flavor”¶

I do this in two passes:

Pass A — Hard constraints (the stuff you must get)¶

Short, direct, non-poetic. No metaphors. No duplicate environment.

Pass B — Optional flavor (only if needed)¶

A small amount of mood/atmosphere, placed at the end.

That’s it.

A trimmed, high-control prompt (based on yours)¶

Version 1: Tight + obedient¶

Prompt

Two young women standing close together in a garden, facing each other, waist-up to full-body.
Right woman: moimeme3, shorter, light skin, light brown hair in a loose updo, subtle smokey eye makeup, eyebrows slightly raised, looking sideways toward the other woman, head slightly tilted toward camera.
Outfit (right): off-the-shoulder corset bodice with flounced sleeves, fitted waist, dark brown dress with embroidered front panel, voluminous skirt with tiered ruffled hem. Pearl stud earrings.
Left woman: celestesh, taller and buxom, medium skin, long brown hair worn down with a simple braid detail, calm expression, looking at the woman on the right.
Outfit (left): muted rose-pink linen gown, square neckline, short puff sleeves with small frill, small buttons down the bodice, delicate pendant necklace.
Background: green hedges and a garden path, soft diffused daylight, shallow depth of field, cinematic realism.

Why it works: it’s basically a casting brief + wardrobe brief.

Version 2: Still controlled, but with a hint of Qwen¶

Prompt

Two young women in a quiet garden, close together, facing one another as if mid-conversation. Soft diffused daylight, gentle cinematic mood, shallow depth of field.
Right: moimeme3, shorter, light skin, light brown hair in a loose updo, subtle smokey eye makeup, raised eyebrows, sidelong glance toward the woman on the left, head slightly tilted toward camera.
Right outfit: off-the-shoulder fitted corset dress, flounced sleeves, embroidered front panel, dark brown fabric, full skirt with tiered ruffled hem, pearl stud earrings.
Left: celestesh, taller, medium skin, long brown hair with a loose braid detail, composed expression, looking at the woman on the right.
Left outfit: muted rose-pink linen gown, square neckline, short puff sleeves with fine frill, small buttons down the bodice, delicate gold pendant necklace.
Background: tall green hedge, manicured lawn, a few roses to one side, distant trees softly blurred.

If you’re using Flux/SRPO with the dual encoders (CLIP + T5)¶

This is a really good trick:

Put this in CLIP (keywords / constraints)¶

  • moimeme3, celestesh
  • two women, standing close, facing each other
  • shorter on right, taller on left
  • dark brown off-shoulder corset dress, ruffled hem, embroidery
  • rose-pink linen dress, square neckline, puff sleeves, buttons
  • garden hedge, soft daylight, shallow depth of field

Put this in T5 (a little flavor, but not a novel)¶

  • “quiet garden, mid-conversation, gentle cinematic mood, soft diffused daylight”

If you put all the poetry into CLIP, it often starts “arguing” with your literal clothing constraints.

A dead-simple pruning rule (works every time)¶

Take the QwenVL prompt and delete anything that is: - metaphor (nature’s curtain, sky’s mercy, whisper movement)
- duplicated
- not visible in one glance
- not part of your “must have” list

You’ll usually cut 70% and gain control without losing quality.

Quick question that changes the whole strategy (but you don’t need to answer now)¶

Do you care more about: 1) the dresses being exact, or
2) the faces/identity being exact?

Because if (1), we tighten wardrobe terms further and reduce makeup/atmosphere.
If (2), we keep wardrobe simpler and let the LoRAs do more heavy lifting.

If you want, paste your current CLIP text and T5 text (just those two fields) and I’ll rewrite them into an “obedient pair” that still keeps the vibe.

Robyn:

A few things: in the attached workflow, I can only see entry points for CLIP. Where would T5 text be entered?

Her head is tilted towards the camera and her eyebrows are raised as she looks sidelong at the woman on the left. off-the-shoulder bodice with flounced shoulders framing her décolletage, cinched at the waist beneath which cascades a voluminous skirt lined with multiple tiers of ruffled hemming that billow slightly outward where wind might have touched them—or perhaps they merely sway due to gravity's tender pull. Along this line, intricate embroidery adorns the front panel—the golden thread forming patterns reminiscent of vines entwined with blossoms—and one can almost hear their whispers as threads dance between loom and wearer. She holds herself upright yet relaxed, palms resting lightly over her abdomen near the belt buckle made visible only partially because of how tightly fitted the corsetry remains despite being worn without ornamentation beyond modest chains holding charms resembling leaves or stars. Her dark brown curls nestle atop her crown, coiled back neatly behind ears trimmed delicately with pearl studs nestled close beside lobes awaiting attention—but none drawn upon now. Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene—not quite dusk nor daybreak but somewhere sweetly suspended mid-sunrise. 2nd woman on the left \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is the taller, buxom woman, wears a gown of muted rose-pink linen, its fabric drape suggesting both grace and subtle weight; it flows like watercolor across her form, catching the ambient light with delicate sheen along creases that whisper movement even when stillness reigns. The dress features softly gathered ruffles at the square neckline and short puffed sleeves edged with fine lace-like frills—a touch of romantic innocence. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. Around her neck rests a slender gold chain bearing a small, ornate pendant shaped like a miniature flower, echoing the floral motifs hinted at nearby blooms. Her hair is styled into a loose yet elegant braid woven through braids above her ear, crowned with a single silver headband that catches the light just enough to shimmer against the earthy tones around her. In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity. Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene—not quite dusk nor daybreak but somewhere sweetly suspended mid-sunrise."],"color":"#432","bgcolor":"#653"},{"id":572,"type":"7b90b761-3eb6-4fea-a398-96d6a2b5e423","pos":[-1358.671667665309,-2009.76923644496],"size":[423.3077642837534,1224.5766109993865],"flags":{},"order":4,"mode":0,"inputs":[{"name":"seed","type":"INT","widget":{"name":"seed"},"link":7128},{"name":"guidance","type":"FLOAT","widget":{"name":"guidance"},"link":null},{"label":"LoRA-A","name":"lora_name","type":"COMBO","widget":{"name":"lora_name"},"link":null},{"label":"LA-strength_model","name":"strength_model","type":"FLOAT","widget":{"name":"strength_model"},"link":null},{"label":"LAstrength_clip","name":"strength_clip","type":"FLOAT","widget":{"name":"strength_clip"},"link":null},{"label":"LoRA B","name":"lora_name_1","type":"COMBO","widget":{"name":"lora_name_1"},"link":null},{"label":"LBstrength_model","name":"strength_model_1","type":"FLOAT","widget":{"name":"strength_model_1"},"link":null},{"label":"LBstrength_clip","name":"strength_clip_1","type":"FLOAT","widget":{"name":"strength_clip_1"},"link":null},{"label":"Setting","name":"value_1","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value_1"},"link":null},{"name":"value","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value"},"link":null},{"name":"value_2","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value_2"},"link":null},{"name":"value_3","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value_3"},"link":null}],"outputs":[{"label":"IMAGE","localized_name":"IMAGE","name":"IMAGE","type":"IMAGE","links":[7127,7132]},{"name":"STRING","type":"STRING","links":[7131]}],"properties":{"proxyWidgets":[["-1","seed"],["-1","guidance"],["-1","lora_name"],["-1","strength_model"],["-1","strength_clip"],["-1","lora_name_1"],["-1","strength_model_1"],["-1","strength_clip_1"],["-1","value_1"],["-1","value"],["-1","value_2"],["-1","value_3"]],"cnr_id":"comfy-core","ver":"0.3.51","ue_properties":{"version":"7.0.1","widget_ue_connectable":{"text":true,"steps":true,"sampler_name":true,"scheduler":true,"width":true,"height":true,"batch_size":true}}},"widgets_values":[603308628458591,3.9,"MoiMeme/moimeme3-04.safetensors",0.95,0.1,"celestesh-04.safetensors",0.95,0.1,"In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity.","1st woman on the right \"moimeme3\", young and shorter, has light skin with professional makeup on her face including smokey eyeshadow, mascara and eyeliner and blusher, her light brown hair is styled in a loose updo. Her head is tilted towards the camera and her eyebrows are raised as she looks sidelong at the woman on the left. She wears a dress with an off-the-shoulder bodice with flounced shoulders framing her décolletage, cinched at the waist beneath which cascades a voluminous skirt lined with multiple tiers of ruffled hemming that billow slightly outward. Along this line, intricate embroidery adorns the front panel. She holds herself upright because of how tightly fitted the corsetry remains. Her dark brown curls nestle atop her crown, coiled back neatly behind ears trimmed delicately with pearl studs nestled close beside lobes awaiting attention—but none drawn upon now.","2nd woman on the left \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is the taller, buxom woman, wears a gown of muted rose-pink linen, its fabric drape suggesting both grace and subtle weight; it flows like watercolor across her form, catching the ambient light with delicate sheen along creases that whisper movement even when stillness reigns. The dress features softly gathered ruffles at the square neckline and short puffed sleeves edged with fine lace-like frills—a touch of romantic innocence. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. Around her neck rests a slender gold chain bearing a small, ornate pendant shaped like a miniature flower, echoing the floral motifs hinted at nearby blooms. Her hair is styled into a loose yet elegant braid woven through braids above her ear.","Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards ominously dark clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene."],"color":"#332922","bgcolor":"#593930"}],"links":[[7124,569,0,571,2,"STRING"],[7127,572,0,570,0,""],[7128,573,0,572,0,"INT"],[7131,572,1,576,0,"*"],[7132,572,0,571,0,"IMAGE"]],"groups":[],"definitions":{"subgraphs":[{"id":"7b90b761-3eb6-4fea-a398-96d6a2b5e423","version":1,"state":{"lastGroupId":1,"lastNodeId":601,"lastLinkId":7239,"lastRerouteId":0},"revision":0,"config":{},"name":"Flux.1Dev-LoRA","inputNode":{"id":-10,"bounding":[-2260.966670185349,-2047.3891440815066,141.390625,280]},"outputNode":{"id":-20,"bounding":[702.1702934243768,-2132.4819807975123,120,80]},"inputs":[{"id":"8f948745-f350-4498-aba2-231500f2195a","name":"seed","type":"INT","linkIds":[7133],"pos":[-2139.576045185349,-2027.3891440815066]},{"id":"90821474-425c-4c53-90e3-600060673de0","name":"guidance","type":"FLOAT","linkIds":[7200],"pos":[-2139.576045185349,-2007.3891440815066]},{"id":"b5baa8e8-74a5-4a2b-8ac8-827597a8e8b6","name":"lora_name","type":"COMBO","linkIds":[7203],"label":"LoRA-A","pos":[-2139.576045185349,-1987.3891440815066]},{"id":"a9c07046-9ed0-45c8-92a7-c8bcb739b8a1","name":"strength_model","type":"FLOAT","linkIds":[7204],"label":"LA-strength_model","pos":[-2139.576045185349,-1967.3891440815066]},{"id":"13a7793d-08c2-4c2e-b242-9da54a0f94b0","name":"strength_clip","type":"FLOAT","linkIds":[7205],"label":"LAstrength_clip","pos":[-2139.576045185349,-1947.3891440815066]},{"id":"a09d1af4-d2a5-49aa-bb72-ffffe73ae45b","name":"lora_name_1","type":"COMBO","linkIds":[7206],"label":"LoRA The file is too long and its contents have been truncated.

tool:

The file contents provided above are truncated/partial snippets. The complete content for these files IS accessible via querying.

If the user asks a question related to these files, and the provided snippets do not clearly answer it, you MUST use the file_search tool to search the full document contents before responding.

How to invoke the file_search tool¶

If the user's query isn't sufficiently answered by the provided snippets, parsed text, or images, you should invoke file_search.msearch. To search the full content of the available files, you must send one message with recipient to=file_search.msearch in the analysis channel. Your message should be structured exactly as follows: 

{
  'queries': ['Example query 1', 'Example query 2']  // List of up to five queries to search for.
}

Carefully balance the number of file_search calls and the quality of your response with the latency incurred by file_search calls.

Important guidelines for query formatting: - Your message must be a valid JSON object. Do not include any extraneous text, backticks, markdown formatting, or comments beyond what is shown. - The queries field must be a list of strings. - One of the queries MUST exactly match the user's original question, but stripped of any extraneous details, and with ambiguous references clearly resolved using context from the conversation. It MUST be a complete sentence. - Even if you suspect the user meant something else, one query MUST still be their original question.

How to handle results from file_search¶

The file search tool will respond with the relevant search results from the full files. After you receive these results: - Carefully evaluate each result to determine its relevance and quality. - Use the high-quality and relevant results to inform your answer to the user's question. - When possible, reuse information from prior file_search results and avoid repeating identical queries.

Tool usage instructions and ordering¶

When the user asks a question, carefully consider whether the provided snippets sufficiently answer the query, whether you need to invoke file_search.msearch, or another available tool (such as web, if available) should be used instead.

Tool-ordering Guidelines: 1. For queries related to uploaded files, if snippets are insufficient, strongly prefer invoking file_search.msearch over other tools. - Avoid using web.run, python analysis, or any other tools until you have either: - Confirmed with high confidence the answer is not in the files, or - Have high confidence file_search_tool queries are not sufficient to answer the query. 2. Avoid usage of web_search/web.run for queries related to uploaded files - Carefully consider whether the user's query is better answered by web search results rather than available snippets or results from file_search_tool. - Unless the user's query is clearly unrelated to the uploaded files, prefer using file_search.msearch. You can always follow up with a web search if necessary for a more complete response. 3. Avoid using python unless necessary or requested by user. - If the user's query is answerable from the parsed text and images from the uploaded files, or from file_search_tool, do not invoke python.

Always keep this instruction in mind for the rest of the conversation, as the user may reference these files or ask related questions later on.

Emily:

In this workflow, the T5 text is entered in the exact same places you’re already entering text — the CLIPTextEncode nodes.

What’s happening is:

  • Your DualCLIPLoader is loading two text encoders into a single “CLIP” object:
  • clip_name1 = t5xxl_fp8_e4m3fn.safetensors ✅ that’s the T5 side

  • clip_name2 = ViT-L-14-... ✅ that’s the CLIP-ViT side
    fileciteturn10file0

  • Then, each CLIPTextEncode node takes that combined “CLIP” object and your prompt text, and (under the hood) it routes/encodes the text through both encoders as needed.

So there is no separate “T5 text input” node in this graph because it’s abstracted into the DualCLIPLoader → CLIPTextEncode path.

If you wanted separate T5 vs CLIP text (optional)¶

That’s only possible if you use a workflow/nodeset that exposes them as separate encoders (i.e., separate “T5 encode” + separate “CLIP encode” nodes, depending on what custom nodes you have installed). Your current workflow doesn’t do that; it intentionally keeps them bundled.

If you tell me what “prompt control” you’re trying to gain by splitting them (e.g., “I want T5 to carry scene prose, but CLIP to carry short tags”), I can suggest a clean pattern for that without turning your graph into spaghetti.

Robyn:

I'm not aware of a context where I would want to do that. I wasn't aware that it was even possible until you mentioned it. But anyway: what I'm reading from the previous prompt analysis is that I would have to manually extract the working data from the fluff generated by QwenVL. Is that correct?

Emily:

Mostly yes — you (or a tool) need to distill it — because QwenVL is writing descriptive prose, not a control-oriented prompt.

But the good news: you don’t have to do it “by feel” every time. You can apply a repeatable rule-set that turns QwenVL fluff into something your SRPO/Flux conditioning will actually obey.

Why you can’t just paste QwenVL as-is¶

QwenVL outputs: - duplicated environment blocks - metaphors + “mood words” that don’t map cleanly to tokens - lots of low-priority background detail - occasional contradictions / drift

Models often respond to that by: - “averaging” the request → losing your hard constraints (shirt colour, dress construction, exact pose) - over-weighting repeated ideas (garden/lighting becomes the main subject) - introducing “pretty” but unasked-for changes (hair, body, neckline, etc.)

So yes: distillation is the control step.

A simple, reliable extraction method¶

When you see a QwenVL blob, sort sentences into 4 buckets:

Bucket 1 — Must-have (keep)¶

Identity + composition + pose + wardrobe + expression: - “two women” - “right woman shorter / left taller” - “moimeme3 / celestesh” - “off-shoulder corset dress…” - “rose-pink linen gown…” - “looking sideways… head tilt…”

Bucket 2 — Helpful but optional (keep 1–3 items max)¶

  • “soft diffused daylight”
  • “shallow depth of field”
  • “garden hedge background”

Bucket 3 — Decorative prose (delete)¶

  • “nature’s curtain separating worlds”
  • “sky’s mercy”
  • “whispers movement”
  • “petals fallen silently” Anything anthropomorphic or metaphorical goes.

Bucket 4 — Micro-detail (delete unless you’re fixing a problem)¶

  • “moss-stained stone slabs”
  • “radiator vertical lines echo architecture”
  • “dew-like moisture beneath her eye”
  • “tiny metallic buttons gleam faintly” (unless buttons are the thing you’re debugging)

Your new default prompt template¶

Use this every time and fill it from Bucket 1:

Two women, [framing: waist-up/full-body], [setting], [lighting].
Right: [identity], [height], [skin], [hair], [expression/gaze]. Outfit: [key garment construction + colour].
Left: [identity], [height], [skin], [hair], [expression/gaze]. Outfit: [key garment construction + colour].
Background: [1–2 simple items]. Style: [realistic/cinematic/shallow DOF].

That’s the whole trick.

If you want, I can make this “automatic”¶

If you paste a QwenVL description, I can return: 1) a tight prompt (obedient) 2) a slightly flavoured prompt (still controlled) 3) a short “keep / drop” list so you learn the pattern fast

But to your question: yes — some manual (or assisted) extraction is necessary if your goal is controllability rather than lyrical description.

Robyn:

Right. In my workflow involving the 2 individuals, one will be on the left, the other on the right. Celeste is always taller, Charlie: shorter. This could be template stuff. What they ar wearing can also be pre-written and plugged in. The real changes are: background, how they are positioned, what they are doing / interacting. Expressions. POV. Lighting. All pluggable.

Emily:

Exactly — you’re describing a prompt kit: fixed identity + fixed spatial roles + swappable “scene modules”. That’s the cleanest way to get consistency without fighting the model every run.

Here’s a practical template you can literally keep as your default and just swap the bracketed parts.

1) The “Two-Person Left/Right” Master Template (single text field)¶

IDENTITY + GEOMETRY (fixed)

Two women, both adults, full-body or 3/4 body, two-person scene.
Left: celestesh, taller, medium skin, long brown hair, confident posture.
Right: moimeme3, shorter, light skin, light brown hair, softer presence.
Composition: celestesh on the left, moimeme3 on the right, clear separation, both faces visible.

You only have to write this once.

2) Add your “Wardrobe Modules” (pick one and paste)¶

Wardrobe Module A (period)¶

Wardrobe: 18th-century inspired dresses, tasteful, no fetish framing.
celestesh wears a muted rose-pink linen gown with square neckline and short puff sleeves.
moimeme3 wears a dark brown off-the-shoulder corset dress with flounced sleeves, embroidered front panel, and a full skirt with tiered ruffled hem.

Wardrobe Module B (modern casual)¶

Wardrobe: modern casual, tasteful.
celestesh wears tailored black trousers and a fitted blouse.
moimeme3 wears a light cardigan and a skirt or jeans.

(Keep 2–3 “modules” tops, otherwise you’ll overthink.)

3) The “Scene Slots” you swap each time¶

A) Background (swap)¶

Setting: [garden hedge + path] / [train platform] / [kitchen] / [library stacks] / [seaside terrace] / [workshop wardrobe room]

Keep it to one line.

B) Blocking & interaction (swap)¶

Choose one verb phrase style and stick to it:

  • “They stand close, angled toward each other, mid-conversation.”
  • “celestesh adjusts moimeme3’s sleeve while moimeme3 watches her hands.”
  • “moimeme3 hands celestesh a folded note; celestesh reads it with a half-smile.”
  • “They walk side-by-side; celestesh speaks, moimeme3 listens.”

One or two actions, no more.

C) Expression & gaze (swap)¶

  • celestesh: [calm, amused, protective, appraising, sympathetic]
  • moimeme3: [nervous, thoughtful, defiant, relieved, curious]

D) POV / framing (swap)¶

  • “eye-level, medium shot”
  • “slight low angle” (gives Celeste authority)
  • “over-the-shoulder from celestesh” (Celeste POV emphasis)
  • “over-the-shoulder from moimeme3” (rare, if you want vulnerability)

E) Lighting (swap)¶

  • “soft diffused daylight”
  • “overcast, gentle contrast”
  • “warm interior lamplight”
  • “late afternoon, long soft shadows”

4) Put it together: a ready-to-use “fill-in” prompt¶

Copy/paste this and only edit the ALL CAPS lines:

Two women, both adults, two-person scene, cinematic realism.
Left: celestesh, taller, medium skin, long brown hair, confident posture.
Right: moimeme3, shorter, light skin, light brown hair, softer presence.
Composition: celestesh on the left, moimeme3 on the right, both faces visible.
Wardrobe: [PASTE WARDROBE MODULE].
Setting: [BACKGROUND].
Action: [BLOCKING / INTERACTION].
Expressions: celestesh is [CELESTE EXPRESSION]; moimeme3 is [CHARLIE EXPRESSION].
Camera: [POV / FRAMING]. Lighting: [LIGHTING]. Shallow depth of field.

That will behave so much better than QwenVL prose.

5) One extra trick that helps with left/right reliability¶

Models sometimes mirror. To reduce that:

  • Always include: “celestesh on the left, moimeme3 on the right”
  • Also include: “taller on the left, shorter on the right”
  • And in the action line, refer to left/right again:
  • “The taller woman on the left looks down slightly at the shorter woman on the right.”

Redundancy here is good — it’s a hard constraint.

6) If you want, I’ll build you 10 “scene modules”¶

Give me 3–5 settings you use a lot (e.g., garden, wardrobe room, train station, seaside terrace, classroom), and I’ll write: - 10 background lines - 10 interaction lines - 10 expression pairs …all in the same consistent syntax so you can mix-and-match like LEGO.

No web browsing needed, and it’ll make your prompting feel like operating a rig instead of wrestling a muse.

Robyn:

That would be awesome, yes please.

Oh, bear in mind that the CLIP text fields for Celeste and Charlie need to be separate, as these each have a LoRA hook for the celestesh LoRA and moimeme4 LoRA, respectively. The two get concatenated afterwards in the workflow. But, to build that original text, scene modules would be amazing!

Emily:

Perfect — if your workflow has two separate CLIP text fields (one per LoRA hook) and then concatenates them, you can still use a “scene module” system cleanly.

The trick is to split your text into:

1) a Shared Scene Block (same words in both fields), and
2) a Character Block (only that person’s identity/wardrobe/pose notes)

That way, both encodes “know” the setting and interaction, but each hook field stays loyal to its own character.

Below are 10 ready-made scene modules (each with Setting + Action + Expressions + Camera + Lighting), plus a reusable Shared Scene Block template, and then Celeste/Charlie blocks you can keep fixed.

How to structure your two CLIP fields¶

Shared Scene Block (paste this into BOTH CLIP text fields)¶

Use this exact scaffold and swap only the bracket parts:

SHARED SCENE BLOCK - two women, adults, cinematic realism, natural proportions
- celestesh on the left, taller on the left; moimeme4 on the right, shorter on the right
- both faces visible, clear separation, coherent anatomy, hands plausible
- Setting: [module setting line]
- Action: [module action line]
- Expressions: [module expression line]
- Camera: [module camera line]
- Lighting: [module lighting line]

Why both fields? Because once concatenated, you want the combined conditioning to agree on the “world” and the blocking. If only one field contains the scene, the other hook sometimes “hallucinates” its own.

Celeste CLIP field (Celeste-only block)¶

Paste after the Shared Scene Block in the celestesh field:

CELESTE BLOCK - celestesh, taller, confident posture, composed presence
- wardrobe: muted rose-pink linen gown, square neckline, short puff sleeves, small buttons down the bodice, delicate pendant necklace
- hair: long brown hair, simple braid detail, tidy and intentional
- avoid fetish framing, respectful tone, character-focused

Charlie CLIP field (Charlie-only block)¶

Paste after the Shared Scene Block in the moimeme4 field:

CHARLIE BLOCK - moimeme4, shorter, softer presence, attentive posture
- wardrobe: dark brown off-the-shoulder corset dress, flounced sleeves, embroidered front panel, full skirt with tiered ruffled hem, pearl stud earrings
- hair: light brown hair in a loose updo, neat but slightly imperfect
- avoid fetish framing, respectful tone, character-focused

Important: In the Celeste field, don’t include “moimeme4” as a keyword (and vice versa) unless you need it for left/right. Over-mentioning the other identity can cause bleed.

10 scene modules you can plug in¶

Each module below is written as 5 lines to paste into the Shared Scene Block.

Module 01 — Garden hedge, whispered check-in¶

  • Setting: quiet garden path with tall green hedge, a few roses, soft background blur
  • Action: celestesh leans slightly toward moimeme4 as if giving calm advice; moimeme4 turns her shoulders toward celestesh, listening
  • Expressions: celestesh is gently amused and steady; moimeme4 is thoughtful, a little unsure but receptive
  • Camera: eye-level medium shot, both in frame, slight emphasis on celestesh as the anchor
  • Lighting: soft diffused daylight, no harsh shadows, shallow depth of field

Module 02 — Wardrobe workroom, competence in motion¶

  • Setting: wardrobe workroom, bolts of fabric, ribbons, sewing table, labeled drawers, tidy clutter
  • Action: celestesh holds up a garment for inspection; moimeme4 steadies the hem with careful hands, both focused on the same detail
  • Expressions: celestesh is appraising and satisfied; moimeme4 is concentrated, quietly proud of getting it right
  • Camera: three-quarter view, hands and fabric visible, faces still readable
  • Lighting: warm interior lamplight, practical and realistic, gentle contrast

Module 03 — Train platform, “move like you belong”¶

  • Setting: train platform with a bench, timetable signage, distant commuters blurred, period-costume contrast implied
  • Action: celestesh stands between moimeme4 and the open space, subtly shielding; moimeme4 adjusts her skirt and steadies her posture
  • Expressions: celestesh is calm and protective without fuss; moimeme4 is apprehensive but trying to look composed
  • Camera: slightly wider shot, full-body or 3/4, so posture and clothing read clearly
  • Lighting: overcast daylight or late afternoon softness, gentle shadows

Module 04 — Seaside terrace, big talk without melodrama¶

  • Setting: seaside terrace overlooking water, railing, wind-touched hair, distant horizon
  • Action: celestesh gestures toward the horizon as she speaks; moimeme4 holds the railing lightly, listening, half-turned toward celestesh
  • Expressions: celestesh is earnest and persuasive; moimeme4 is conflicted but attentive, weighing the cost
  • Camera: medium-wide, slight low angle that gives celestesh quiet authority
  • Lighting: cool coastal daylight, soft highlights, no glossy “romance poster” vibe

Module 05 — Donor reception, polite danger¶

  • Setting: elegant hall or garden reception, blurred guests, a suggestion of wealth and scrutiny
  • Action: celestesh greets someone off-camera with poised confidence; moimeme4 stands half a step behind, attentive, hands folded naturally
  • Expressions: celestesh is socially flawless with a private edge; moimeme4 is neutral and careful, reading the room
  • Camera: shoulder-level framing, celestesh slightly forward in composition, both faces visible
  • Lighting: warm event lighting, soft bokeh, realistic

Module 06 — Corridor pause, “are you okay?”¶

  • Setting: quiet corridor outside wardrobe/rooms, doors and wall sconces, hush of backstage space
  • Action: celestesh turns moimeme4 gently by the elbow to face her; moimeme4 exhales and nods, as if admitting something
  • Expressions: celestesh is concerned but controlled; moimeme4 is relieved to be checked on, still tense
  • Camera: close-medium, intimate but not romanticized; emphasis on faces and small gestures
  • Lighting: dimmer interior light with soft falloff, calm mood

Module 07 — Garden bench, practical coaching¶

  • Setting: garden bench near trimmed hedges, gravel path, a place for quiet instruction
  • Action: celestesh demonstrates skirt movement with a small step and hand placement; moimeme4 mirrors her, concentrating
  • Expressions: celestesh is gently directive, almost teacher-like; moimeme4 is focused, slightly embarrassed but determined
  • Camera: full-body or 3/4 to show leg/skirt mechanics clearly, both centered
  • Lighting: late afternoon sun filtered through leaves, soft and believable

Module 08 — Mirror check, “does this read as natural?”¶

  • Setting: dressing area with a tall mirror, fabric draped over a chair, pins and combs on a table
  • Action: moimeme4 looks at her reflection, hands still; celestesh stands beside her, adjusting one small detail near the shoulder or collar
  • Expressions: celestesh is precise and reassuring; moimeme4 is wary but calmer after the adjustment
  • Camera: mirror-inclusive composition (no weird reflections), medium shot, faces clear
  • Lighting: clean indoor light, neutral warmth, realistic skin tones

Module 09 — Rain at the windows, confidential tone¶

  • Setting: interior room with rain-streaked windows, muted background, soft hush
  • Action: celestesh speaks low, hands loosely clasped; moimeme4 sits or stands close, listening with full attention
  • Expressions: celestesh is reflective, slightly softened; moimeme4 is serious, quietly moved
  • Camera: close-medium, stable framing, minimal background distraction
  • Lighting: gray daylight from window, soft shadows, subdued palette

Module 10 — After-hours tidy-up, shared ritual¶

  • Setting: wardrobe room after closing, neatly stacked garments, empty hangers, swept floor, calm order
  • Action: celestesh hands moimeme4 folded fabric; moimeme4 accepts it carefully, their hands briefly aligning on the same bundle
  • Expressions: celestesh is quietly satisfied, fond in a restrained way; moimeme4 is grateful, steadier than before
  • Camera: medium shot, hands visible, faces visible, balanced composition
  • Lighting: warm practical light, end-of-day calm

One complete “assembled” example (ready to paste)¶

Celeste CLIP field example¶

  • two women, adults, cinematic realism, natural proportions
  • celestesh on the left, taller on the left; moimeme4 on the right, shorter on the right
  • both faces visible, clear separation, coherent anatomy, hands plausible
  • Setting: wardrobe workroom, bolts of fabric, ribbons, sewing table, labeled drawers, tidy clutter
  • Action: celestesh holds up a garment for inspection; moimeme4 steadies the hem with careful hands, both focused on the same detail
  • Expressions: celestesh is appraising and satisfied; moimeme4 is concentrated, quietly proud of getting it right
  • Camera: three-quarter view, hands and fabric visible, faces still readable
  • Lighting: warm interior lamplight, practical and realistic, gentle contrast
  • celestesh, taller, confident posture, composed presence
  • wardrobe: muted rose-pink linen gown, square neckline, short puff sleeves, small buttons down the bodice, delicate pendant necklace
  • hair: long brown hair, simple braid detail, tidy and intentional
  • avoid fetish framing, respectful tone, character-focused

Charlie CLIP field example¶

(same Shared Scene Block, then:) - moimeme4, shorter, softer presence, attentive posture
- wardrobe: dark brown off-the-shoulder corset dress, flounced sleeves, embroidered front panel, full skirt with tiered ruffled hem, pearl stud earrings
- hair: light brown hair in a loose updo, neat but slightly imperfect
- avoid fetish framing, respectful tone, character-focused

If you want to push “Celeste POV” without changing workflow¶

Add one line (in BOTH fields) in the Shared Scene Block: - Camera: slight over-the-shoulder from celestesh, celestesh foreground soft, moimeme4 midground sharp, celestesh still readable

That tends to preserve the “Celeste is the lens” feeling while still keeping both faces intact.

If you tell me the top 3 recurring locations you want (e.g., train platform, wardrobe room, garden, seaside terrace, classroom), I can generate another 20 modules tailored to those settings, including a few “high tension but clean” variants (donor scrutiny, staff corridor, public gaze) that keep the story’s social bite without feeding the wrong audience.

Robyn:

Before we go any further, would you be able to generate a .json, a workflow of these (what look like) sequential text CLIP blocks? I've never seen that. I've seen CLIP noodles come out of LoRA modules and then into text encoders, but the text encoders only ever output conditioning. So, I have trouble parsing what you were suggesting.

Emily:

Yep — what I was suggesting isn’t “sequential CLIP Text Encode blocks” (because you’re right: CLIP Text Encode only outputs conditioning). The “blocks” are STRING nodes that you concatenate before CLIP Text Encode.

So the flow is:

(Text blocks as strings) → StringConcatenate → one final prompt string → CLIP Text Encode → conditioning

That’s exactly how your LoRA-hook workflow is already structured (those PrimitiveStringMultiline + StringConcatenate nodes inside the group). You just didn’t have a mental model for it yet.

Here’s a standalone demo workflow .json that contains modular prompt blocks (Setting / Action / Camera / Style) and then produces two final strings: - LEFT_TEXT = shared blocks + Celeste block
- RIGHT_TEXT = shared blocks + Charlie block

You then plug those two strings into your two separate CLIP Text Encode nodes (the ones that are later concatenated in your pipeline).

Download the workflow JSON

How to use it in your 2-character LoRA-hook setup¶

  • Keep doing separate text encodes for left/right (as you already are).
  • Replace your current “single big prompt per person” text with:
  • the shared modules (location/action/camera/style)
  • plus the per-character module
  • Feed the resulting LEFT_TEXT into the left character CLIP Text Encode “text” input, and RIGHT_TEXT into the right character CLIP Text Encode.

Where does T5 text go in Flux/SRPO workflows?¶

In many Flux/SRPO setups, the “CLIP” object you pass around effectively covers the needed text-encoder stack (CLIP-L + T5) behind the scenes, so you still enter text once, and it gets encoded appropriately. If a workflow truly exposes separate encoders, you’d see separate “encode” nodes/paths for each. In your LoRA-hook workflows, the “text blocks” are simply string assembly feeding the encode(s).

If you want, paste a screenshot of the exact two CLIP Text Encode nodes you’re using in the SRPO version (the ones that error-highlight), and I’ll tell you exactly which “text” socket to feed with LEFT_TEXT/RIGHT_TEXT in your graph.

Robyn:

Beautiful, Emily. I'll have a study first thing in the morning. Need to get to bed as I'll be on deck making capucinos all morning... thank you for all your help!

Emily:

Night night, Robyn — and good luck with the cappuccino brigade. ☕️🙂

When you look at the JSON tomorrow, the only “conceptual” thing to remember is:

We’re not chaining CLIP encoders — we’re chaining strings before the encoder.
So you end up with two clean prompt strings (LEFT/RIGHT) that drop straight into your two CLIP Text Encode nodes.

If anything looks odd when you load it (node pack mismatch / missing nodes), tell me what ComfyUI highlights and I’ll adapt it to whatever string nodes you do have installed.

Robyn:

Hi Emily. I've incorporated your workflow into mine. It works, sort-of. The figures are very... Flux-y. They don't intereact much. Have a look - tell me what you think.

tool:

held out by voluminous dark petticoats underneath the skirt, black with a square neckline, intricate lace details on the bodice and sleeves.
